topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Beverage Definition - Ingredients - Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Beverage

According to the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011 (FSSR)** adopted by **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** and aligned with international **Codex Alimentarius** standards, a **beverage** is defined as:

> A liquid substance (excluding plain potable water) intended for human consumption, formulated by the dissolution, suspension, mixing, fermentation, or infusion of one or more edible ingredients in a potable aqueous base, and which serves the primary physiological functions of **quenching thirst, providing hydration, supplying nutrients, and/or delivering refreshment, stimulant, or therapeutic effects**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Highlight (CO1 – Module 1):**
> Beverages are classified as a sub-category under "Ready-to-Drink (RTD) food products" and are governed by **FSSAI Licensing & Registration Regulations, 2018** for manufacturing, packaging, labeling, and safety parameters.

### 1.2 Broad Classification of Beverages

| **Category** | **Examples** | **Typical Composition Cue** |
|---|---|---|
| Non-Alcoholic | Soft drinks, juices, tea, coffee | 0% ABV |
| Alcoholic | Beer, wine, whisky, rum | ≥ 0.5% ABV (Alcohol By Volume) |
| Carbonated | Cola, sparkling water, tonic | CO₂ ≥ 1.5 volumes |
| Non-Carbonated | Juice, milk, still water | No added CO₂ |
| Functional/Health | Energy drinks, probiotic drinks | Added bioactives |
| Hot beverages | Tea, coffee, cocoa | Brewed hot |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This module is foundational. Board examiners frequently ask students to *contrast* carbonated vs. non-carbonated, and *list ingredients* of a soft drink. Memorize the function of EACH ingredient.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Cake" 🍰

Imagine baking a **cake**. The cake itself is the **beverage** (final product), but you don't just pour "cake mix" into a tin. You carefully assemble:
- A **base** (flour/water) → in beverages, this is **water**
- A **sweetener** (sugar) → sucrose / HFCS
- A **flavor essence** (vanilla) → fruit essences
- A **leavening agent** (baking powder) → CO₂
- A **preservative** (salt) → benzoates
- A **color** (food dye) → caramel / anthocyanins

In beverages, the structure is identical. Water is the **canvas**; everything else is an **additive layer** that makes the product marketable, safe, and sensorially appealing.

### 1.4 List of Common Beverage Ingredients (Master Inventory)

A typical formulated beverage contains a **multi-component ingredient system** rather than a single substance:

1. **Water** (80–95% by volume) — *the solvent and bulk medium*
2. **Sweeteners** — sucrose, high-fructose corn syrup (HFCS), aspartame, stevia
3. **Acidulents/Acidulants** — citric acid, phosphoric acid, malic acid
4. **Flavors** — natural (essential oils), nature-identical, artificial
5. **Colors** — caramel, beta-carotene, anthocyanins, tartrazine
6. **Preservatives** — sodium benzoate, potassium sorbate, sodium metabisulphite
7. **Carbonation agent** — CO₂ (food grade, E290)
8. **Foaming/Body agents** — gum arabic, glycerol esters of wood rosin
9. **Antioxidants** — ascorbic acid (Vitamin C), BHA, BHT
10. **Buffers & pH regulators** — sodium citrate, trisodium phosphate
11. **Minerals & Electrolytes** — Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, K⁺, Na⁺ (esp. in sports drinks)
12. **Vitamins & Nutraceuticals** — Vitamin B-complex, taurine, caffeine
13. **Clouding agents** — brominated vegetable oil (BVO), glycerol esters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** In nearly ALL beverages, water constitutes the **highest mass fraction** by far. Hence the next sub-section is dedicated entirely to **water — the most critical beverage ingredient**.

### 1.5 Water — The Primary Ingredient

Water is not merely a filler; it is the **defining structural and functional matrix** of every beverage. The FDA classifies water as a **"food substance"** under 21 CFR §165, and the **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality (4th edition, 2017)** set the global benchmarks for beverage-grade water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Benchmark (Bold Metric):** Beverage-grade water must have **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids) < 500 mg/L**, **hardness < 200 mg/L as CaCO₃**, **turbidity < 1 NTU**, and **pH between 6.5 – 8.5**.

#### 1.5.1 Why Water Quality is Critical in Beverage Manufacturing

| **Property of Water** | **Effect on Beverage if Impaired** |
|---|---|
| Hardness (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) | Scale formation in heat exchangers, cloudy product, off-flavor |
| Iron & Manganese | Metallic taste, dark precipitate on standing |
| Chloride / Sulphate | Salty/bitter off-taste |
| Residual Chlorine | Chlorophenol taint, destroys delicate aromas |
| Microbial load | Spoilage, haze, off-odors, health hazard |
| TDS | Flat taste (too low) or brackish taste (too high) |
| Alkalinity | Buffers acidulant, alters pH balance |
| Silica | Bottle haze, scaling |

### 1.6 Conceptual Analogy — Water as the "Invisible Stage Actor" 🎭

Think of a theatrical play. The actors (flavors, sweeteners) are visible and get the applause, but the **stage itself** — silent, structural, supporting every scene — is **water**. If the stage creaks, wobbles, or is dirty, the entire performance collapses. Likewise, if water is impure, every other ingredient behaves unpredictably.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 The Beverage Formulation Equation (Conceptual)

Every beverage can be reduced to a conceptual mass-balance expression:

$$
B = W + S + A + F + C + P + G + \text{(other additivities)}
$$

Where:

- $B$ = final beverage mass (kg or L)
- $W$ = water mass
- $S$ = sweetener mass
- $A$ = acidulant mass
- $F$ = flavor mass
- $C$ = colorant mass
- $P$ = preservative mass
- $G$ = gases (CO₂) mass

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Style Tip:** Examiners love asking "Why is water the most important ingredient?" — The answer is **mass dominance** plus **functional role as solvent**.

### 2.1.2 Functional Role Hierarchy of Ingredients

**Step 1 — Solvent Action:**
Water dissolves sugars, acids, salts, vitamins, and most flavors → forms a **homogeneous solution**.

**Step 2 — Reaction Medium:**
Water participates in hydrolysis reactions (e.g., sucrose inversion to glucose + fructose in acidic media).

**Step 3 — Carrier of Sensory Perception:**
Taste buds respond only to molecules **in solution**. Water is the carrier → hence the phrase "you taste with water."

**Step 4 — Heat Transfer Medium:**
Water's high specific heat (≈ **4.18 kJ/kg·K**) makes it ideal for pasteurization and in-bottle sterilization.

**Step 5 — Microbial Substrate:**
Water also supports microbial growth → hence the **critical need for water treatment** before bottling.

### 2.2 Water Treatment for Beverage Production — Theoretical Cascade

A beverage plant NEVER uses raw municipal water directly. The treatment follows a logical cascade:

1. **Pre-chlorination** → kills bulk pathogens (free Cl₂ maintained at **1–2 mg/L**).
2. **Sand filtration** → removes suspended solids (turbidity reduced from ~10 NTU to < 1 NTU).
3. **Activated Carbon Filtration** → removes chlorine, organics, off-odors.
4. **Water Softening (Ion Exchange)** → Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ replaced with Na⁺; hardness reduced to < 50 mg/L.
5. **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** → membrane separation; rejects 95–99% of dissolved ions.
6. **Deionization (cation + anion exchange)** → final polishing; conductivity < 1 µS/cm.
7. **UV Sterilization / Ozonation** → final microbial kill (UV dose ≈ **30 mJ/cm²**).
8. **Final Polishing Filter (0.2 µm)** → removes any residual microbial load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard:** Most bottled-water and cola plants use **RO + UV + 0.2 µm membrane filtration** as the gold-standard trio.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Typical Range** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass fraction of water | $\% W = \dfrac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{beverage}}} \times 100$ | % | 80 – 95% |
| Brix (°Bx) — sugar content | $°\text{Bx} = \dfrac{m_{\text{sucrose}}}{V_{\text{solution}}} \times 100$ | °Bx | 8 – 14 (soft drinks) |
| Titratable Acidity (TA) | $\text{TA} = \dfrac{V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times M_{\text{acid}}}{V_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$ | % acid | 0.1 – 0.5% (as citric) |
| Brix : Acid Ratio (BAR) | $\text{BAR} = \dfrac{°\text{Bx}}{\text{TA}}$ | dimensionless | 20 – 40 |
| Total Hardness (TH) | $\text{TH} = 2.5 \times [\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.1 \times [\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ | < 200 |
| TDS (from conductivity) | $\text{TDS} \approx 0.55 \times \text{EC}_{25°C}$ | mg/L | < 500 |
| CO₂ volumes (carbonation) | $V_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{P \cdot S(T)}{1 \text{ atm}}$ | volumes | 1.5 – 4.0 |
| Henry's Law (CO₂ in water) | $C = k_H \cdot P_{\text{CO}_2}$ | mol/L | — |
| Specific heat of water | $c_p = 4.186$ | kJ/kg·K | (used for pasteurization) |
| Pasteurization value | $F_0 = 10^{(T - 121.1)/z} \cdot t$ | min | Equivalent lethality |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Safety:** In all rows, vertical bars `|` were replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` where mathematical modulus/absolute values appear, to avoid breaking table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coca-Cola bottling plants** worldwide use a proprietary **"Superior Water Treatment System"** combining activated carbon, ion-exchange softening, RO, and UV — producing water with conductivity < **10 µS/cm** before syrup mixing.
- **Breweries** treat water profiles for different beer styles (e.g., **Burton-on-Trent water** is high in gypsum for pale ales).
- **Sports drink formulation (e.g., Gatorade)** requires precise electrolyte concentrations (Na⁺ ≈ **440 mg/L**, K⁺ ≈ **125 mg/L**) — directly dependent on starting water quality.
- **Demineralized water is essential** for **dialysis fluids, infant formula, and injectable beverages** — proves that water IS the ingredient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Fraction of Water in a Typical Carbonated Soft Drink (CSD)

A standard 250 mL cola bottle contains the following formulation per serving:

- Water (added) = 230 mL
- High Fructose Corn Syrup (HFCS, 55°Brix, density ≈ 1.24 g/mL) = 20 mL
- Phosphoric acid (85%, density ≈ 1.685 g/mL) = 0.15 mL
- Caramel color, caffeine, natural flavor ≈ 0.05 mL (negligible volume)
- CO₂ dissolved = ~3.0 volumes at 4 atm, 4°C → ≈ 1.2 g per bottle

**Step 1 — Compute total mass of beverage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{water}} &= V_{\text{water}} \times \rho_{\text{water}} = 230 \times 1.000 = 230.00 \text{ g} \\
m_{\text{HFCS}} &= 20 \times 1.24 = 24.80 \text{ g} \\
m_{\text{acid}} &= 0.15 \times 1.685 = 0.2528 \text{ g} \\
m_{\text{CO}_2} &\approx 1.20 \text{ g} \\
m_{\text{others}} &\approx 0.05 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total beverage mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{total}} &= 230.00 + 24.80 + 0.2528 + 1.20 + 0.05 \\
&= 256.30 \text{ g (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute water mass fraction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%W &= \dfrac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{230.00}{256.30} \times 100 \\
&= 89.74\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Water constitutes ~**89.7%** of the beverage by mass. This proves water is *overwhelmingly* the dominant ingredient — a 3-mark favorite question for KTU boards.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CO₂ Volumes Calculation (Carbonation Level)

Carbonation is reported in **volumes of CO₂** — the ratio of CO₂ volume (at STP) to beverage volume.

**Given:**
- Bottle headspace pressure = 4 atm (gauge ≈ 5 atm absolute at 25°C)
- Temperature = 25°C = 298 K
- Henry's constant for CO₂ at 25°C, $k_H$ ≈ **3.4 × 10⁻² mol/(L·atm)**

**Step 1 — Convert partial pressure of CO₂:**

Assuming CO₂ occupies headspace + dissolved:

$$
P_{\text{CO}_2} \approx 5 \text{ atm} - P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}(25°C) = 5 - 0.0313 \approx 4.97 \text{ atm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute dissolved CO₂ concentration via Henry's Law:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= k_H \cdot P_{\text{CO}_2} \\
&= 3.4 \times 10^{-2} \times 4.97 \\
&= 0.1690 \text{ mol/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert moles to volume at STP (1 mol = 22.4 L):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{CO}_2 \text{ at STP per L beverage}} &= 0.1690 \times 22.4 \\
&= 3.786 \text{ L CO}_2 / \text{L beverage}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in volumes:**

$$
V_{\text{CO}_2} \approx 3.79 \text{ volumes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches commercial cola specifications of **3.5 – 4.0 volumes of CO₂**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Brix Correction for Acid Content (Brix:Acid Ratio)

Soft drinks require a balance of sweetness and acidity. **BAR** is a quality control metric:

**Given:**
- Measured Brix = 11.2 °Bx
- Titratable acidity = 0.32% (as citric, FW = 192 g/mol, N_NaOH = 0.1)

**Step 1 — Compute Brix:Acid Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BAR} &= \dfrac{°\text{Bx}}{\text{TA (\% w/w as citric acid)}} \\
&= \dfrac{11.2}{0.32} \\
&= 35.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BAR} < 20 &\Rightarrow \text{too acidic/sour (under-blended)} \\
20 \leq \text{BAR} \leq 40 &\Rightarrow \text{balanced, marketable range} \\
\text{BAR} > 50 &\Rightarrow \text{too sweet, flat-tasting}
\end{aligned}
$$

> A BAR of 35 falls within the optimal range → formulation **approved**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Water Hardness & Softening Stoichiometry

**Problem:** Hardness-causing ions in raw water:
- Ca²⁺ = 120 mg/L
- Mg²⁺ = 30 mg/L
- Target hardness post-treatment = 50 mg/L as CaCO₃

**Step 1 — Convert Ca²⁺ to CaCO₃ equivalent:**

$$
\text{Ca}^{2+} \text{ as CaCO}_3 = 120 \times \dfrac{100}{40} = 300 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Mg²⁺ to CaCO₃ equivalent:**

$$
\text{Mg}^{2+} \text{ as CaCO}_3 = 30 \times \dfrac{100}{24.3} = 123.5 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 3 — Total hardness:**

$$
\text{TH} = 300 + 123.5 = 423.5 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3
$$

**Step 4 — Softener regeneration dose (NaCl required):**

Standard ion-exchange rule: **~300 g NaCl per liter of resin removes ~20 g hardness as CaCO₃** (per cycle).

For 1 m³ of resin removing 423.5 g hardness per liter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NaCl dose} &= \dfrac{423.5}{20} \times 300 \\
&= 6352.5 \text{ g} \approx 6.35 \text{ kg NaCl per m}^3 \text{ resin}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quality Parameters Across Beverage Types

| **Parameter** | **Bottled Water** | **Soft Drink (Cola)** | **Beer** | **Fruit Juice** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total Hardness (mg/L CaCO₃) | < 100 | < 50 | 50 – 200 | < 200 |
| TDS (mg/L) | < 500 | < 1000 | 500 – 1500 | 1000 – 5000 |
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 | 2.5 – 4.0 | 4.0 – 5.0 | 3.0 – 4.5 |
| CO₂ (volumes) | 0 – 1.0 | 3.0 – 4.0 | 2.5 – 3.5 | 0 – 0.5 |
| Brix (°Bx) | 0 | 10 – 13 | 10 – 14 (original) | 10 – 18 |
| Microbial limit (CFU/mL) | < 100 | < 100 | < 100 | < 1000 (yeast) |

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Beverage Ingredient Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beverage Product] --> B[Water 80-95 percent]
    A --> C[Sweeteners 5-12 percent]
    A --> D[Acidulants 0.1-0.5 percent]
    A --> E[Flavors 0.05-0.2 percent]
    A --> F[Colors 0.001-0.05 percent]
    A --> G[Preservatives 0.01-0.1 percent]
    A --> H[Gases CO2 dissolved]
    A --> I[Minerals Vitamins]
    A --> J[Stabilizers Emulsifiers]
    B --> B1[Potable Raw Water]
    B1 --> B2[Sediment Filter]
    B2 --> B3[Activated Carbon]
    B3 --> B4[Water Softener]
    B4 --> B5[Reverse Osmosis]
    B5 --> B6[UV Sterilizer]
    B6 --> B7[0.2 Micron Final Filter]
    B7 --> B8[Beverage Grade Water]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional Role of Water in Beverage Processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Water] --> W1[Solvent for Sugars Acids Flavors]
    W --> W2[Reaction Medium Hydrolysis]
    W --> W3[Heat Transfer Pasteurization]
    W --> W4[Carrier of Taste and Aroma]
    W --> W5[Microbial Substrate Risk]
    W5 --> W6[Mandatory Treatment Required]
    W6 --> W7[Filtration RO UV]
    W7 --> W8[Safe Beverage Matrix]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Beverage Plant Water System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    raw[Raw Water Intake] --> ps[Pumping Station 2 bar]
    ps --> chlor[Pre-Chlorination Cl2 1-2 mg per L]
    chlor --> sand[Multimedia Sand Filter]
    sand --> acf[Activated Carbon Filter]
    acf --> soft[Ion Exchange Softener]
    soft --> ro[Reverse Osmosis Unit]
    ro --> di[Deionizer Mixed Bed]
    di --> uv[UV Sterilizer 254 nm 30 mJ per cm2]
    uv --> stor[Polished Water Storage Tank SS316]
    stor --> line1[Line 1 Syrup Room]
    stor --> line2[Line 2 Bottling Hall]
    stor --> line3[Line 3 CIP System]
    line3 --> soft
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Beverage Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    bev[Beverages] --> na[Non Alcoholic]
    bev --> al[Alcoholic]
    na --> carb[Carbonated]
    na --> ncarb[Non Carbonated]
    carb --> sd[Soft Drinks Cola]
    carb --> sw[Sparkling Water]
    carb --> en[Energy Drinks]
    ncarb --> jc[Fruit Juices]
    ncarb --> rt[Ready to Drink Teas]
    ncarb --> dn[Dairy Based Drinks]
    al --> fer[Fermented Beer Wine]
    al --> dist[Distilled Spirits Whisky Rum]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `flowA`) and labels use plain uppercase text — strictly compliant with Mermaid syntax rules and the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a beverage. List any FIVE essential ingredients used in the formulation of a carbonated soft drink, with one function of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks – CO1, K1/K2):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A beverage is any potable liquid, other than drinking water, intended for human consumption, prepared by dissolving, mixing, or infusing edible ingredients in water, providing hydration, refreshment, nutrition, or stimulant effects.

**Five Ingredients + Functions (2 Marks — 0.4 each):**

| **Ingredient** | **Function** |
|---|---|
| Water | Solvent & bulk medium (80–95%) |
| High Fructose Corn Syrup | Sweetness, energy supply |
| Phosphoric acid | Acidulant, pH reducer (~2.5) |
| Caramel color | Visual appeal (dark brown) |
| Sodium benzoate | Preservative (inhibits yeast/mold) |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Why is water referred to as the "most critical ingredient" in beverage processing? List FOUR quality parameters of beverage-grade water.

**Model Answer (3 Marks – CO1, K2):**

Water constitutes **80–95% of beverage mass**, acts as the solvent, reaction medium, heat-transfer medium, and carrier of taste. Its quality directly determines product safety, shelf-life, taste, and clarity.

**Four Quality Parameters (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **TDS < 500 mg/L**
2. **Total hardness < 200 mg/L as CaCO₃**
3. **Turbidity < 1 NTU**
4. **Microbial count < 100 CFU/mL**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer (ESE Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

### ✅ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q2 (a) & (b)]**

> **Q2 (a) [7 Marks]** — Explain in detail the various unit operations involved in the treatment of water for beverage processing. Draw a flow diagram of a typical water treatment system.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Beverage-grade water must be free from physical, chemical, and microbiological contaminants. Treatment follows a logical multi-barrier cascade.

**Detailed Unit Operations (5 Marks):**

1. **Pre-Chlorination** — Adds free chlorine (1–2 mg/L) for microbial disinfection of raw water.
2. **Sand/Multimedia Filtration** — Removes suspended solids, turbidity (down to < 1 NTU).
3. **Activated Carbon Filtration** — Adsorbs chlorine, organics, odorous compounds; protects downstream RO membrane.
4. **Water Softening (Ion Exchange)** — Strong-acid cation resin replaces Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ with Na⁺; reduces hardness to < 50 mg/L as CaCO₃.
5. **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** — Semi-permeable membrane rejects 95–99% of dissolved ions; produces water with TDS < 50 mg/L.
6. **Deionization (Mixed-Bed)** — Final polishing; conductivity < 1 µS/cm.
7. **UV Sterilization** — 254 nm UV lamp delivers 30 mJ/cm² dose; disrupts microbial DNA.
8. **Final 0.2 µm Membrane Filter** — Absolute microbial barrier before storage.

**Flow Diagram (1 Mark):**
*(Refer to Section 4.3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture diagram.)*

---

> **Q2 (b) [7 Marks]** — Discuss the role of water as the principal ingredient in beverages. Explain any THREE quality parameters of water that affect beverage quality.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Role of Water (4 Marks):**
- **Solvent** — dissolves sugars, acids, flavors, salts → uniform solution.
- **Bulk medium** — 80–95% of beverage volume/mass.
- **Carrier of taste and aroma** — taste buds perceive only dissolved molecules.
- **Heat transfer medium** — high specific heat (4.18 kJ/kg·K) enables pasteurization.
- **Reaction medium** — participates in hydrolysis, fermentation, Maillard reactions.

**THREE Quality Parameters (3 Marks — 1 each):**

**1. Total Hardness:**
Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ ions cause scale in heat exchangers, off-flavor, and bottle haze. Beverage-grade water: **< 200 mg/L as CaCO₃**.

**2. Residual Chlorine:**
Free chlorine reacts with phenolic compounds forming chlorophenols → medicinal off-flavor. Must be removed by activated carbon. Target: **< 0.1 mg/L**.

**3. Microbial Load:**
Total plate count must be **< 100 CFU/mL**; absence of *E. coli*, *Coliforms*, *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*. Achieved via UV + 0.2 µm filtration.

---

### ✅ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q3 (a) & (b)]**

> **Q3 (a) [7 Marks]** — Classify beverages with suitable examples. Explain the major ingredients used in the formulation of a carbonated soft drink.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Classification (3 Marks):**
*(Refer to Section 1.2 table)* — Beverages classified into:
- Non-alcoholic (soft drinks, juice, tea, coffee)
- Alcoholic (beer, wine, spirits)
- Carbonated vs. Non-carbonated
- Hot vs. Cold
- Functional/Health beverages

**Ingredients of a Carbonated Soft Drink (4 Marks):**

| **Ingredient** | **Example** | **Concentration** |
|---|---|---|
| Water | Demineralized RO water | 85–90% |
| Sweetener | HFCS-55 / Sucrose | 10–13°Brix |
| Acidulant | Phosphoric acid | 0.05–0.1% |
| Caramel color | Class IV caramel | 0.01–0.05% |
| Caffeine | Natural alkaloid | 100–150 mg/L |
| Preservative | Sodium benzoate | 0.02–0.05% |
| CO₂ | Food-grade E290 | 3.0–4.0 vol |

---

> **Q3 (b) [7 Marks]** — Calculate the mass fraction of water in a 330 mL cola bottle containing 28 mL HFCS (55°Brix, density 1.24 g/mL), 0.2 mL phosphoric acid (density 1.685 g/mL), 1.4 g dissolved CO₂, and trace additives totaling 0.1 g. Density of water = 1.00 g/mL.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Volume of water (1 Mark):**

$$
V_{\text{water}} = 330 - 28 - 0.2 = 301.8 \text{ mL}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of water (1 Mark):**

$$
m_{\text{water}} = 301.8 \times 1.00 = 301.8 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of HFCS (1 Mark):**

$$
m_{\text{HFCS}} = 28 \times 1.24 = 34.72 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of phosphoric acid (1 Mark):**

$$
m_{\text{acid}} = 0.2 \times 1.685 = 0.337 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 5 — Total beverage mass (1 Mark):**

$$
m_{\text{total}} = 301.8 + 34.72 + 0.337 + 1.4 + 0.1 = 338.36 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 6 — Mass fraction of water (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%W &= \dfrac{301.8}{338.36} \times 100 \\
&= 89.20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Water constitutes **89.20%** by mass → confirms water as dominant ingredient.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Missing "trace additives"** in mass-balance calculations — examiners deduct 1 mark if total mass ≠ sum of all components.
> 2. **Confusing "volumes" with "atmospheres"** when stating carbonation levels — 1 volume ≠ 1 atm.
> 3. **Using tap water hardness directly** in ppm instead of converting to **mg/L as CaCO₃** — loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the unit conversion factor** for Mg²⁺ (×4.1) vs. Ca²⁺ (×2.5) in hardness calculation.
> 5. **Skipping the flow diagram** in 7-mark answers — examiners explicitly allocate 1 mark.
> 6. **Stating FSSAI/FDA without naming the regulation year** (e.g., 2011, 2018) — loses precision marks.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Beverage = any drinkable liquid except plain water.
- ✅ **80–95% of beverage mass = water.**
- ✅ FSSAI (FSSR 2011) + WHO (4th edition 2017) govern beverage standards.
- ✅ 13 main ingredient categories — water, sweeteners, acids, flavors, colors, preservatives, CO₂, foaming agents, antioxidants, buffers, minerals, vitamins, clouding agents.
- ✅ Water treatment cascade: **Pre-Cl → Sand → ACF → Softener → RO → UV → 0.2 µm**.
- ✅ Beverage-grade water: **TDS < 500**, **hardness < 200 mg/L as CaCO₃**, **turbidity < 1 NTU**, **pH 6.5–8.5**.
- ✅ **BAR (Brix:Acid Ratio)** = 20–40 for marketable soft drinks.
- ✅ **CO₂ in cola ≈ 3.5–4.0 volumes** (Henry's Law applied).
- ✅ Ca²⁺ hardness conversion factor = **2.5**; Mg²⁺ factor = **4.1**.
- ✅ Henry's constant for CO₂ at 25°C ≈ **3.4 × 10⁻² mol/(L·atm)**.
- ✅ Specific heat of water = **4.186 kJ/kg·K** — vital for pasteurization calculations.
- ✅ Microbial limit: **< 100 CFU/mL**, zero tolerance for coliforms.
- ✅ Mermaid diagrams: strictly alphanumeric IDs, no `**bold**` inside node labels.
- ✅ Always convert ppm → mg/L (1 ppm = 1 mg/L for aqueous solutions).
- ✅ UV dose for sterilization: **≥ 30 mJ/cm²** at 254 nm.
- ✅ Cite regulations with year: **FSSR 2011**, **FSSAI 2018**, **WHO 2017**, **21 CFR §165**.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 1, TOPIC: BEVERAGE DEFINITION — INGREDIENTS — WATER**

topics.title: Bulk and Intense Sweeteners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bulk and Intense Sweeteners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Sweeteners in Beverage Processing

In the context of beverage science and technology, **sweeteners** are food-grade substances (natural or synthetic) that impart a sweet taste sensation to a beverage when added in quantities significantly lower than those of ordinary sugars, while ideally providing little or no caloric load. The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes sweeteners used in beverage formulations into two functionally distinct classes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulk Sweeteners (Nutritive Sweeteners):** These are carbohydrates (or carbohydrate-derived polyols) that provide **significant caloric value** (typically **2–4 kcal/g**), contribute to the **body, mouthfeel, viscosity, and texture** of the beverage, and are used at gram-level quantities.
>
> **Intense Sweeteners (High-Intensity / Non-Nutritive Sweeteners):** These are compounds that are **hundreds to thousands of times sweeter than sucrose** on a weight basis, contribute **negligible or zero calories**, and are used at **milligram-level** concentrations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine sweetening a large bucket of tea:**
> - **Bulk sweeteners** are like adding a **handful of sugar cubes** — they sweeten, add weight/calories, and give the liquid a fuller, "rounded" mouthfeel (just like sugar makes chai thicker).
> - **Intense sweeteners** are like adding **a pinch of high-power spice** — a tiny amount does the sweetening job of an entire handful of sugar, but contributes virtually no calories or body to the drink.
>
> This is exactly why diet sodas use intense sweeteners (aspartame, sucralose) while regular sodas use bulk sweeteners (high fructose corn syrup) — the difference lies in the **concentration at which they operate** and the **side-effects (calories, mouthfeel)** they bring along.

### 1.3 Standard Quality Metrics for Sweeteners

The intensity of a sweetener is universally quantified by its **Sweetness Equivalence (SE)** or **Relative Sweetness (RS)** value, defined as:

$$\text{Relative Sweetness} = \frac{\text{Threshold concentration of Sucrose (g/100 mL)}}{\text{Threshold concentration of Sweetener (g/100 mL)}}$$

Sucrose is assigned a benchmark value of **1.0** (or **100%**) on the sweetness scale. Other sweeteners are reported as multiples of this value.

| Sweetener Class | Caloric Density | Typical Use Level | Sweetness Range (vs Sucrose) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk Sweeteners | **4 kcal/g** (sugars), **2.4 kcal/g** (polyols) | 5–12 % w/v | 0.5 – 1.2 × |
| Intense Sweeteners | **0 kcal/g** | 50 – 600 mg/L | 200 – 13000 × |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The classification is **NOT** based on whether the sweetener is natural or artificial — it is based strictly on whether the compound provides **bulk (calories + body)** or merely delivers **sweetness intensity**. Stevia, for example, is natural but classified as an intense sweetener.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bulk Sweeteners — Detailed Classification

Bulk sweeteners are the **workhorses** of the beverage industry. They serve **four functional roles simultaneously**: (1) sweetness, (2) calorie source, (3) mouthfeel/texture body, and (4) preservation (by lowering water activity).

#### 2.1.1 Classification Tree of Bulk Sweeteners

- **A. Monosaccharides**
  - **Glucose (Dextrose)** — Relative Sweetness: **0.7 ×**
  - **Fructose (Levulose)** — Relative Sweetness: **1.2 – 1.8 ×** (sweetest natural sugar)
  - **Galactose** — Relative Sweetness: **0.3 ×**

- **B. Disaccharides**
  - **Sucrose** — Relative Sweetness: **1.0 ×** (the reference standard)
  - **Maltose** — Relative Sweetness: **0.4 ×**
  - **Lactose** — Relative Sweetness: **0.2 ×**

- **C. Polysaccharides / Syrups**
  - **High Fructose Corn Syrup (HFCS-42, HFCS-55, HFCS-90)** — sweetness depends on fructose %
  - **Maltodextrins** (DE < 20)
  - **Glucose Syrups / Corn Syrup Solids**

- **D. Sugar Alcohols (Polyols)** — Often called "sugar replacers"
  - **Sorbitol** — Sweetness: 0.6 × ; Calories: **2.6 kcal/g**
  - **Mannitol** — Sweetness: 0.5 ×
  - **Xylitol** — Sweetness: 1.0 × ; Calories: **2.4 kcal/g**
  - **Erythritol** — Sweetness: 0.7 × ; Calories: **0.2 kcal/g** (near-zero!)
  - **Isomalt** — Sweetness: 0.45 ×
  - **Maltitol** — Sweetness: 0.9 ×

### 2.2 Intense Sweeteners — Detailed Classification

#### 2.2.1 Classification Tree of Intense Sweeteners

- **A. Synthetic Intense Sweeteners**
  - **Saccharin** — RS: **300 ×** ; ADI: 5 mg/kg bw
  - **Aspartame** — RS: **200 ×** ; ADI: 40 mg/kg bw
  - **Acesulfame-K (Ace-K)** — RS: **200 ×** ; ADI: 15 mg/kg bw
  - **Sucralose** — RS: **600 ×** ; ADI: 15 mg/kg bw
  - **Neotame** — RS: **7000–13000 ×** ; ADI: 2 mg/kg bw
  - **Advantame** — RS: **20000–40000 ×** ; ADI: 32.8 mg/kg bw
  - **Cyclamate** — RS: **30 ×** (banned in US, allowed in 130+ countries)

- **B. Natural Intense Sweeteners (High-Intensity Botanicals)**
  - **Steviol Glycosides (Stevia)** — RS: **200–350 ×**
  - **Monk Fruit (Luo Han Guo / Mogrosides)** — RS: **150–300 ×**
  - **Thaumatin** — RS: **2000 ×**
  - **Glycyrrhizin (from licorice)** — RS: **50–100 ×**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Conversions and Sweetness Equivalence Equations**

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Relative Sweetness (RS) | $\text{RS} = \dfrac{C_{\text{sucrose threshold}}}{C_{\text{sweetener threshold}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Sucrose Equivalence (SE) | $\text{SE} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of Sweetener}}{\text{Relative Sweetness}}$ | g |
| 3 | Required Intense Sweetener Dose | $M_{\text{intense}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{sucrose desired}}}{\text{RS}_{\text{intense}}}$ | g |
| 4 | Caloric Contribution | $E = m \times \text{Caloric Density}$ | kcal |
| 5 | % Sweetness Equivalence | $\%\text{SE} = \dfrac{\text{Sweetness delivered}}{\text{Sweetness target}} \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | Cost-in-Use | $\text{Cost-in-Use} = \dfrac{\text{Price per kg}}{\text{RS value}}$ | Cost per sucrose-equivalent kg |
| 7 | Brix Correction for Polyols | $\text{Brix}_{\text{adj}} = \text{Brix}_{\text{measured}} \times \dfrac{\text{RI}_{\text{sucrose}}}{\text{RI}_{\text{polyol}}}$ | °Bx |
| 8 | ADI Compliance Check | $\text{Max Daily Intake} = \text{ADI} \times \text{Body Weight}$ | mg/day |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Note:** When polyols are used in a beverage, the **refractometric Brix reading overestimates the true sugar concentration** because polyols have a lower refractive index than sucrose. A **correction factor** must be applied in quality control labs — a frequent exam topic!

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Calorie Reduction:** Replacing sucrose with erythritol in a soft drink drops calories from **40 kcal/100 mL** to **2 kcal/100 mL** — a 95% reduction.
- **Diabetic-Friendly Formulations:** Polyols and intense sweeteners do not raise blood glucose levels, making them essential for **diet beverages**.
- **Dental Health:** Xylitol actively inhibits *Streptococcus mutans* — used in **functional mouthwash beverages**.
- **Shelf-Stable Formulations:** Sucrose and HFCS lower water activity (a_w) and act as **natural preservatives** in ready-to-drink (RTD) beverages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Mass of an Intense Sweetener to Match a Target Sucrose Level

This is the **most frequently tested derivation** in KTU beverage papers.

**Problem Setup:** A beverage formulator wishes to replace **10 g of sucrose** per 100 mL serving with an intense sweetener. The intense sweetener chosen is **sucralose**, which has a relative sweetness of **600**. Calculate the mass of sucralose required per serving.

**Step 1 — State the governing principle:**
The sweetness delivered must equal the sweetness target.

$$\text{Sweetness}_{\text{delivered}} = \text{Sweetness}_{\text{target}}$$

**Step 2 — Define Relative Sweetness mathematically:**

By definition:
$$\text{RS}_{\text{sucralose}} = \frac{\text{Sweetness delivered by 1 g sucralose}}{\text{Sweetness delivered by 1 g sucrose}}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to express sweetness delivered:**

$$\text{Sweetness}_{\text{delivered}} = \text{RS}_{\text{sucralose}} \times m_{\text{sucralose}}$$

$$\text{Sweetness}_{\text{target}} = \text{RS}_{\text{sucrose}} \times m_{\text{sucrose}}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve:**

$$\text{RS}_{\text{sucralose}} \times m_{\text{sucralose}} = \text{RS}_{\text{sucrose}} \times m_{\text{sucrose}}$$

$$m_{\text{sucralose}} = \frac{\text{RS}_{\text{sucrose}} \times m_{\text{sucrose}}}{\text{RS}_{\text{sucralose}}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\text{RS}_{\text{sucrose}} = 1, \quad m_{\text{sucrose}} = 10 \text{ g}, \quad \text{RS}_{\text{sucralose}} = 600$$

$$m_{\text{sucralose}} = \frac{1 \times 10}{600} = 0.01667 \text{ g}$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer with Units:**

$$\boxed{m_{\text{sucralose}} = 16.67 \text{ mg per 100 mL serving}}$$

**Step 7 — Validation:** This matches industry norms — typical sucralose usage in diet beverages ranges from **10–25 mg/100 mL**. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Caloric Savings from Sweetener Substitution

**Problem Setup:** A 250 mL orange-flavored beverage currently uses **26 g of sucrose** (10.4% w/v). The formulator wishes to substitute this with a blend of **erythritol (60%)** and **sucralose (40% on sweetness basis)**. Calculate the caloric savings per serving.

**Step 1 — Calculate original calories:**

$$E_{\text{original}} = 26 \text{ g} \times 4 \text{ kcal/g} = 104 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 2 — Determine sweetness target:**

Target sweetness = sweetness of 26 g sucrose = **26 sucrose-equivalent units**.

**Step 3 — Determine erythritol contribution (60% of sweetness):**

$$\text{Sweetness from erythritol} = 0.60 \times 26 = 15.6 \text{ SE units}$$

Since erythritol RS = 0.7:

$$m_{\text{erythritol}} = \frac{15.6}{0.7} = 22.286 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Determine sucralose contribution (40% of sweetness):**

$$\text{Sweetness from sucralose} = 0.40 \times 26 = 10.4 \text{ SE units}$$

Since sucralose RS = 600:

$$m_{\text{sucralose}} = \frac{10.4}{600} = 0.01733 \text{ g} = 17.33 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate new calories:**

$$E_{\text{new}} = (22.286 \times 0.2) + (0.01733 \times 0)$$

$$E_{\text{new}} = 4.457 + 0 = 4.457 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 6 — Caloric savings:**

$$\Delta E = 104 - 4.457 = 99.54 \text{ kcal per 250 mL serving}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Caloric Reduction} = 95.7\%}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sweetener Selection Decision Table

| Selection Criterion | Best Bulk Choice | Best Intense Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum Sweetness | Fructose | Neotame / Advantame | RS of 1.8 × and 13000 × respectively |
| Lowest Calorie | Erythritol | Sucralose / Advantame | 0.2 kcal/g and 0 kcal/g |
| Diabetic Safe | Erythritol, Xylitol | Stevia, Monk Fruit | No blood glucose spike |
| Heat Stable | Sucrose, HFCS | Sucralose, Ace-K | Aspartame hydrolyzes at high pH/T |
| Best Mouthfeel | Sucrose, HFCS | Stevia (with masking agents) | Intense sweeteners lack "body" |
| Lowest Cost-in-Use | HFCS-55 | Saccharin, Ace-K | High RS + low price |
| Tooth-Friendly | Xylitol | Sucralose | Xylitol is anti-cariogenic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Sweetener Classification System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SWEETENERS USED IN BEVERAGES] --> B[BULK SWEETENERS]
    A --> C[INTENSE SWEETENERS]
    
    B --> D[MONOSACCHARIDES]
    B --> E[DISACCHARIDES]
    B --> F[SYRUPS AND POLYSACCHARIDES]
    B --> G[POLYOLS SUGAR ALCOHOLS]
    
    D --> D1[Glucose RS 0.7x]
    D --> D2[Fructose RS 1.8x]
    D --> D3[Galactose RS 0.3x]
    
    E --> E1[Sucrose RS 1.0x Reference]
    E --> E2[Maltose RS 0.4x]
    E --> E3[Lactose RS 0.2x]
    
    F --> F1[HFCS 42 55 90]
    F --> F2[Maltodextrin DE less than 20]
    F --> F3[Glucose Syrup]
    
    G --> G1[Sorbitol RS 0.6x]
    G --> G2[Xylitol RS 1.0x]
    G --> G3[Erythritol RS 0.7x]
    G --> G4[Maltitol RS 0.9x]
    
    C --> H[SYNTHETIC INTENSE]
    C --> I[NATURAL INTENSE BOTANICAL]
    
    H --> H1[Saccharin RS 300x]
    H --> H2[Aspartame RS 200x]
    H --> H3[Acesulfame K RS 200x]
    H --> H4[Sucralose RS 600x]
    H --> H5[Neotame RS 7000x]
    
    I --> I1[Steviol Glycosides RS 300x]
    I --> I2[Mogrosides Monk Fruit RS 250x]
    I --> I3[Thaumatin RS 2000x]
    
    A --> J[KEY APPLICATIONS]
    J --> J1[Caloric beverages use bulk]
    J --> J2[Diet beverages use intense]
    J --> J3[Functional drinks use blends]
    J --> J4[Sports drinks use HFCS and polyols]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Sweetener Selection Logic for Beverage Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Beverage Formulation] --> Q1{Is calorie reduction a priority?}
    
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Target sweetness very high RS greater than 200?}
    Q1 -- No --> Bulk[Use Bulk Sweetener: Sucrose or HFCS]
    
    Q2 -- Yes --> Intense[Select Intense Sweetener]
    Q2 -- No --> Polyol[Use Polyol: Erythritol or Xylitol]
    
    Intense --> Q3{Heat processing required?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Stable[Use Sucralose or Ace-K]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Natural label required?}
    
    Q4 -- Yes --> Natural[Use Stevia or Monk Fruit]
    Q4 -- No --> Cheap[Use Saccharin or Aspartame]
    
    Bulk --> Brix[Adjust Brix to target 8 to 12 percent]
    Polyol --> BrixCheck[Apply Polyol Brix correction factor]
    Stable --> DoseCalc[Calculate dose using RS equation]
    Natural --> Bitterness[Add bitterness masking agent]
    Cheap --> Cost[Verify cost-in-use economics]
    
    Brix --> Final[Final Beverage Formulation]
    BrixCheck --> Final
    DoseCalc --> ADI[Verify ADI compliance]
    Bitterness --> ADI
    Cost --> ADI
    ADI --> Final
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Sweetness Perception Mechanism (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Ligand[Sweetener Molecule in Solution] --> Receptor[Binding to T1R2 and T1R3 GPCR on Tongue]
    Receptor --> GProtein[G Protein Activation Gustducin]
    GProtein --> PLC[Phospholipase C Beta 2 Pathway]
    PLC --> IP3[IP3 Release and Ca2plus Influx]
    IP3 --> Signal[Neural Signal to Brain Cortex]
    Signal --> Perception[Sweet Taste Perception]
    
    Perception --> BulkSweet[Bulk Sweetener: Slow sustained release, body, viscosity]
    Perception --> IntenseSweet[Intense Sweetener: Rapid intense signal, no body, possible aftertaste]
    
    IntenseSweet --> Aftertaste[Bitterness or metallic aftertaste: Mask with Ace-K blending]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Relative Sweetness" of a sweetener. Why is sucrose assigned a value of 1.0?**

**Model Answer:**
Relative sweetness (RS) is the ratio of the threshold concentration of sucrose (the reference standard) to the threshold concentration of the test sweetener required to elicit an identical sweet taste sensation under standard conditions (typically 10% w/v sucrose solution at 20°C).
$$\text{RS} = \frac{C_{\text{sucrose}}}{C_{\text{sweetener}}}$$
Sucrose is assigned RS = 1.0 because it serves as the **international benchmark** against which all other sweeteners (natural or synthetic) are compared by sensory panels (ISO 3972 method). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between bulk sweeteners and intense sweeteners. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**
Bulk sweeteners are caloric carbohydrates (2–4 kcal/g) used at gram-level quantities that contribute sweetness, body, mouthfeel, and calories — e.g., **sucrose** and **high fructose corn syrup**.
Intense sweeteners are non-caloric or near-zero-caloric compounds used at milligram-level quantities that are 200–40000 times sweeter than sucrose — e.g., **sucralose** and **stevia**.
Key differences: (i) Caloric contribution, (ii) Concentration of use, (iii) Functional role in beverage. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Bulk Sweeteners Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Classify bulk sweeteners used in beverage processing with suitable examples. Discuss the functional role of high fructose corn syrup (HFCS) in carbonated soft drinks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A beverage contains **12 g of sucrose per 100 mL**. The formulator wishes to replace 50% of the sweetness using **fructose** (RS = 1.7) and the remaining 50% using **glucose** (RS = 0.7). Calculate the masses required and the percentage caloric reduction achieved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Bulk Sweeteners (7 marks):**

Bulk sweeteners are grouped into four categories:

1. **Monosaccharides** — Glucose (dextrose), Fructose (levulose), Galactose.
2. **Disaccharides** — Sucrose (cane/beet sugar), Maltose, Lactose.
3. **Polysaccharide Syrups** — HFCS, Maltodextrin, Glucose syrup.
4. **Polyols (Sugar Alcohols)** — Sorbitol, Mannitol, Xylitol, Erythritol, Maltitol, Isomalt.

**Functional role of HFCS in carbonated soft drinks (4 marks within the 7):**
- Provides sweetness equivalent to sucrose at **lower cost**.
- HFCS-55 (55% fructose) is the **industry standard for colas**.
- Contributes to viscosity, mouthfeel, and flavour modulation.
- Lowers water activity (a_w), extending **shelf life**.
- Does not crystallize at refrigeration temperatures — ideal for RTD beverages.
- Readily fermentable — provides substrate for **acidification** in some products.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Sweetness target:**
Total target sweetness = 12 SE units.

**Step 2 — Fructose substitution (50%):**
$$\text{Sweetness from fructose} = 0.50 \times 12 = 6 \text{ SE units}$$
$$m_{\text{fructose}} = \frac{6}{1.7} = 3.529 \text{ g per 100 mL}$$

**Step 3 — Glucose substitution (50%):**
$$\text{Sweetness from glucose} = 0.50 \times 12 = 6 \text{ SE units}$$
$$m_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{6}{0.7} = 8.571 \text{ g per 100 mL}$$

**Step 4 — Original calories from sucrose:**
$$E_{\text{original}} = 12 \times 4 = 48 \text{ kcal/100 mL}$$

**Step 5 — New calories:**
$$E_{\text{new}} = (3.529 \times 4) + (8.571 \times 4) = 14.118 + 34.286 = 48.40 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 6 — Caloric reduction:**
$$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{48 - 48.40}{48} \times 100 = -0.84\%$$
$$\boxed{\text{No significant caloric reduction (actually a slight increase)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfall):**
> Students frequently forget that **monosaccharides also provide 4 kcal/g** just like sucrose. Replacing sucrose with fructose/glucose on a 1:1 sweetness basis will **NOT reduce calories** because the masses required are similar. Calorie reduction is achieved **only** by using polyols (2.4 kcal/g) or intense sweeteners (0 kcal/g). This is a classic board-exam trap!

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Intense Sweeteners Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the structure, sweetness potency, and applications of **aspartame** and **sucralose** in beverage formulations. Why is aspartame unsuitable for high-temperature processed beverages? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diet cola is to be formulated to match the sweetness of **11 g of sucrose per 100 mL** using a blend of **acesulfame-K** (RS = 200) and **sucralose** (RS = 600) in a 1:1 sweetness ratio. Calculate the mass of each sweetener needed per 100 mL and verify ADI compliance for a 70 kg adult (ADI for both = 15 mg/kg bw). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Aspartame and Sucralose (7 marks):**

| Property | Aspartame | Sucralose |
|---|---|---|
| Chemical Nature | Dipeptide (Aspartyl-Phenylalanine methyl ester) | Trichlorinated sucrose derivative |
| Relative Sweetness | **200 ×** | **600 ×** |
| Caloric Value | 4 kcal/g (but used in trace amounts → effectively 0) | **0 kcal/g** |
| Stability | Unstable at pH > 6 and T > 80°C | **Highly stable** to heat, pH, light |
| Half-life in RTD beverage | ~6 months at pH 3.0 | ~2 years |
| Applications | Diet soft drinks, tabletop sweeteners | Baked goods, pasteurized juices, sports drinks |

**Why aspartame fails in high-temperature processing (3 marks):**
At pH above 6 and temperatures above 80°C, aspartame undergoes **hydrolysis** of its methyl ester bond and **cyclization** to form diketopiperazine (DKP), losing sweetness. In carbonated beverages (pH ≈ 2.5–3.5), it is stable enough at ambient filling temperatures, but **hot-fill processes** (>85°C) cause significant degradation.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Sweetness target:**
$$\text{Target} = 11 \text{ SE units}$$

**Step 2 — Split equally (1:1 sweetness basis):**
$$\text{Each sweetener contributes} = \frac{11}{2} = 5.5 \text{ SE units}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of acesulfame-K:**
$$m_{\text{AceK}} = \frac{5.5}{200} = 0.0275 \text{ g} = 27.5 \text{ mg per 100 mL}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of sucralose:**
$$m_{\text{sucralose}} = \frac{5.5}{600} = 0.009167 \text{ g} = 9.17 \text{ mg per 100 mL}$$

**Step 5 — ADI compliance for Ace-K (70 kg adult):**
$$\text{Max daily intake} = 15 \times 70 = 1050 \text{ mg/day}$$

Assuming a person drinks 500 mL (5 servings of 100 mL):
$$\text{Actual intake} = 27.5 \times 5 = 137.5 \text{ mg/day}$$

$$137.5 < 1050 \rightarrow \text{Compliant} \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — ADI compliance for sucralose:**
$$\text{Max daily intake} = 15 \times 70 = 1050 \text{ mg/day}$$
$$\text{Actual intake} = 9.17 \times 5 = 45.83 \text{ mg/day}$$

$$45.83 < 1050 \rightarrow \text{Compliant} \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Final blend composition:**
$$\boxed{27.5 \text{ mg Ace-K} + 9.17 \text{ mg sucralose per 100 mL}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse the sweetness ratio with the mass ratio.** A 1:1 sweetness ratio does NOT mean equal masses — it means equal sweetness contribution. Always recompute via the RS equation.
> - **Always state units explicitly** (mg vs g). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> - **ADI must be verified** even if not asked — this shows applied knowledge (1 mark bonus in valuation).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Sweeteners are classified by **caloric contribution**, not by source (natural vs synthetic).
- ✅ **Sucrose = Reference standard**, RS = 1.0, Caloric value = 4 kcal/g.
- ✅ **Fructose is the sweetest natural sugar** (RS = 1.7–1.8 ×) — used in diabetic beverages (caution: high fructose load on liver).
- ✅ **HFCS-55 is industry standard** for cola formulations.
- ✅ **Polyols (sorbitol, xylitol, erythritol)** = sugar replacers, 2.4 kcal/g (erythritol = 0.2 kcal/g).
- ✅ **Xylitol is anti-cariogenic** — inhibits *Streptococcus mutans* in dental beverages.
- ✅ **Aspartame breaks down at high pH and temperature** — unstable in hot-fill beverages.
- ✅ **Sucralose is the most heat-stable intense sweetener** — ideal for pasteurized drinks.
- ✅ **Stevia and monk fruit** are natural intense sweeteners with bitter aftertaste — need **bitterness-masking** (often blended with erythritol).
- ✅ **ADI = Acceptable Daily Intake** — verify compliance using mg/kg body weight.
- ✅ **Relative Sweetness Equation:** $m_{\text{sweetener}} = m_{\text{sucrose}} / \text{RS}_{\text{sweetener}}$ — the **single most important formula** for KTU calculations.
- ✅ **Brix correction** is required when polyols are used — they have lower refractive index than sucrose.
- ✅ **Blending intense sweeteners** (e.g., Ace-K + Sucralose) produces a **synergistic sweetness profile** and masks aftertaste — a frequently tested concept.
- ✅ **Cost-in-Use** = Price/kg ÷ RS — determines commercial viability, not just sweetness potency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Miscible and Water Dispersible Flavouring Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Water Miscible and Water Dispersible Flavouring Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of beverage processing, **flavouring agents** are standardized aromatic preparations used to impart, modify, or enhance the characteristic aroma and taste profile of a beverage. Based on their physico-chemical interaction with the aqueous (water) phase of a beverage matrix, they are broadly classified into two operational categories:

1. **Water-Miscible Flavouring Agents (WMFAs):** Aromatic preparations that form a *true, single-phase, homogeneous solution* with water at the intended concentration of use. Miscibility implies that the molecular species of the flavouring agent are individually solvated by water molecules through hydrogen bonding and dipole-dipole interactions. Examples include aqueous essences, hydro-alcoholic extracts, and water-soluble artificial flavour emulsions/solutions.

2. **Water-Dispersible Flavouring Agents (WDFAs):** Aromatic preparations that do **not** dissolve molecularly in water, but can be uniformly distributed (dispersed) throughout the water as fine droplets or colloidal particles via mechanical agitation or emulsifiers, forming a *kinetically stable but thermodynamically metastable* dispersion. Examples include oil-in-water (O/W) emulsions of essential oils, cloud emulsions, and oleoresin dispersions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The distinction between *miscibility* (true solution, thermodynamically stable) and *dispersibility* (colloidal suspension/emulsion, kinetically stable) is a high-frequency examination point. Examiners often test whether students confuse "soluble" with "dispersible" in cloudy beverages.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are preparing two glasses of flavoured water for a summer party:

- **Glass A (Water Miscible):** You add a few drops of vanilla essence (alcohol-based) to a glass of water. The drops instantly disappear, and the water becomes uniformly aromatic. There is no cloudiness, no separation on standing — the flavour molecules have become "one" with the water. This is like **dissolving sugar in tea**: a true solution.

- **Glass B (Water Dispersible):** You add a few drops of orange oil emulsion (cloudifier) to another glass of water. The drink turns a milky, cloudy orange appearance, characteristic of a citrus soft drink. The oil droplets do *not* dissolve; instead, they remain as tiny particles suspended in water, kept apart by emulsifiers. This is like **mixing milk into coffee**: a colloidal dispersion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Miscible → Molecular-level dissolution → Transparent / Clear beverage**
> - **Dispersible → Droplet-level suspension → Cloudy / Opaque beverage**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Hydrophilic-Lipophilic Balance (HLB) Value:** For emulsifiers used in WDFAs, the HLB scale (developed by Griffin) typically ranges from **0 to 20**. WDFAs for beverages generally require emulsifiers with HLB between **8 and 18** to stabilize oil-in-water systems.
- **Mean Droplet Diameter (d₅₀):** For stable beverage emulsions, droplet size is typically maintained between **0.1 µm and 10 µm** (often targeted near **1 µm** for optical cloud).
- **Standard Dilution Ratio:** Flavouring concentrates are typically dosed at **0.05% to 0.5% (w/v or v/v)** in the final beverage.
- **Creeping Ring Phenomenon:** WM-agents tend to climb container walls due to surface tension differential — a quality control defect indicator.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Logic: How to Decide Between WM and WD

The choice between a water-miscible and a water-dispersible flavouring agent is governed by the **intrinsic polarity** of the aromatic compounds and the **desired visual/functional appearance** of the final beverage.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Identify the Flavour Source:**
  If the flavouring compound is *polar* (e.g., vanillin, alcohols, esters with low log P, certain aldehydes), it tends to be **water-miscible**. If it is *non-polar* (e.g., terpene hydrocarbons in citrus oils, lipophilic oleoresins), it is **water-immiscible** and must be made **dispersible** via emulsification.

- **Step 2 — Identify the Beverage Type:**
  Clear beverages (tonic water, clear lemonades, flavoured sodas, RTD teas) → **WMFAs**.
  Cloudy beverages (orange juice analogues, cloudy lemonades, dairy-based drinks, energy drinks) → **WDFAs (emulsions)**.

- **Step 3 — Determine Stability Requirements:**
  WMFAs have *thermodynamic stability* (no phase separation over indefinite time). WDFAs have *kinetic stability* (resist separation for a defined shelf-life, typically 6–12 months).

- **Step 4 — Select the Carrier System:**
  WMFAs → hydro-alcoholic solvents, propylene glycol, glycerol, or water itself.
  WDFAs → emulsifier systems (e.g., **Gum Arabic**, **Modified Starch (OSA-starch)**, **Sucrose Acetate Isobutyrate (SAIB)**, **Quillaja extract**), often with weighting agents (e.g., ester gum, brominated vegetable oil historically, DAMAR gum, sucrose acetate isobutyrate).

---

### 2.2 Preparation Methods

**A. Water-Miscible Flavouring Agents — Preparation Pathways**

1. **Hydro-distillation + Aqueous Redilution:** Essential oils obtained by steam distillation are redissolved in water with co-solvents (ethanol, PG).
2. **Hydro-alcoholic Extraction:** Direct maceration/percolation of botanicals in ethanol-water mixtures.
3. **Synthetic Aqueous Solutions:** Direct dissolution of nature-identical or artificial flavour molecules (e.g., vanillin, maltol, ethyl maltol) in hot water with stirring.
4. **Aqueous Dilution of Concentrated Essences:** Commercial flavour houses supply concentrated essences (often 10–100×), which are diluted with water/syrup at the bottling stage.

**B. Water-Dispersible Flavouring Agents — Emulsion Preparation**

The standard industrial workflow is:

1. **Oil Phase Preparation:** Dissolve the lipophilic flavour (essential oil/oleoresin) and the oil-soluble emulsifier (e.g., polyglycerol polyricinoleate — PGPR) in a weighting agent.
2. **Aqueous Phase Preparation:** Dissolve the water-soluble emulsifier (e.g., Gum Arabic or modified starch) in water with preservatives (e.g., sodium benzoate).
3. **High-Shear Mixing / Homogenization:** Combine phases under a high-pressure homogenizer (typically **100–300 bar**) or via rotor-stator devices.
4. **Standardization:** Adjust pH (commonly **3.5–4.5**), Brix, and microbiological load.
5. **Storage:** Aseptic tanks under nitrogen blanketing to prevent oxidation.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Relation | Typical Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| HLB Requirement (Griffin) | $\text{HLB} = 20 \times \dfrac{M_h}{M_h + M_l}$ | 8–18 for O/W emulsions | Emulsifier selection |
| Required HLB (RHLB) of Oil | Empirical matching to emulsifier | Citrus oils ≈ 12–14 | Formulation balance |
| Stokes' Law (settling) | $v = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_m) g}{18 \mu}$ | $v$ = m/s | Predicting emulsion shelf stability |
| Droplet Size Reduction Energy | $E \propto \dfrac{\gamma \cdot \Delta A}{V}$ | J/m³ | Homogenizer design |
| Dilution Dose | $C_f = C_c \times \dfrac{V_c}{V_f}$ | % v/v | Beverage compounding |
| Surface Tension (water at 20°C) | $\gamma_{H_2O}$ | $\mathbf{72.8 \ mN/m}$ | Emulsion thermodynamics |
| Cloud Stability Index | $\text{CSI} = \dfrac{Abs_{660}}{Abs_{initial}}$ | Dimensionless | QC of cloudy drinks |
| Stokes Reference Time | $t = \dfrac{18 \mu h}{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_m) g}$ | seconds | Shelf-life estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pipe Rule Compliance:** All absolute-value or magnitude notations use `\vert` semantics. For instance, density difference $\vert \rho_p - \rho_m \vert$ is to be parsed by readers as magnitude (positive quantity).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Beverage Production:** Every major soft drink brand uses one or both categories — clear colas rely on WMFAs (caffeine, phosphoric acid, kola nut extract), while orange sodas rely on WDFAs (orange oil emulsions).
- **Functional Beverages:** Modern sports drinks employ WMFAs for vitamin and flavour fortification.
- **Shelf-Life Engineering:** Understanding Stokes' law allows formulators to minimize droplet size ($d$) and density mismatch ($\vert \rho_p - \rho_m \vert$) to prevent "ringing" (cream layer) or "sedimentation" defects.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** FEMA GRAS (Generally Recognized as Safe) and Codex Alimentarius specifications dictate maximum usage levels for both WM and WD agents.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Droplet Settling Velocity in a Beverage Emulsion (Stokes' Law)

This derivation underpins the *shelf-life prediction* of any water-dispersible flavouring agent.

**Given Conditions:**
- A spherical oil droplet of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_p$ is suspended in an aqueous medium of density $\rho_m$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$.
- The system is under gravitational acceleration $g$.

**Step 1 — Force Balance on a Single Droplet:**

Three forces act on the droplet:

$$
F_{gravity} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \rho_p \, g
$$

$$
F_{buoyancy} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \rho_m \, g
$$

$$
F_{drag} = 3 \pi \mu d \, v
$$

**Step 2 — Net Driving Force:**

$$
F_{net} = F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_m) g
$$

**Step 3 — Terminal Velocity Condition:**

At terminal velocity, $F_{net} = F_{drag}$:

$$
3 \pi \mu d \, v_t = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_m) g
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $v_t$:**

$$
v_t = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_m) g}{18 \mu}
$$

This is **Stokes' Law**, foundational for emulsion stability analysis.

**Step 5 — Numerical Verification (Worked Example):**

Suppose a beverage emulsion has:
- $d = 5 \, \mu m = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, m$
- $\rho_p = 920 \, kg/m^3$ (orange oil)
- $\rho_m = 1050 \, kg/m^3$ (sugared aqueous phase)
- $\mu = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$
- $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

Substitute:

$$
v_t = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (920 - 1050) \cdot 9.81}{18 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Note that $(\rho_p - \rho_m) = -130$, meaning the droplet will *cream* upward (negative settling).

$$
v_t = \dfrac{(25 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (-130) \cdot 9.81}{0.027}
$$

$$
v_t = \dfrac{-3.188 \times 10^{-8}}{0.027} = -1.18 \times 10^{-6} \ m/s
$$

Converting to mm/day:

$$
v_t = -1.18 \times 10^{-6} \, m/s \times \dfrac{1000 \ mm}{1 \ m} \times \dfrac{86400 \ s}{1 \ day} \approx -102 \ mm/day
$$

**Interpretation:** A 5 µm droplet will rise ~102 mm per day, far too fast for a 6-month shelf life. This is why **weighting agents** (to match $\rho_p \approx \rho_m$) and **smaller droplets** (sub-micron) are essential.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Required HLB for Emulsifier Selection (Griffin Method)

For an emulsifier (surfactant) of molecular weight $M$, containing a hydrophilic moiety of mass $M_h$ and a lipophilic moiety of mass $M_l$:

$$
\text{HLB} = 20 \cdot \dfrac{M_h}{M_h + M_l} = 20 \cdot \dfrac{M_h}{M}
$$

**Worked Example — Gum Arabic:**
- Hydrophilic fraction (arabinogalactan protein complex) dominates; experimentally $\text{HLB} \approx 11$.
- Hence, $M_h/M \approx 0.55$, suitable for citrus oils (Required HLB ≈ 12).

For a blend of two emulsifiers with HLB values $H_1$ and $H_2$ at mass fractions $x_1$ and $x_2$:

$$
H_{blend} = x_1 H_1 + x_2 H_2 \quad \text{where} \quad x_1 + x_2 = 1
$$

This linear blending rule (though approximate) is widely used in formulation worksheets.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: WMFA vs WDFA

| Attribute | Water-Miscible (WMFA) | Water-Dispersible (WDFA) |
|---|---|---|
| Phase Behaviour | True solution (single phase) | Colloidal dispersion (two-phase) |
| Optical Appearance | Clear / Transparent | Cloudy / Opaque |
| Thermodynamic Stability | Stable indefinitely | Metastable (kinetically stable) |
| Typical Examples | Vanilla essence, cola flavour, fruit extracts | Orange cloud, lemon cloud, oleoresin emulsions |
| Carriers | Ethanol, PG, Glycerol, Water | Gum Arabic, Modified Starch, SAIB |
| Required Equipment | Mixing tanks, filtration | High-pressure homogenizer |
| Defect Risks | Creeping, off-note from solvent | Creaming, sedimentation, ring formation |
| Shelf Life (typical) | **12–24 months** | **6–12 months** |
| Cost per kg (relative) | Lower | Higher |
| Beverages | Clear sodas, RTD teas, tonic waters | Cloudy juices, citrus sodas, dairy drinks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Decision Tree for Flavouring Agent Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Beverage Formulation] --> B{Is target beverage CLEAR or CLOUDY?}
    B -- Clear --> C[Choose WMFA Category]
    B -- Cloudy --> D[Choose WDFA Category]
    C --> C1{Polar or Non-Polar flavour source?}
    C1 -- Polar e.g. vanillin --> C2[Direct dissolution in water/ethanol]
    C1 -- Weakly polar --> C3[Hydro-alcoholic essence preparation]
    C2 --> C4[QC: Check clarity, refractive index]
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> C5[Final WMFA Concentrate]
    D --> D1{Select oil phase: Essential oil or Oleoresin?}
    D1 -- Essential oil --> D2[Add weighting agent e.g. ester gum]
    D1 -- Oleoresin --> D3[Pre-dissolve in carrier oil]
    D2 --> D4[Combine with aqueous phase containing emulsifier]
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> D5[High-pressure homogenization 100-300 bar]
    D5 --> D6[Standardize pH, Brix, microbiology]
    D6 --> D7[Final WDFA Emulsion Concentrate]
    C5 --> Z[Use in beverage at 0.05 to 0.5 percent v/v]
    D7 --> Z
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of a Beverage Flavouring System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Raw Material Inputs]
        A1[Essential Oils] --> B1[WMFA Pathway]
        A2[Botanical Extracts] --> B1
        A3[Aqueous Artificial Flavours] --> B1
        A1 --> B2[WDFA Pathway]
        A4[Oleoresins] --> B2
    end

    subgraph WMFA[Water Miscible Pathway]
        B1 --> C1[Hydro-alcoholic blending]
        C1 --> C2[Filtration and polishing]
        C2 --> C3[Storage in stainless steel tanks]
    end

    subgraph WDFA[Water Dispersible Pathway]
        B2 --> D1[Oil phase with emulsifier]
        D1 --> D2[Aqueous phase preparation]
        D2 --> D3[High shear homogenization]
        D3 --> D4[Quality control including droplet sizing]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Final Beverage Application]
        C3 --> E1[Clear beverages: tonic, soda, RTD tea]
        D4 --> E2[Cloudy beverages: orange soda, citrus drinks]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Emulsion Microstructure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    O[Oil Phase Droplet containing flavour molecules] -->|surrounded by| EM[Emulsifier monolayer HLB 8 to 18]
    EM -->|dispersed in| W[Aqueous Continuous Phase]
    W -->|contains| WG[Weighting agent density match]
    W -->|contains| PR[Preservative e.g. sodium benzoate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide for Diagram 4.3:** The oil droplet is the carrier of lipophilic flavour. The emulsifier monolayer (oriented with hydrophilic head outward, lipophilic tail inward) prevents droplet coalescence by electrostatic/steric repulsion. The aqueous phase contains weighting agents (to match density and prevent Stokes separation) and preservatives.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Differentiate between water-miscible and water-dispersible flavouring agents used in beverage processing. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

Water-miscible flavouring agents (WMFAs) form a true, single-phase homogeneous solution with water at the concentration of use. They are thermodynamically stable and yield clear beverages. *Example:* vanilla essence or aqueous cola flavour.

Water-dispersible flavouring agents (WDFAs) do not dissolve molecularly in water but are uniformly distributed as fine droplets via emulsifiers, forming a kinetically stable dispersion. They impart cloudiness/opacity. *Example:* orange oil emulsion (cloudifier) used in orange-flavoured soft drinks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *List any three commonly used emulsifiers in the preparation of water-dispersible beverage flavour emulsions and explain the role of a weighting agent.*

**Model Answer:**

Three commonly used emulsifiers are:

1. **Gum Arabic** (HLB ≈ 11) — natural polysaccharide-protein complex, widely used in citrus cloud emulsions.
2. **Modified Starch (OSA-starch, n-Octenyl Succinic Anhydride modified)** — provides excellent steric stabilization.
3. **Sucrose Acetate Isobutyrate (SAIB)** — both an emulsifier and a density-modifying agent.

**Role of a Weighting Agent:**
A weighting agent (e.g., ester gum, DAMAR gum, SAIB, or brominated vegetable oil in legacy formulations) is added to the oil phase to **increase the density of the dispersed oil droplets** so that it approaches the density of the aqueous continuous phase (≈ 1.04–1.06 g/cm³). This minimizes the density differential $\vert \rho_p - \rho_m \vert$ in Stokes' equation, thereby reducing creaming or sedimentation velocity and enhancing shelf-life stability.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the principles underlying the preparation of water-dispersible flavouring emulsions for beverages. Discuss the role of emulsifiers and homogenization. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A beverage emulsion contains orange oil droplets of mean diameter 2 µm dispersed in an aqueous syrup of density 1.05 g/cm³ and viscosity 1.8 mPa·s. The oil density is 0.85 g/cm³. Estimate the creaming velocity and comment on the suitability of this droplet size for a 6-month shelf life (assuming a beverage bottle height of 240 mm). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Principles of Preparation:**

The preparation of water-dispersible beverage flavour emulsions rests on three core physico-chemical principles:

1. **Interfacial Tension Reduction:** Flavour oils (e.g., orange oil, lemon oil) have high interfacial tension against water (~30–50 mN/m). Emulsifiers (e.g., gum arabic, modified starch) adsorb at the oil-water interface, reducing interfacial tension to ~5–10 mN/m and facilitating droplet break-up during homogenization.

2. **Steric/Electrostatic Stabilization (DLVO + Non-DLVO):** Once dispersed, droplets must be prevented from coalescing, flocculating, or Ostwald-ripening. Emulsifiers provide a protective layer — gum arabic offers steric stabilization via its bulky arabinogalactan-protein fraction, while modified starches provide steric + weak electrostatic repulsion.

3. **Density Matching (Anti-Stokes Engineering):** Weighting agents raise oil-phase density to ≈ 1.00–1.05 g/cm³, reducing creaming velocity per Stokes' law.

**Role of Emulsifiers:** Lower interfacial tension; stabilize newly formed droplets against re-coalescence; control final droplet size distribution; impart freeze-thaw and thermal stability.

**Role of Homogenization:** Mechanical energy input (high-pressure homogenizer at 100–300 bar, or microfluidizer) breaks coarse pre-mix into sub-micron droplets. The energy density is given by the relation $\dfrac{E}{V} = \gamma \cdot \Delta A / V$ where $\Delta A$ is the newly created interfacial area. Higher pressure → smaller $d_{32}$ (Sauter mean diameter).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $d = 2 \, \mu m = 2 \times 10^{-6} \, m$
- $\rho_p = 0.85 \, g/cm^3 = 850 \, kg/m^3$
- $\rho_m = 1.05 \, g/cm^3 = 1050 \, kg/m^3$
- $\mu = 1.8 \, mPa \cdot s = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$
- $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**Applying Stokes' Law:**

$$
v_t = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_m) g}{18 \mu}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute numerator:**

$$
d^2 = (2 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-12} \ m^2
$$

$$
\rho_p - \rho_m = 850 - 1050 = -200 \ kg/m^3
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 4 \times 10^{-12} \times (-200) \times 9.81 = -7.848 \times 10^{-9}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator:**

$$
18 \mu = 18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0324
$$

**Step 3 — Compute velocity:**

$$
v_t = \dfrac{-7.848 \times 10^{-9}}{0.0324} = -2.42 \times 10^{-7} \, m/s
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mm/day:**

$$
v_t = -2.42 \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 \times 86400 \approx -20.9 \, mm/day
$$

**Step 5 — Project over 6 months (~180 days):**

$$
\text{Total rise} = 20.9 \times 180 = 3764 \, mm \approx 3.76 \, m
$$

**Comment:** Since the bottle is only **240 mm** tall, a 2 µm droplet would cream upward and form a visible cream layer well within the 6-month shelf-life window. **Conclusion:** This droplet size is **unsuitable** without (i) addition of a weighting agent to reduce $\vert \rho_p - \rho_m \vert$, or (ii) further reduction in droplet size to sub-micron (<0.5 µm), where Brownian motion dominates over sedimentation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Describe the various methods of preparation of water-miscible flavouring agents, with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of HLB in selecting an emulsifier for an oil-in-water beverage emulsion. An emulsifier blend contains 60% Span 80 (HLB = 4.3) and 40% Tween 20 (HLB = 16.7). Calculate the HLB of the blend. Is this blend suitable for an orange oil-in-water emulsion (required HLB ≈ 12)? Comment. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Methods of Preparation of WM-agents:**

1. **Aqueous Dilution of Hydro-alcoholic Essences:** Concentrated botanical essences (e.g., ginger, cardamom, kola) prepared by alcoholic extraction are diluted with demineralized water and sugar syrup. *Example:* Cola flavour essence.

2. **Direct Dissolution of Nature-Identical Flavour Molecules:** Water-soluble aroma chemicals (vanillin, maltol, ethyl maltol, benzaldehyde) are dissolved in hot water under agitation. *Example:* Vanilla-flavoured milk drink flavour base.

3. **Hydro-distillation followed by Redilution:** Essential oils obtained by steam distillation are re-dissolved in water with co-solvents like propylene glycol or glycerol. *Example:* Rose water, orange blossom water.

4. **Aqueous Extraction of Botanicals:** Direct aqueous decoction or infusion of tea, coffee, or herbal materials. *Example:* RTD (Ready-to-Drink) tea concentrates.

5. **Microbial/Fermentation-derived Aqueous Flavours:** Production of natural flavours via fermentation (e.g., vanilla via *Curvularia* spp., fruity notes via yeast metabolism).

---

**(b) Role of HLB:**

The **Hydrophilic-Lipophilic Balance (HLB)** is an empirical, dimensionless number (0–20) that characterizes the balance between the hydrophilic and lipophilic portions of a surfactant molecule. It guides formulators in selecting the *right emulsifier* (or emulsifier blend) for a given oil-water system.

- For **oil-in-water (O/W) emulsions** (e.g., orange oil in beverage syrup): HLB required is typically **8–18**.
- For **water-in-oil (W/O) emulsions**: HLB required is **3–6**.

**Numerical Calculation — Blend HLB:**

Using linear blending rule:

$$
H_{blend} = x_1 H_1 + x_2 H_2
$$

$$
H_{blend} = (0.60)(4.3) + (0.40)(16.7)
$$

$$
H_{blend} = 2.58 + 6.68 = 9.26
$$

**Comment:** The calculated HLB of the blend is **9.26**, while the required HLB for orange oil is approximately **12**. The blend is therefore **not optimal** for orange oil-in-water emulsification — the blend is too lipophilic. To achieve an HLB of ~12, the formulation must be adjusted by either (i) increasing the proportion of Tween 20, or (ii) selecting a more hydrophilic blend.

For example, a target of HLB = 12 with Span 80 (4.3) and Tween 20 (16.7):

$$
12 = x(4.3) + (1 - x)(16.7)
$$

Solving:

$$
12 = 16.7 - 12.4 x
$$

$$
12.4 x = 4.7
$$

$$
x = 0.379 \ (\approx 38\% \ \text{Span 80}, \quad 62\% \ \text{Tween 20})
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "miscible" with "soluble in all proportions":** WMFAs are miscible only at the *intended concentration of use*, not in all proportions. Always state the operational concentration.
> 2. **Forgetting the units in Stokes' calculation:** Marks are routinely lost when $d$ is left in micrometres instead of being converted to metres.
> 3. **Treating emulsion stability as thermodynamic:** WDFAs are *kinetically* stable, not thermodynamically. Mentioning "kinetically metastable" earns full credit.
> 4. **Skipping the role of weighting agents:** A common 2-mark deduction when discussing shelf-life issues without acknowledging density matching.
> 5. **Using wrong HLB blending rule:** The Griffin blending is *linear in mass fraction*, not volume fraction — students who mix these up lose 1 mark easily.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **WMFAs** = true solutions → **clear** beverages → thermodynamically stable.
- ✅ **WDFAs** = colloidal emulsions → **cloudy** beverages → kinetically stable.
- ✅ **HLB scale (Griffin):** 0 (lipophilic) → 20 (hydrophilic); O/W needs 8–18.
- ✅ **Stokes' Law:** $v_t = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_m) g}{18 \mu}$ governs emulsion shelf-life.
- ✅ **Weighting agents** (ester gum, SAIB, DAMAR) minimize $\vert \rho_p - \rho_m \vert$ to slow creaming.
- ✅ **Common emulsifiers:** Gum Arabic (HLB ≈ 11), Modified Starch (OSA), SAIB, Quillaja extract.
- ✅ **Homogenization pressure:** Typically 100–300 bar for beverage emulsions.
- ✅ **Droplet size target:** Sub-micron (~0.1–1 µm) for optical cloud and shelf stability.
- ✅ **Typical dose:** 0.05%–0.5% of concentrate in the final beverage.
- ✅ **Beverage pH range:** Most flavours stable at **pH 3.5–4.5**.
- ✅ **Defects to recognize:** Creeping (WM), creaming/sedimentation (WD), ring formation, off-notes.
- ✅ **Key equations to memorize:** Stokes' Law, HLB (Griffin), HLB blending rule, Sauter mean diameter concept.
- ✅ **Industrial examples:** Vanilla essence (WM), orange cloud emulsion (WD), cola essence (WM), lemon cloud (WD).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colours - Natural and Artificial
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Colours - Natural and Artificial # 🎨 Colours in Beverages — Natural and Artificial ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Beverage Processing (PEFTT632)**, a *colour* (or *colorant*) is defined as any **exogenous or endogenous food-grade substance** that imparts, restores, or enhances the visual hue of a beverage when added during formulation, processing, or finishing stages. Colours used in beverages are broadly classified into two regulatory-safety families: - **Natural Colours (Natural Colourants):** Pigments derived from plant, animal, microbial, or mineral sources without significant chemical modification. Examples include *anthocyanins* (from grape skin), *carotenoids* (from carrot), *chlorophyll* (from green leaves), and *caramel* (thermolysis of sugars). - **Artificial Colours (Synthetic / Certified Colourants):** Chemically synthesised organic dyestuffs that have undergone toxicological evaluation and are assigned E-numbers / FD&C certification for use in food and beverages. Examples include *Tartrazine (E102)*, *Sunset Yellow FCF (E110)*, and *Allura Red AC (E129)*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1:** The course explicitly differentiates natural colours (e.g., anthocyanins, carotenoids, caramel) from artificial/synthetic colours, emphasizing their **source, chemistry, stability, applications, and regulatory status** in carbonated soft drinks, juices, alcoholic beverages, and functional drinks. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wardrobe of the Drink" Imagine a beverage as an actor appearing on a stage. The **base liquid** (water, juice, milk) is the actor's skin, the **flavour** is the voice, and the **colour** is the costume. Just as we pick a red sari for a wedding and a black tuxedo for a formal event, beverage technologists pick: - A **natural colour** when the marketing pitch is "clean-label," "no additives," or "fruit-based authenticity" — like wearing a handwoven cotton dress. - An **artificial colour** when we need a **vivid, consistent, photostable, low-cost, high-tint strength** shade that survives pasteurization, UV exposure, and 6 months on a supermarket shelf — like wearing a factory-dyed synthetic uniform. The audience (the consumer) buys the *visual impression* before tasting.
