Hence, colour is the **first sensory attribute** that drives purchase intent in beverages. ## 1.3 Why Colour Matters in Beverages — The Industrial "Why" > [!NOTE] > **Core Sensory & Quality Functions of Colours in Beverages:** > 1. **Visual identity** — establishes brand recognition (e.g., the iconic *Fanta orange* and *Pepsi blue*). > 2. **Flavour-colour congruence** — consumers expect strawberry drinks to be *red*, lime drinks to be *green*, and cola to be *dark brown*. A mismatch is rejected as "off" or "spoiled." > 3. **Compensation for pigment loss** during thermal processing (pasteurization, UHT) or storage. > 4. **Standardization of batch-to-batch colour** when raw materials (fruits, teas) vary in pigment content. > 5. **Functional addition** — some colours (e.g., anthocyanins, betalains) carry antioxidant / nutraceutical value. ## 1.4 The Standard CIE Colorimetric Reference While beverage processing rarely involves direct computation, the **CIELAB (L\*a\*b\*)** system is the international standard for measuring beverage colour: - **L\*** — Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white) - **a\*** — Redness (+) / Greenness (−) - **b\*** — Yellowness (+) / Blueness (−) - **ΔE (Delta E)** — Total colour difference, computed between batches for QA/QC. The **Tint Strength** of a colourant is expressed as **E₁%₁cm** — the absorbance of a **1% (w/v) solution** measured in a **1 cm path-length cuvette** at the wavelength of maximum absorption ($\lambda_{max}$). This is the **industry-standard metric** for comparing colourants on a per-gram basis. # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Natural vs. Artificial Colours ## 2.1 Classification Architecture Beverage colourants are classified along **three orthogonal axes**: | Axis | Natural Colourant | Artificial Colourant | |---|---|---| | **Source** | Plant, animal, microbial, mineral | Petrochemical / azo-dye synthesis | | **Solubility** | Often water-soluble (anthocyanins) or oil-soluble (carotenoids) | Predominantly water-soluble salts | | **Regulatory class** | "Nature-Identical" or "Exempt from certification" | "Certified colour" (FDA / FSSAI / EU list) | | **Tint strength (typical)** | Low to moderate (10–500 E₁%₁cm) | Very high (400–1500 E₁%₁cm) | | **Stability to pH/light/heat** | Variable, often poor | Engineered for robustness | | **Cost per kg of finished colour** | Higher | Lower | | **Consumer perception** | "Clean-label," premium | "Additive," mass-market | ## 2.2 Natural Colourants — Chemical Families ### 2.2.1 Anthocyanins (E163) - **Chemistry:** Polyhydroxy-2-phenylbenzopyrylium (flavylium cation) glycosides. - **Hue range:** Red (pH < 3) → Purple (pH 4–5) → Blue (pH > 6). - **Sources:** Grape skin (*Vitis vinifera*), blackberry, blueberry, red cabbage, elderberry, hibiscus (*Hibiscus sabdariffa* — used in *agua de jamaica* and herbal teas). - **Stability profile:** Highly sensitive to **pH, SO₂, ascorbic acid, light, and heat**. Co-pigmentation with flavones and metal ions (Al³⁺) stabilizes blue tones. - **Beverage applications:** Grape sodas, berry juices, wine, hibiscus iced teas. ### 2.2.2 Carotenoids (E160a–E160f) - **Chemistry:** C₄₀ isoprenoid polyene hydrocarbons (e.g., β-carotene) and oxygenated xanthophylls (lutein, zeaxanthin). - **Hue range:** Yellow → Orange → Red. - **Sources:** Carrot (*Daucus carota*), tomato (lycopene), paprika (*Capsicum annuum* — capsanthin), annatto (*Bixa orellana* — bixin/norbixin), saffron (*Crocus sativus* — crocin). - **Stability profile:** Sensitive to **oxidation, light, and heat**; stabilized as emulsions or water-dispersible beadlets. - **Beverage applications:** Orange juice fortification, energy drinks, "natural" orange sodas, butter-yellow milk beverages. ### 2.2.3 Chlorophylls (E140, E141) - **Chemistry:** Magnesium-porphyrin complexes — chlorophyll *a* (blue-green) and chlorophyll *b* (yellow-green). - **Hue range:** Green. - **Sources:** Spinach, alfalfa, nettle, *Spirulina* (cyanobacterial source of phycocyanin — E10). - **Stability profile:** Converts to **pheophytin** (olive-brown) on heat/acid, and to **chlorophyllin** (E141, Cu-Na salt) on alkaline treatment — the latter is more photostable. - **Beverage applications:** Mint drinks, matcha lattes, vegetable juices, "green" detox beverages. ### 2.2.4 Betalains (E162) - **Chemistry:** Nitrogen-containing, tyrosine-derived pigments — *betacyanins* (red-violet) and *betaxanthins* (yellow). - **Hue range:** Red-violet to yellow. - **Source:** Red beetroot (*Beta vulgaris*) — *betanin* is the principal pigment. - **Stability profile:** Stable at pH 3–7; light-sensitive; not compatible with anthocyanins (mutual exclusion in plants). - **Beverage applications:** Fruit punches, sports drinks, "beet-berry" functional beverages. ### 2.2.5 Curcumin (E100) - **Chemistry:** Polyphenolic diarylheptanoid from turmeric (*Curcuma longa*). - **Hue:** Bright yellow. - **Stability:** Poor water solubility, light-sensitive; co-formulated with emulsifiers. - **Beverage applications:** "Golden milk," turmeric lattes, Indian-style beverage mixes. ### 2.2.6 Caramel Colour (E150a–E150d) - **Chemistry:** Heat-induced Maillard reaction products of sugars — polymeric melanoidins. - **Four classes (per EU/FSSAI):** - **E150a — Plain caramel:** No ammonium salts; mild, beer-like hue. - **E150b — Caustic sulphite caramel:** Sulphite-ammonia process; brown. - **E150c — Ammonia caramel:** Used in bakery, not beverages. - **E150d — Sulphite ammonia caramel:** Highest tint strength; **used in cola beverages** for the iconic dark-brown shade. Contains **4-MEI (4-methylimidazole)**, which has flagged regulatory limits in California (Prop 65). - **Stability:** Excellent; acid-, heat-, and light-stable. ### 2.2.7 Tannins & Polyphenols - **Chemistry:** Proanthocyanidins, catechins. - **Sources:** Tea (*Camellia sinensis*), coffee, cocoa, oak (for spirits aging). - **Beverage relevance:** Provide astringency + brown colour; in tea, oxidation converts catechins to *theaflavins* (bright red-brown) and *thearubigins* (dark brown). ## 2.3 Artificial (Synthetic) Colourants — The Certified List | Common Name | E-Number | FD&C | Chemical Class | Hue | λ_max (nm) | E₁%₁cm (approx.) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Tartrazine | **E102** | Yellow 5 | Azo dye | Lemon yellow | 425 | 530 | | Sunset Yellow FCF | **E110** | Yellow 6 | Azo dye | Orange-yellow | 480 | 555 | | Carmoisine / Azorubine | **E122** | — | Azo dye | Red | 516 | 510 | | Amaranth | **E123** | — | Azo dye | Reddish-purple | 521 | 440 | | Ponceau 4R | **E124** | — | Azo dye | Strawberry red | 505 | 435 | | Allura Red AC | **E129** | Red 40 | Azo dye | Cherry red | 504 | 540 | | Patent Blue V | **E131** | — | Triphenylmethane | Deep blue | 638 | 2000 | | Brilliant Blue FCF | **E133** | Blue 1 | Triphenylmethane | Royal blue | 630 | 1640 | | Indigo Carmine | **E132** | Blue 2 | Indigoid | Indigo blue | 610 | 480 | | Quinoline Yellow | **E104** | — | Quinoline | Greenish-yellow | 411 | 865 | | Green S | **E142** | — | Triphenylmethane | Green | 632 | 1610 | > [!NOTE] > **KDU High-Yield Point — Azo Dyes vs. Non-Azo:** Azo dyes (containing the **–N=N–** chromophore) dominate the synthetic palette because the azo linkage is a powerful chromophore, easily tuned by ring substitution. Triphenylmethane dyes (Patent Blue, Brilliant Blue) are intensely coloured but less light-stable. ## 2.4 Stability Matrix — The KTU High-Yield Comparison | Stress Factor | Natural (Anthocyanin) | Natural (Carotenoid) | Natural (Caramel) | Artificial (Azo Dye) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Heat (pasteurization)** | Poor to moderate | Moderate (oxidation) | Excellent | Excellent | | **Light (UV) | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Moderate to good | | **pH < 3 (acidic beverage)** | Stable red | Stable | Stable | Stable | | **pH > 6 (neutral/alkaline)** | Blue-shift, may degrade | Stable | Stable | Variable | | **Ascorbic acid** | Bleaches rapidly | Slow degradation | Stable | Slow fading | | **SO₂ (preservative)** | Bleaches | Stable | Stable | Stable | | **Oxygen** | Slow oxidation | Rapid oxidation | Stable | Stable | | **Metal ions (Fe³⁺, Al³⁺)** | Co-pigments / shifts | Catalyzes oxidation | Stable | Can precipitate | ## 2.5 Regulatory Limits in Beverages (Indicative) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Numerical Awareness — Typical Permitted Levels (FSSAI / EU Codex, mg/kg or mg/L of beverage):** > - **Tartrazine (E102):** 100–500 mg/L > - **Sunset Yellow (E110):** 50–250 mg/L > - **Allura Red (E129):** 50–300 mg/L > - **Brilliant Blue (E133):** 50–200 mg/L > - **Curcumin (E100):** 50–600 mg/L > - **Caramel (E150d):** up to 1500 mg/L (cola) > > **Total colour load** (sum of synthetic dyes) is typically capped at **300 mg/L** in soft drinks per Codex Alimentarius guidance. ## 2.6 The "Why" Behind the Choice — Decision Tree for a Beverage Formulator 1. **Is the product positioned as "natural / organic / clean-label"?** → Choose natural (anthocyanin, caramel, beta-carotene). 2. **Does the product require a specific brand colour (e.g., neon green, electric blue)?** → Choose synthetic (Tartrazine + Brilliant Blue blends). 3. **Will the beverage be exposed to UV light in a clear bottle?** → Avoid anthocyanin; prefer caramel, beta-carotene beadlets, or selected synthetic azo dyes. 4. **Is the pH below 3.5 (typical soft drink)?** → Anthocyanin is safe; betalain is safe; chlorophyll will turn olive-brown — avoid. 5. **Is the cost ceiling tight (mass-market carbonated drink)?** → Synthetic dyes win on cost-per-tint-unit. 6. **Is the regulatory environment restrictive (e.g., EU ban on certain dyes)?** → Substitute with natural alternatives (e.g., spirulina blue, butterfly pea flower anthocyanin). ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Quality Control Labs** in beverage factories run **spectrophotometric assays** to verify colourant concentration in every batch. - **Pilot Plant Trials** test colourant stability across the **process window** (pH, temperature, residence time) before scale-up. - **Sensory Panels** correlate CIELAB L\*a\*b\* values with consumer acceptance scores — bridging instrumental and hedonic data. # 🧮 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples > Since beverage colour science is **applied and chemistry-driven** (not a heavy calculus subject), the "derivations" below are structured as **worked analytical examples** linking CIELAB theory, Beer-Lambert absorbance, and mass-balance colourant dosing — the type of numerical problem KTU 2024 board examiners set. --- ## 3.1 Example 1 — Beer–Lambert Calculation of Dye Concentration in a Beverage A beverage technologist prepares a standard solution of **Tartrazine (E102)** in citrate buffer. Using a **1 cm path-length cuvette**, the absorbance at **λ_max = 425 nm** is measured as **A = 0.840**. The literature reports **E₁%₁cm = 530** for Tartrazine. Calculate the **concentration (g/100 mL)** of the stock solution. ### Step 1 — Recall the Beer–Lambert equation in the form used for colourants $$ A = E_{1\%}^{1cm} \cdot c \cdot l $$ where: - $A$ = absorbance (dimensionless) - $E_{1\%}^{1cm}$ = specific absorbance of a **1% (w/v)** solution in a **1 cm cell** = **530** - $c$ = concentration in **g/100 mL** (% w/v) - $l$ = path length in cm = **1** ### Step 2 — Rearrange for c $$ c = \frac{A}{E_{1\%}^{1cm} \cdot l} $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values $$ c = \frac{0.840}{530 \cdot 1} = 0.001585 \text{ g/100 mL} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert to mg/L for a beverage-context unit Since **1 g/100 mL = 10 g/L = 10,000 mg/L**, we have: $$ c = 0.001585 \cdot 10{,}000 = 15.85 \text{ mg/L} $$ > **Interpretation:** The Tartrazine stock contains **15.85 mg/L**, well within the FSSAI/Codex permitted band of 100–500 mg/L for soft drinks — so it can be used as a working dilution for beverage fortification. --- ## 3.2 Example 2 — CIELAB ΔE Calculation Between Two Beverage Batches A bottling line produces two batches of a grape-flavoured drink. The CIELAB values measured by a colorimeter are: - **Batch 1 (Reference):** $L_1^{*}=45.0$, $a_1^{*}=32.0$, $b_1^{*}=10.0$ - **Batch 2 (Test):** $L_2^{*}=46.5$, $a_2^{*}=30.0$, $b_2^{*}=12.5$ Determine the **total colour difference (ΔE\*)** and interpret it for QA release. ### Step 1 — Recall the CIELAB ΔE formula (CIE76) $$ \Delta E^{*} = \sqrt{(L_2^{*} - L_1^{*})^{2} + (a_2^{*} - a_1^{*})^{2} + (b_2^{*} - b_1^{*})^{2}} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute each squared difference $$ \begin{aligned} (\Delta L^{*})^{2} &= (46.5 - 45.0)^{2} = (1.5)^{2} = 2.25 \\ (\Delta a^{*})^{2} &= (30.0 - 32.0)^{2} = (-2.0)^{2} = 4.00 \\ (\Delta b^{*})^{2} &= (12.5 - 10.0)^{2} = (2.5)^{2} = 6.25 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Sum and take the square root $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta E^{*} &= \sqrt{2.25 + 4.00 + 6.25} \\ &= \sqrt{12.50} \\ &= 3.54 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Interpret using the industry thresholds | ΔE\* | Sensory Meaning | Decision | |---|---|---| | < 1 | Not perceptible | Pass | | 1 – 2 | Perceptible on close look | Pass (with note) | | **2 – 3.5** | **Clearly perceptible, acceptable** | **Pass — typical beverage QC threshold** | | 3.5 – 5 | Perceptible at a glance | Investigate | | > 5 | Obvious colour shift | Reject batch | > **Interpretation:** With **ΔE\* = 3.54**, Batch 2 is at the **upper edge of the acceptable range** for beverage QC. The technologist should re-check the anthocyanin dose and consider a small adjustment before release. --- ## 3.3 Example 3 — Mass-Balance Calculation for a Colour Blend A beverage formulator needs to prepare **10,000 L** of a green-coloured sports drink using a blend of **Tartrazine (E102, λ_max = 425 nm)** and **Brilliant Blue FCF (E133, λ_max = 630 nm)**. The target shade requires the **final beverage** to contain: - Tartrazine at **120 mg/L** - Brilliant Blue at **40 mg/L** The factory holds a **Tartrazine stock solution of 0.50% w/v** and a **Brilliant Blue stock solution of 0.20% w/v**. Calculate the **volume of each stock** to dose into the batch. ### Step 1 — Compute the total mass of each dye required in the batch $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mass}_{\text{Tartrazine}} &= 120 \text{ mg/L} \cdot 10{,}000 \text{ L} = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ mg} = 1200 \text{ g} \\ \text{Mass}_{\text{Brilliant Blue}} &= 40 \text{ mg/L} \cdot 10{,}000 \text{ L} = 400{,}000 \text{ mg} = 400 \text{ g} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Convert each stock concentration to mg/mL For Tartrazine (0.50% w/v = 0.50 g/100 mL = 5 mg/mL): $$ [\text{Tartrazine}]_{\text{stock}} = 5 \text{ mg/mL} $$ For Brilliant Blue (0.20% w/v = 0.20 g/100 mL = 2 mg/mL): $$ [\text{Brilliant Blue}]_{\text{stock}} = 2 \text{ mg/mL} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the required stock volume $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{Tartrazine}} &= \frac{\text{Mass}_{\text{Tartrazine}}}{[\text{Tartrazine}]_{\text{stock}}} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000 \text{ mg}}{5 \text{ mg/mL}} = 240{,}000 \text{ mL} = 240 \text{ L} \\ V_{\text{Brilliant Blue}} &= \frac{\text{Mass}_{\text{Brilliant Blue}}}{[\text{Brilliant Blue}]_{\text{stock}}} = \frac{400{,}000 \text{ mg}}{2 \text{ mg/mL}} = 200{,}000 \text{ mL} = 200 \text{ L} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Verify the dose against typical industrial limits | Dye | Dose in beverage | Typical FSSAI/EU limit | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | Tartrazine | 120 mg/L | ≤ 500 mg/L | ✅ Within limit | | Brilliant Blue FCF | 40 mg/L | ≤ 200 mg/L | ✅ Within limit | > **Answer:** Dose **240 L of Tartrazine stock** and **200 L of Brilliant Blue stock** into the 10,000 L batch, under high-shear agitation, to achieve the target green shade. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Natural vs. Artificial Colourants at a Glance | Parameter | Anthocyanin | Carotenoid | Caramel (E150d) | Tartrazine (E102) | Allura Red (E129) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Hue | Red–Blue (pH dependent) | Yellow–Red | Dark brown | Lemon yellow | Cherry red | | λ_max (nm) | 510–540 | 440–480 | 610 (broad) | 425 | 504 | | E₁%₁cm (typical) | 200–400 | 250–550 | 0.4–0.6 (very high dilution needed) | 530 | 540 | | Solubility | Water | Oil (needs emulsion) | Water | Water | Water | | pH stability | 1–4 (best) | 3–9 | 2–10 | 2–9 | 2–9 | | Heat stability | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent | Good | | Light stability | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Moderate | Good | | Cost (USD/kg, indicative) | 80–300 | 50–500 | 5–20 | 20–60 | 30–80 | | Regulatory class | Natural | Natural | Natural | Synthetic (azo) | Synthetic (azo) | | Typical beverage | Grape soda, hibiscus tea | Orange juice, energy drink | Cola, stout beer | Lime soda, candy drinks | Strawberry drink | # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Flow of Beverage Colourants ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Beverage Colourants"]:::root --> B["Natural"]:::nat A --> C["Artificial / Synthetic"]:::art A --> D["Nature-Identical"]:::ni B --> B1["Plant-derived"] B --> B2["Animal-derived"] B --> B3["Microbial-derived"] B --> B4["Mineral-derived"] B1 --> B1a["Anthocyanin - E163
Grape, Berry, Hibiscus"] B1 --> B1b["Carotenoid - E160a-f
Carrot, Paprika, Annatto"] B1 --> B1c["Chlorophyll - E140
Spinach, Spirulina"] B1 --> B1d["Betalain - E162
Beetroot"] B1 --> B1e["Curcumin - E100
Turmeric"] B1 --> B1f["Caramel - E150a-d
Sugar Maillard"] B2 --> B2a["Cochineal - E120
Carmine from scale insect"] B3 --> B3a["Monascus Red
Red yeast rice"] B4 --> B4a["Titanium Dioxide - E171
White opacifier"] C --> C1["Azo Dyes -N=N-"] C --> C2["Triphenylmethane"] C --> C3["Quinoline"] C --> C4["Indigoid"] C1 --> C1a["Tartrazine E102 - Yellow 5"] C1 --> C1b["Sunset Yellow E110 - Yellow 6"] C1 --> C1c["Allura Red E129 - Red 40"] C1 --> C1d["Ponceau 4R E124"] C2 --> C2a["Brilliant Blue E133 - Blue 1"] C2 --> C2b["Patent Blue V E131"] C2 --> C2c["Green S E142"] C3 --> C3a["Quinoline Yellow E104"] C4 --> C4a["Indigo Carmine E132 - Blue 2"] D --> D1["Beta-Carotene synthesised
identical to natural"] D --> D2["Riboflavin - E101
Vitamin B2"] classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef nat fill:#a8e6cf,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000000 classDef art fill:#ffd6e0,stroke:#c9184a,color:#000000 classDef ni fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Stability Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Colourant ```mermaid flowchart TD S["Start: Select beverage colourant"]:::start --> P["What is the target pH?"]:::ask P --> P1["pH less than 3.5 - Acidic soft drink"] P --> P2["pH 3.5 to 6.0 - Juice, sports drink"] P --> P3["pH greater than 6.0 - Dairy, plant milk"] P1 --> T1["Bottled in clear glass or PET?"]:::ask T1 --> T1a["Yes - Light exposure high"] T1 --> T1b["No - Opaque can or carton"] T1a --> A1["Avoid Anthocyanin
Use Caramel E150d or
stable azo dye blend"] T1b --> A2["Anthocyanin or Betalain
acceptable"] P2 --> T2["Heat-processed - pasteurized?"]:::ask T2 --> T2a["Yes - HTST or UHT"] T2 --> T2b["No - Cold-fill aseptic"] T2a --> B1["Use Carotenoid beadlet
or caramel, avoid
anthocyanin"] T2b --> B2["Anthocyanin or
curcumin emulsion
acceptable"] P3 --> T3["Need bright green or blue?"]:::ask T3 --> T3a["Yes"] T3 --> T3b["No - natural tones"] T3a --> C1["Use Chlorophyllin E141 Cu-Na
or Brilliant Blue E133"] T3b --> C2["Spirulina phycocyanin
or caramel"] classDef start fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef ask fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#5a3825,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Beverage Production Line — Where Colourants Are Dosed ```mermaid flowchart LR R1["Raw Water
Treatment"]:::unit --> R2["Sugar Syrup
Preparation"]:::unit R2 --> R3["Acidulant &
Flavour Addition"]:::unit R3 --> R4["Colourant
Dosing Station"]:::unit R4 --> R5["Carbonation
or Mixing"]:::unit R5 --> R6["HTST Pasteurizer
or Cold-Fill"]:::unit R6 --> R7["Filling Line
Bottle, Can, Pouch"]:::unit R7 --> R8["QC Lab
CIELAB Check
Spectrophotometry"]:::qc R8 --> R9["Warehouse
Shelf Life Test"]:::unit R9 --> R10["Distribution
and Retail"]:::unit QC1["In-process QC"]:::qc -.-> R4 QC2["Final QC"]:::qc -.-> R7 classDef unit fill:#caf0f8,stroke:#0077b6,color:#000000 classDef qc fill:#ffd166,stroke:#9c5400,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Block-Level Functional Architecture Note:** The diagram above maps the **functional interactions** of a typical carbonated-soft-drink (CSD) line rather than depicting physical equipment. The **colourant dosing station (R4)** is the critical control point — a precision metering pump injects the dye blend after flavour and acid, but **before** pasteurization, so that thermal stress acts uniformly on the dispersed colourant. The **QC lab loop** verifies CIELAB L\*a\*b\* values and ΔE against the reference standard at every stage. # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Differentiate between natural and artificial (synthetic) colourants used in beverages. Give two examples of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks — keyword-rich):** | Aspect | Natural Colourant | Artificial Colourant | |---|---|---| | **Source** | Extracted from plants, animals, microbes, or minerals | Chemically synthesized in a laboratory (mostly azo dyes) | | **Chemical nature** | Often complex mixtures of bio-actives (e.g., anthocyanins, carotenoids) | Single, well-defined organic molecules (e.g., Tartrazine) | | **Tint strength** | Generally lower; larger doses needed | Very high; effective at <100 mg/L | | **Stability** | Variable; often pH-, light-, and heat-sensitive | Engineered for pH-, heat-, and light-stability | | **Cost** | Higher per kg | Lower per kg | | **Consumer perception** | "Clean label," "natural" | "Additive," "synthetic" | - **Natural examples (1 mark):** *Anthocyanin* (E163, from grapes), *Caramel* (E150d, from sugar). - **Artificial examples (1 mark):** *Tartrazine* (E102, Yellow 5), *Allura Red AC* (E129, Red 40). > **Allocation:** 1 mark for table-based differentiation, 1 mark for natural examples, 1 mark for artificial examples. --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]` **Explain the pH-dependent colour change of anthocyanins and its significance in beverage formulation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Anthocyanins exist as a mixture of four interconvertible molecular forms whose relative proportions depend on the **pH of the solution**: 1. **Flavylium cation (red)** — dominant at **pH < 3** (e.g., grape soda, cranberry juice). 2. **Carbinol pseudo-base (colourless)** — formed near **pH 4–5**. 3. **Chalcone (yellowish)** — formed at **pH 5–6**. 4. **Quinoidal base (blue/purple)** — dominant at **pH > 7** in the presence of co-pigments and metal ions (e.g., aluminium or iron complexes in blue pea flower tea). **Significance in beverages (1.5 marks):** - A strawberry-coloured drink formulated at pH 3.0 will turn **purple-blue** if the pH accidentally rises above 6 — a classic quality defect. - Formulators must therefore **lock the pH** (citrate/phosphate buffer) and **select the anthocyanin source** accordingly: grape for red wines, red cabbage for blue shades in alkaline dairy alternatives. - Co-pigmentation with **flavones or metal ions** is exploited to engineer stable blue/red hues in "natural" beverages. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]` **(a)** Classify the major natural colourants used in beverages. Discuss the chemistry, hue range, sources, and stability of **anthocyanins** and **carotenoids** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are **artificial (synthetic) colourants**? List any **five certified azo dyes** used in beverages with their E-numbers and applications. Discuss the **regulatory and safety concerns** associated with synthetic dyes. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **1. Classification of natural colourants (2 marks):** Natural colourants in beverages are classified by **chemical family**: - **Flavonoid-derived:** Anthocyanins (E163), flavones, chalcones. - **Isoprenoid-derived:** Carotenoids (E160a–f) — carotenes & xanthophylls. - **Porphyrin-derived:** Chlorophylls (E140), chlorophyllins (E141). - **Nitrogen-containing:** Betalains (E162 — betacyanins, betaxanthins). - **Diarylheptanoid:** Curcumin (E100). - **Maillard products:** Caramels (E150a–d). - **Animal-derived:** Cochineal / carmine (E120). **2. Anthocyanins — detailed discussion (2.5 marks):** - **Chemistry:** Water-soluble **flavylium (2-phenylbenzopyrylium) cation** glycosides; basic skeleton C₆–C₃–C₆. - **Hue range:** Red (pH < 3) → Purple (pH 4–5) → Blue (pH > 6, with co-pigments). - **Sources:** Grape skin, blackberry, blueberry, red cabbage, elderberry, hibiscus. - **Stability:** - **pH** — red at acidic pH, fades at pH 4–5, blue at alkaline pH. - **SO₂** — bleaches by addition to the C-4 position of the flavylium cation. - **Ascorbic acid** — condenses with anthocyanin to form a colourless product. - **Light & heat** — degrade the glycoside, yielding brown polymers. - **Stabilization** — co-pigmentation with flavones, metal-ion complexation (Al³⁺), acylation with cinnamic acids. **3. Carotenoids — detailed discussion (2.5 marks):** - **Chemistry:** **C₄₀ isoprenoid polyenes** with extensive conjugated double bonds; carotenes are hydrocarbons, xanthophylls bear oxygen functions (–OH, =O, epoxide). - **Hue range:** Yellow → Orange → Red (number of conjugated double bonds determines hue). - **Sources:** β-carotene (carrot, palm oil), lycopene (tomato, watermelon), capsanthin (paprika), bixin/norbixin (annatto), crocin (saffron), lutein (marigold). - **Stability:** - **Oxygen & light** — cause **cis-trans isomerization** and oxidative cleavage → bleaching. - **Heat** — moderate stability; can be improved by **antioxidant addition** (BHA, BHT, ascorbyl palmitate) and **emulsification** as water-dispersible beadlets. - **pH** — generally stable in the 3–9 range. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **1. Definition of artificial colourants (1 mark):** Artificial (synthetic) colourants are **chemically synthesized organic dyestuffs** that have been **toxicologically tested and certified** (e.g., FDA, FSSAI, EFSA) for use in food and beverages. They are characterized by **high tint strength, batch-to-batch consistency, low cost, and engineered stability** to pH, heat, and light. **2. Five certified azo dyes (3 marks):** | Dye | E-Number | Hue | Common Beverage Application | |---|---|---|---| | Tartrazine | **E102** | Lemon yellow | Lime soda, "lemon" drinks, candy-flavoured beverages | | Sunset Yellow FCF | **E110** | Orange-yellow | Orange sodas, mango drinks | | Ponceau 4R | **E124** | Strawberry red | Strawberry / cherry drinks | | Allura Red AC | **E129** | Cherry red | Cola blends, fruit punches | | Carmoisine (Azorubine) | **E122** | Deep red | Cranberry and raspberry beverages | All five contain the **–N=N– azo chromophore** responsible for visible-light absorption. **3. Regulatory and safety concerns (3 marks):** - **Hyperactivity in children** — The 2007 **Southampton Study** (UK FSA) linked mixtures of azo dyes (Tartrazine, Sunset Yellow, Carmoisine, Ponceau 4R, Allura Red) with **increased hyperactivity** in children. The EU mandates a **warning label** ("may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children") on foods containing these dyes. - **Allergic reactions** — Tartrazine can trigger **urticaria, asthma, and aspirin intolerance** in sensitive individuals. - **Carcinogenicity concerns** — Some azo dyes (e.g., Sudan I) are **banned** in food as they are genotoxic carcinogens; their absence is verified by routine HPLC. - **4-MEI in caramel** — Class III & IV caramels (E150c, E150d) contain **4-methylimidazole**, listed under California Prop 65 with a NSRL of **29 µg/day**. Beverages using E150d must keep 4-MEI below regulatory thresholds. - **Maximum permitted levels** — Set by Codex / FSSAI / EFSA; total synthetic dye load is typically capped at **300 mg/L** of beverage. - **Batch certification** — Each batch must be **certified for purity** (no lead, no unreacted intermediates, no subsidiary colours above specification) by the regulatory authority. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]` **(a)** Explain the **chemistry, classification, and beverage applications of caramel colour (E150)**. Discuss the differences between E150a, E150b, E150c, and E150d classes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **stability challenges of natural colourants** during beverage processing and storage. Suggest **engineering and formulation strategies** to overcome these challenges, with specific examples. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **1. Chemistry of caramel colour (2 marks):** Caramel colour is produced by the **controlled heat treatment (Maillard reaction)** of food-grade carbohydrates — typically **sucrose, glucose, invert sugar, or high-DE glucose syrup**. The Maillard reaction between the carbonyl of the sugar and the amine group of an ammonium-containing reactant (or just thermal degradation if no reactant is used) generates **polymeric high-molecular-weight melanoidin** polymers. The four classes differ in the **reactant used** and the **pH** during the reaction: **2. Classification table (3 marks):** | Class | Reactant Used | pH | Tinctorial Strength | Beverage Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **E150a — Plain caramel** | None (thermal only) | 3–4 | Low | Beer, fruit wines | | **E150b — Caustic sulphite caramel** | SO₂ + NaOH / KOH | 4–5 | Medium | Vinegar, dark sauces | | **E150c — Ammonia caramel** | NH₃ / (NH₄)₂CO₃ | 4–8 | High | Bakery, sauces (not beverages) | | **E150d — Sulphite ammonia caramel** | SO₂ + NH₃ | 4–5 | **Highest** | **Cola beverages, dark beers** | **3. Beverage applications (2 marks):** - **Cola drinks** universally use **E150d** for the iconic deep brown hue at doses up to **1500 mg/L**. - **Stout and porter beers** use **E150a** (plain caramel) because it is beer-compatible (no ammonia). - **Spirit-based dark beverages** (dark rum, coffee liqueur) use **E150d** for consistent dark colour. - **Vinegar and Worcestershire-type sauces** use **E150b**. The colour arises from melanoidin polymers that absorb broadly across the visible spectrum, giving a dark brown appearance. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Stability Challenges of Natural Colourants (3.5 marks):** | Challenge | Mechanism | Affected Colourant | |---|---|---| | **pH sensitivity** | Structural interconversion of flavylium forms | Anthocyanins | | **Light-induced fading** | Photolysis of conjugated double bonds | Anthocyanins, betalains, curcumin | | **Thermal degradation** | Glycoside hydrolysis, oxidation | Anthocyanins, chlorophyll | | **Oxidation** | Singlet-oxygen attack on conjugated polyenes | Carotenoids | | **SO₂ bleaching** | Bisulphite addition to flavylium cation | Anthocyanins | | **Ascorbic acid bleaching** | Condensation followed by oxidation | Anthocyanins | | **Metal-ion precipitation** | Complexation with Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺ | Carminic acid, anthocyanin complexes | **Engineering & Formulation Strategies (3.5 marks):** 1. **pH buffering** — Use citrate/phosphate buffer to lock pH at 3.0–3.5 for anthocyanin stability. 2. **Co-pigmentation** — Add **flavones (quercetin, rutin)** or **metal ions (Al³⁺ at 50–100 mg/L)** to stabilize anthocyanin blue shades in alkaline beverages. 3. **Microencapsulation** — Convert oil-soluble carotenoids into **water-dispersible beadlets** (e.g., spray-dried with modified starch + antioxidant) — protects against O₂ and light. 4. **Antioxidant co-formulation** — Add **ascorbic acid (for oxidation protection of carotenoids, NOT for anthocyanin)**, **BHA/BHT (0.02%)**, or **mixed tocopherols** to extend shelf life. 5. **Modified-atmosphere packaging (MAP)** — Flush bottles with **N₂ or CO₂** to displace O₂, especially critical for anthocyanin-rich and carotenoid-rich beverages. 6. **Opaque or amber packaging** — Use **amber PET bottles or aluminium cans** to block UV light below 450 nm. 7. **Use of nature-identical alternatives** — E.g., **β-carotene from fermentation (Blakeslea trispora)** instead of carrot extract for higher purity and lower cost. 8. **Enzymatic stabilization** — Treat anthocyanin extracts with **laccase to remove polyphenol oxidase** or use **glutaraldehyde cross-linking** to fix colour in packaging films. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing chlorophyllin (E141) with chlorophyll (E140):** E140 is fat-soluble and olive-brown in acid; E141 is water-soluble Cu-Na salt and stays green in acid. > 2. **Writing "Tartrazine is carcinogenic"** — this is *not* the current regulatory position. The accepted concern is **hyperactivity in children**, not carcinogenicity. Examiners will deduct marks for this error. > 3. **Stating that "all natural colourants are unstable"** — this is false. Caramel (E150d) is exceptionally stable. Always qualify your statements. > 4. **Misidentifying the colour of carminic acid / cochineal** — it is **red** (not purple or orange). > 5. **Confusing β-carotene (orange) with lycopene (red)** — both are carotenoids but have different hues due to different conjugated-double-bond counts. > 6. **Forgetting to mention E-numbers** — KTU examiners expect both the E-number and the chemical/family name in any short or long answer. > 7. **Mismatching colour with beverage** — e.g., claiming "anthocyanin is used in cola" — this will be marked wrong. Anthocyanins are NOT used in cola; **caramel E150d** is. --- ## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Colours: Natural and Artificial** - ✅ **Two families:** **Natural** (anthocyanin, carotenoid, chlorophyll, betalain, curcumin, caramel, cochineal) vs. **Artificial / Synthetic** (azo dyes — Tartrazine, Sunset Yellow, Allura Red, Ponceau 4R; triphenylmethane — Brilliant Blue, Patent Blue; indigoid — Indigo Carmine; quinoline — Quinoline Yellow). - ✅ **Anthocyanin hue is pH-driven:** red (pH < 3) → colourless (pH 4–5) → blue (pH > 6 with co-pigment). - ✅ **Caramel E150d** = sulphite ammonia caramel = **cola beverage** colourant (highest tint strength). - ✅ **Tartrazine (E102)** = Yellow 5, azo dye, λ_max ≈ 425 nm, lemon yellow. - ✅ **Allura Red AC (E129)** = Red 40, azo dye, λ_max ≈ 504 nm, cherry red. - ✅ **Brilliant Blue FCF (E133)** = Blue 1, triphenylmethane, λ_max ≈ 630 nm, royal blue. - ✅ **Stability order (most to least stable):** Caramel > Synthetic azo > Carotenoid beadlet > Betalain > Anthocyanin > Chlorophyll in acid. - ✅ **Hyperactivity warning** (EU 2007 Southampton Study) is the key safety concern for synthetic azo dyes. - ✅ **4-MEI in E150d** is a regulatory concern under California Prop 65. - ✅ **E₁%₁cm** is the standard tint-strength metric; A = E₁%₁cm · c · l is the Beer–Lambert equation for colourants. - ✅ **CIELAB ΔE\*** is the standard for batch-to-batch colour QA; ΔE\* < 2 is preferred for beverage release. - ✅ **Decision rule for formulators:** *natural* for clean-label positioning, *synthetic* for photostable, vivid, low-cost mass-market drinks; *caramel E150d* specifically for cola. - ✅ **Maximum permitted synthetic dye load** in beverages is typically **≤ 300 mg/L** total. - ✅ **No vertical pipe characters** in any Beer–Lambert or absorbance equation during the exam — write $\vert A \vert$ or $\lvert A \rvert$ if absolute value is needed (rare in colourimetry). - ✅ **Always cite E-number AND common name AND chemical family** for full credit in KTU 2024 answers.

topics.title: Micro and Nano Emulsions of Flavours and Colours in Beverages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Micro and Nano Emulsions of Flavours and Colours in Beverages # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) A **colloidal dispersion system** in which one immiscible liquid is dispersed as fine droplets within another liquid, stabilized by a surfactant (or surfactant + co-surfactant) film, is termed an **emulsion**. When the dispersed droplet diameter is reduced to the sub-micron range, the system is classified as a **microemulsion** or **nanoemulsion**, depending on its thermodynamic nature and droplet size. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Box — Beverage Emulsions** > - **Microemulsion:** A *thermodynamically stable*, optically transparent (or translucent), isotropic dispersion of oil and water stabilized by a surfactant film, with droplet diameter typically in the range of **10 – 100 nm**. > - **Nanoemulsion:** A *kinetically stable* (metastable), often translucent dispersion with droplet diameter in the range of **20 – 200 nm**, requiring high-energy input or specific low-energy phase transitions for formation. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Detergent Trick" Imagine you are trying to mix vinegar (water) with ground pepper (oil). The two will stubbornly separate because water and oil hate each other (high interfacial tension). Now add a drop of **dish soap (surfactant)** and stir. The soap molecules have one end that loves water (hydrophilic head) and another that loves oil (hydrophobic tail). They form a protective "fence" around the tiny oil droplets, preventing them from clumping back together. > A **microemulsion** is what you get when the soap is so effective and the droplets are so incredibly tiny (smaller than the wavelength of visible light, ~400–700 nm) that the mixture becomes crystal clear, like a magic potion. A **nanoemulsion** is a slightly larger cousin — still very stable but takes vigorous shaking or a special blender to make. ## 1.3 Why Beverages Need Emulsions Most beverage-grade flavour and colour compounds are **lipophilic (oil-soluble)**: | Compound Class | Examples | Native Solubility | |----------------|----------|-------------------| | Essential Oils | Limonene (citrus), Menthol (mint) | Oil / Insoluble in water | | Carotenoid Colours | β-carotene, Lycopene, Lutein | Oil / Insoluble in water | | Spice Extracts | Capsaicin, Curcumin | Oil / Insoluble in water | | Vitamins | Vitamin A, D, E, K | Oil / Insoluble in water | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (CO1 – KTU 2024):** Beverages are predominantly **aqueous (water-based)** systems. To deliver lipophilic bioactives in a stable, organoleptically acceptable, and bioavailable form, they must be converted into **water-dispersible delivery systems** — this is the engineering role of **micro- and nanoemulsions**. ## 1.4 Key Physico-Chemical Metrics (in **bold**) - **Droplet Diameter (d):** **10 – 200 nm** (sub-micron range). - **Interfacial Tension (γ):** Reduced to ultra-low values of **10⁻³ – 10⁻⁶ N/m** (versus ~ 50 mN/m for pure oil-water interface). - **Polydispersity Index (PDI):** Ideally **< 0.3** for monodisperse systems. - **Zeta Potential (ζ):** Absolute value **> 30 mV** indicates good electrostatic stability. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of the Three Components A beverage emulsion is a **four-component (or more)** system. Understanding the role of each component is critical for KTU exam answers. | Component | Function | Common Examples (Beverage Grade) | |-----------|----------|----------------------------------| | **Oil Phase (O)** | Carrier for lipophilic flavour/colour | MCT (Medium-Chain Triglyceride), Orange oil, Lemon oil | | **Aqueous Phase (W)** | Continuous medium (beverage base) | Water, Sugar syrup, Acidulants (citric acid) | | **Surfactant (S)** | Reduces interfacial tension; forms film | Tween 20/40/80, Span 20/80, Quillaja saponin, Lecithin | | **Co-Surfactant (CoS)** | Penetrates surfactant film, fluidizes interface | Ethanol, Propylene glycol, Glycerol, Short-chain alcohols | ## 2.2 Microemulsion vs Nanoemulsion — The Critical Distinction > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-Examiner Focus (KTU 2024):** The single most frequently asked 7-mark question is: *"Differentiate between microemulsion and nanoemulsion."* Memorize the table below precisely. | Parameter | Microemulsion | Nanoemulsion | |-----------|---------------|--------------| | **Thermodynamic Stability** | Stable (thermodynamically) | Metastable (kinetically stable) | | **Droplet Size** | **10 – 100 nm** | **20 – 200 nm** | | **Optical Appearance** | Transparent | Translucent to opaque | | **Energy Input** | Spontaneous (low energy) | High energy (homogenization) | | **Surfactant Concentration** | High (10 – 30 %) | Low (3 – 10 %) | | **Co-surfactant Required** | Yes (usually) | Not necessarily | | **Formation Driver** | Negative Gibbs free energy | Surfactant + Energy input | | **Birefringence** | None | None | | **Phase Diagram Location** | Winsor III / IV region | Outside microemulsion zone | | **Typical Use in Beverages** | Clarity drinks, fortified waters | Cloudy drinks, flavours, colours | ## 2.3 Bancroft's Rule & HLB Selection > **Bancroft's Rule (1913):** *"The type of emulsion (O/W or W/O) is determined by the phase in which the surfactant is more soluble."* - Surfactant more soluble in water → **O/W emulsion** (typical for beverages). - Surfactant more soluble in oil → **W/O emulsion**. > **Griffin's HLB Scale (Hydrophilic–Lipophilic Balance):** > - **HLB 4 – 6** → W/O emulsifier > - **HLB 7 – 9** → Wetting agent > - **HLB 8 – 18** → O/W emulsifier (used in beverages) > [!NOTE] > **Example (Beverage):** To emulsify orange oil (O) in a soft drink (W), we need an **O/W emulsifier** with **HLB 9 – 12**. Tween 80 (HLB = 15) and Tween 20 (HLB = 16.7) are excellent choices. ## 2.4 Methods of Preparation ### A. High-Energy Methods (for Nanoemulsions) 1. **High-Pressure Homogenization (HPH):** Fluid forced through a narrow orifice at **5,000 – 50,000 psi**. Droplets broken by shear, turbulence, and cavitation. 2. **Microfluidization:** Fluid streams split into micro-channels and collided at high velocity. 3. **Ultrasonication:** High-frequency sound waves (20 kHz) generate **cavitation bubbles** that collapse and disrupt droplets. ### B. Low-Energy Methods (for Microemulsions / some Nanoemulsions) 1. **Phase Inversion Temperature (PIT):** Temperature change converts W/O → O/W. Non-ionic surfactants (e.g., polyethoxylated) change HLB with temperature. 2. **Phase Inversion Composition (PIC):** Gradual addition of water (or oil) causes spontaneous inversion. 3. **Spontaneous Emulsification:** Mixing oil + surfactant + water without external energy, driven by negative Gibbs free energy. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in all table cells below to preserve markdown table integrity.** | # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units | |---|---------|---------|---------------------| | 1 | Gibbs Free Energy of Formation | $\Delta G_{form} = \Delta A \cdot \gamma - T \cdot \Delta S$ | $\Delta G_{form} < 0$ ⇒ Microemulsion; $\Delta G_{form} > 0$ ⇒ Nanoemulsion (kinetically trapped) | | 2 | Laplace (Disjoining) Pressure | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \cdot \gamma}{r}$ | Pressure difference across curved interface; $\gamma$ in N/m, $r$ in m | | 3 | Critical Micelle Concentration | $CMC = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta G_{mic}}{RT}\right)$ | Minimum surfactant conc. for micelle formation (mol/L) | | 4 | Stokes' Law Settling Velocity | $v = \dfrac{2 r^{2} \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot g}{9 \eta}$ | $r$ = droplet radius, $\eta$ = viscosity, $\Delta \rho$ = density difference | | 5 | Stokes–Einstein Diffusivity | $D = \dfrac{k_{B} T}{6 \pi \eta r}$ | $k_{B}$ = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** (Boltzmann const.) | | 6 | Zeta Potential Criterion | $\vert \zeta \vert > 30$ mV | Threshold for electrostatic stability | | 7 | HLB Required (for O/W) | $HLB_{mix} = \sum_{i} x_{i} \cdot HLB_{i}$ | Weighted average HLB of mixed emulsifier | | 8 | Rayleigh Scattering Criterion | $\dfrac{d}{\lambda} < 0.1$ | For optical transparency ($d$ = droplet, $\lambda$ ≈ 550 nm visible) | | 9 | Weber Number (Cavitation) | $We = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^{2} \cdot L}{\sigma}$ | $We > 1$ required for droplet breakup | | 10 | Ostwald Ripening Rate | $\omega = \dfrac{d r^{3}}{dt} = \dfrac{8 \cdot \gamma \cdot V_{m} \cdot D \cdot C_{\infty}}{9 R T}$ | Solubility-driven droplet growth | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility in Beverages - **Fortified Waters & Sports Drinks:** Curcumin-nanoemulsions for anti-inflammatory benefit. - **Soft Drinks:** β-carotene nanoemulsions provide natural orange colour + Vitamin A fortification. - **Functional Beverages:** Essential-oil nanoemulsions (e.g., limonene) preserve volatile aroma. - **Clear Beverages:** Microemulsions (droplets < 50 nm) appear as clear, single-phase liquids — crucial for "crystal clear" fortified waters. - **Improved Bioavailability:** Sub-micron droplets increase surface-area-to-volume ratio by **10³ – 10⁶ fold**, enhancing intestinal absorption of lipophilic bioactives. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Thermodynamic Criterion for Microemulsion Formation We begin with the classical **thermodynamic stability criterion** for a colloidal dispersion. ### Step 1 — Define the Total Free Energy Change For an emulsion to form *spontaneously*, the total Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G_{form}$ must be negative: $$ \Delta G_{form} = \Delta H_{form} - T \cdot \Delta S_{form} $$ *Where $\Delta H_{form}$ is the enthalpy change (mainly due to creation of new interfacial area) and $\Delta S_{form}$ is the entropy change (mainly configurational, due to dispersion).* ### Step 2 — Express the Enthalpy Term via Interfacial Energy The enthalpy change equals the product of new interfacial area $\Delta A$ and the interfacial tension $\gamma$: $$ \Delta H_{form} = \gamma \cdot \Delta A $$ *This is because creating a new interface between oil and water requires work against the interfacial tension $\gamma$ (in J/m² or N/m).* ### Step 3 — Express the Entropy Term The configurational entropy gain upon dispersion of a bulk phase into $N$ droplets is given by: $$ \Delta S_{form} = -k_{B} \cdot N \cdot \left[ \ln \phi + \left(\dfrac{1-\phi}{\phi}\right) \ln(1-\phi) \right] $$ *Where $k_{B}$ is the Boltzmann constant, $N$ is the number of droplets, and $\phi$ is the volume fraction of dispersed phase.* ### Step 4 — Combine to Obtain the Final Criterion Combining the steps above yields the **microemulsion spontaneity criterion**: $$ \Delta G_{form} = \gamma \cdot \Delta A - T \cdot \Delta S_{config} < 0 $$ *Substituting numerical estimates for typical surfactants:* if $\gamma$ is reduced to ultra-low values (**10⁻³ N/m** or lower) by an efficient surfactant, the entropic term dominates, and $\Delta G_{form} < 0$ → **spontaneous microemulsion** forms. ### Step 5 — Conclude > If $\gamma$ is *not* sufficiently reduced, $\Delta G_{form} > 0$. External energy (e.g., high-pressure homogenization) must be supplied to overcome the energy barrier → **nanoemulsion** (kinetically trapped, not thermodynamically stable). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Laplace Pressure Across a Curved Droplet Interface The pressure inside a small droplet exceeds the pressure outside due to surface curvature. This pressure difference is the **Laplace pressure**, and it drives **Ostwald ripening**. ### Step 1 — Consider a Spherical Droplet of Radius r Let the internal pressure be $P_{in}$ and external pressure be $P_{out}$. The interfacial film of tension $\gamma$ exerts a contractile force per unit length equal to $\gamma$. ### Step 2 — Force Balance on a Hemispherical Cap For a hemispherical cap, the projected area is $\pi r^{2}$ and the perimeter of the contact line is $2\pi r$. Equilibrium of forces gives: $$ (P_{in} - P_{out}) \cdot \pi r^{2} = \gamma \cdot 2\pi r $$ ### Step 3 — Simplify Cancel $\pi r$ on both sides: $$ (P_{in} - P_{out}) \cdot r = 2 \gamma $$ ### Step 4 — Express Final Laplace Pressure Equation $$ \Delta P = P_{in} - P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \gamma}{r} $$ *This shows that for very small droplets (r ↓), the internal pressure is extremely high — explaining why nano-droplets are prone to **Ostwald ripening** (small droplets dissolve, large ones grow).* ### Step 5 — Consequence for Beverage Emulsions Adding a **ripening inhibitor** (e.g., a small fraction of a highly insoluble oil like long-chain triglyceride) is a common industrial practice to slow Ostwald ripening in beverage emulsions. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Critical Droplet Size for Optical Transparency A beverage labelled *"crystal clear"* requires the droplets to be much smaller than the wavelength of visible light. The **Rayleigh scattering criterion** is used. ### Step 1 — Rayleigh Scattering Condition Scattering becomes negligible when: $$ \dfrac{d}{\lambda} < 0.1 $$ *Where $d$ is droplet diameter and $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light in the medium.* ### Step 2 — Use Mid-Visible Wavelength For a beverage, take $\lambda \approx 550$ nm (green light, mid-spectrum): $$ \lambda = 550 \text{ nm} $$ ### Step 3 — Solve for Maximum Allowed Droplet Diameter $$ d_{max} = 0.1 \cdot \lambda = 0.1 \cdot 550 \text{ nm} $$ ### Step 4 — Final Result $$ \boxed{d_{max} = 55 \text{ nm}} $$ *Therefore, for a beverage to appear perfectly clear, the emulsion droplets must be smaller than approximately 55 nm. This is why **microemulsions** (10–100 nm) are preferred for clear beverages, while **nanoemulsions** (which can extend up to 200 nm) tend to be slightly hazy.* --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Emulsion Type for a Beverage Application | Beverage Target | Colour/Flavour | Droplet Need | HLB Range | Surfactant Choice | Emulsion Type | |-----------------|----------------|--------------|-----------|-------------------|---------------| | Clear orange drink | Limonene + β-carotene | < 50 nm (clarity) | 9 – 12 | Tween 20 / Quillaja | **Microemulsion** | | Cloudy orange juice analog | Orange oil + carotenoid | 100 – 500 nm (cloud) | 7 – 9 | Modified starch + Span | **Macro/Nanoemulsion** | | Fortified lemon water | Limonene + Vitamin C | < 50 nm (clarity) | 11 – 14 | Tween 80 | **Microemulsion** | | Functional sports drink (curcumin) | Curcumin | 50 – 100 nm (clarity + bioavail.) | 10 – 13 | Tween 80 + Lecithin | **Nanoemulsion** | | Mint-flavoured clear water | Menthol + Eucalyptol | < 30 nm | 13 – 16 | Tween 80 / Polysorbate | **Microemulsion** | | Cola with caramel natural colour | Caramel + Caffeine | Mostly aqueous | N/A | None needed | **True solution** | --- ## 3.5 Derivation: HLB of a Mixed Surfactant System When two surfactants with different HLB values are blended, the mixture's HLB is the *weighted average* of the individual HLBs. ### Step 1 — Define Weight Fractions Let $x_{1}$ and $x_{2}$ be the mass fractions of surfactants 1 and 2, with $HLB_{1}$ and $HLB_{2}$ respectively. ### Step 2 — Write the Weighted Average Equation $$ HLB_{mix} = x_{1} \cdot HLB_{1} + x_{2} \cdot HLB_{2} $$ ### Step 3 — Generalize to n Surfactants $$ \boxed{HLB_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{i} \cdot HLB_{i}} $$ ### Step 4 — Worked Example A formulator blends 60 % Tween 80 (HLB = 15) and 40 % Span 80 (HLB = 4.3) to emulsify orange oil (target HLB ≈ 11). $$ HLB_{mix} = (0.60 \times 15) + (0.40 \times 4.3) = 9.0 + 1.72 = 10.72 $$ ### Step 5 — Conclude > The calculated $HLB_{mix} = 10.72$ is within the target range (9–12), confirming this blend is suitable for an O/W beverage emulsion. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Architecture of a Beverage Micro/Nanoemulsion ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Beverage Base
Aqueous Phase W] --> D[Continuous Phase
Water + Sugar + Acid] B[Oil Phase O
Flavour or Colour] --> E[Dispersed Droplets
d = 10 to 200 nm] C[Surfactant Film
Tween or Quillaja] --> F[Interfacial Layer
HLB 9 to 16] D --> G[Micro or Nanoemulsion System] E --> G F --> G G --> H{Optical Property} H -->|d less than 55 nm| I[Transparent
Clear Beverage] H -->|d more than 55 nm| J[Translucent or Cloudy
Hazy Beverage] G --> K{Stability} K -->|Thermodynamic| L[Microemulsion
Spontaneous] K -->|Kinetic| M[Nanoemulsion
Energy Driven] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Preparation Routes ```mermaid flowchart LR P[O W S Mixture] --> Q{Method} Q --> R[High Energy Route] Q --> S[Low Energy Route] R --> R1[High Pressure
Homogenizer] R --> R2[Microfluidizer] R --> R3[Ultrasonicator] S --> S1[Phase Inversion
Temperature PIT] S --> S2[Phase Inversion
Composition PIC] S --> S3[Spontaneous
Emulsification] R1 --> X[Nanoemulsion
20 to 200 nm] R2 --> X R3 --> X S1 --> Y[Microemulsion
10 to 100 nm] S2 --> Y S3 --> Y X --> Z[Finished Beverage] Y --> Z ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Application Map for Beverage Industry ```mermaid flowchart TB App[Beverage Emulsion
Applications] --> F[Flavours] App --> C[Colours] App --> V[Vitamins and
Nutraceuticals] F --> F1[Citrus Oils
Limonene] F --> F2[Mint Oils
Menthol] F --> F3[Spice Oils
Capsaicin] C --> C1[Carotenoids
Beta Carotene] C --> C2[Curcuminoids
Curcumin] C --> C3[Anthocyanins
Lipid Soluble Fractions] V --> V1[Vitamin A
Retinol] V --> V2[Vitamin D
Cholecalciferol] V --> V3[Vitamin E
Tocopherol] F1 --> Bev[Finished Beverage] F2 --> Bev F3 --> Bev C1 --> Bev C2 --> Bev C3 --> Bev V1 --> Bev V2 --> Bev V3 --> Bev ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stability and Failure Mechanisms ```mermaid flowchart TB Stab[Emulsion Stability
Pathways] --> St[Stable System] Stab --> Un[Unstable System] Un --> U1[Ostwald Ripening
Small Droplets Dissolve] Un --> U2[Coalescence
Droplets Merge] Un --> U3[Flocculation
Droplets Aggregate] Un --> U4[Creaming and
Sedimentation] U1 --> Prev[Prevention Strategy] U2 --> Prev U3 --> Prev U4 --> Prev Prev --> P1[Add Ripening Inhibitor] Prev --> P2[Increase Surfactant] Prev --> P3[Adjust pH and Zeta] Prev --> P4[Add Xanthan Gum] P1 --> Out[Long Shelf Life
Beverage] P2 --> Out P3 --> Out P4 --> Out ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram: Surfactant Orientation at the Oil-Water Interface ```mermaid flowchart LR Oil[Oil Phase
Lipophilic Core] --> IF[Interfacial Film] IF --> Sur[Surfactant Molecules] Sur --> H1[Hydrophilic Head
Faces Water] Sur --> H2[Hydrophobic Tail
Faces Oil] H1 --> W1[Water Phase
Hydrophilic] H2 --> O1[Oil Phase
Lipophilic] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** *Define microemulsion and nanoemulsion. State the role of surfactants in beverage emulsions.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A **microemulsion** is a thermodynamically stable, transparent, isotropic colloidal dispersion of oil and water with droplet size **10 – 100 nm**, stabilized by a surfactant film, often with a co-surfactant. (**1 mark**) - A **nanoemulsion** is a kinetically stable (metastable) dispersion with droplet size **20 – 200 nm**, requiring high-energy input for formation. (**1 mark**) - **Role of surfactants:** They adsorb at the oil–water interface, lower the interfacial tension from ~ 50 mN/m to **10⁻³ – 10⁻⁶ N/m**, form a mechanical/electrostatic barrier against droplet coalescence, and — by Bancroft's rule — determine the emulsion type (O/W or W/O). (**1 mark**) --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]** *What is the Hydrophilic–Lipophilic Balance (HLB) of a surfactant? Mention the HLB range suitable for oil-in-water (O/W) emulsions used in beverages.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The **HLB (Hydrophilic–Lipophilic Balance)** is an empirical scale (developed by Griffin, 1949) that quantifies the relative affinity of a surfactant molecule for water versus oil. It ranges from 0 (totally lipophilic) to 20 (totally hydrophilic). (**1.5 marks**) - For **O/W emulsions in beverages**, the required HLB range is **8 – 18** (typically 9 – 14 for beverage flavours and colours). (**1.5 marks**) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — *Mechanism and Stabilization* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice Option A]** **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, describe the **structural architecture** of a microemulsion. Explain the role of **surfactant and co-surfactant** in detail. **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **mechanism of droplet stabilization** in beverage nanoemulsions, citing Bancroft's rule, Gibbs free energy criterion, and Laplace pressure. #### Model Solution — Part (a) The structural architecture of a microemulsion consists of three key components: 1. **Dispersed (Oil) Phase** — The internal phase that contains the lipophilic flavour or colour compound. Droplet diameter = **10 – 100 nm**. (**1 mark**) 2. **Continuous (Aqueous) Phase** — The external water-based beverage medium (water, sugar, acid, minerals). (**1 mark**) 3. **Interfacial Film** — A *mixed film* of surfactant + co-surfactant molecules, oriented with **hydrophilic heads** in water and **hydrophobic tails** in oil. (**1 mark**) > **Role of Surfactant (3 marks):** > - **Reduces interfacial tension** from ~ 50 mN/m to ultra-low values (10⁻³ – 10⁻⁶ N/m), allowing spontaneous droplet formation. > - **Provides mechanical barrier** against coalescence. > - **Determines emulsion type** via Bancroft's rule. > - Common beverage-grade surfactants: **Tween 20, 40, 80**, **Quillaja saponin**, **Lecithin**, **Modified starch**. > **Role of Co-Surfactant (1 mark):** > - Co-surfactants (e.g., ethanol, propylene glycol, short-chain alcohols) **penetrate the surfactant film**, fluidize the interface, and further reduce interfacial tension. > - They allow formation of microemulsions with surfactant concentrations that would otherwise be too rigid. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Bancroft's Rule (2 marks):** > *"The phase in which the surfactant is more soluble becomes the continuous phase."* > For beverages, water-soluble surfactants (high HLB) → **O/W emulsion** is formed. **Gibbs Free Energy Criterion (2.5 marks):** > For spontaneous microemulsion formation, $\Delta G_{form} = \gamma \cdot \Delta A - T \cdot \Delta S_{config} < 0$. > The surfactant reduces $\gamma$ to ultra-low values; the entropic term ($-T \cdot \Delta S_{config}$) becomes dominant → spontaneous formation. **Laplace Pressure (2.5 marks):** > $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma}{r}$. > Even at low $\gamma$, small droplets experience high internal pressure, driving **Ostwald ripening**. This is mitigated in nanoemulsions by adding a ripening inhibitor (e.g., a small fraction of highly insoluble long-chain triglyceride). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Preparation and Applications* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice Option B]** **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **high-energy methods** (high-pressure homogenization, microfluidization, ultrasonication) for the preparation of beverage nanoemulsions. **(b) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **application of micro- and nanoemulsions in beverage processing** with specific examples of flavours and colours. #### Model Solution — Part (a) **High-Pressure Homogenization (HPH) — 2.5 marks:** > The coarse pre-mix is forced through a narrow orifice at **5,000 – 50,000 psi**. Three mechanisms break droplets: > 1. **Shear stress** (viscous forces) > 2. **Turbulent flow** (eddy disruption) > 3. **Cavitation** (vapor-bubble collapse) > Typical droplet size: **50 – 200 nm** after multiple passes. **Microfluidization — 2.5 marks:** > The pre-mix is split into two micro-channels and the streams collide head-on at high velocity. The combined effect of shear, impact, and cavitation produces very fine, narrow-size distributions. Droplet size: **20 – 100 nm**. **Ultrasonication — 2 marks:** > High-frequency sound waves (**20 kHz – 100 kHz**) generate cavitation bubbles in the liquid; bubble collapse produces localized **shock waves** that fragment droplets. Suitable for lab-scale beverage emulsion preparation. Droplet size: **30 – 150 nm**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only the *names* of methods. To get full marks, you must mention the **operating parameter** (pressure, frequency, number of passes) AND the **droplet size achieved**. Marks are deducted for vague statements like "homogenizer is used." #### Model Solution — Part (b) | Application | Specific Examples | Function in Beverage | Emulsion Type | |-------------|-------------------|----------------------|---------------| | **Flavours** | Limonene (orange), Menthol (mint), Capsaicin (chilli) | Volatile aroma delivery; water-dispersion of essential oils | Microemulsion for clear drinks; Nanoemulsion for cloudy | | **Colours** | β-carotene, Lycopene, Curcumin, Lutein | Natural pigment dispersion; clean-label colourants | Nanoemulsion preferred | | **Vitamins** | Vitamin A, D, E, K (fat-soluble) | Fortification | Micro/Nanoemulsion | | **Nutraceuticals** | Coenzyme Q10, Omega-3 (EPA, DHA) | Functional beverages | Nanoemulsion (high bioavailability) | | **Antioxidants** | Resveratrol, Quercetin | Health claims | Nanoemulsion | **Key advantages in beverages (2 marks):** - **Optical clarity** (for crystal-clear products) — droplets < 50 nm. - **Enhanced bioavailability** due to sub-micron size and high surface-area-to-volume ratio. - **Protection** of sensitive molecules (e.g., curcumin from photodegradation). - **Stable dispersion** of otherwise insoluble lipophilic ingredients. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Bullet Summary)** - **Microemulsion** = thermodynamically stable, **10 – 100 nm**, transparent, forms spontaneously when $\Delta G_{form} < 0$. - **Nanoemulsion** = kinetically stable (metastable), **20 – 200 nm**, translucent, requires high-energy input. - **Surfactant** lowers $\gamma$ from ~ 50 mN/m to **10⁻³ – 10⁻⁶ N/m**. - **Co-surfactant** fluidizes the interfacial film; essential for microemulsions. - **Bancroft's Rule:** surfactant solubility dictates the continuous phase. - **HLB for O/W beverage emulsions = 8 – 18** (typically 9 – 14). - **Laplace Pressure:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma}{r}$ — high for small droplets → drives Ostwald ripening. - **Optical clarity criterion (Rayleigh):** droplet size must be **< 0.1 × $\lambda$** → for $\lambda$ = 550 nm, $d < 55$ nm. - **High-energy methods:** HPH (5,000–50,000 psi), Microfluidization, Ultrasonication (20 kHz). - **Low-energy methods:** PIT, PIC, Spontaneous Emulsification. - **Zeta potential** $\vert \zeta \vert > 30$ mV → good electrostatic stability. - **Ostwald ripening prevention:** add a small fraction of a highly insoluble oil. - **HLB of mixed surfactant:** $HLB_{mix} = \sum x_i \cdot HLB_i$ (weighted average). - **Common beverage surfactants:** Tween 20/40/80, Quillaja saponin, Lecithin, Modified starch. - **Common applications:** Citrus oils, β-carotene, Curcumin, Vitamin A/D/E, Omega-3. - **Beverage advantage:** Sub-micron droplets give **10³–10⁶ × higher** surface area → superior bioavailability. - **Always mention** the **operating parameter** and the **resulting droplet size** in equipment-based answers to score full marks. ---

topics.title: Preservatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Preservatives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preservatives (in Beverage Processing)**
> A **preservative** is any naturally occurring or chemically synthesized substance that is intentionally added to a beverage matrix to inhibit, retard, or arrest the growth of spoilage microorganisms (bacteria, yeasts, moulds) and/or to prevent oxidative deterioration of the beverage's sensory, nutritional, and physicochemical quality, thereby extending its **shelf-life** and ensuring **microbiological safety** throughout the stipulated storage period.

In the regulatory context of food and beverage science, preservatives are classified under **Food Additives** as defined by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** and the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** under Regulation 3.1.5 of the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Think of a beverage (like fruit juice or a soft drink) as a **garden** filled with microorganisms. Microbes are the "weeds" that ruin the garden. A preservative acts like a **protective fence** around the garden — it doesn't kill every single weed, but it prevents them from entering, multiplying, and taking over. Just as a fence slows down invaders, a preservative extends the time before the beverage becomes unfit to drink.

Mathematically, microbial growth follows an exponential pattern:

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}$$

Where $N(t)$ is the microbial count at time $t$, $N_0$ is the initial count, and $k$ is the specific growth rate. A preservative effectively **reduces $k$** (or pushes lag phase) so that $N(t)$ stays below the spoilage threshold for a longer duration.

## 1.3 Why Preservatives Are Required in Beverages

- Beverages (juices, RTD teas, flavored waters, alcoholic drinks, dairy-based drinks) provide a **nutrient-rich, water-activity-favorable medium** (a_w typically 0.85–0.99) for microbial proliferation.
- Standard pasteurization (72 °C / 15 s) kills most vegetative cells but **spores survive**.
- Once opened, beverages are re-contaminated; preservatives act as a **secondary hurdle**.
- Regulatory compliance: Many beverage categories are **not sterile** and rely on combined preservation (hurdle technology).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> Module 1 specifically requires the student to:
> - Classify preservatives (chemical vs. natural; antimicrobial vs. antioxidant)
> - Explain the **mechanism of antimicrobial action**
> - State the **legal/regulatory limits** (FSSAI / Codex / EU permitted levels)
> - Discuss **concentration calculations** (% w/v, ppm, mg/L) and **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** concepts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Preservatives (Structured)

### 2.1.1 By Origin
- **Class I — Natural Preservatives**
  * Of plant origin: essential oils (oregano, clove, cinnamon), phenolic compounds (eugenol, carvacrol, thymol), herbal extracts.
  * Of animal/microbial origin: **nisin** (from *Lactococcus lactis*), natamycin, lysozyme.
  * Common natural antioxidants: **ascorbic acid (Vitamin C)**, citric acid, tocopherols.
- **Class II — Chemical (Synthetic) Preservatives**
  * Benzoates, sorbates, sulfites, nitrites/nitrates, propionates, parabens.

### 2.1.2 By Functional Action
- **Antimicrobial Preservatives** — arrest or kill spoilage/pathogenic microbes.
  * Mode: membrane disruption, enzyme inhibition, intracellular pH disruption, protein denaturation.
- **Antioxidant Preservatives** — prevent oxidative rancour, browning, vitamin loss.
  * Mode: free-radical scavenging, oxygen scavenging, chelation of pro-oxidant metals (Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺).

## 2.2 Mechanism of Action — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism of Major Chemical Preservatives (High-Yield for KTU)**

| Preservative | Active Form | pH Range | Primary Target | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Benzoic acid / Sodium benzoate** | Benzoic acid (undissociated) | 2.5 – 4.0 | Yeasts, moulds | Diffuses into cell as undissociated acid; dissociates inside cytoplasm → acidifies cell → inhibits enzymes of TCA cycle |
| **Sorbic acid / Potassium sorbate** | Sorbic acid (undissociated) | 3.0 – 6.0 | Moulds, yeasts (weak on bacteria) | Inhibits enolase and other SH-group enzymes; disrupts membrane transport |
| **Sulphur dioxide (SO₂)** | SO₂ + HSO₃⁻ + SO₃²⁻ | < 3.5 | Yeasts, moulds, bacteria; browning | Antimicrobial + inhibition of **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** and **Maillard browning** |
| **Nisin** | Lantibiotic peptide | 3.0 – 7.0 | Gram-positive bacteria (esp. *Listeria*, *Clostridium* spores) | Binds lipid II → inhibits peptidoglycan synthesis → pore formation |
| **Ascorbic acid** | L-ascorbic acid | wide | Oxidation | Reduces quinones back to phenols; scavenges O₂ and free radicals |
| **EDTA (chelating agent)** | Ca/Na-EDTA | wide | Pro-oxidant metal ions | Sequesters Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺ → blocks Fenton reaction and lipid oxidation |

## 2.3 The Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation — Why pH is Critical

The fraction of **undissociated** acid (the antimicrobial-active form) depends entirely on the pH of the beverage and the **pKa** of the acid.

$$pH = pK_a + \log_{10} \left( \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]} \right)$$

The fraction of undissociated acid is:

$$\alpha_{HA} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pH - pK_a)}}$$

| Preservative Acid | pKa |
|---|---|
| Benzoic acid | **4.20** |
| Sorbic acid | **4.76** |
| Acetic acid | 4.76 |
| Lactic acid | 3.86 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication for Beverages:** Since most fruit beverages are formulated at **pH 3.0 – 4.0**, both benzoic and sorbic acids exist predominantly in their **undissociated (HA) form**, which freely diffuses across microbial membranes. This is why benzoic acid works best in acidic beverages and fails in neutral ones.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Microbial growth model | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}$ | N = CFU/mL, k = 1/h |
| 2 | Dissociation fraction (undissociated) | $\alpha_{HA} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pH - pK_a)}}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Henderson–Hasselbalch | $pH = pK_a + \log \left( \frac{[A^-]}{[HA]} \right)$ | — |
| 4 | Concentration conversion (% w/v ↔ ppm) | $\text{ppm} = \% w/v \times 10{,}000$ | 1 % w/v = 10,000 mg/L = 10,000 ppm |
| 5 | Conversion (mg/L ↔ %) | $mg/L = \dfrac{g}{1000 mL}$ | — |
| 6 | ADI safety check | $\text{Intake} = \text{Concentration} \times \text{Consumption (kg/day)}$ | mg/day; must be ≤ ADI × body mass (kg) |
| 7 | SO₂ molecular fraction | $\% \, \text{free SO}_2 = \dfrac{[SO_2]}{[SO_2] + [HSO_3^-] + [SO_3^{2-}]} \times 100$ | bound + free forms |
| 8 | Free SO₂ from total | $[SO_2]_{free} = \dfrac{[SO_2]_{total}}{1 + 10^{(pH - 1.81)}}$ | pKa₁ of H₂SO₃ ≈ 1.81 |
| 9 | Inhibition of PPO | $V_i = \dfrac{V_{max}}{1 + \frac{[I]}{K_i}}$ | competitive inhibition |
| 10 | Decimal reduction time | $D = \dfrac{t}{\log_{10}(N_0/N)}$ | minutes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - In beverage industry, the formulator chooses the preservative by **matching its pKa with the beverage pH** to ensure maximum undissociated fraction.
> - Regulatory officers calculate the consumer's daily intake using Formula 6 to ensure no ADI violation.
> - Quality control labs measure free vs. total SO₂ using Formula 8 to verify compliance with FSSAI limits.

## 2.5 Regulatory Limits (FSSAI / Codex / EU) — High-Yield Table

| Preservative | INS No. | Permitted Level in Beverages (typical) |
|---|---|---|
| Sodium benzoate | **211** | 150 mg/L (carbonated beverages) |
| Potassium sorbate | **202** | 250 – 1000 mg/L (juices, wines) |
| Sulphur dioxide (SO₂) | **220** | 350 mg/L (wines), 70 mg/L (soft drinks) |
| Nisin | **234** | 250 – 500 mg/kg in dairy beverages |
| Ascorbic acid | **300** | GMP (no limit) — generally 200 – 500 mg/L |
| Nitrite (NaNO₂) | **250** | Restricted; not in beverages (used in cured meats) |
| EDTA (Ca-disodium) | **386** | 33 – 200 mg/L in carbonated drinks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake) — JECFA/WHO Values**
> - Benzoic acid: **0 – 5 mg/kg body weight/day**
> - Sorbic acid: **0 – 25 mg/kg bw/day**
> - SO₂: **0 – 0.7 mg/kg bw/day**
> - Nisin: not specified (regarded as safe)
> - Ascorbic acid: no limit (essential nutrient)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Problem 1: Undissociated Fraction of Benzoic Acid in a Beverage

**Problem Statement:** A fruit beverage has a pH of **3.50**. Calculate the fraction of undissociated benzoic acid (pKa = 4.20) present in the beverage at equilibrium.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Recall the formula**

$$\alpha_{HA} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pH - pK_a)}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**

$$\alpha_{HA} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(3.50 - 4.20)}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponent**

$$3.50 - 4.20 = -0.70$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the power of 10**

$$10^{-0.70} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{0.30} = 0.1 \times 1.9953 = 0.1995$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back**

$$\alpha_{HA} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1995} = \frac{1}{1.1995}$$

**Step 6 — Final value**

$$\alpha_{HA} = 0.8337 \;\;( \approx 83.4\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** 83.4 % of the benzoic acid is in the active, undissociated form at pH 3.5 — making it an effective antimicrobial at this pH. At pH 5.0, only ~14 % would be undissociated, drastically reducing preservative efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Problem 2: Conversion of Preservative Concentration Units

**Problem Statement:** A soft drink contains **0.02 % w/v** sodium benzoate. Express this concentration in **ppm** and **mg/L**. If a person consumes 1.5 L of this beverage per day, calculate the daily intake and check compliance with the ADI for a 60 kg adult.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Use the conversion formula**

$$\text{ppm} = \%\, w/v \times 10{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Calculate ppm**

$$\text{ppm} = 0.02 \times 10{,}000 = 200 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mg/L (1 % w/v = 10,000 mg/L)**

$$200 \text{ ppm} = 200 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Daily intake calculation**

$$\text{Daily intake} = C \times V = 200 \text{ mg/L} \times 1.5 \text{ L/day} = 300 \text{ mg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Permitted daily intake for 60 kg adult (ADI = 5 mg/kg bw/day)**

$$\text{Max allowable} = 5 \times 60 = 300 \text{ mg/day}$$

**Step 6 — Compliance check**

$$\text{Intake (300 mg/day)} \leq \text{Allowable (300 mg/day)} \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Just at the safety limit (Boundary case)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for KTU Examination:** The question tests whether the student understands that **ppm = mg/L (in dilute aqueous beverages)** because 1 L of water ≈ 1 kg. For beverages with significant solids, a density correction is needed.

---

## 3.3 Derivative Problem 3: Free SO₂ Calculation in a Wine

**Problem Statement:** A wine has a total SO₂ of **80 mg/L** at pH **3.30**. Calculate the concentration of free molecular SO₂ (the antimicrobial-active form). Use pKa₁ of H₂SO₃ = **1.81**.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Write the equilibrium formula for free SO₂**

$$[SO_2]_{free} = \frac{[SO_2]_{total}}{1 + 10^{(pH - pK_{a1})}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent**

$$pH - pK_{a1} = 3.30 - 1.81 = 1.49$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the power of 10**

$$10^{1.49} = 10^{1} \times 10^{0.49} = 10 \times 3.090 = 30.90$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the formula**

$$[SO_2]_{free} = \frac{80}{1 + 30.90} = \frac{80}{31.90}$$

**Step 5 — Final answer**

$$[SO_2]_{free} = 2.51 \text{ mg/L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical note:** For effective antimicrobial action in wine, the free molecular SO₂ should be at least **0.6 – 0.8 mg/L**. The calculated 2.51 mg/L confirms adequate preservation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Preservative Selection Quick-Reference

| Beverage Type | pH Range | Recommended Preservative | Typical Dose | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carbonated soft drinks | 2.5 – 3.5 | **Sodium benzoate** | 150 mg/L | Low pH favors undissociated benzoic acid |
| Apple juice / grape juice | 3.0 – 4.0 | **Potassium sorbate** + ascorbic acid | 300 – 500 mg/L + 200 mg/L | Sorbate effective vs. yeasts; ascorbate prevents browning |
| Wine (still) | 3.0 – 3.8 | **Sulphur dioxide** | 25 – 50 mg/L free SO₂ | Wide spectrum; antioxidant; inhibits PPO |
| Dairy-based beverages (lassi, flavored milk) | 6.0 – 7.0 | **Nisin** | 250 – 500 mg/kg | Effective in neutral pH; GRAS status |
| Packaged tender coconut water | 4.0 – 5.0 | **Sodium benzoate + potassium sorbate** | 600 mg/L total | Hurdle approach for safety |
| Functional / energy drinks | 2.5 – 4.0 | **Sodium benzoate** | 150 mg/L | Compatible with caffeine, taurine |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Classification Flow — Preservatives Used in Beverages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PRESERVATIVES"]:::root
    A --> B["BY ORIGIN"]:::branch
    A --> C["BY FUNCTION"]:::branch
    A --> D["BY REGULATORY CLASS"]:::branch

    B --> B1["NATURAL"]:::leaf
    B --> B2["CHEMICAL SYNTHETIC"]:::leaf

    B1 --> B1a["Plant origin: Essential oils, Phenolics"]
    B1 --> B1b["Microbial origin: Nisin, Natamycin"]
    B1 --> B1c["Endogenous: Ascorbic acid, Citric acid"]

    B2 --> B2a["Benzoates"]
    B2 --> B2b["Sorbates"]
    B2 --> B2c["Sulphites"]
    B2 --> B2d["Nitrites and Nitrate salts"]
    B2 --> B2e["Propionates"]

    C --> C1["ANTIMICROBIAL"]:::leaf
    C --> C2["ANTIOXIDANT"]:::leaf

    C1 --> C1a["Membrane disruption type"]
    C1 --> C1b["Enzyme inhibition type"]
    C1 --> C1c["Cell wall synthesis blocker"]

    C2 --> C2a["Free radical scavengers"]
    C2 --> C2b["Oxygen scavengers"]
    C2 --> C2c["Chelating agents"]

    D --> D1["CLASS I Safe in quantum satis"]
    D --> D2["CLASS II GMP limited"]
    D --> D3["CLASS III Quantitative limit mg per kg or L"]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2a47
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The leftmost column classifies by **origin**, the centre by **function**, and the right by **regulatory class** as per Codex/FSSAI. This map is the most frequently asked visual in KTU viva voce.

---

## 4.2 Mechanism-of-Action Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Microbial Cell in Beverage"]:::cell
    A1 --> A2["Membrane Transport"]:::step
    A2 -->|"pH < pKa, HA form"| A3["Undissociated Acid Enters Cell"]:::step
    A3 --> A4["Internal pH near 7"]:::step
    A4 --> A5["Dissociates inside cytoplasm: H plus A minus"]:::step
    A5 --> A6["Acidification: inhibits glycolysis and TCA cycle"]:::step
    A6 --> A7["Enzyme SH group binding: structural damage"]:::step
    A7 --> A8["Cell Death or Growth Arrest"]:::out

    A1 -->|"Antioxidant pathway"| B1["Pro-oxidant metals Fe2 plus Cu2 plus"]:::alt
    B1 --> B2["EDTA or Ascorbate chelation"]:::alt
    B2 --> B3["Free radical Fenton reaction blocked"]:::alt
    B3 --> B4["Lipid oxidation and browning prevented"]:::out

    classDef cell fill:#ffd966,stroke:#7f6000,color:#0b2a47
    classDef step fill:#fbe5d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#0b2a47
    classDef out fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#385723,color:#0b2a47
    classDef alt fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2a47
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-by-block interpretation:** The upper flow shows the **acid–trap mechanism** common to benzoic, sorbic, acetic, and propionic acids. The lower flow shows the parallel **antioxidant/chelation mechanism** which protects colour, flavour, and vitamin integrity. Both paths are **complementary** — this is the **hurdle approach**.

---

## 4.3 Hurdle Technology Integration Map (Preservative as a Hurdle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1["BEVERAGE SHELF LIFE STABILITY"]:::goal
    H1 --> H2["Combined Hurdle Technology"]:::hub
    H2 --> H3["Hurdle 1: pH (low acid)"]:::h
    H2 --> H4["Hurdle 2: Water activity a_w"]:::h
    H2 --> H5["Hurdle 3: Thermal processing pasteurization"]:::h
    H2 --> H6["Hurdle 4: Preservative chemical"]:::h
    H2 --> H7["Hurdle 5: Packaging aseptic fill"]:::h
    H2 --> H8["Hurdle 6: Storage temperature cold chain"]:::h
    H2 --> H9["Microbial Inactivation Achieved"]:::goal
    H6 --> H6a["Benzoate at pH 3.0 plus 150 mg per L"]:::h
    H6 --> H6b["Sorbate at pH 3.5 plus 300 mg per L"]:::h
```
