```mermaid
    H6 --> H6c["Nisin in dairy beverage 500 mg per kg"]:::h
    H6 --> H6d["Sulphur dioxide in wine 30 mg per L free"]:::h

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** No single hurdle is sufficient in modern beverage design; the preservative is **one of six** typical hurdles. The synergy reduces the intensity required from each — for example, lower thermal load is possible when a preservative is also present.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question Paper]**

### Q1. Define preservatives. Differentiate between antimicrobial and antioxidant preservatives with two examples each. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer (Concise, Board-Exam Style):**

> A **preservative** is a substance added to beverages to inhibit microbial growth and/or oxidative deterioration, thereby prolonging shelf-life (FSSAI definition).
> 
> **Antimicrobial preservatives** — arrest or kill microorganisms.
> *Examples:* Sodium benzoate (in soft drinks), Nisin (in dairy beverages).
> 
> **Antioxidant preservatives** — prevent oxidation reactions.
> *Examples:* Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C), Citric acid.

---

### Q2. State the Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI) values for benzoic acid, sorbic acid, and sulphur dioxide as per JECFA/WHO. Why is ADI expressed in mg per kg body weight per day? `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> - Benzoic acid: **0 – 5 mg/kg bw/day**
> - Sorbic acid: **0 – 25 mg/kg bw/day**
> - SO₂: **0 – 0.7 mg/kg bw/day**
> 
> ADI is expressed per kg body weight because the toxicological load of a substance is directly proportional to the body mass. This normalises the safety limit across different age/body-weight groups.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 1, Dec 2023 Model Paper Pattern]**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Classify preservatives used in beverage processing with suitable examples. Explain the mechanism of action of **benzoic acid/sodium benzoate** in beverage preservation. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** A carbonated soft drink is preserved with **0.015 % w/v sodium benzoate** at **pH 3.20**. Calculate the **undissociated fraction** of benzoic acid (pKa = 4.20) and determine whether a 1.2 L/day consumer weighing **55 kg** stays within the ADI limit. `[7 Marks]`

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Classification:**

1. **By Origin:**
   * *Natural* — Nisin (dairy beverages), ascorbic acid (fruit juices), essential oils (functional drinks).
   * *Synthetic/Chemical* — Sodium benzoate, potassium sorbate, sulphur dioxide, nitrites, propionates.

2. **By Function:**
   * *Antimicrobial* — Sodium benzoate, potassium sorbate, nisin, SO₂.
   * *Antioxidant* — Ascorbic acid, EDTA, BHA, BHT, citric acid.

**Mechanism of sodium benzoate:**

- In aqueous solution, sodium benzoate dissociates into Na⁺ and benzoate ion (C₆H₅COO⁻).
- At the low pH of beverages (2.5 – 4.0), the equilibrium shifts towards the **undissociated benzoic acid (HA) form**:

$$C_6H_5COO^- + H^+ \rightleftharpoons C_6H_5COOH$$

- The undissociated HA form is **lipophilic** and passively diffuses across the microbial plasma membrane.
- Once inside the microbial cytoplasm (pH ≈ 7), the molecule **re-dissociates**, releasing H⁺ ions.
- This causes **cytoplasmic acidification**, disrupting the proton-motive force, inhibiting glycolysis and the **tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle**, and denaturing key enzymes — leading to **growth inhibition or cell death**.
- The acid is most effective against **yeasts and moulds**; bacteria are comparatively more resistant.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the undissociated fraction at pH 3.20**

$$\alpha_{HA} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pH - pK_a)}} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(3.20 - 4.20)}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{1 + 10^{-1}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1} = \frac{1}{1.1} = 0.909$$

So **~90.9 % of the benzoic acid** is in the active undissociated form — confirming the high effectiveness of benzoate at pH 3.2.

**Step 2 — Convert concentration to mg/L**

$$0.015 \%\, w/v = 0.015 \times 10{,}000 \text{ mg/L} = 150 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — Daily intake for 1.2 L/day consumer**

$$\text{Daily intake} = 150 \times 1.2 = 180 \text{ mg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Permissible ADI for 55 kg person (5 mg/kg bw/day)**

$$\text{Maximum allowable} = 5 \times 55 = 275 \text{ mg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Compliance verdict**

$$180 \, mg/day < 275 \, mg/day \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Safe; well within ADI.}$$

> ✅ The consumer is **safe**. The consumption is at ~65 % of the ADI limit, providing a safety margin.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **Hurdle Technology** concept as applied to beverage preservation. How does sulphur dioxide act as both an antimicrobial and antioxidant in wines? `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** A wine has **total SO₂ = 100 mg/L at pH 3.10**. Calculate the **free molecular SO₂**. If the minimum required free SO₂ for microbial stability is **0.8 mg/L**, comment on the wine's safety. (pKa₁ of H₂SO₃ = 1.81) `[7 Marks]`

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Hurdle Technology Concept (Leistner & Gorris, 1995 — standard KTU reference):**

> Hurdle technology employs **multiple gentle preservation factors** (hurdles) simultaneously, each at a sub-lethal intensity. Together, they **synergistically** inactivate microbes — allowing milder processing with retained sensory quality.

In beverages the major hurdles are:

1. **pH (low acid)** — most fruit beverages at pH 2.5 – 4.5
2. **Water activity (a_w)** — reduced by sugars/salts
3. **Thermal process** — pasteurization (72 °C/15 s or 90 °C/1 min)
4. **Preservatives** — chemical hurdle
5. **Packaging** — aseptic fill, hermetic seal
6. **Storage temperature** — cold chain

**Sulphur dioxide (SO₂) — dual role in wine:**

*Antimicrobial action:*
- In aqueous solution: $\text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HSO}_3^- + \text{H}^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{SO}_3^{2-} + 2\text{H}^+$
- The **molecular SO₂ form** is the most antimicrobial; it diffuses into microbial cells and reacts with **disulphide bonds** in enzymes, blocking their activity. It is particularly effective against **yeasts, moulds**, and acetic acid bacteria.
- In wine, the typical free SO₂ range is **25 – 50 mg/L** for stability.

*Antioxidant action:*
- SO₂ inhibits **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** and **laccase**, the enzymes responsible for enzymatic browning.
- It also reacts with **acetaldehyde, quinones, and carbonyls**, preventing oxidative browning and the loss of colour/aroma compounds.
- It scavenges dissolved O₂ in the headspace, protecting anthocyanins and flavour esters.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Recall the free SO₂ formula**

$$[SO_2]_{free} = \frac{[SO_2]_{total}}{1 + 10^{(pH - pK_{a1})}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$[SO_2]_{free} = \frac{100}{1 + 10^{(3.10 - 1.81)}}$$

**Step 3 — Exponent**

$$3.10 - 1.81 = 1.29$$

**Step 4 — Power of 10**

$$10^{1.29} = 10^{1} \times 10^{0.29} = 10 \times 1.9498 = 19.498$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation**

$$[SO_2]_{free} = \frac{100}{1 + 19.498} = \frac{100}{20.498} = 4.878 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with minimum requirement**

$$4.878 \, mg/L \;\; \gg \;\; 0.8 \, mg/L \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{Wine is microbiologically safe.}$$

> ✅ The free molecular SO₂ is **~6× the minimum required** for stability. (Note: in real winemaking, this is the reason winemakers measure free SO₂; the total may be high but pH-dependent free form is what matters.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units** when stating concentrations: ppm ≠ mg/L in all beverages (only in dilute aqueous). Always write the units explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing benzoate with benzoic acid** — they are different forms; only the undissociated acid is antimicrobial. The formulator calculates the dose of sodium benzoate, but the active species is benzoic acid.
> 3. **Sign error in Henderson–Hasselbalch** — placing the wrong term in numerator. Memorise: $pH = pK_a + \log(\text{salt}/\text{acid})$.
> 4. **Forgetting density correction** for viscous beverages (e.g., nectars with pulp) when using ppm = mg/L.
> 5. **Writing "preservatives kill all microbes"** — the correct term is "inhibit / arrest / retard growth" since the goal is shelf-life extension, not sterilisation.
> 6. **Not stating the pKa** of the acid in numerical problems — without pKa, the Henderson–Hasselbalch calculation is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **[Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorise Before Exam]**

- 📌 A **preservative** is a substance that extends beverage shelf-life by inhibiting microbes or oxidation.
- 📌 Preservatives are classified as **Natural vs Synthetic**, **Antimicrobial vs Antioxidant**, and by **regulatory class** (Codex).
- 📌 **Sodium benzoate (INS 211)** → most common in soft drinks; effective at **pH < 4.0**.
- 📌 **Potassium sorbate (INS 202)** → most common in juices, wines; effective at **pH 3 – 6**.
- 📌 **Sulphur dioxide (INS 220)** → wine preservation; antimicrobial **and** antioxidant; pKa₁ = 1.81.
- 📌 **Nisin (INS 234)** → lantibiotic used in dairy beverages; effective against **Gram-positive** bacteria and spores.
- 📌 **Ascorbic acid (INS 300)** → natural antioxidant; **GMP** (no numerical limit).
- 📌 The **active antimicrobial form** of weak-acid preservatives is the **undissociated HA form**; the fraction is given by the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation.
- 📌 **1 % w/v = 10,000 ppm = 10,000 mg/L** (for dilute aqueous beverages).
- 📌 **ADI (JECFA):** Benzoic = 5, Sorbic = 25, SO₂ = 0.7 mg/kg bw/day.
- 📌 Beverage formulator must verify: **Intake = Concentration × Daily Consumption ≤ ADI × Body Weight**.
- 📌 **Hurdle Technology** uses multiple mild preservation factors in combination — preservative is one of several hurdles.
- 📌 Always state **pKa of the acid** and the **beverage pH** in numerical questions on dissociation.
- 📌 FSSAI (India) follows Codex Alimentarius; numerical limits vary for each beverage category — **memorise the high-yield table** in Section 2.5.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emulsifiers and Stabilizers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Emulsifiers and Stabilizers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emulsifier (Surfactant / Surface-Active Agent):** A food-grade amphiphilic molecule possessing both a hydrophilic (water-loving) polar head group and a lipophilic (oil-loving) non-polar hydrocarbon tail. It reduces the interfacial tension between two immiscible phases (typically oil and water), enabling the formation of a thermodynamically stable or kinetically persistent **emulsion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stabilizer (Thickening / Suspending / Bodying Agent):** A hydrocolloid (high-molecular-weight polysaccharide or protein) that increases the viscosity of the continuous phase, retards droplet coalescence, and prevents phase separation (creaming, sedimentation, flocculation) in colloidal beverage systems.

### Beverage-Specific Emulsion Types Encountered in Module 1

| System Type | Dispersed Phase | Continuous Phase | Classic Beverage Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Oil-in-Water (O/W)** | Oil / Lipid droplets | Aqueous serum | Flavoured milk, soft drinks with clouding agents |
| **Water-in-Oil (W/O)** | Water / Aqueous phase | Oil phase | Certain liqueurs, cream liqueurs |
| **Multiple / Double Emulsion** | W/O + O/W | Variable | Reduced-fat cocoa beverages |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Soap Bridge" Analogy**
> Imagine you are trying to mix cooking oil and vinegar (a classic household emulsion failure). They violently separate because the oil and water molecules are *mutually repelling* — like two rival cricket teams on the same field.
>
> Now imagine an **emulsifier** as a *diplomat with two arms* — one arm grabs the oil team captain, the other arm shakes hands with the water team captain. The diplomat (emulsifier) literally stands at the interface, holding the two immiscible phases together and preventing the teams from walking away from each other.
>
> A **stabilizer**, on the other hand, acts like a *thick, slow-moving crowd of spectators* filling the stadium (the continuous phase). Even if the players (droplets) try to swim upward (cream) or sink (sediment), the thick crowd slows them down so much that they cannot separate during the shelf life of the product.

### Why Are They Indispensable in Beverages?

- **Optical Clarity / Turbidity Control:** Clouding agents (e.g., brominated vegetable oil in citrus soft drinks) require emulsification to mimic juice opacity.
- **Flavour Delivery:** Lipid-soluble flavours (essential oils, oleoresins) must be dispersed in an aqueous medium.
- **Mouthfeel Engineering:** Stabilizers create the *body* and *texture* expected by consumers (e.g., smoothie-thick fruit beverages, protein shakes).
- **Shelf-Life Extension:** Prevention of ring formation, serum separation, and sediment.
- **Standard Emulsion Metrics (Beverage Industry):** Droplet size target **0.1 – 10 µm**; Creaming index threshold **< 5 %** over 30 days; Zeta-potential stability margin **± 30 mV**.

### Standard Emulsifier Categories Used in Beverages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrophilic-Lipophilic Balance (HLB) Range — Industry Rule of Thumb (Griffin, 1949):**
> - HLB **4 – 6** → W/O emulsifiers
> - HLB **8 – 18** → O/W emulsifiers
> - HLB **> 10** → Solubilising / Micelle-forming agents for clear beverages
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" — Mechanistic Breakdown

### A. Mechanism of Emulsifier Action (5-Step Logic Chain)

1. **Adsorption at the Interface** — Amphiphilic molecules diffuse from the bulk phase to the oil–water interface, displacing higher-energy solvent molecules.
2. **Reduction of Interfacial Tension (γ)** — Molecules orient with the polar head in water and the non-polar tail in oil, lowering the Gibbs free energy of the system.
3. **Mechanical Barrier Formation (DLVO + Steric)** — A coherent film is formed around each droplet, preventing coalescence via electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance.
4. **Droplet Size Reduction (during homogenisation)** — External shear (high-pressure homogeniser) breaks coarse droplets; emulsifier molecules rapidly re-adsorb onto freshly created surfaces.
5. **Long-Term Kinetic Stability** — Brownian motion + viscosity control + repulsive forces keep the dispersion intact far beyond the thermodynamic equilibrium.

### B. Mechanism of Stabiliser Action (4-Step Logic Chain)

1. **Hydration & Swelling** — Hydrocolloid molecules absorb water, uncoil, and form entangled random coils.
2. **Viscosity Enhancement** — The continuous phase becomes a structured pseudo-network, raising viscosity (often 50 – 5 000 mPa·s).
3. **Yield Stress Development** — At sufficient concentration, the gel network exhibits a yield stress that physically suspends particles and droplets.
4. **Steric / Electrostatic Stabilisation** — Macromolecules adsorb onto droplet surfaces, providing a polymeric cushion that prevents close-contact coalescence.

## 2.2 Bancroft's Rule (Foundational Theory)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bancroft's Rule (1913):** *The phase in which the emulsifier is more soluble tends to become the continuous phase.*
> - An emulsifier with high water-solubility (high HLB) → forms **O/W** emulsion.
> - An emulsifier with high oil-solubility (low HLB) → forms **W/O** emulsion.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> **Table 1 — Critical Equations for Emulsifier / Stabiliser Calculations**

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Boundary / Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HLB by Griffin (non-ionic) | $\text{HLB} = 20 \times \dfrac{M_h}{M}$ | $M_h$ = mass of hydrophilic portion; $M$ = total molar mass | dimensionless |
| 2 | Required HLB of an Oil Phase | $\text{HLB}_{\text{req}} = \sum_{i} (\phi_i \cdot \text{HLB}_i)$ | $\phi_i$ = mass fraction of oil $i$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Bancroft Criterion | Continuous phase = emulsifier-soluble phase | W/O : HLB 4–6; O/W : HLB 8–18 | qualitative |
| 4 | Stokes' Law (creaming velocity) | $v = \dfrac{2 r^2 \, (\rho_p - \rho_c) \, g}{9 \eta}$ | Laminar flow; $r$ = droplet radius | m $\cdot$ s$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | Critical Micelle Concentration | $v_{\text{CMC}}$ = surfactant-specific | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$ mol $\cdot$ L$^{-1}$ | mol $\cdot$ L$^{-1}$ |
| 6 | Laplace Pressure (droplet) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma}{r}$ | $\gamma$ = interfacial tension; $r$ = radius | Pa |
| 7 | Einstein Viscosity (dilute) | $\eta_r = 1 + 2.5 \phi$ | $\phi \le 0.02$ (dilute) | dimensionless |
| 8 | Power-Law Viscosity | $\eta = K \, \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ | $n<1$ = shear-thinning (xanthan) | Pa $\cdot$ s |
| 9 | DLVO Total Energy | $V_T = V_R + V_A$ | $V_R$ repulsion; $V_A$ attraction | J |
| 10 | Zeta Potential Stability | $\vert \zeta \vert > 30$ mV | Optimal range ±30 to ±60 mV | mV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Stokes' law shows that creaming velocity scales with the **square of the droplet radius**. Therefore, halving the droplet size reduces the creaming rate by a factor of 4 — this is why high-pressure homogenisation is the single most effective unit operation in beverage emulsion manufacture.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in the Beverage Industry

- **Citrus Soft Drinks:** Glycerol ester of wood rosin (GEWR) + brominated vegetable oil maintain cloudiness and prevent ring formation in the neck of PET bottles.
- **Sports & Energy Drinks:** Xanthan gum + gellan gum provide suspension of vitamins, amino acids, and colour particles.
- **Plant-Based Milks:** Lecithin + carrageenan + gellan gum create stable O/W emulsions of almond, soy, or oat lipids.
- **Cream Liqueurs:** Sodium caseinate + carrageenan deliver a long-term stable W/O/W double emulsion.
- **Functional Beverages:** Pectin (HM/LM) stabilises acidified protein drinks against sedimentation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Required HLB of a Blended Oil Phase

> **Problem Statement:** A beverage formulator wishes to emulsify a citrus-flavour oil blend consisting of **60 %** orange peel oil (HLB = **14**) and **40 %** lemon terpene oil (HLB = **12**). Calculate the *required* HLB of the oil phase to design a matching emulsifier system.

### Step-by-Step Algebraic Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation (mass-fraction weighted average).**

$$
\text{HLB}_{\text{req}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \phi_i \cdot \text{HLB}_i
$$

**Step 2 — Insert the known values with explicit mass fractions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HLB}_{\text{req}} &= \phi_{\text{orange}} \cdot \text{HLB}_{\text{orange}} \;+\; \phi_{\text{lemon}} \cdot \text{HLB}_{\text{lemon}} \\[4pt]
&= (0.60)(14) \;+\; (0.40)(12)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Execute the numerical multiplication for each term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.60)(14) &= 8.40 \\
(0.40)(12) &= 4.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the two contributions to obtain the required HLB.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HLB}_{\text{req}} &= 8.40 + 4.80 \\
&= 13.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result using the HLB selection table.**

$$
\text{HLB}_{\text{req}} = 13.20 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{O/W emulsifier, high-water-solubility class}
$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check using Bancroft's Rule.**

$$
\text{HLB} \gg 10 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{continuous phase = water} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{O/W confirmed}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{\text{HLB}_{\text{req}} = 13.20}$ — the emulsifier blend should be tuned to a combined HLB of **13.20** to deliver a kinetically stable citrus-flavour emulsion in an aqueous beverage matrix.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Stokes' Law Creaming Rate in a Citrus Beverage

> **Problem Statement:** A clouding emulsion in a soft drink has a dispersed oil phase with droplet radius $r = 1.0 \,\mu\text{m}$, density $\rho_p = 920 \,\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-3}$, dispersed in an aqueous phase of density $\rho_c = 1010 \,\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-3}$ and viscosity $\eta = 1.5 \,\text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$. Determine the *creaming velocity* in a standard 0.25 L PET bottle (effective vertical height $h = 12 \,\text{cm}$). Will creaming be visible within 30 days?

### Step-by-Step Algebraic Solution

**Step 1 — State Stokes' law for a single spherical droplet under gravity.**

$$
v = \frac{2 \, r^{2} \, \Delta\rho \, g}{9 \, \eta}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values with explicit SI unit conversion.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} \\
\Delta\rho &= \rho_c - \rho_p = 1010 - 920 = 90\ \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-3} \\
g &= 9.81\ \text{m}\cdot\text{s}^{-2} \\
\eta &= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $r^{2}$.**

$$
r^{2} = (1.0 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator $2 \, r^{2} \, \Delta\rho \, g$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \, r^{2} \, \Delta\rho \, g &= 2 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-12}) \times 90 \times 9.81 \\
&= 1.7658 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{(SI units)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator $9 \, \eta$.**

$$
9 \, \eta = 9 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.35 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Divide the numerator by the denominator to obtain the creaming velocity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{1.7658 \times 10^{-9}}{1.35 \times 10^{-2}} \\
&= 1.308 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}\cdot\text{s}^{-1} \\
&\approx 0.131\ \mu\text{m}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to mm per day for industrial relevance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{day}} &= 1.308 \times 10^{-7} \times 86400\ \text{s}\cdot\text{day}^{-1} \\
&= 1.13 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}\cdot\text{day}^{-1} \\
&= 11.3\ \text{mm}\cdot\text{day}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute time required to traverse the bottle height $h = 0.12$ m.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{cream}} &= \frac{h}{v} = \frac{0.12}{1.308 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 9.17 \times 10^{5}\ \text{s} \\
&\approx 10.6\ \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Decision on stability criterion.**

$$
t_{\text{cream}} = 10.6\ \text{days} \quad < \quad 30\ \text{days (target shelf life)}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{v = 1.308 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}}$; the droplet would traverse the full bottle height in **~10.6 days**, *failing* the 30-day stability criterion. The formulator must therefore **reduce the droplet radius below ~0.55 µm** (since $t \propto 1/r^{2}$) or **increase viscosity to ~4.0 mPa·s** to meet the shelf-life target.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — HLB by Griffin Method (Numerical)

> **Problem Statement:** A polyoxyethylene (20) sorbitan monooleate (Tween 80) molecule has a hydrophilic mass of 1310 g/mol and a total molecular mass of 1310 g/mol. Determine its HLB value.

**Step 1 — Apply the Griffin equation.**

$$
\text{HLB} = 20 \times \frac{M_h}{M}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert the molar masses.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_h &= 1310\ \text{g}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1} \\
M &= 1310\ \text{g}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\frac{M_h}{M} = \frac{1310}{1310} = 1.00
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 20.**

$$
\text{HLB} = 20 \times 1.00 = 15.0
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{\text{HLB}_{\text{Tween 80}} = 15.0}$ — confirms Tween 80 is a high-HLB, **O/W emulsifier** widely used in beverage and pharmaceutical systems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of Stabiliser by Beverage Type

> **Table 2 — Stabiliser Selection Matrix**

| Beverage System | Primary Function | Recommended Stabiliser | Typical Dose (%) | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Citrus soft drink | Cloud retention | Ester gum / BVO | 0.01 – 0.02 | Density matching |
| Sports drink | Particle suspension | Xanthan gum | 0.05 – 0.20 | Viscosity + yield stress |
| Acidified protein drink | Protein stabilisation | Pectin (HM) | 0.20 – 0.50 | Steric + electrostatic |
| Plant-based milk | Emulsion stability | Gellan + lecithin | 0.02 – 0.10 + 0.10 | Gel network + interfacial |
| Cream liqueur | Long-term stability | Carrageenan + caseinate | 0.03 + 0.50 | Double-emulsion gel |
| Aloe vera drink | Pulp suspension | CMC + xanthan | 0.10 + 0.05 | Shear-thinning viscosity |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Emulsifier & Stabiliser Action in a Beverage Matrix

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **multi-stage interaction** between emulsifiers, stabilisers, processing equipment, and the final beverage product. This satisfies the KTU requirement of *functional flow visualisation* for an otherwise physical process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Intake] --> B[Oil Phase]
    A --> C[Water Phase with Hydrocolloid]
    B --> D[High-Speed Pre-Mix Tank]
    C --> D
    D --> E[High-Pressure Homogeniser]
    E --> F[Droplet Size Reduction to 0.1 to 10 micron]
    F --> G[Cooling and Deaeration]
    G --> H[Carbonation and Ingredient Dosing]
    H --> I[In-Process Quality Check]
    I --> J{Stability Pass?}
    J --> K[Yes - Filling and Sealing]
    J --> L[No - Reformulation Loop]
    L --> C
    K --> M[Finished Beverage with O plus W Emulsion]
    M --> N[Shelf Life Monitoring]
    N --> O[End Consumer]
    O --> P[Feedback Loop to R and D]
    P --> A
```

**Reading the Architecture:**

- The **"High-Pressure Homogeniser"** node (E) is the operational heart where emulsifier molecules *first encounter* the freshly created oil–water interface.
- The decision diamond (J) embodies the **KTU quality gate** — zeta-potential, creaming index, and droplet-size distribution are evaluated.
- The reformulation loop (L) demonstrates the iterative **stabiliser dose adjustment** central to industrial beverage development.

## 4.2 Functional Role Map — Emulsifier vs Stabiliser

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTER[Interfacial Domain]
        E1[Emulsifier Molecule]
    end
    subgraph BULK[Bulk Continuous Phase]
        S1[Stabiliser Macromolecule]
    end
    subgraph OIL[Oil Droplet]
        D1[Lipid Core]
    end
    subgraph WATER[Aqueous Serum]
        W1[Water plus Sugar plus Acid]
    end
    E1 -- polar head in water --> WATER
    E1 -- non polar tail in oil --> OIL
    S1 -- viscosity and yield stress --> WATER
    S1 -- steric adsorption on droplet --> OIL
    D1 -- surrounded by emulsifier film --> INTER
```

**Interpretation of the Role Map:**

- The **interfacial domain** is governed entirely by the emulsifier — short-timescale (microseconds to seconds) events.
- The **bulk domain** is governed by the stabiliser — long-timescale (days to months) kinetic stability.
- The two systems operate on **different length scales** (nm vs µm) and **different timescales**, which is why both are required in any commercial beverage emulsion.

## 4.3 HLB-Based Emulsifier Selection Tree (Decision Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Beverage Type] --> B{Is the product clear or cloudy?}
    B --> C1[Clear Beverage] --> D1[Use High HLB Surfactant greater than 13]
    B --> C2[Cloudy Beverage] --> D2{Is oil phase less than 5 percent?}
    D2 --> E1[Yes - dilute O plus W] --> F1[Use HLB 9 to 12]
    D2 --> E2[No - concentrated emulsion] --> F2[Use HLB 8 to 10]
    D1 --> G[End: Single Surfactant Selection]
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Verify with Bancroft Rule and Lab Trial]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Emulsifier / Stabiliser Questions**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Stokes' Law:** Students frequently forget to convert microns to metres, leading to a velocity error of ~10$^{12}$×. Always write SI units in the substitution step.
> 2. **Confusing HLB Scale Direction:** Higher HLB = more hydrophilic (not more lipophilic). Mixing up this convention invalidates the entire selection logic.
> 3. **Griffin's Method Limitation:** Griffin's equation is valid **only for non-ionic emulsifiers**. Using it for sodium stearoyl lactylate (SSL) yields a nonsense value — use Davies' method instead.
> 4. **Stabiliser ≠ Emulsifier:** Students interchange the two terms. Remember — an emulsifier **enables** an emulsion; a stabiliser **preserves** it. Markers deduct up to 2 marks for this conceptual slip.
> 5. **Neglecting Bancroft's Rule:** Many students quote HLB but fail to verify with Bancroft. A complete answer always cites both.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define an emulsifier. How does its amphiphilic structure enable it to stabilise an oil-in-water beverage emulsion?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> An emulsifier is a surface-active food additive possessing both a polar (hydrophilic) head and a non-polar (lipophilic) tail within the same molecule. In an O/W beverage, the polar head orients into the aqueous phase while the lipophilic tail embeds itself in the oil droplet. This **amphiphilic orientation at the oil–water interface reduces the interfacial tension (γ)** and creates a coherent mechanical film around each droplet, preventing coalescence. The result is a kinetically stable emulsion. **Examples used in beverages:** lecithin (HLB ≈ 9), Tween 80 (HLB = 15), and sucrose monoester. **(3)**

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between an emulsifier and a stabiliser, giving one beverage example for each.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **Emulsifier** — Low-molecular-weight surfactant that **forms** an emulsion by reducing interfacial tension. *Example: Tween 80 in flavoured milk.*
> - **Stabiliser** — High-molecular-weight hydrocolloid (polysaccharide / protein) that **maintains** an already-formed emulsion by increasing continuous-phase viscosity and imparting yield stress. *Example: Xanthan gum in a fruit juice beverage.*
> - **Key Distinction:** Emulsifiers act at the **interface (nm scale, fast)**; stabilisers act in the **bulk (µm-to-mm scale, slow)**. **(3)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> **Module 1 Choice Pattern: Answer ANY ONE of the following**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Griffin HLB system** in detail. Describe its significance in selecting emulsifiers for **oil-in-water (O/W) beverage systems**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A beverage oil blend contains **70 %** orange oil (HLB 14) and **30 %** lemon oil (HLB 11). Calculate the **required HLB** of the oil phase. Recommend a **commercially available emulsifier** with a matching HLB. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **(7 Marks)**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** HLB (Hydrophilic-Lipophilic Balance) is an empirical, dimensionless scale (0–20) developed by **Griffin (1949)** that quantifies the relative affinity of a non-ionic surfactant for water and oil.
2. **Griffin's Equation (2 marks):** $\text{HLB} = 20 \times \dfrac{M_h}{M}$, where $M_h$ = mass of hydrophilic portion, $M$ = total molar mass. Valid for **non-ionic** emulsifiers only.
3. **HLB Selection Chart (2 marks):**
   - 4–6 → W/O emulsifiers
   - 7–9 → Wetting agents
   - 8–18 → O/W emulsifiers
   - 15–18 → Solubilisers
4. **Application to Beverage O/W Systems (2 marks):** Most beverage emulsions are O/W; the emulsifier HLB must be tuned to match the **required HLB of the oil phase** for the smallest mean droplet size and longest stability. Industrial procedure: identify oil-phase HLB → select emulsifier with HLB within ±1 unit.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Apply the mass-fraction weighted equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HLB}_{\text{req}} &= (0.70)(14) + (0.30)(11) \\
&= 9.80 + 3.30 \\
&= 13.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Recommend emulsifier:** *Tween 80 (polysorbate 80)*, HLB = **15.0**, falls within the O/W beverage range and matches the required HLB within the acceptable ±1 unit window. Alternative: Tween 60 (HLB 14.9).

**Step 3 — Cross-verification with Bancroft's Rule:**

$$
\text{HLB} = 15.0 \gg 10 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{water-soluble} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{O/W} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Final Statement (1 mark):** An emulsifier blend tuned to **HLB ≈ 13.10** will deliver a kinetically stable O/W citrus-flavour beverage emulsion.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **mechanisms of action of food stabilisers** in beverage systems, with special reference to **xanthan gum** and **carrageenan**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An O/W beverage emulsion has droplet radius $r = 2 \,\mu\text{m}$, $\Delta\rho = 80 \,\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\eta = 2 \,\text{mPa \cdot s}$. Using **Stokes' law**, determine the creaming velocity and the **time required to traverse a 0.15 m tall bottle**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **(7 Marks)**

1. **General Mechanism of Stabilisers (2 marks):** Food stabilisers are high-molecular-weight hydrocolloids that operate via (i) **viscosity enhancement** of the continuous phase, (ii) **gel network formation** providing yield stress, (iii) **steric / electrostatic repulsion** at droplet surfaces, and (iv) **water activity / mobility reduction**.
2. **Xanthan Gum (2.5 marks):** An extracellular heteropolysaccharide produced by *Xanthomonas campestris*. It forms **helical, rod-like** molecular conformations in aqueous solution, generating **high low-shear viscosity** and strong **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** behaviour (power-law index $n \approx 0.1$–$0.3$). At concentrations as low as **0.05 %**, it suspends particles, prevents creaming, and imparts a smooth mouthfeel. Highly stable across wide pH and temperature ranges — making it ideal for fruit-based and sports beverages.
3. **Carrageenan (2.5 marks):** A sulfated galactan extracted from red seaweeds. Three commercial types — **kappa (κ)** forms strong, brittle gels with K$^{+}$; **iota (ι)** forms soft, elastic gels with Ca$^{2+}$; **lambda (λ)** is a non-gelling thickener. In beverages, **κ-carrageenan** is particularly valuable for **protein interaction** (e.g., chocolate milk) — it binds to casein via the sulfate groups, forming a stabilising network that prevents sedimentation and serum separation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — State Stokes' Law.**

$$
v = \frac{2 r^{2} \Delta\rho \, g}{9 \eta}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert the numerical values (SI units).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= 2.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} \\
\Delta\rho &= 80\ \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-3} \\
g &= 9.81\ \text{m}\cdot\text{s}^{-2} \\
\eta &= 2.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $r^{2}$.**

$$
r^{2} = (2.0 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 4.0 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \, r^{2} \, \Delta\rho \, g &= 2 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-12} \times 80 \times 9.81 \\
&= 6.278 \times 10^{-9}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator.**

$$
9\eta = 9 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} = 1.8 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 6 — Creaming velocity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{6.278 \times 10^{-9}}{1.8 \times 10^{-2}} \\
&= 3.488 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Time to traverse $h = 0.15$ m.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{h}{v} = \frac{0.15}{3.488 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 4.30 \times 10^{5}\ \text{s} \\
&\approx 119.4\ \text{hours} \approx 4.97\ \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation (1 mark):** Creaming in **~5 days** fails a 30-day shelf-life criterion. The formulator must **reduce droplet size to ~0.5 µm** or **increase viscosity to ~12 mPa·s** to extend the creaming time beyond 30 days.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Emulsifiers & Stabilisers**

- **Emulsifier** = surface-active agent that *creates* an emulsion by reducing interfacial tension; acts on the **second (minute) timescale**.
- **Stabiliser** = high-MW hydrocolloid that *preserves* an emulsion by viscosity and gel-network effects; acts on the **second-to-month timescale**.
- **Bancroft's Rule:** continuous phase = phase in which emulsifier is **more soluble**.
- **Griffin HLB Scale:** 0–20; O/W emulsifiers fall in **8–18**; W/O in **4–6**.
- **Required HLB** of a multi-oil system is the **mass-fraction weighted average** of individual HLB values.
- **Stokes' Law** is the foundational equation for predicting creaming/sedimentation velocity in dilute, non-interacting dispersions.
- **Creaming time** scales as $1/r^{2}$ — halving droplet size quadruples shelf stability.
- **Common Beverage Emulsifiers:** Lecithin, Mono-/di-glycerides, Tweens, Spans, Sucrose esters, SSL.
- **Common Beverage Stabilisers:** Xanthan gum, Gellan gum, Carrageenan, Pectin, CMC, Guar gum, Sodium alginate.
- **Stabiliser Selection Map:** Xanthan → particle suspension; Carrageenan → protein stabilisation; Pectin → acidified drinks; Gellan → plant-milk gels; CMC → neutral pH drinks.
- **Quality Metrics to Memorise:** Droplet size **0.1–10 µm**; Zeta-potential **±30 mV**; Creaming index **< 5 %** over 30 days; target viscosity **1–5 mPa·s** for fluid beverages.
- **Industrial Unit Operation:** High-pressure homogenisation at **150–300 bar** is the standard for producing sub-micron beverage emulsions.
- **Regulatory Note (FSSAI / Codex):** All emulsifiers and stabilisers used in beverages must fall within permitted usage levels (e.g., xanthan gum GMP, carrageenan ≤ 250 mg/kg in certain applications).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FSSAI Standards for Beverages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** FSSAI Standards for Beverages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** is the apex statutory body established under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSS Act, 2006)** — operating under the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India. FSSAI is responsible for protecting public health by **regulating, supervising, and ensuring the safety of food (including beverages)** manufactured, stored, distributed, sold, or imported in India.

For the domain of **beverage processing**, FSSAI functions as the **legal-quality benchmark authority** that publishes a consolidated set of mandatory regulations covering:
- Product identity & composition
- Permitted additives, colours, preservatives, and artificial sweeteners
- Contaminant, toxin, pesticide residue & heavy metal limits
- Microbiological criteria
- Packaging, labelling, and shelf-life declarations
- Licensing, registration, and recall protocols

### 1.2 Key Statutory Instruments Governing Beverages

| Regulation | Year | Beverage Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Food Safety and Standards Act | **2006** | Parent Act — defines offences, penalties, enforcement |
| FSS (Food Products Standards & Food Additives) Regulations | **2011** | Defines identity, composition, additives for every beverage class |
| FSS (Contaminants, Toxins & Residues) Regulations | **2011** | Heavy metals, pesticide MRLs, naturally occurring toxins |
| FSS (Packaging & Labelling) Regulations | **2011** | Mandatory nutritional info, veg/non-veg logo, FSSAI logo & license no. |
| FSS (Prohibition & Restriction on Sales) Regulations | **2011** | Restrictions on sub-standard / unsafe products |
| FSS (Import) Regulations | **2017** | Imported beverage clearance & testing |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Driving License + Traffic Rules" of the Beverage Industry**
>
> Imagine a country's roads. Without traffic lights, lane markings, and licensing of drivers, every journey would be chaos. **FSSAI plays the exact same role for beverages**. The *Act (2006)* is the constitutional "rule book". The *Regulations (2011)* are the "traffic signals" (e.g., maximum safe sugar, permissible colours, heavy-metal ceilings). The *License Number (14-digit)* printed on every bottle is like a **driving license** — it identifies the manufacturer and makes them accountable. And just as police conduct random alcohol/challan checks, FSSAI Authorised Officers conduct *raids, sampling, and lab-testing* on retail outlets. If a beverage fails the test, it is "sent off the road" — a product recall, destruction order, or prosecution follows.

### 1.4 Salient Features of FSSAI Standards for Beverages

- **Universal applicability** — covers packaged water, soft drinks, juices, energy drinks, dairy-based beverages, alcoholic beverages, instant tea/coffee, and novel beverages.
- **Vertical standards** — separate product-wise standards exist (one standard for *cola-type* drink, another for *fruit juice*, another for *packaged drinking water*).
- **Horizontal standards** — common limits for contaminants, additives, and labelling apply across all categories.
- **Risk-based licensing** — **Central License** for large manufacturers (≥ ₹ 20 crore turnover), **State License** for medium units, and **Registration** for small petty manufacturers including petty retailers of beverages.
- **Mandatory display of FSSAI logo and 14-digit license number** on every primary, secondary, and tertiary beverage pack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Per the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, students are expected to map every regulatory parameter (e.g., **Brix, acidity, CO₂ pressure, preservative levels**) to the **FSSAI compliance range**. Memorization of *numerical limits* carries high weightage in Module 1 evaluation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

### 2.1 Classification of Beverages under FSSAI

FSSAI does **not** treat all beverages uniformly. Standards are issued **product-by-product**, and the manufacturer must know the *exact* standard applicable to their product before formulation.

**Logical Hierarchy of Beverage Classification:**

1. **Non-Alcoholic Beverages (NAB)**
   - **Packaged Drinking Water** (other than mineral water) — `FSSAI Standard 2.10.6`
   - **Mineral Water** — `FSSAI Standard 2.10.7`
   - **Carbonated Water / Soda Water** — `FSSAI Standard 2.10.4`
   - **Carbonated Fruit Beverages / Fruit Drinks** — `FSSAI Standard 2.10.6`
   - **Synthetic / Aerated Soft Drinks (Cola, Lime, Orange, etc.)** — `FSSAI Standard 2.10.6`
   - **Fruit Juices** (100 % juice, Reconstituted, Concentrated) — `FSSAI Standard 2.10.1, 2.10.2`
   - **Nectars** — `FSSAI Standard 2.10.3`
   - **Energy Drinks, Isotonic/Sports Drinks, Functional Beverages** — `FSSAI Standard 2.10.6 / 2.11`
   - **Tea & Coffee Based Ready-to-Drink Beverages** — `FSSAI Standard 2.10.7`
2. **Alcoholic Beverages**
   - **Beer, Wine, Distilled Spirits** — Standards under *Excise + FSSAI labelling & additive norms*
3. **Special Category**
   - **Plant-based / Dairy-analogue beverages** (almond, soy, oat) — recent FSSAI regulations
   - **Infant formula & beverages for young children** — stricter norms under `Standard 2.1`

### 2.2 Critical Compliance Parameters — "The Why & The How"

For each parameter, the **"Why"** (food safety rationale) and the **"How"** (analytical method of enforcement) are explained:

- **Brix / Total Soluble Solids (TSS)**
  - *Why*: Indicates fruit content, sweetness authenticity, and adulteration status. A 25 % juice drink below a specified Brix = dilution fraud.
  - *How*: **Refractometric** measurement at **20 °C**, expressed in **°Brix**.
- **Acidity (as citric acid, % w/w)**
  - *Why*: Influences pH, microbial stability, and taste. Excess acidity = natural or added acid masking.
  - *How*: **Titration with 0.1 N NaOH**, phenolphthalein indicator.
- **pH**
  - *Why*: Determines microbial hazard classification. Beverages with **pH < 4.6** are *acid-stable*; above that they are *potentially hazardous*.
  - *How*: **Potentiometric** using calibrated pH meter.
- **CO₂ Pressure (carbonated beverages only)**
  - *Why*: Defines fizziness, mouthfeel, and shelf-life (CO₂ inhibits aerobic spoilage).
  - *How*: **Pressure gauge at 20 °C** (typical **2.5 – 3.5 volumes of CO₂**).
- **Benzoates / Sorbates (preservatives)**
  - *Why*: Microbial control. FSSAI caps them to **consumer safety** (chronic toxicity).
  - *How*: **HPLC / Spectrophotometry**.
- **Heavy Metals (Pb, As, Cd, Hg)**
  - *Why*: Cumulative neurotoxicity, carcinogenicity, and organ damage.
  - *How*: **AAS / ICP-MS** on acid-digested beverage sample.
- **Synthetic Food Colours**
  - *Why*: Subject to **banned-colour** and **permitted-MPL** (Maximum Permitted Level) lists.
  - *How*: **Spectrophotometric / HPLC**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Limits Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> All values below are as per **FSSAI Regulations, 2011 (as amended up to 2024)**. These numerical limits are *high-frequency* in KTU Module 1 questions.

| Parameter | Beverage Type | FSSAI Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| TSS (as °Brix, min) | Carbonated Fruit Beverage / Drink | **≥ 10** | °Brix |
| TSS (min) | Synthetic Soft Drink (Lime/Lemon) | **≥ 5** | °Brix |
| TSS (min) | Cola-type Drink | **≥ 6** | °Brix |
| Juice content (min) | Carbonated Fruit Drink | **≥ 10** | % v/v |
| Juice content (min) | Fruit Nectar | **≥ 20** | % v/v |
| Acidity (as citric acid) | Synthetic Soft Drink | **0.5 – 3.5** | % w/w |
| pH (range) | Carbonated Soft Drink | **2.5 – 4.5** | — |
| Benzoic acid (max) | Carbonated Beverages | **600** | mg/kg (ppm) |
| Sodium Benzoate (max) | Carbonated Beverages | **600** | mg/kg (ppm) |
| Sulphur Dioxide (max) | Fruit Juices / Nectars | **700** | mg/kg |
| Saccharin (max) | Carbonated Beverages | **100** | mg/kg |
| Aspartame (max) | Carbonated Beverages | **600** | mg/kg |
| Acesulfame-K (max) | Carbonated Beverages | **300** | mg/kg |
| Lead (Pb) (max) | All Beverages | **0.05** | mg/kg |
| Arsenic (As) (max) | All Beverages | **0.05** | mg/kg |
| Cadmium (Cd) (max) | All Beverages | **0.05** | mg/kg |
| Mercury (Hg) (max) | All Beverages | **0.01** | mg/kg |
| Tin (Sn) (max) | Canned Beverages | **250** | mg/kg |
| Total Plate Count (max) | Pasteurized Juice | **50** | CFU/ml |
| Yeast & Mould (max) | Carbonated Beverages | **50** | CFU/ml |
| Coliforms | All Beverages | **Absent in 100 ml** | — |
| E. coli | All Beverages | **Absent in 100 ml** | — |
| Salmonella | All Beverages | **Absent in 25 ml** | — |
| Total CO₂ (min) | Carbonated Soft Drink | **1.5** | volumes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Note for Students:** Quantitative limits like *mg/kg* and *ppm* are **dimensionally equivalent** for dilute aqueous solutions. Always report heavy metals in **mg/kg** or **ppm** (parts per million) and microbiological counts in **CFU/ml** or **CFU/g**.

### 2.4 Heavy-Metal Limit Derivation (Sample Logic Chain)

FSSAI adopts the **risk-assessment approach** used by **JECFA (Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives)**:

1. **Identify the hazard** (e.g., Lead causes nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity).
2. **Determine the Provisional Tolerable Weekly Intake (PTWI)** = **25 µg/kg body weight/week** (Pb).
3. **Allocate a portion of PTWI** to beverages based on consumption data.
4. **Compute MPL** in beverage such that average daily consumption (≈ 0.5 – 1.0 L) stays well within PTWI.
5. **Set the legal ceiling** with safety factor → **0.05 mg/kg** (Pb) for beverages.

This is the same `PTWI ÷ consumption × safety factor` chain used across contaminants.

### 2.5 Real-World Industry Utility

- **Product Formulation**: Beverage R&D teams formulate to be *centrally within* the FSSAI range (avoiding borderline values) to **survive lab-to-lab variation** during enforcement sampling.
- **Export Compliance**: Indian manufacturers exporting beverages must also satisfy **Codex Alimentarius (FAO/WHO)** and the importing country's standards (e.g., EU, US FDA). FSSAI compliance is the *baseline* for export readiness.
- **Audits & Third-Party Certifications**: FSSAI licensing is a prerequisite for **FSSC 22000, BRC, SQF** audits.
- **Recall Risk Mitigation**: A failure in *coliforms* or *heavy metals* triggers **Section 26** of the FSS Act — mandatory recall at the manufacturer's cost.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — "Is this Soft Drink FSSAI-Compliant?"

**Problem Statement:**
A laboratory analyses a 330 mL carbonated orange drink sample with the following reported values:

- TSS = **8.5 °Brix**
- Acidity (as citric acid) = **0.62 % w/w**
- pH = **3.4**
- Sodium Benzoate = **450 mg/kg**
- Saccharin = **75 mg/kg**
- Lead (Pb) = **0.06 mg/kg**
- Yeast & Mould Count = **20 CFU/ml**
- E. coli (per 100 ml) = **Absent**

> Determine whether the sample conforms to FSSAI (2011) standards for **carbonated fruit drink / soft drink**. Identify the parameter(s) of non-compliance, if any.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the correct FSSAI standard to apply.**

The product is *orange-flavoured, carbonated, contains fruit juice* → it falls under **FSSAI Standard 2.10.6 — Carbonated Fruit Beverages (Fruit Drink)**. We use this standard's parametric table.

**Step 2 — Compare each parameter against its corresponding FSSAI MPL.**

| # | Parameter | Observed | FSSAI MPL | Status | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TSS | 8.5 °Brix | ≥ 10 °Brix | 8.5 < 10 | **FAIL** |
| 2 | Acidity | 0.62 % w/w | 0.5 – 3.5 % w/w | within range | PASS |
| 3 | pH | 3.4 | 2.5 – 4.5 | within range | PASS |
| 4 | Sodium Benzoate | 450 mg/kg | ≤ 600 mg/kg | within range | PASS |
| 5 | Saccharin | 75 mg/kg | ≤ 100 mg/kg | within range | PASS |
| 6 | Lead (Pb) | 0.06 mg/kg | ≤ 0.05 mg/kg | 0.06 > 0.05 | **FAIL** |
| 7 | Yeast & Mould | 20 CFU/ml | ≤ 50 CFU/ml | within range | PASS |
| 8 | E. coli (per 100 ml) | Absent | Absent | conforms | PASS |

**Step 3 — Declare the verdict.**

The sample is **NON-COMPLIANT** with FSSAI on **two parameters**:

- **TSS shortfall** of **1.5 °Brix** (insufficient fruit/sugar solids).
- **Lead excess** of **0.01 mg/kg** above the ceiling (i.e., **20 % above the MPL**).

**Step 4 — Compute the percent deviation for each failed parameter.**

This is the **standard KTU valuation step** examiners expect:

For TSS shortfall:
$$\text{Shortfall \%} = \frac{\text{MPL} - \text{Observed}}{\text{MPL}} \times 100$$

Substitute the numerical values:
$$\text{Shortfall \%} = \frac{10 - 8.5}{10} \times 100 = \frac{1.5}{10} \times 100 = 15 \, \%$$

For Lead excess:
$$\text{Excess \%} = \frac{\text{Observed} - \text{MPL}}{\text{MPL}} \times 100$$

Substitute the numerical values:
$$\text{Excess \%} = \frac{0.06 - 0.05}{0.05} \times 100 = \frac{0.01}{0.05} \times 100 = 20 \, \%$$

**Step 5 — Conclude and recommend corrective action.**

> The beverage fails the FSSAI compliance check. **Recommended corrective actions**:
> 1. **Reformulate** the drink to achieve **TSS ≥ 10.5 °Brix** (leaving a safety margin above the statutory 10) by adjusting fruit juice and sugar concentrations.
> 2. **Investigate lead source** in the manufacturing chain — likely culprits are raw water (use treated/deionized water conforming to **IS 14543**) or metallic process equipment (replace lead-based solders/bronze fittings).
> 3. **Retest** the reformulated batch for all eight parameters and obtain a *Certificate of Analysis* (COA) before market release.

**Step 6 — Penalty implication under FSS Act, 2006.**

> Under **Section 26 (Recall)** and **Section 27 (Penalties)** of the FSS Act 2006, manufacturing for sale a beverage *not conforming* to prescribed standards attracts a penalty of **up to ₹ 5 lakh** (first offence) and **up to ₹ 10 lakh** (repeat), with possible imprisonment of up to **six months**.

---

### 3.3 Second Worked Numerical — Daily Lead Intake Check

**Problem Statement:**
An average adult (body weight = **60 kg**) consumes **0.6 L per day** of a beverage containing **Lead = 0.04 mg/kg**. FSSAI PTWI for Lead = **25 µg/kg body weight/week**. Verify if daily consumption stays within safe limits.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute daily Lead intake:
$$\text{Daily Lead Intake} = \text{Concentration} \times \text{Daily Volume}$$
$$= 0.04 \, \text{mg/kg} \times 0.6 \, \text{kg/day} = 0.024 \, \text{mg/day} = 24 \, \mu\text{g/day}$$

Step 2 — Convert to weekly intake:
$$\text{Weekly Lead Intake} = 24 \, \mu\text{g/day} \times 7 \, \text{days} = 168 \, \mu\text{g/week}$$

Step 3 — Compute PTWI based on body weight:
$$\text{PTWI for 60 kg adult} = 25 \, \frac{\mu\text{g}}{\text{kg·week}} \times 60 \, \text{kg} = 1500 \, \mu\text{g/week}$$

Step 4 — Compare with PTWI:
$$\text{Safety Margin} = 1500 \, \mu\text{g/week} - 168 \, \mu\text{g/week} = 1332 \, \mu\text{g/week}$$

Step 5 — Compute the % of PTWI used:
$$\text{\% PTWI used} = \frac{168}{1500} \times 100 = 11.2 \, \%$$

> The beverage only consumes **11.2 %** of the safe weekly lead budget — well within FSSAI risk thresholds. **Result: SAFE**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Quick Reference for KTU

| Compliance Parameter | FSSAI Logic | Industry Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Brix shortfall | Probable **adulteration / under-concentration** | Product batch *rejection* |
| High benzoate | Preservative overdose | Reformulate, reduce dose |
| Detected pesticide | Contamination of raw material | Source change, supplier audit |
| pH out of range | Microbial risk, organoleptic failure | Acidulant rebalancing |
| Heavy metal exceed | Process water / equipment contamination | Replace metallic parts, RO water |
| Coliform detected | **Critical — Sanitary failure** | Plant shut-down, recall |
| Labelling missing FSSAI logo | Punishable offence (Section 49) | Pull stock, relabel |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FSSAI Statutory & Operational Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ministry of Health and Family Welfare Govt of India] --> B[FSSAI Apex Body]
    B --> C[Scientific Panels and Scientific Committee]
    B --> D[Central Advisory Committee]
    B --> E[State Food Safety Authorities]
    E --> F[Designated Officer]
    E --> G[Food Safety Officer FSO]
    F --> H[Licensing and Registration of Beverage Units]
    G --> I[Inspection and Sampling]
    I --> J[Reference Laboratories NABL Accredited]
    J --> K[Enforcement Action or Closure]
    K --> L[Product Recall Section 26 FSS Act]
    L --> M[Court Prosecution and Penalty Section 27]
    H --> N[Beverage Manufacturer Compliant]
    N --> O[Consumer Market]
    M --> P[Final Adjudication Food Safety Appellate Tribunal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The architecture is *pyramidal*. At the top is the **Government → FSSAI apex body → scientific panels** that *frame the standards*. The middle layer is the **state enforcement machinery** (FSOs, Designated Officers). The bottom layer is the **industry + consumer interface** where compliance, recall, and penalty actions occur.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define FSSAI. List any six classes of beverages covered under FSSAI Food Products Standards Regulations, 2011.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** is the apex statutory body established under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006** to lay down food (including beverage) standards, regulate manufacture, storage, distribution, sale, and import, and to ensure safe and wholesome food for human consumption. [1 mark for definition]
>
> **Six classes of beverages under FSSAI 2011 Regulations:** [½ mark each, 3 × 0.5 = 1.5 marks]
> 1. **Packaged Drinking Water** (Standard 2.10.6)
> 2. **Mineral Water** (Standard 2.10.7)
> 3. **Carbonated Beverages** — synthetic and fruit-based (Standard 2.10.6)
> 4. **Fruit Juices and Nectars** (Standards 2.10.1 to 2.10.3)
> 5. **Tea / Coffee-based beverages** (Standard 2.10.7)
> 6. **Energy drinks and functional / sports beverages** (Standard 2.11)
>
> [0.5 mark for proper class structure]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State the FSSAI permissible limits (with units) for the following in a carbonated soft drink:*
*(a) Sodium Benzoate, (b) Saccharin, (c) Lead.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Parameter | FSSAI MPL | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Sodium Benzoate | **600** | mg/kg |
| Saccharin | **100** | mg/kg |
| Lead (Pb) | **0.05** | mg/kg |

> FSSAI's *Food Products Standards and Food Additives Regulations, 2011* (and its subsequent amendments) specify the above Maximum Permissible Levels (MPL) for carbonated soft drinks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Note for students**: In KTU 2024 ESE, Part B questions of a module offer an *internal choice* (either A or B). Both options below are full-mark alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Describe the **statutory framework of FSSAI** with reference to the **FSS Act, 2006** and the **major regulations (2011)** that govern the beverage industry in India. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A carbonated fruit drink sample is analysed and found to contain:
- TSS = **9.2 °Brix**, Acidity = **0.45 % w/w**, pH = **3.6**, Sodium Benzoate = **550 mg/kg**, Saccharin = **90 mg/kg**, Pb = **0.04 mg/kg**, E. coli = **Absent in 100 ml**, Yeast & Mould = **30 CFU/ml**.

Determine **compliance with FSSAI** for each parameter. Comment on the verdict and the regulatory consequences. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The FSSAI framework operates on a *statute → regulations → enforcement* hierarchy. [1 mark]

1. **The Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSS Act)** — It is the *parent statute* enacted by the Parliament of India to *consolidate* the various food laws that existed before (Prevention of Food Adulteration Act 1954, Fruit Products Order 1955, etc.). It establishes FSSAI, defines key terms (food, manufacturer, recall), and lays down *penalties, offences, and procedures*. [1.5 marks]

2. **FSS (Food Products Standards & Food Additives) Regulations, 2011** — Provides the *product-by-product standards* (Brix, acidity, juice content) and the *positive list* of permitted additives, colours, preservatives, and artificial sweeteners with their **Maximum Permissible Levels (MPLs)**. For beverages, **Chapter 2.10** is the key reference (Standards 2.10.1 to 2.10.7). [1.5 marks]

3. **FSS (Contaminants, Toxins & Residues) Regulations, 2011** — Imposes *horizontal ceilings* on contaminants across all food and beverage classes: heavy metals (Pb, As, Cd, Hg, Sn), pesticide residues, and naturally occurring toxins (e.g., patulin in apple juice, ochratoxin in coffee). [1 mark]

4. **FSS (Packaging & Labelling) Regulations, 2011** — Mandates display of the **FSSAI logo**, the **14-digit license number**, the **vegetarian/non-vegetarian symbol**, **best-before/expiry date**, **nutritional information per 100 g or 100 ml**, the **name and address of manufacturer**, and the **country of origin for imports**. [1 mark]

5. **Enforcement and Penalty Mechanism** — State-level **Designated Officers** and **Food Safety Officers (FSOs)** conduct licensing, sampling, and lab-testing. Penalties are prescribed under **Sections 26–28** (recall, seizure, destruction) and **Sections 48–55** (penalties, prosecutions, imprisonment). [1 mark]

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

Comparing each parameter against the FSSAI MPL (Standard 2.10.6, Carbonated Fruit Drink):

| # | Parameter | Observed | FSSAI MPL | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TSS | 9.2 °Brix | ≥ 10 °Brix | **FAIL — shortfall 0.8 °Brix** |
| 2 | Acidity | 0.45 % w/w | 0.5 – 3.5 % w/w | **FAIL — below minimum** |
| 3 | pH | 3.6 | 2.5 – 4.5 | PASS |
| 4 | Sodium Benzoate | 550 mg/kg | ≤ 600 mg/kg | PASS |
| 5 | Saccharin | 90 mg/kg | ≤ 100 mg/kg | PASS |
| 6 | Lead | 0.04 mg/kg | ≤ 0.05 mg/kg | PASS |
| 7 | E. coli (100 ml) | Absent | Absent | PASS |
| 8 | Yeast & Mould | 30 CFU/ml | ≤ 50 CFU/ml | PASS |

**Step-by-step deviation analysis:**

For TSS shortfall:
$$\text{Shortfall \%} = \frac{10 - 9.2}{10} \times 100 = 8.0 \, \%$$

For acidity below minimum:
$$\text{Shortfall \%} = \frac{0.5 - 0.45}{0.5} \times 100 = 10.0 \, \%$$

**Verdict:** *Non-compliant* on two parameters (TSS and Acidity). All microbiological, heavy-metal, additive, and sweetener parameters are within limits. [1 mark for verdict + 1 mark for tabulation]

**Regulatory consequences (under FSS Act 2006):**
- **Section 26** — designated officer may direct the manufacturer to *recall* the batch.
- **Section 27** — the product is *unsafe* and the manufacturer commits an offence.
- **Penalty (Section 48)** — up to **₹ 5 lakh** fine and/or **6 months imprisonment** for first offence; up to **₹ 10 lakh** for repeat. [1 mark]

**Corrective action:** Reformulate to TSS ≥ 10.5 °Brix and acidity ≥ 0.55 % w/w (safety margin). Retest and obtain fresh COA. [0.5 mark]

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **FSSAI classification of beverages** with examples. Discuss the **microbiological standards** prescribed for packaged fruit juices and carbonated soft drinks. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A fruit nectar sample is labelled "100 % natural, no preservatives". FSSAI inspectors find **sulphur dioxide = 850 mg/kg** and **benzoic acid = 250 mg/kg**. Comment on the labelling claim and the compliance status. What are the **corrective actions and penalties** under the FSS Act, 2006? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Classification of Beverages (FSSAI 2011):**
- *Carbonated beverages* — Standard 2.10.6: synthetic soft drinks (cola, lime, orange flavour), carbonated fruit beverages (must contain ≥ 10 % fruit juice/ pulp), soda water.
- *Fruit juices* — Standards 2.10.1–2.10.3: fresh, reconstituted, concentrated; **nectar** has min 20 % juice content.
- *Packaged drinking water & mineral water* — Standards 2.10.6 and 2.10.7; mineral water must originate from a *natural source* and have *TDS ≤ 500 ppm* (per IS 14543).
- *Energy drinks, isotonic/sports drinks* — Standard 2.11 — caffeine cap at 200 mg/L, vitamins within RDA, no high-calorie sweeteners beyond MPL.
- *Tea/Coffee RTD* — Standard 2.10.7 — milk solids / sugar / tea extract limits.
- *Alcoholic beverages* — falls under separate state excise + FSSAI labelling / additive norms. [3 marks]

**Microbiological Standards:**
- *Total Plate Count* — pasteurized juice ≤ **50 CFU/ml**, carbonated soft drink ≤ **50 CFU/ml**.
- *Yeast & Mould* — carbonated beverages ≤ **50 CFU/ml**, juices ≤ **100 CFU/ml**.
- *Coliforms* — **Absent in 100 ml** for all beverages.
- *E. coli* — **Absent in 100 ml**.
- *Salmonella* — **Absent in 25 ml**.
- *S. aureus* — **Absent per 100 ml** in carbonated drinks. [3 marks for table]

**One-line summary** — FSSAI microbiological standards are designed to ensure the beverage is *shelf-stable* and *free from pathogens of public-health concern*. [1 mark]

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Labelling claim analysis:** "100 % natural, no preservatives" is a *claim* under FSS Packaging & Labelling Regulations. If SO₂ or benzoic acid is detected, the claim is **misleading** and in violation of **Section 23 (Packaging and labelling) of FSS Act, 2006**. [1 mark]

**Comparison with FSSAI limits:**

| Parameter | Observed | FSSAI MPL for Fruit Nectar (Std. 2.10.3) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sulphur Dioxide | 850 mg/kg | ≤ 700 mg/kg | **FAIL — excess 21.4 %** |
| Benzoic Acid | 250 mg/kg | ≤ 600 mg/kg | PASS (but contradicts "no preservatives" claim) |

Compute excess % for SO₂:
$$\text{Excess \%} = \frac{850 - 700}{700} \times 100 = 21.4 \, \%$$

[1 mark for numerical calculation]

**Verdict:**
- *Exceeds* FSSAI MPL for SO₂ → *unsafe* under Section 26.
- *Detected preservatives* → *misbranded* under Section 52 (penalty for misbranded food). [1 mark]

**Corrective Actions:**
1. **Recall** the batch (Section 26).
2. **Reformulate** to reduce SO₂ to ≤ 600 mg/kg.
3. **Relabel** honestly — declare "contains permitted preservatives (SO₂)" on the pack.
4. **Quarantine and destroy** non-conforming stock or down-blend only after lab verification. [2 marks]

**Penalties under FSS Act, 2006:**
- Misbranded food — fine up to **₹ 3 lakh** (Section 52).
- Sub-standard / unsafe food — fine up to **₹ 5 lakh** (Section 48) and possible imprisonment up to **6 months**.
- Repeat offence — fine up to **₹ 10 lakh** and imprisonment up to **1 year**. [1 mark]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 — Confusing "Standard 2.10.6"**: Students *paste the same* FSSAI numerical limits for every beverage type. KTU examiners specifically check if you have identified the **correct standard** for the *given* beverage — cola-type drink (Std. 2.10.6), fruit nectar (Std. 2.10.3), packaged water (Std. 2.10.6), and mineral water (Std. 2.10.7) all have *different* values. **Mismatching the standard = automatic 2-mark deduction.**
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 — Reporting units incorrectly**: Heavy metal limits must be in **mg/kg** (or ppm). Preservative limits in **mg/kg**. Microbial limits in **CFU/ml**. If a student reports "0.5 mg/L" for Lead, examiner deducts 1 mark because the legal unit is mg/kg. Conversion: 1 mg/L of an aqueous beverage ≈ 1 mg/kg.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 — Skipping the *deviation %* calculation**: Examiners reward the *computation step* (Shortfall % or Excess %) separately. Always compute.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 — Citing repealed acts**: Do *not* refer to the **Prevention of Food Adulteration Act (PFA), 1954** — it has been *superseded* by FSS Act 2006. KTU 2024 explicitly tests awareness of the *current* statutory framework.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5 — Forgetting to mention the 14-digit FSSAI License Number** in any packaging/label answer. This is a *favourite* 1-marker.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **FSSAI** = statutory body under **FSS Act, 2006**, Ministry of Health & Family Welfare.
- Parent act: FSS Act, 2006. Operating regulations: **2011** (with later amendments).
- **Vertical standards** = product-specific identity (Chapter 2.10 for beverages).
- **Horizontal standards** = contaminants, additives, packaging, labelling, microbiology.
- **14-digit FSSAI License Number** is *mandatory* on every beverage pack.
- **Beverage classes under FSSAI**: packaged drinking water, mineral water, carbonated beverages, fruit juices, nectars, RTD tea/coffee, energy/sports drinks.
- **Key FSSAI numerical limits to memorize (carbonated soft drink)**:
  - TSS (fruit drink) ≥ **10 °Brix**; TSS (cola) ≥ **6 °Brix**; TSS (lime) ≥ **5 °Brix**.
  - Sodium Benzoate / Benzoic Acid ≤ **600 mg/kg**.
  - Saccharin ≤ **100 mg/kg**; Aspartame ≤ **600 mg/kg**; Acesulfame-K ≤ **300 mg/kg**.
  - SO₂ in juices/nectars ≤ **700 mg/kg**.
  - Lead ≤ **0.05 mg/kg**; Arsenic ≤ **0.05 mg/kg**; Cadmium ≤ **0.05 mg/kg**; Mercury ≤ **0.01 mg/kg**.
- **Microbiological (carbonated drink)**: TPC ≤ 50 CFU/ml, Yeast & Mould ≤ 50 CFU/ml, *Coliforms and E. coli* **Absent in 100 ml**, *Salmonella* **Absent in 25 ml**.
- **Penalties under FSS Act 2006**: Sec. 26 (Recall), Sec. 27 (Unsafe food), Sec. 48 (Fine up to ₹ 5 L), Sec. 52 (Misbranded fine up to ₹ 3 L).
- **Always compute** *deviation percentage* for any non-compliant parameter.
- **Always state units** explicitly — mg/kg, CFU/ml, °Brix, % w/w.
- **Always identify** the *applicable* FSSAI Standard number (e.g., 2.10.6, 2.10.3) before evaluating.
- **Labelling must include**: FSSAI logo, 14-digit license, veg/non-veg symbol, best-before date, batch/lot, manufacturer name & address, nutritional info per 100 ml.
- **Risk-based licensing**: Central License (large units, ≥ ₹ 20 cr), State License (medium), Registration (petty).
- **PTWI** (JECFA basis) is the *starting point* for FSSAI heavy-metal limit-setting.
- **Recall procedure** (Section 26) and **penalties** (Section 48–55) — know them cold for the 14-mark question.
- **FSSAI standard reference format**: *FSSAI Standard X.Y.Z — Title*. Always cite it correctly in answers.
- **A beverage failing *any one* parameter = non-compliant = sub-standard / unsafe under FSS Act.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Control Analysis for Beverages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Quality Control Analysis for Beverages ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quality Control (QC)** in beverage processing is defined as the systematic set of **operational techniques and physico-chemical, microbiological, and sensory procedures** used to **measure, monitor, and regulate** the raw materials, in-process intermediates, and finished beverage products against **predefined specification limits** established by regulatory bodies (FSSAI, BIS, Codex Alimentarius) and internal company standards. The KTU 2024 syllabus defines three interrelated pillars that a student must clearly distinguish: | Pillar | Definition | Scope | |--------|-----------|-------| | **Quality Control (QC)** | Operational testing activities | Lab-based analytical checks | | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | All planned systematic activities | Entire process (farm-to-glass) | | **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | Management philosophy (Kaizen, PDCA) | Organization-wide continuous improvement | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Beverage Doctor Check-up" 🍹 > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are a doctor examining a patient. > - **Sensory evaluation** = Asking the patient "How do you feel?" (subjective but vital). > - **Chemical/Instrumental analysis** = Blood test, MRI scan, X-ray (objective, precise). > - **Microbiological testing** = Checking for infections/pathogens. > - **Process control** = Monitoring heart rate, BP daily to prevent future issues. > - **Standards (FSSAI/BIS)** = The textbook of "what a healthy human should be." > > Just as a doctor combines all reports to certify health, a beverage technologist combines sensory, chemical, microbiological, and physical data to certify that a soft drink, beer, or juice is **safe, nutritious, and acceptable**. ### 1.3 Key Standard Bodies (Bolded per KTU 2024) - **FSSAI** — Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (mandatory in India) - **BIS** — Bureau of Indian Standards (IS specifications) - **Codex Alimentarius** — International FAO/WHO food standards - **ISO 9001:2015** — Quality Management System certification - **AOAC International** — Official Methods of Analysis - **ISO 17025** — Competence of testing and calibration laboratories ### 1.4 Critical QC Parameters in Beverages (Bolded Standard Metrics) - **°Brix** (Total Soluble Solids content, measured at 20°C) - **pH** (hydrogen ion activity, target 2.5–4.5 for most acidic beverages) - **Titratable Acidity (TA)** (% citric/malic/tartaric acid) - **TSS** (Total Soluble Solids) - **TAV / ABV** (% Alcohol by Volume for alcoholic beverages) - **CO₂ pressure** (in carbonated beverages, ~2.5–4.0 volumes) - **Haze** (NTU — Nephelometric Turbidity Units) - **CFU/mL** (Colony Forming Units per millilitre) - **L*a*b* colour coordinates** (CIELAB system) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Beverage QC ``` Quality Control (Beverages) │ ├── 1. Sensory Analysis (Panel / Instrumental) ├── 2. Chemical & Physico-chemical Analysis ├── 3. Microbiological Analysis ├── 4. Instrumental / Physical Analysis └── 5. Statistical Quality Control (SQC) + Documentation ``` ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown **Pillar 1 — Sensory Evaluation** - **Why:** Beverage "flavour" is the #1 driver of consumer acceptance (75% repurchase decisions). - **How:** A trained panel of 8–12 assessors evaluates colour, aroma, taste, mouthfeel, aftertaste under controlled conditions (ISO 8589). - **Tests used:** Triangle test, Duo-Trio test, Hedonic scale (9-point), Ranking test, Descriptive analysis (QDA). **Pillar 2 — Chemical & Physico-chemical Analysis** - **Why:** Sensory perception is subjective; chemical data is **objective and reproducible**. - **How:** Measures °Brix, pH, TA, sugar-acid ratio, preservatives (e.g., benzoic acid < 150 mg/kg as per FSSAI), antioxidants (e.g., ascorbic acid). **Pillar 3 — Microbiological Analysis** - **Why:** Beverages are water-rich, sugar-rich media — ideal for microbial proliferation. - **How:** Total Plate Count (TPC), Yeast & Mould, Coliform, *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria* enumeration. - **Standard limits:** TPC < 50 CFU/mL for soft drinks; zero coliforms per 100 mL. **Pillar 4 — Instrumental / Physical Analysis** - **Why:** Required for trace contaminants, pesticides, heavy metals, mycotoxins. - **How:** HPLC, GC-MS, ICP-OES, FTIR, UV-Vis spectrophotometer, Refractometer, pH meter, Turbidimeter, Colourimeter. **Pillar 5 — Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** - **Why:** To detect trends, drifts, and assignable causes before out-of-spec production. - **How:** Control charts (X-bar, R-chart, p-chart, CUSUM). ### 2.3 Sensory Evaluation — Test Selection Logic | Test Type | What it Determines | When Used | |-----------|--------------------|-----------| | **Triangle Test** | Whether a sensory difference exists | R&D, packaging changes | | **Duo-Trio Test** | Difference vs. reference | Supplier changes | | **Hedonic 9-point** | Degree of liking | Consumer acceptance | | **Ranking Test** | Preference order | Comparative products | | **QDA (Quantitative Descriptive Analysis)** | Quantify descriptors in detail | New product development | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are high-yield for KTU ESE 14-mark derivations.** | # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Standard | Units / Reference Value | |---|-------------------|---------------------|------------------------| | 1 | **°Brix to Specific Gravity (SG)** | $SG_{20/20} = 1 + 0.0038 \cdot {}^\circ Brix$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | **°Brix to °Baumé (Bé)** | ${}^\circ B\acute{e} = 0.55 \cdot {}^\circ Brix$ | Degrees Baumé | | 3 | **pH definition** | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^+]$ | Log scale (no units) | | 4 | **Titratable Acidity (TA)** | $TA(\%) = \dfrac{(V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times E)}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | % w/w (E = equivalent weight) | | 5 | **Brix-Acid Ratio (BAR)** | $BAR = \dfrac{{}^\circ Brix}{TA(\% \text{ citric})}$ | Target: 15–25 for orange juice | | 6 | **Alcohol by Volume (ABV)** | $ABV(\%) = \dfrac{(OE - RE) \times 1.06}{0.79} \times \dfrac{1}{SG_{wort}}$ | % v/v | | 7 | **CO₂ volumes in beer** | $V_{CO_2} = (P + 1.013) \times (0.01821 + 0.09011 \times e^{-0.1134 \cdot T})$ | Volumes of CO₂ | | 8 | **Mean (X̄) of n samples** | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i}{n}$ | Same as variable | | 9 | **Standard Deviation (σ)** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ | Same as variable | | 10 | **Range (R)** | $R = X_{max} - X_{min}$ | Same as variable | | 11 | **X-bar Control Chart UCL** | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$ | Process mean + limit | | 12 | **X-bar Control Chart LCL** | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$ | Process mean − limit | | 13 | **R-chart UCL** | $UCL_R = D_4 \cdot \bar{R}$ | Process variability limit | | 14 | **R-chart LCL** | $LCL_R = D_3 \cdot \bar{R}$ | Process variability limit | | 15 | **Process Capability (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Cp ≥ 1.33 = capable | | 16 | **Six-Sigma DPMO** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{O}$ | 3.4 DPMO = 6σ | | 17 | **CFU/mL dilution factor** | $CFU/mL = \dfrac{\text{Colonies counted}}{\text{Volume plated} \times \text{Dilution factor}}$ | CFU/mL | | 18 | **Beer Colour (EBC)** | $EBC = 25 \times A_{430}$ (10 mm cell) | EBC units | | 19 | **CIE L*a*b* colour difference** | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$ | Visual threshold ΔE > 3 | | 20 | **Haze in NTU** | Measured by nephelometer at 90° | NTU (target < 1 for clear beverages) | ### 2.5 HACCP — Hazard Analysis Critical Control Points (7 Principles) > [!NOTE] > The 7 HACCP principles per **Codex Alimentarius (1993, rev. 2020)** — very high yield for KTU questions: > 1. **Hazard Analysis** (Biological, Chemical, Physical) > 2. **Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs)** — e.g., pasteurization, metal detection > 3. **Establish Critical Limits** (e.g., 72°C × 15 s for juice pasteurization) > 4. **Monitor CCPs** (continuous temperature loggers, pH probes) > 5. **Establish Corrective Actions** (divert, re-pasteurize, reject) > 6. **Verification Procedures** (audits, lab tests) > 7. **Record-keeping & Documentation** (HACCP plan, monitoring logs) ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Carbonated soft drinks:** In-line refractometer + pH probe + conductivity sensor → real-time Brix monitoring at 30,000 bottles/hour filling lines. - **Beer brewing:** In-process NIR (Near-Infrared) spectroscopy tracks fermentable sugar → alcohol conversion without sampling. - **Fruit juice:** HTST pasteurization (High Temperature Short Time: 88°C × 1 min) validated by *E. coli* O157:H7 5-log reduction. - **Bottled water:** Turbidity < 1 NTU, TDS < 500 mg/L, zero coliforms per 100 mL (FSSAI 2017). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation A: Brix-Acid Ratio (BAR) — Worked Example (7-mark standard) > **Problem:** A freshly squeezed orange juice sample has °Brix = 11.8 and titratable acidity = 0.68% (as citric acid). Calculate the Brix-Acid Ratio (BAR) and comment on consumer acceptability. **Step 1 — Recall the formula:** $$ BAR = \dfrac{{}^\circ Brix}{TA(\% \text{ citric acid})} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the given values:** $$ BAR = \dfrac{11.8}{0.68} $$ **Step 3 — Perform the long division:** $$ \dfrac{11.8}{0.68} = \dfrac{1180}{68} = \dfrac{590}{34} = \dfrac{295}{17} $$ **Step 4 — Continue division:** $$ 295 \div 17 = 17.35 \text{ (remainder } 6 \text{ → } 6/17 \approx 0.35) $$ **Step 5 — Final result:** $$ BAR \approx 17.35 $$ **Step 6 — Comment on acceptability:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Acceptable BAR for orange juice = 15 to 25.** A BAR of **17.35** falls within the acceptable range. The juice will taste **balanced (sweet-sour)** and is likely to be **commercially acceptable** to Indian consumers who prefer a moderately sweet-tart profile. --- ### 3.2 Derivation B: X-bar Control Chart Limits — Full Construction (7-mark) > **Problem:** A beverage plant monitors the °Brix of its finished orange juice daily. The grand mean ($\bar{\bar{X}}$) from 25 subgroups is **11.5 °Brix** and the mean range ($\bar{R}$) is **0.4 °Brix**. For subgroup size **n = 5**, the control chart constant **A₂ = 0.577**.
