Construct the X-bar control chart and identify out-of-control points. **Step 1 — Recall UCL and LCL formulas:** $$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R} $$ $$ LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values:** $$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = 11.5 + (0.577 \times 0.4) $$ **Step 3 — Compute the product:** $$ 0.577 \times 0.4 = 0.2308 $$ **Step 4 — Add to grand mean:** $$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = 11.5 + 0.2308 = 11.7308 \text{ °Brix} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the LCL:** $$ LCL_{\bar{X}} = 11.5 - (0.577 \times 0.4) = 11.5 - 0.2308 = 11.2692 \text{ °Brix} $$ **Step 6 — Centre line:** $$ CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = 11.5 \text{ °Brix} $$ **Step 7 — Final control limits (rounded to 4 decimals):** $$ \boxed{UCL = 11.7308, \quad CL = 11.5000, \quad LCL = 11.2692} $$ **Step 8 — Western Electric Rule (Rule 1):** Any single subgroup mean **outside [11.2692, 11.7308]** indicates an **out-of-control signal** → investigate assignable cause (e.g., batch Brix variation, instrument drift). > [!NOTE] > **Western Electric Rules (4 supplementary):** > - Rule 2: 2 of 3 consecutive points beyond ±2σ on the same side. > - Rule 3: 4 of 5 consecutive points beyond ±1σ on the same side. > - Rule 4: 8 consecutive points on the same side of the centre line (shift detection). --- ### 3.3 Derivation C: Standard Deviation & Cp from Sample Data (7-mark) > **Problem:** The °Brix of 7 finished juice batches was: 11.3, 11.5, 11.4, 11.6, 11.5, 11.4, 11.5. Specifications: LSL = 11.0, USL = 12.0. Calculate σ and the process capability index Cp. **Step 1 — Calculate the sample mean:** $$ \bar{X} = \dfrac{11.3 + 11.5 + 11.4 + 11.6 + 11.5 + 11.4 + 11.5}{7} $$ $$ \bar{X} = \dfrac{80.2}{7} = 11.4571 $$ **Step 2 — Compute squared deviations (X_i − X̄)²:** | X_i | X_i − X̄ | (X_i − X̄)² | |-----|---------|------------| | 11.3 | −0.1571 | 0.0247 | | 11.5 | 0.0429 | 0.0018 | | 11.4 | −0.0571 | 0.0033 | | 11.6 | 0.1429 | 0.0204 | | 11.5 | 0.0429 | 0.0018 | | 11.4 | −0.0571 | 0.0033 | | 11.5 | 0.0429 | 0.0018 | | **Σ** | — | **0.0571** | **Step 3 — Compute sample standard deviation (n−1 denominator):** $$ \sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n - 1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0571}{6}} = \sqrt{0.00952} $$ $$ \sigma = 0.0976 \text{ °Brix} $$ **Step 4 — Compute process capability index Cp:** $$ C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{12.0 - 11.0}{6 \times 0.0976} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.5856} $$ $$ \boxed{C_p = 1.708} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cp = 1.708 > 1.33** → Process is **capable**. Excellent quality control margin. --- ### 3.4 Derivation D: Titratable Acidity (TA) of Orange Juice — Worked Problem (5-mark) > **Problem:** 10 mL of orange juice (density 1.04 g/mL) is titrated with 0.1 N NaOH using phenolphthalein indicator. The titre value is 8.5 mL. Calculate the % titratable acidity (as citric acid, E = 0.064 g/meq). **Step 1 — Recall the formula:** $$ TA(\%) = \dfrac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times E}{W_{sample}} \times 100 $$ **Step 2 — Compute the sample mass:** $$ W_{sample} = 10 \text{ mL} \times 1.04 \text{ g/mL} = 10.4 \text{ g} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$ TA(\%) = \dfrac{8.5 \times 0.1 \times 0.064}{10.4} \times 100 $$ **Step 4 — Numerator:** $$ 8.5 \times 0.1 = 0.85, \quad 0.85 \times 0.064 = 0.0544 $$ **Step 5 — Divide by sample mass and multiply by 100:** $$ TA(\%) = \dfrac{0.0544}{10.4} \times 100 = 0.00523 \times 100 = 0.523 $$ **Step 6 — Final result:** $$ \boxed{TA \approx 0.523\% \text{ (as citric acid)}} $$ **Step 7 — Validation against standard:** Typical orange juice TA = 0.5–1.0% citric acid. **Result is acceptable.** --- ### 3.5 Derivation E: Conversion of °Brix to Specific Gravity and °Baumé **Step 1 — Brix to SG:** $$ SG_{20/20} = 1 + 0.0038 \cdot {}^\circ Brix $$ **For 11.5 °Brix:** $$ SG = 1 + 0.0038 \times 11.5 = 1 + 0.0437 = 1.0437 $$ **Step 2 — Brix to Baumé:** $$ {}^\circ B\acute{e} = 0.55 \times {}^\circ Brix $$ **For 11.5 °Brix:** $$ {}^\circ B\acute{e} = 0.55 \times 11.5 = 6.325 \approx 6.33 \text{ °Bé} $$ --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — QC Method Selection Guide | Quality Parameter | Instrument / Method | Standard Reference | Typical Spec | |-------------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------| | °Brix | Refractometer (digital, 0–32 °Brix) | ICUMSA Method GS4-13 | 10–13 for orange juice | | pH | Digital pH meter (calibrated 3-point) | IS 1060 | 3.0–4.0 (juice) | | TA | Titration with 0.1 N NaOH | AOAC 942.15 | 0.5–1.0% citric | | CO₂ (beer) | Zahm & Nagel piercing device, or Anton Paar CarboQC | ASBC Beer-13 | 2.5–3.5 volumes | | Colour | HunterLab ColorFlex L*a*b* | CIE 015:2018 | ΔE ≤ 3 vs. standard | | Haze | HACH 2100N turbidimeter (NTU) | ISO 7027 | < 1 NTU (clear) | | TPC | Pour plate, 35°C / 48 h, PCA | IS 5402 | < 50 CFU/mL | | Yeast/Mould | Spread plate, YGC agar, 25°C / 5 d | IS 5404 | < 10 CFU/mL | | *E. coli* | MPN or membrane filtration | IS 5887 (Part I) | Absent per 100 mL | | Pesticides | GC-MS / LC-MS/MS | EN 15662 QuEChERS | ppb level | | Heavy metals | ICP-OES / AAS | AOAC 2015.01 | Pb < 0.01 mg/kg | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Beverage Quality Control — End-to-End Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Material Receipt
Fruits, Water, Sugar, CO2] --> B[In-process QC
Refractometer, pH probe, conductivity] B --> C[Process Stage
Pasteurization / Fermentation / Carbonation] C --> D[Finished Product QC
Sensory + Chemical + Micro + Instrumental] D --> E{Specification
Met?} E -- Yes --> F[Release to Market
FSSAI / BIS / ISO Compliance] E -- No --> G[Corrective Action
Re-work, Reject, Investigate Root Cause] G --> H[Root Cause Analysis
5-Why, Ishikawa, Pareto] H --> B F --> I[Customer Feedback Loop
Sensory + Claim Analysis] I --> D ``` ### 4.2 HACCP Decision Tree (CCP Identification) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Hazard Identified at Process Step] --> Q2{Does preventive
control exist?} Q2 -- No --> N1[Not a CCP
Apply GMP] Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Will hazard
be eliminated
or reduced to
safe level?} Q3 -- No --> N1 Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Could contamination
occur or increase
to unacceptable
level?} Q4 -- No --> CCP1[CCP Identified] Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Will subsequent
step eliminate or
reduce hazard?} Q5 -- No --> CCP1 Q5 -- Yes --> N1 CCP1 --> CCP2[Set Critical Limits
Monitor Corrective Action
Verify and Document] ``` ### 4.3 Statistical Quality Control (SQC) — X-bar and R Chart Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Collect Subgroup Samples
n=5] --> S2[Compute X-bar and R] S2 --> S3[Plot on Control Chart
X-bar: n=25 subgroups
R-chart: same data] S3 --> S4{All points within
3-sigma limits?} S4 -- Yes --> S5[Process In Control
Continue Monitoring] S4 -- No --> S6[Out of Control Signal
Apply Western Electric Rules] S6 --> S7[Identify Assignable Cause
Tool: Pareto, Ishikawa, 5-Why] S7 --> S8[Corrective Action
Update SOPs] S8 --> S1 ``` ### 4.4 Sensory Evaluation Panel Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Panelist Recruitment
Screening Tests] --> P2[Training Phase
8 to 12 weeks
Reference Standards] P2 --> P3[Calibration Sessions
Identify Descriptors
Establish Reference Vocabulary] P3 --> P4[Test Type Selection
Triangle, Duo-Trio,
Hedonic, QDA, Ranking] P4 --> P5[Booth Setup
ISO 8589 Compliant
20 to 22 °C, Red Light] P5 --> P6[Sample Presentation
Randomized, Balanced
3-digit Codes] P6 --> P7[Data Collection
Hedonic Score / QDA Intensity] P7 --> P8[Statistical Analysis
ANOVA, Tukey HSD,
PCA, Spider Plots] P8 --> P9[Report and Decision
Accept / Reject / Reformulate] ``` ### 4.5 Beverage Quality Parameter Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Physical PH1[Brix TSS] PH2[pH Acidity] PH3[Colour L*a*b*] PH4[Turbidity NTU] PH5[Viscosity cP] PH6[CO2 Volumes] end subgraph Chemical CH1[TA Percent] CH2[Sugar Profile HPLC] CH3[Organic Acids HPLC] CH4[Preservatives] CH5[Antioxidants] CH6[Trace Metals] end subgraph Microbiological MB1[TPC PCA] MB2[Yeast and Mould] MB3[Coliforms] MB4[Pathogen Screen] end subgraph Sensory SN1[Appearance] SN2[Aroma] SN3[Taste] SN4[Mouthfeel] SN5[Aftertaste] end subgraph Compliance CM1[FSSAI] CM2[BIS IS] CM3[Codex] CM4[ISO 22000] end ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Brix-Acid Ratio. Why is it an important quality parameter for citrus juices?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Brix-Acid Ratio (BAR)** is defined as the ratio of total soluble solids (°Brix) to the titratable acidity (expressed as % of the predominant organic acid) of a juice. > > Mathematically: > > $$ > BAR = \dfrac{{}^\circ Brix}{TA(\% \text{ citric acid})} > $$ > > **Importance (1.5 marks):** It directly governs the **sensory balance** between sweetness and sourness in a citrus juice. The ratio is the single most important indicator of **maturity, harvest timing, consumer acceptability, and price** in orange, grapefruit, lemon, and lime juices. A standard BAR of 15–25 indicates a well-balanced commercial orange juice. A ratio below 10 indicates the juice is too sour (immature/unripe fruit); above 30 indicates over-ripe or over-diluted fruit with a flat taste. > > **Regulatory use:** Mandatory declaration on citrus juice labels under FSSAI; used internationally for juice grading by the Codex Alimentarius Citrus Juice Standard (CODEX STAN 45-1981, rev. 2005). --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **List any three microbiological tests performed in a beverage quality control laboratory and state the standard limits for each as per FSSAI.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **1. Total Plate Count (TPC) — IS 5402:2012** > Limit: **< 50 CFU/mL** for carbonated soft drinks; < 100 CFU/mL for non-carbonated beverages. > > **2. Yeast and Mould Count — IS 5404:2012** > Limit: **< 10 CFU/mL**; must be absent in sterile-filtered beverages. > > **3. Coliform Count — IS 5887 (Part I)** > Limit: **Absent in 100 mL** sample (i.e., MPN < 2/100 mL). > > **Optional 4th:** *E. coli* and *Salmonella* — must be **absente** per 25 mL of the final product, especially for unpasteurized fruit juices per FSSAI (2017 amendment) where mandatory 5-log reduction via pasteurization is enforced. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - For 14-mark questions, students commonly **skip the numerical step-by-step**, deducting 3–4 marks. > - Avoid one-line definitions; KTU award **2 marks for definition, 3 for diagram/flowchart, 6–7 for explanation, and 2 for example/regulation citation**. > - Always **conclude with a regulatory standard** (FSSAI/BIS/Codex) — failing to do so costs 1 mark consistently. > - In SQC questions, students confuse **A₂, D₃, D₄** constants — memorize: A₂ for X-bar, D₃/D₄ for R-chart. > - In sensory questions, students forget to mention **booth conditions (ISO 8589)** — losing 1 mark. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Full-length SQC + Brix-Acid Ratio problem* **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Q8]** **(a)** Define Statistical Quality Control (SQC). With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the construction and use of an **X-bar control chart** in a beverage processing plant. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A beverage plant monitors the °Brix of its finished orange juice. The data from 5 subgroups (n = 5) are: | Subgroup | X1 | X2 | X3 | X4 | X5 | |----------|----|----|----|----|----| | 1 | 11.2 | 11.3 | 11.1 | 11.4 | 11.0 | | 2 | 11.5 | 11.4 | 11.6 | 11.5 | 11.3 | | 3 | 11.8 | 11.7 | 11.6 | 11.9 | 11.5 | | 4 | 11.0 | 11.1 | 11.2 | 11.0 | 10.9 | | 5 | 11.4 | 11.5 | 11.3 | 11.6 | 11.5 | Given A₂ = 0.577, calculate the **grand mean (X̄̄), mean range (R̄), and the upper and lower control limits** of the X-bar chart. Plot the chart (tabulate) and comment on the state of statistical control. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (Question A)** **(a) [7 marks] — SQC and X-bar Chart** **Definition (1.5 marks):** > Statistical Quality Control (SQC) is the application of statistical techniques — chiefly control charts — to monitor, control, and continuously improve a manufacturing process by distinguishing between **common-cause (random) variation** and **assignable-cause (special) variation**, ensuring the process remains within predefined specification limits. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sample n=5 bottles] --> B[Measure X] B --> C[Compute X-bar and R] C --> D{X-bar within
UCL to LCL?} D -- Yes --> E[In Control
Common Cause Variation] D -- No --> F[Out of Control
Assignable Cause] F --> G[Investigate] G --> H[Corrective Action] H --> A E --> I[Continue Production] ``` **Construction of X-bar Chart (2.5 marks):** 1. Collect **k = 20 to 25 subgroups** of size n = 2 to 10 (typically n = 5). 2. Compute the mean of each subgroup: $\bar{X}_i = \dfrac{\sum X_j}{n}$ 3. Compute the range of each subgroup: $R_i = X_{max} - X_{min}$ 4. Compute grand mean: $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k}$ 5. Compute mean range: $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum R_i}{k}$ 6. Compute control limits using constants from standard control chart tables: - $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$ - $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$ 7. Plot points, draw UCL/CL/LCL lines. **Use in Beverages (1 mark):** Monitors °Brix, pH, fill volume, CO₂ pressure, alcohol content, etc. --- **(b) [7 marks] — Numerical X-bar Chart Construction** **Step 1 — Compute subgroup means and ranges:** | Subgroup | Values | Sum | X̄ᵢ | Rᵢ | |----------|--------|-----|-----|-----| | 1 | 11.2, 11.3, 11.1, 11.4, 11.0 | 56.0 | **11.20** | 0.4 | | 2 | 11.5, 11.4, 11.6, 11.5, 11.3 | 57.3 | **11.46** | 0.3 | | 3 | 11.8, 11.7, 11.6, 11.9, 11.5 | 58.5 | **11.70** | 0.4 | | 4 | 11.0, 11.1, 11.2, 11.0, 10.9 | 55.2 | **11.04** | 0.3 | | 5 | 11.4, 11.5, 11.3, 11.6, 11.5 | 57.3 | **11.46** | 0.3 | **Step 2 — Grand Mean (X̄̄):** $$ \bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{11.20 + 11.46 + 11.70 + 11.04 + 11.46}{5} = \dfrac{56.86}{5} = 11.372 $$ **Step 3 — Mean Range (R̄):** $$ \bar{R} = \dfrac{0.4 + 0.3 + 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.3}{5} = \dfrac{1.7}{5} = 0.34 $$ **Step 4 — Upper Control Limit:** $$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R} = 11.372 + (0.577 \times 0.34) $$ $$ 0.577 \times 0.34 = 0.1962 $$ $$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = 11.372 + 0.1962 = \boxed{11.5682} $$ **Step 5 — Lower Control Limit:** $$ LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R} = 11.372 - 0.1962 = \boxed{11.1758} $$ **Step 6 — Tabulated Chart:** | Subgroup | X̄ᵢ | LCL | CL | UCL | Status | |----------|------|------|------|------|--------| | 1 | 11.20 | 11.18 | 11.37 | 11.57 | In Control | | 2 | 11.46 | 11.18 | 11.37 | 11.57 | In Control | | 3 | **11.70** | 11.18 | 11.37 | 11.57 | **OUT OF CONTROL** | | 4 | **11.04** | 11.18 | 11.37 | 11.57 | **OUT OF CONTROL** | | 5 | 11.46 | 11.18 | 11.37 | 11.57 | In Control | **Step 7 — Comment (1 mark):** > Subgroup 3 exceeds the UCL and Subgroup 4 falls below the LCL → **process is out of statistical control.** Possible assignable causes: **batch variation in Brix** (overripe fruit), **refratcometer miscalibration**, or **ambient temperature drift** affecting density measurement. Action: investigate Subgroups 3 and 4, perform root cause analysis (Ishikawa 4M: Man, Machine, Material, Method), and recalibrate the instrument. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Sensory + Microbiological + HACCP* **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Q9]** **(a)** Explain the **principles and procedure of sensory evaluation** of beverages. List any four sensory tests with their specific applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **seven principles of HACCP** as per the Codex Alimentarius. Identify **at least three Critical Control Points (CCPs)** in the production of pasteurized fruit juice, and state their critical limits. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (Question B)** **(a) [7 marks] — Sensory Evaluation** **Definition (1 mark):** > Sensory evaluation is a scientific discipline used to **evoke, measure, analyse, and interpret human responses to beverages** as perceived by the senses of sight, smell, taste, touch, and hearing. **General Procedure (3 marks):** 1. **Panel Selection:** Recruit 8–12 panelists, screen for health, allergies, ability to distinguish basic tastes. 2. **Panel Training:** 8–12 weeks; exposure to reference standards (e.g., sucrose = sweet, citric acid = sour, NaCl = salty, caffeine = bitter, MSG = umami). 3. **Test Booth Setup:** ISO 8589 booths with controlled temperature (20–22 °C), humidity, red light (to mask colour differences), positive pressure, isolated booths. 4. **Sample Presentation:** 3-digit coded samples, randomized order, balanced design, 30 mL serving at 20 °C, water + unsalted cracker for palate cleansing. 5. **Data Collection:** Use 9-point hedonic, 15-cm unstructured line scale, or QDA intensity scales. 6. **Statistical Analysis:** ANOVA, Tukey's HSD test, Principal Component Analysis (PCA), spider/radar plots. 7. **Reporting:** Mean scores, significance, conclusions → accept / reformulate / reject. **Four Sensory Tests (3 marks):** | Test | Application | |------|-------------| | **Triangle Test** | Detect if a sensory difference exists between two products (e.g., new vs. old packaging, supplier change). | | **Hedonic 9-Point Scale** | Consumer acceptance study; quantifies "liking" from 1 = dislike extremely to 9 = like extremely. | | **Ranking Test** | Compare multiple products; rank by preference or specific attribute (e.g., sweetness). | | **QDA (Quantitative Descriptive Analysis)** | Develop detailed sensory profile of a product using trained panel; output: spider plot of descriptors. | --- **(b) [7 marks] — HACCP Principles & CCPs** **Seven HACCP Principles (3.5 marks):** 1. **Hazard Analysis:** Identify biological (*E. coli*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*), chemical (pesticides, heavy metals, allergens), and physical (glass, metal, stone) hazards at each process step. 2. **Determine CCPs:** Use the Codex CCP decision tree (refer to Section 4.2 diagram). 3. **Establish Critical Limits:** Maximum/minimum values that control the hazard (e.g., T = 88°C, t = 1 min). 4. **Monitor CCPs:** Continuous recording of temperature, pH, flow rate, metal detector signal. 5. **Corrective Actions:** Divert product, re-pasteurize, hold for review, reject. 6. **Verification:** Periodic microbiological testing, calibration, audits. 7. **Record-Keeping:** HACCP plan, monitoring logs, deviation reports, corrective action records. **Three CCPs in Pasteurized Fruit Juice (3.5 marks):** | CCP | Process Step | Hazard | Critical Limit | Monitoring | |-----|--------------|--------|----------------|------------| | **CCP-1** | Sorting / Washing | Pesticide, microbial load | Visual + chlorinated water wash 50–200 ppm free chlorine | Wash water Cl₂ probe | | **CCP-2** | Pasteurization (HTST) | *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, moulds | **T ≥ 88°C for ≥ 1 min** (FSSAI 2017) | Continuous thermocouple data logger | | **CCP-3** | Metal Detection | Metal fragments (ferrous, non-ferrous, SS) | Ferrous: ≤ 1.5 mm; Non-Fe: ≤ 2.0 mm; SS: ≤ 2.5 mm | In-line metal detector with auto-reject | | **CCP-4 (Optional)** | Filling pH | Pathogen survival in low-acid juice | pH < 4.6 (acidified) | In-line pH probe with auto-divert | **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** > A well-implemented HACCP plan, in conjunction with **Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP)** and **ISO 22000**, ensures the juice is **microbiologically safe, chemically compliant, and legally fit for market release** under FSSAI regulations. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield Bulletins for KTU ESE** - **QC vs QA vs TQM:** QC = lab testing; QA = entire process; TQM = management philosophy (PDCA cycle). - **Five Pillars of Beverage QC:** Sensory + Chemical + Microbiological + Instrumental + Statistical (SQC). - **°Brix** is measured by **refractometer at 20°C** (ICUMSA GS4-13). - **BAR (Brix-Acid Ratio)** = °Brix / TA(% citric). **Target = 15–25 for orange juice**. - **pH range for safe juice** = below 4.6 (acidic environment inhibits *Clostridium botulinum*). - **HTST pasteurization standard** for juice = **88°C × 1 min** (FSSAI 2017). - **HACCP = 7 principles**; CCP decision tree is from Codex. - **FSSAI microbial limits:** TPC < 50 CFU/mL; coliforms = absent in 100 mL; *E. coli* and *Salmonella* = absent in 25 mL. - **X-bar chart formulas:** $UCL = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ and $LCL = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$. - **A₂ for n=5 = 0.577**; D₃ = 0 (for n ≤ 6); D₄ for n=5 = 2.114. - **Cp ≥ 1.33** = process capable; **Cp < 1** = incapable (re-engineer). - **Six-Sigma** = 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO); 99.99966% quality. - **Sensory booth standards** = ISO 8589; 20–22°C, red light, isolated booths. - **Triangle test** = difference test; **Hedonic 9-point** = liking test; **QDA** = descriptive profiling. - **Beer CO₂** in volumes calculated from P (bar) and T (°C); 2.5–3.5 vol typical. - **Haze measurement** = nephelometer at 90° scattering, expressed in **NTU**; < 1 NTU for clear beverages. - **Colour measurement** = **CIE L*a*b\*** system; **ΔE > 3** = visually noticeable difference. - **Refractometer** (Brix), **pH meter**, **Conductivity probe**, **NIR spectrometer**, **HPLC**, **GC-MS**, **ICP-OES** — the standard modern QC instrument panel. - **Continuous monitoring** (in-line) > offline testing for high-speed lines (30,000+ bottles/hour). - **Western Electric Rules** (1–4) for control chart interpretation — Rule 1 is the basic 3σ rule. - **Always cite a regulatory standard** (FSSAI/BIS/Codex/ISO) at the end of every answer for bonus marks.

topics.title: Ingredients - Malt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Ingredients - Malt # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Malt** is a **modified, germinated cereal grain** (predominantly *Hordeum vulgare* — two-row or six-row barley) that has been subjected to a controlled, three-stage biochemical transformation — **steeping, germination, and kilning** — under strictly regulated parameters of temperature, humidity, and time. The malting process deliberately activates the grain's native hydrolytic enzyme systems (amylases, proteases, β-glucanases, and cytolytic enzymes) while preserving a starchy endosperm that has been structurally weakened to facilitate subsequent **mashing** in the brewhouse. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Malt is *not* simply "dried grain." It is a **biologically active raw material** whose enzyme potential and starch availability are the direct result of controlled germination, terminated at the optimum point by kilning. **Standardized Metrics for Malt (EBC / MEBAK conventions):** - **Moisture Content:** **3 – 5 %** (target ≈ 4 %) - **Fine Grind Extract (FGE):** ≥ **81 %** dry basis (for well-modified Pilsner malt) - **Diastatic Power (DP):** ≥ **220 °WK** (Windisch-Kolbach units) - **Color:** **1.5 – 2.5 °L (Lovibond)** for Pilsner; up to **600 °L** for Black/Patent malt - **Kolbach Index (KI):** **38 – 45 %** for well-modified malt ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English) Think of a barley kernel as a **tiny sealed seed with a locked pantry inside**. The pantry is full of **starch granules** (long, branching chains of glucose) — perfect food for a baby plant, but useless to a brewer because yeast cannot ferment long starch chains directly. **Malt is the act of "unlocking the pantry":** 1. **Steeping** = Wetting the seed so it thinks it has been planted in soil. The seed wakes up. 2. **Germination** = The seed starts to grow. To do this, it manufactures its own **scissors (enzymes)** to snip the long starch chains into shorter sugar pieces. The brewer is the *thief* who steals the snack before the plant can use it. 3. **Kilning** = The brewer halts the germination by blowing hot air, simultaneously *toasting* the kernel to develop the **flavour, colour, and aroma** that define each malt variety. > [!NOTE] > **Why barley dominates malting:** Among cereals, barley has the **highest enzyme-to-starch ratio**, a **husk that acts as a natural filter bed** in the lauter tun, and **low gelatinisation temperature** (≈ 60 °C), making it the brewer's grain of choice. ## 1.3 The Place of Malt in Beverage Processing Malt is the **single most important ingredient** in the production of: - **Beer** (primary fermentable source — typically 60 – 100 % of grist) - **Whisky** (maizes and other grains must first be malted to provide enzymes for conversion) - **Vinegar, malt-based soft drinks, malt syrups** (confectionery) - **Non-alcoholic malt beverages (NAB)** and breakfast cereals # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three-Stage Malting Process — Stepwise Logic ### Stage 1: Steeping (Immersion) - **Objective:** Raise grain moisture from **12 %** to **42 – 46 %** (wet basis). - **Procedure:** Grain is alternately submerged in water (wet stand) and exposed to air (dry stand) over **30 – 50 hours**. - **Why wet-dry cycles?** Continuous submersion suffocates the embryo; air rests restore oxygen for respiration. - **Critical control:** Temperature held at **12 – 18 °C**; water pH adjusted (8.0 – 8.5) with lime to reduce microbial load. - **End-point indicator:** "Chit" appearance — the root sheath (coleorhiza) breaks through the husk. ### Stage 2: Germination (Modification) - **Objective:** Synthesise enzymes and partially degrade endosperm cell walls. - **Duration:** **4 – 6 days** (commonly 5 days for Pilsner). - **Conditions:** **15 – 20 °C**, **> 95 % relative humidity**, **CO₂ ≤ 1.5 %** to prevent excessive respiration. - **Turning (Couching):** Pneumatic or mechanical turning every 8 – 12 h prevents rootlet matting. - **Modification markers:** - **Acrospire (coleoptile)** length = ¾ to full kernel length - **Friability** of the endosperm - Falling Number (Hagberg) indicator - **Biochemical events:** - **α-amylase** and **β-amylase** synthesised in the scutellum and aleurone layer - **Endosperm cell walls** (β-glucans, pentosans) degraded by β-glucanases - **Storage proteins** (hordeins) partially hydrolysed by endopeptidases ### Stage 3: Kilning (Curing) - **Objective:** Reduce moisture to **3 – 5 %**, halt germination, generate colour and flavour via the **Maillard reaction** and **caramelisation**. - **Procedure:** - **Free drying phase:** 50 – 60 °C, removes bulk water. - **Intermediate phase:** 60 – 75 °C, colour and aroma precursors formed. - **Curing phase:** 80 – 85 °C, drives off bound water. - **Specialty malts:** Higher temperatures (150 – 230 °C) for caramel/roasted types. - **Energy input:** ≈ **35 – 45 MJ / 100 kg malt** (modern single-vessel systems). ## 2.2 Classification of Malts (KTU High-Yield Taxonomy) | Malt Category | Colour (°L) | Kilning Temp (°C) | Function in Beer | |---|---|---|---| | **Pilsner (Pale)** | 1.5 – 2.5 | 80 – 85 | Base malt; light lagers | | **Vienna** | 3 – 5 | 85 – 90 | Amber lagers; body and maltiness | | **Munich** | 6 – 25 | 100 – 110 | Bock, Märzen; malty backbone | | **Caramel / Crystal** | 10 – 150 | 110 – 160 (after stewing) | Body, mouthfeel, red-amber hues | | **Amber / Brown** | 30 – 90 | 130 – 160 | Nutty, biscuit notes | | **Chocolate** | 300 – 500 | 200 – 230 | Porters, stouts; deep roast | | **Black / Patent** | 500 – 600+ | 230 – 250 | Dry stouts; sharp bitterness | | **Roasted Barley** | 400 – 600 | 220 – 240 | Guinness-style stouts (technically not malt) | | **Wheat Malt** | 2 – 4 | 80 | Improves foam stability (Weissbier) | | **Acidulated Malt** | 2 – 4 | 80 | Lowers mash pH (≤ 4.0) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The formulas below are the exact ones KTU examiners test in numerical sub-parts. Memorise units, definitions, and ranges.** | Symbol / Term | Definition | Formula | Standard Range / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | **MC** | Moisture Content (wet basis) | $MC\% = \dfrac{m_{\text{wet}} - m_{\text{dry}}}{m_{\text{wet}}} \times 100$ | 3 – 5 % | | **E** | Extract Yield (dry basis) | $E\%_{\text{db}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{dry extract}}}{m_{\text{grist}}} \times 100$ | 79 – 83 % | | **FCD** | Fine-Coarse Difference (Modification Index) | $FCD = FGE - CGE$ | ≤ 2.0 % (well-mod ≤ 1.2 %) | | **KI** | Kolbach Index (Soluble N Ratio) | $KI\% = \dfrac{N_{\text{soluble}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | 38 – 45 % | | **DP** | Diastatic Power (Windisch-Kolbach) | $DP = f(G_{\text{red,sugar}})$ measured vs. starch | 220 – 380 °WK | | **FAN** | Free Amino Nitrogen | $FAN = [\text{ninhydrin-reactive N}]$ | 120 – 220 mg/L (wort) | | **β** | β-Glucan Content | $C_{\beta} = \dfrac{m_{\beta}}{m_{\text{malt}}}$ | < 200 mg/kg (well-mod) | | **Bü** | Buchner (Final Attenuation) | $B\ddot{u} = \dfrac{EOE - RE}{EOE} \times 100$ | 75 – 85 % | | **SG** | Specific Gravity of Wort | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{wort}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | 1.030 – 1.060 (typical) | | **°P** | Plato (Apparent Extract) | $^\circ P = \dfrac{m_{\text{dry extract}}}{V_{\text{wort}}} \times 100$ | 10 – 14 °P | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering utility:** These parameters are the basis of **Malt Quality Acceptance Protocols** in breweries; spec sheets from suppliers (e.g., Cargill, Malteurop) report all values above. A single deviation triggers a *whole-batch rejection* costing 5 – 8 lakh INR per batch. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Brewhouse yield** is directly proportional to **Fine Grind Extract (FGE)**. A 1 % drop in FGE = ≈ **3 – 4 % reduction in brewhouse yield** (costing millions annually for large plants). - **Diastatic Power (DP)** dictates **adjunct tolerance** — how much unmalted rice, maize, or sorghum can be substituted for malt. - **Kolbach Index (KI)** controls **yeast nutrition (FAN)** and **beer haze stability**. - **β-glucan** control prevents **lauter-tun run-off problems** and **haze in finished beer**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. KTU examiners award 1 – 2 marks for the *transition*; missing transitions cost full marks.** ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Moisture Content from Wet and Dry Masses A KTU standard problem: *"A 250 g sample of malt loses 12.5 g upon oven-drying at 105 °C for 3 hours. Calculate the moisture content on a wet basis."* **Step 1 — Identify given quantities** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{wet}} &= 250 \;\text{g} \\[4pt] m_{\text{loss}} &= 12.5 \;\text{g} \\[4pt] m_{\text{dry}} &= m_{\text{wet}} - m_{\text{loss}} = 250 - 12.5 = 237.5 \;\text{g} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the moisture content definition (wet basis)** $$ \begin{aligned} MC\%_{\text{wb}} &= \frac{m_{\text{wet}} - m_{\text{dry}}}{m_{\text{wet}}} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{12.5}{250} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= 0.05 \times 100 \\[6pt] &= 5.0\;\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation** $$ \text{Result} = 5.0\;\% $$ > **Examiner's note:** This is *acceptable* for storage stability, but the upper limit is reached. A target of **4.0 – 4.5 %** is preferred for extended shelf-life. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Convert Wet-Basis to Dry-Basis Moisture If a supplier reports moisture on a **dry basis**, conversion to wet basis is required for EBC conformity. **Step 1 — Define dry-basis moisture** $$ MC_{\text{db}} = \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{dry}}} \times 100 $$ **Step 2 — Establish algebraic identity** Since $m_{\text{dry}} = m_{\text{wet}} - m_{\text{water}}$, substitute: $$ \begin{aligned} MC_{\text{db}} &= \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{wet}} - m_{\text{water}}} \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $m_{\text{water}}$ in terms of $m_{\text{wet}}$ and $MC_{\text{db}}$** $$ \begin{aligned} MC_{\text{db}} \cdot (m_{\text{wet}} - m_{\text{water}}) &= 100 \cdot m_{\text{water}} \\[4pt] MC_{\text{db}} \cdot m_{\text{wet}} - MC_{\text{db}} \cdot m_{\text{water}} &= 100 \cdot m_{\text{water}} \\[4pt] MC_{\text{db}} \cdot m_{\text{wet}} &= (100 + MC_{\text{db}}) \cdot m_{\text{water}} \\[4pt] m_{\text{water}} &= \frac{MC_{\text{db}} \cdot m_{\text{wet}}}{100 + MC_{\text{db}}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the wet-basis formula** $$ \begin{aligned} MC_{\text{wb}} &= \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{wet}}} \times 100 \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{m_{\text{wet}}} \times \frac{MC_{\text{db}} \cdot m_{\text{wet}}}{100 + MC_{\text{db}}} \times 100 \\[4pt] MC_{\text{wb}}\% &= \frac{MC_{\text{db}}}{100 + MC_{\text{db}}} \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ **Worked example:** $MC_{\text{db}} = 4.17\;\%$ $$ \begin{aligned} MC_{\text{wb}}\% &= \frac{4.17}{100 + 4.17} \times 100 \\[4pt] &= \frac{4.17}{104.17} \times 100 \\[4pt] &= 4.00\;\% \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Extract Yield, Brewhouse Yield, and Economic Impact **Step 1 — Define Fine Grind Extract (FGE) from a laboratory mashing congress** $$ E_{\text{FGE}}\%_{\text{db}} = \frac{m_{\text{dry extract in wort}}}{m_{\text{malt grist}}} \times 100 $$ **Step 2 — Calculate *brewhouse yield* in litres of 12 °P wort per kg of grist** $$ Y = \frac{1000 \cdot m_{\text{malt}} \cdot E_{\text{FGE}}\%_{\text{db}} \cdot (1 - MC/100)}{\rho_{\text{wort}} \cdot 12} $$ where $\rho_{\text{wort}}$ is in g/L at 12 °P (≈ 1004.8 g/L). **Step 3 — Numerical Example** A brewery uses 5000 kg of malt with $FGE = 81\;\%$, $MC = 4.5\;\%$, target wort 12 °P. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Dry extract mass} &= 5000 \times 0.81 \times (1 - 0.045) \\[4pt] &= 5000 \times 0.81 \times 0.955 \\[4pt] &= 3867.75 \;\text{kg extract} \end{aligned} $$ Volume of wort at 12 °P: $$ \begin{aligned} V &= \frac{3867.75}{12 \times 0.9988} \;\; \text{(using 12 g extract / 100 g wort, density correction)} \\[4pt] V &\approx 32{,}266 \;\text{L} \;\;\text{(approx. 32.3 kL of 12 °P wort)} \end{aligned} $$ **Economic implication:** If FGE drops by 1 % (to 80 %): $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta E &= 5000 \times (0.81 - 0.80) \times 0.955 = 47.75 \;\text{kg extract lost} \\[4pt] \text{Revenue loss} &\approx 47.75 \times 1000 \;\text{L/tonne-equivalent} \times \text{₹ 80/L} \\[4pt] &\approx \text{₹ 3.8 lakh per brew batch} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Malt for a Beer Style | Beer Style | Required Malt Body | Target Colour (°L) | Extract Range (%) | DP Needed (°WK) | Special Requirement | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Pilsner (Czech)** | 100 % Pilsner | 2 – 3 | ≥ 81 | ≥ 250 | Plump, low-protein barley | | **Hefeweizen** | 60 % Wheat + 40 % Pilsner | 3 – 5 | ≥ 80 | ≥ 220 | Foam-positive wheat malt | | **India Pale Ale (IPA)** | 90 % Pale Ale + 10 % Crystal | 6 – 12 | ≥ 80 | ≥ 240 | High FAN for hop tolerance | | **Bock** | 100 % Munich | 15 – 25 | ≥ 79 | ≥ 220 | High KI (> 42 %) for body | | **Imperial Stout** | 70 % Pale + 20 % Chocolate + 10 % Black | 70 – 120 | ≥ 76 | ≥ 200 | Accept low DP (no adjuncts) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Malting Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Barley Intake
QC Check 12 percent MC] --> B[Cleaning and Grading
Size Uniformity 2.5 mm plus] B --> C[Steeping Vessel
12 to 18 C, 30 to 50 h] C --> D[Germination Floor
15 to 20 C, 4 to 6 days] D --> E[Kilning Oven
50 to 85 C, 24 to 48 h] E --> F[Deculming and Polishing
Rootlet Removal 4 percent] F --> G[Storage Silo
3 to 5 percent MC, CO2 Blanketing] G --> H[Malt to Brewhouse
or Specialty Mill] C -. Chitting.-> I[Quality Check 1
Moisture 42 to 46 percent] D -. Acrospire 3/4.-> J[Quality Check 2
Modification Index] E -. Color and Moisture.-> K[Quality Check 3
EBC Analysis] K -->|Pass| L[Approved Malt] K -->|Fail| M[Rework or Reject] ``` ## 4.2 Malt Classification — Hierarchical Block Map ```mermaid flowchart LR MALT[Malt Universe] --> A[Base Malts] MALT --> B[Specialty Malts] A --> A1[Pilsner] A --> A2[Maris Otter] A --> A3[Premium Pale] A --> A4[Wheat Malt] B --> C[Kilned Specialty] B --> D[Roasted Specialty] B --> E[Stewed Specialty] C --> C1[Munich] C --> C2[Vienna] C --> C3[Amber] D --> D1[Chocolate] D --> D2[Black Patent] D --> D3[Roasted Barley] E --> E1[Crystal 60L] E --> E2[Crystal 120L] E --> E3[Caramunich] ``` ## 4.3 Mash-Tun Equivalent — Malt-Enzyme Substrate Map ```mermaid flowchart TD MALT[Malt Kernel] --> HUSK[Husk - Luter Filter] MALT --> PERI[Pericarp - Protection] MALT --> ALEU[Aleurone Layer - Enzyme Synthesis] MALT --> ENDO[Starchy Endosperm - Substrate] ALEU --> EA[Alpha Amylase] ALEU --> EB[Beta Amylase] ALEU --> ED[Debranching Enzyme] ALEU --> EG[Beta Glucanase] ALEU --> EP[Protease] ENDO --> STARCH[Starch Granules] STARCH --> MALTOSE[Maltose - Fermentable] STARCH --> DEXTRIN[Dextrin - Unfermentable] EA --> STARCH EB --> STARCH ED --> STARCH EG --> ENDO EP --> AA[Free Amino Nitrogen FAN] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** In the mash tun, the **enzyme zone** (aleurone) attacks the **substrate zone** (endosperm). The husk (left) becomes a natural filter bed in the lauter tun — one of the key reasons barley is preferred over rice or sorghum as a base grain. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > Define *malt*. List any three types of malt with their characteristic colour ranges (in °Lovibond). **Model Answer:** Malt is a **modified germinated cereal grain** (usually barley) that has been steeped, germinated, and kilned under controlled conditions to develop hydrolytic enzymes and partial starch degradation, preparing it for mashing. Three types of malt and their colour: | Type | Colour (°L) | |---|---| | Pilsner malt | 1.5 – 2.5 | | Munich malt | 6 – 25 | | Chocolate malt | 300 – 500 | (3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for listing + 1 for correct colour ranges) --- > **Question 2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** > What is *Diastatic Power* (DP)? State the minimum acceptable DP for a Pilsner malt in °WK. **Model Answer:** Diastatic Power (DP) is a measure of the **combined starch-hydrolysing enzyme activity** (primarily α-amylase and β-amylase) in malt, expressed in **Windisch-Kolbach (°WK) units**, as determined by the official EBC method using a standardised starch substrate. Minimum acceptable DP for Pilsner malt: **≥ 220 °WK**. (3 marks: 1.5 marks for definition with units, 1.5 marks for the correct threshold value) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **Question A (14 Marks).** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]** > > **(a)** Describe the **three stages of malting** with the operating conditions (temperature, time, moisture) and the biochemical purpose of each stage. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A malt sample of 200 g loses 9.0 g upon oven drying at 105 °C. Calculate the **moisture content** and the **mass of dry malt** obtained. If the same malt has $FGE = 80.5\;\%_{\text{db}}$ and $CGE = 79.0\;\%_{\text{db}}$, calculate the **Fine-Coarse Difference (FCD)** and comment on the **degree of modification**. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — (a) Three Stages of Malting | Stage | Conditions | Biochemical Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Steeping** | 12 – 18 °C; 30 – 50 h; moisture raised from 12 % → 42 – 46 % | Activates embryo; initiates gibberellin release; "chits" appear | | **Germination** | 15 – 20 °C; 4 – 6 days; RH > 95 %; CO₂ ≤ 1.5 % | Synthesis of α-amylase, β-amylase, proteases, β-glucanases; partial endosperm hydrolysis | | **Kilning** | 50 – 60 °C → 80 – 85 °C; 24 – 48 h; final MC 3 – 5 % | Halts germination; develops colour and flavour via Maillard and caramelisation; preserves enzymes | (7 marks: 2 marks per stage for conditions + 1 mark for biochemical role summary) ### Model Solution — (b) Numerical **Step 1 — Moisture content** $$ m_{\text{dry}} = 200 - 9.0 = 191.0 \;\text{g} $$ $$ MC\%_{\text{wb}} = \frac{9.0}{200} \times 100 = 4.5\;\% $$ **Step 2 — Fine-Coarse Difference** $$ FCD = FGE - CGE = 80.5 - 79.0 = 1.5\;\% $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation** $$ \text{FCD} = 1.5\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Well-modified malt} \;\;(\text{ideal} \leq 1.2 - 1.5\%) $$ The malt is **adequately modified**; cellular breakdown is sufficiently complete, ensuring good extract yield and rapid lautering. (7 marks: 1 MC formula + 1 numerical + 1 dry mass + 1 FCD formula + 1 numerical + 1 interpretation + 1 supporting comment) --- > **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]** > > **(a)** Explain the **classification of malts** with a comparative table covering **base malts, kilned specialty malts, and roasted specialty malts**. State the role of each in beer. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Define **Kolbach Index (KI)**. A malt has total nitrogen of **1.55 %** and soluble nitrogen of **0.62 %**. Calculate the KI and state whether the malt is well-modified. A brewery uses 8000 kg of this malt at MC 4.0 % and FGE 80.2 % (db) to brew 13 °P wort. Calculate the **volume of wort produced** (assume wort density at 13 °P ≈ 1.0052 g/mL). **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — (a) Classification of Malts | Category | Examples | Colour (°L) | Role in Beer | |---|---|---|---| | **Base Malts** | Pilsner, Maris Otter, Vienna, Munich, Wheat | 1.5 – 25 | Provide bulk extract (sugars) and enzymes | | **Kilned Specialty** | Crystal/Caramel, Caramunich, Biscuit, Victory | 10 – 150 | Body, colour, sweet/caramel notes, foam | | **Roasted Specialty** | Chocolate, Black/Patent, Roasted Barley | 300 – 600+ | Dry-roast, espresso bitterness, opaque stouts | (7 marks: 2 for structure of table + 3 for correct examples + 1 each for roles) ### Model Solution — (b) Kolbach Index and Wort Volume **Step 1 — Definition** Kolbach Index (KI), also called the *Soluble Nitrogen Ratio*, is the ratio of soluble to total malt nitrogen, expressed as a percentage. It is an indicator of **proteolytic modification** during germination. $$ KI\% = \frac{N_{\text{soluble}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100 $$ **Step 2 — Calculate KI** $$ KI\% = \frac{0.62}{1.55} \times 100 = 40.0\;\% $$ **Step 3 — Modification assessment** $$ KI = 40.0\% \;\;\in\;\; [38, 45]\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Well-modified malt} $$ **Step 4 — Wort volume at 13 °P** Dry malt mass: $$ m_{\text{dry malt}} = 8000 \times (1 - 0.04) = 8000 \times 0.96 = 7680 \;\text{kg} $$ Extract mass (db): $$ m_{\text{extract}} = 7680 \times 0.802 = 6160.4 \;\text{kg} $$ Wort mass at 13 °P (1 °P = 1 g extract / 100 g wort = 10 g/L): $$ m_{\text{wort}} = \frac{6160.4 \;\text{kg extract}}{0.13} = 47{,}387.7 \;\text{kg wort} $$ Volume: $$ V = \frac{m_{\text{wort}}}{\rho} = \frac{47{,}387{,}700 \;\text{g}}{1.0052 \;\text{g/mL}} \approx 47{,}141 \;\text{L} \approx 47.1 \;\text{kL} $$ (7 marks: 1.5 KI definition + 1.5 KI numerical + 1 well-modified comment + 0.5 dry mass + 1 extract mass + 1.5 volume) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Zones (KTU 2024 Valuation Patterns):** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert MC to wet basis** when the problem implicitly assumes dry basis, or vice versa → **−1 mark**. > 2. **Writing °L (Lovibond) instead of EBC** (or mixing the two) → EBC ≈ °L × 1.97. Examiners deduct **½ mark** per occurrence. > 3. **Failing to comment on modification** in FCD / KI questions. The numerical answer is worth 4 marks; the **interpretation carries 2 – 3 marks** and is the most-skipped step. > 4. **Unit omission on °WK, °L, °P** → strict **½ mark deduction** per KTU 2024 marking scheme. > 5. **Confusing "malt" with "roasted barley"** — the latter is *unmalted* barley, roasted to 230 °C, used in stouts. Marking check often includes this distractor. > 6. **Skipping acrospire length (¾ grain) as a modification indicator** — frequently tested as a 1-mark short note. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this):** > > - **Malt definition** → *germinated, kilned cereal* (NOT just dried grain). > - **Primary grain** → Two-row barley (*Hordeum vulgare*); reasons: husk, enzymes, low gelatinisation temp. > - **Three stages:** **Steep → Germinate → Kiln.** Memorise temperatures and durations. > - **Moisture:** Start **12 %** → End of steep **42 – 46 %** → Final malt **3 – 5 %**. > - **Base malt** = bulk extract provider (Pilsner, Pale, Munich). > - **Specialty malt** = colour and flavour (Crystal, Chocolate, Black). > - **Roasted barley** = *not malt*; unmalted; used in dry stouts. > - **FGE ≥ 81 %** (db) for well-modified Pilsner; **FCD ≤ 2 %** indicates good modification. > - **DP ≥ 220 °WK** is the minimum enzyme potential; controls adjunct substitution. > - **KI 38 – 45 %** → well-modified; affects FAN and beer body. > - **β-glucan < 200 mg/kg** to avoid lautering and haze problems. > - **Colour units:** **°Lovibond (°L)** for malt; **EBC** for beer/wort (EBC ≈ 1.97 × °L). > - **Economic impact:** A 1 % drop in FGE ≈ **3 – 4 % drop in brewhouse yield**; ≈ **₹ 3 – 4 lakh per batch** in mid-size plants. > - **Glucose yield hierarchy** from amylases: **Dextrin → Maltotriose → Maltose → Glucose** (β-amylase yields maltose; α-amylase is an endo-glucosidase). > - **Maillard reaction** at **> 100 °C** kilning creates melanoidins → colour and flavour. > - **Wort gravity** is measured in **°Plato (°P)** or **Specific Gravity (SG)**. 12 °P ≈ SG 1.048. > - **Caramel/Crystal malts** are *stewed* (held at 65 – 75 °C in syrup form) *before* kilning, hence high sugar content. > **End of Module 2 — Ingredients: Malt Notes**

topics.title: Hops - Adjuncts - Water
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hops - Adjuncts - Water

# 🍺 HOPS – ADJUNCTS – WATER | Module 2 Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hops (Humulus lupulus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Hops are the dried female inflorescences (strobiles/cones) of the perennial climbing vine **Humulus lupulus**, belonging to the family Cannabaceae. They are the principal bittering, flavoring, and aroma-imparting ingredient in beer brewing, contributing α-acids (humulones), β-acids (lupulones), essential oils (myrcene, humulene, caryophyllene, farnesene), and polyphenolic tannins.*

**Conceptual Analogy 🍃**
Think of hops as the *spice rack* of a brewer. Just like how cumin, coriander, and black pepper transform a plain lentil soup into a culinary experience, hops transform a bland sugary liquid (wort) into the balanced, bitter, aromatic drink we call beer. Without hops, your wort would taste like sweet, flat bread-water.

**Key Physical Metrics:**
- **α-acid content:** 3 – 20 % w/w (depending on cultivar)
- **β-acid content:** 3 – 12 % w/w
- **Storage Index (HSI):** Standard measure of hop aging; fresh hops have **HSI ≤ 0.30**
- **Hop Utilization (U):** Typically 25 – 35 % of added α-acids actually isomerize into iso-α-acids during boiling

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **bittering hops** (high α-acid, added early in boil), **flavor hops** (mid-boil), and **aroma hops** (late-hop or whirlpool/dry-hop).

---

### 1.2 Adjuncts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Adjuncts are any unmalted carbohydrate sources — other than barley malt — that are used by the brewer to supplement the fermentable extract of the wort, reduce production cost, lighten body/color, modify flavor, or tailor the nutritional profile of the final beer.*

**Conceptual Analogy 🌾**
Imagine making tea. Pure Assam tea gives a strong, malty, dark cup. But if you mix it with lighter teas (Darjeeling, green tea) or add herbs and spices, you create new blends. Adjuncts serve the same role in brewing — they "blend" with malt to create lighter, crisper, or uniquely flavored beers (e.g., Budweiser uses rice adjunct; many Belgian witbiers use wheat and oats).

**Major Categories of Adjuncts:**

| Category | Examples | Function |
|----------|----------|----------|
| **Cereal Grains** | Corn (maize), rice, sorghum, wheat, oats, rye | Increase fermentable extract, lighten flavor |
| **Sugars** | Sucrose, invert sugar, glucose, fructose | Boost alcohol, lighten body |
| **Syrups** | High-maltose corn syrup, malt extract | Increase fermentables efficiently |
| **Unmalted Barley** | Raw barley | Foam stability, head retention |

---

### 1.3 Water

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Water constitutes 90 – 95 % of finished beer by mass, making it the single largest ingredient by volume. Brewing water is characterized by its ionic profile (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, HCO₃⁻/CO₃²⁻), pH, total hardness, alkalinity, and residual alkalinity (RA), all of which profoundly influence mash enzyme activity, wort pH, hop utilization, yeast metabolism, and final beer flavor.*

**Conceptual Analogy 💧**
If hops are the spice and malt is the dough, **water is the canvas**. The same recipe brewed with water from Pilsen (very soft) versus Burton-on-Trent (very hard, gypsum-rich) produces dramatically different beers — a Pilsner vs. a strong Bitter. Water is the silent architect of beer character.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Fact (KTU Favourite Question):** The famous brewing cities owe their beer styles entirely to their local water profiles:
> - **Pilsen (Czech Republic)** → Soft water → Pilsner
> - **Munich (Germany)** → Carbonate-rich → Dark lagers/Märzen
> - **Dublin (Ireland)** → High bicarbonate → Dry Irish Stout
> - **London (England)** → High carbonate, high chloride → Pale Ales
> - **Burton-on-Trent (England)** → High gypsum (CaSO₄) → India Pale Ale (IPA)

**Key Standard Metrics (Bold):**
- **Total Hardness:** **100 – 300 mg/L CaCO₃** (typical brewing range)
- **Target Mash pH:** **5.2 – 5.6**
- **Residual Alkalinity (RA):** Calculated in **mg/L as CaCO₃**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hops — Chemistry, Function, and Boil Kinetics

#### 2.1.1 Resin Chemistry
Hops contain two major soft resin fractions:

1. **α-acids (Humulones):** n-Humulone, Ad-Humulone, Co-Humulone
   - On boiling (≥ 90 °C, pH > 5), they thermally **isomerize** into **iso-α-acids** — the actual bittering compounds.
   - **Co-Humulone** content dictates harshness; high Co = coarse bitterness.

2. **β-acids (Lupulones):** Oxidize slowly, contribute minimal bitterness but add rustic/aged flavors.

#### 2.1.2 Essential Oils (Hop Aroma)
| Compound | Aroma Character | Boil Behavior |
|----------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Myrcene** | Resinous, herbal, piney | Highly volatile — lost in first 10 min of boil |
| **Humulene** | Woody, noble hop | Partially retained (BP ~123 °C) |
| **Caryophyllene** | Spicy, earthy | Partially retained |
| **Farnesene** | Floral, citrus (Saaz-type) | Very volatile |
| **Linalool** | Floral, citrus | More water-soluble; survives longer |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pearl:** Late-hop additions, whirlpool stands, and dry-hopping are used because essential oils are heat-labile. Boil > 30 min destroys delicate aroma oils.

#### 2.1.3 Hop Addition Phases
| Stage | Time Added | Purpose | Examples of Cultivars |
|-------|-----------|---------|------------------------|
| Bittering | 60 min into boil | Maximum iso-α-acid yield | Magnum, Warrior, Apollo |
| Flavor | 15 – 30 min | Balanced bitterness + flavor | Cluster, Eroica |
| Aroma (Late) | 5 – 15 min | Preserves volatile oils | Saaz, Hallertau, Tettnanger |
| Whirlpool | After flame-off | Hot-side aroma | Citra, Mosaic |
| Dry-hop | Fermentation/maturation | True aroma (no bitterness) | Galaxy, Nelson Sauvin |

#### 2.1.4 Iso-α-acid Utilization Equation
The amount of α-acid actually contributing bitterness is given by:

$$\text{IBU} = \frac{W_{\text{hops}} \times \%\alpha_{\text{acid}} \times U \times 1000}{V_{\text{beer}}}$$

Where:
- $W_{\text{hops}}$ = weight of hops (oz or g)
- $\%\alpha_{\text{acid}}$ = alpha-acid fraction (decimal, e.g., 0.12)
- $U$ = utilization factor (0.10 – 0.35 depending on boil time & gravity)
- $V_{\text{beer}}$ = final beer volume (L)

---

### 2.2 Adjuncts — Processing and Brewing Logic

#### 2.2.1 Why Use Adjuncts?
1. **Economic:** Malt is expensive; adjuncts are cheaper fermentables.
2. **Sensory:** Produces lighter-bodied, crisper, more refreshing beers.
3. **Nutritional:** Adds vitamins, minerals, fiber (oats, wheat).
4. **Regional Tradition:** Rice lagers (Japan, USA), sorghum beers (Africa), corn lagers (Mexico).

#### 2.2.2 Mashing Considerations
Adjuncts must be **gelatinized** before enzymatic breakdown because they lack the amylases that malt provides. Two methods:

- **Cereal Cooker Method:** Adjunct boiled separately at 100 °C with adjunct amylase or malt, then blended with main mash.
- **Infusion Mash with Pre-gelatinized Flakes:** Most convenient; flakes are pre-gelled by manufacturer.

---

### 2.3 Water Chemistry — The Most Important "Ingredient"

#### 2.3.1 Major Ions and Their Brewing Roles

| Ion | Symbol | Brewing Effect | Typical Range (mg/L) |
|-----|--------|----------------|------------------------|
| Calcium | $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ | Lowers mash pH; aids enzyme activity; yeast flocculation | 50 – 150 |
| Magnesium | $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ | Slight pH drop; yeast nutrient (>50 = bitter) | 0 – 30 |
| Sodium | $\text{Na}^{+}$ | Enhances malt sweetness/ fullness at low levels | 0 – 150 |
| Potassium | $\text{K}^{+}$ | pH buffering; yeast nutrition | 0 – 10 |
| Chloride | $\text{Cl}^{-}$ | Enhances maltiness, fullness, mouthfeel | 0 – 250 |
| Sulfate | $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ | Accentuates hop bitterness, dryness | 0 – 350 |
| Bicarbonate | $\text{HCO}_3^{-}$ | Raises mash pH (alkaline) | 0 – 300 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Brewing Water:** The **Cl⁻ : SO₄²⁻ ratio** dictates whether hops or malt dominates the palate.
> - **Cl⁻ > SO₄²⁻** → Malt-forward, fuller, softer beer (Münchner, Märzen)
> - **SO₄²⁻ > Cl⁻** → Hop-forward, drier, sharper beer (Burton IPA, Pilsner)

#### 2.3.2 Hardness vs. Alkalinity

- **Total Hardness (TH)** = sum of Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ ions (expressed as mg/L CaCO₃)
- **Temporary Hardness (TH-temp)** = Ca(HCO₃)₂ + Mg(HCO₃)₂ — removable by boiling
- **Permanent Hardness (TH-perm)** = CaSO₄ + MgSO₄ + CaCl₂ + MgCl₂ — not removable by boiling
- **Alkalinity** = concentration of $\text{HCO}_3^{-}$ + $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ (expressed as mg/L CaCO₃)

$$\text{TH} = \text{TH}_{\text{temp}} + \text{TH}_{\text{perm}}$$

#### 2.3.3 Residual Alkalinity (RA) — Kolbach Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most important water calculation a brewer makes.** RA predicts how much the brewing water will raise mash pH, allowing the brewer to acidify appropriately.

$$\boxed{\text{RA} = \text{Alkalinity} - \left(\frac{\text{Ca}^{2+}}{3.5} + \frac{\text{Mg}^{2+}}{7}\right)}$$

All values in **mg/L as CaCO₃** equivalents. The constants **3.5** and **7** are the equivalent weights of Ca and Mg salts (specifically CaCO₃ ≈ 50 / Ca²⁺ atomic mass × 100; the empirical Kolbach values used industry-wide).

**Interpretation Guide:**
- **RA < 0** → Water acidifies mash → Pilsner profile (use acidulated malt)
- **RA ≈ 0** → Balanced (Pale Ale)
- **RA > 0** → Water alkalizes mash → Dark lager profile (Munich, Dublin)

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $\text{IBU} = \dfrac{W_h \times \alpha\% \times U \times 1000}{V_b}$ | $W_h$ hops, $\alpha\%$ alpha fraction, $U$ utilization, $V_b$ beer volume | mg/L | Bittering strength |
| 2 | $\text{BU:GU} = \dfrac{\text{IBU}}{\text{OG} - 1 \times 1000}$ | OG = original gravity | ratio | Bitterness balance |
| 3 | $\text{RA} = \text{Alk} - \left(\dfrac{\text{Ca}}{3.5} + \dfrac{\text{Mg}}{7}\right)$ | Alk = alkalinity, Ca/Mg = calcium/magnesium hardness | mg/L as CaCO₃ | Mash pH prediction |
| 4 | $\text{TH} = 2.5 \times [\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.12 \times [\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ | Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ concentrations | mg/L CaCO₃ | Total hardness calc |
| 5 | $\text{HSI} = \dfrac{-S_{\text{HS}} + S_{\text{HCV}} + 0.1 \times \text{HS}}{S_{\text{HCV}}}$ | Conductivity values of hop samples | dimensionless | Hop freshness |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{adj}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{adj}}}{E_{\text{total wort}}} \times 100\%$ | $E$ = extract (°P) | % | Adjunct contribution |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol in Tables:** Note that all mathematical "divided by" or absolute value bars use $\div$ or `vert` representation to keep the markdown table parser stable.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Brewing Industry:** Every commercial brewery uses RA calculations to design brewing liquor before mashing.
- **Food Processing:** Same ion-balance principles apply to mineral water production, soft drink formulation, and dairy processing.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Hardness and alkalinity measurements identical to wastewater treatment protocols.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Residual Alkalinity (RA) Equation

**Starting Concept:** When a brewer adds calcium and magnesium salts to mash, these divalent cations **precipitate phosphate ions** present in the malt, releasing $\text{H}^+$ ions that lower mash pH. Conversely, **bicarbonate** neutralizes these $\text{H}^+$ ions, raising pH. The net effect is the Residual Alkalinity.

**Step 1 — Equivalence Expression:**
The acid-neutralizing capacity of bicarbonate equals its concentration expressed as CaCO₃ equivalence.

$$\text{Alkalinity (as CaCO}_3\text{)} = \text{Alk}$$

**Step 2 — Acidifying Power of Calcium:**
Each mole of Ca²⁺ (40 g) precipitates phosphate, releasing a stoichiometric amount of $\text{H}^+$ equivalent to neutralizing 50 g of CaCO₃ alkalinity. Therefore, the equivalent-weight factor for calcium is:

$$f_{\text{Ca}} = \frac{50}{40 \div 2} = 3.5$$

Explanation: 1 ppm Ca²⁺ has the acidifying power equivalent to 3.5 ppm CaCO₃.

**Step 3 — Acidifying Power of Magnesium:**
For magnesium (atomic mass 24.3), the equivalent factor is:

$$f_{\text{Mg}} = \frac{50}{24.3 \div 2} = 7.0$$

Therefore, 1 ppm Mg²⁺ equals the acidifying power of 7.0 ppm CaCO₃.

**Step 4 — Combining Effects:**
The net residual alkalinity is the difference between alkaline input and acidifying input.

$$\text{RA} = \text{Alk}_{\text{CaCO}_3} - \left(\frac{\text{Ca}^{2+}}{f_{\text{Ca}}} + \frac{\text{Mg}^{2+}}{f_{\text{Mg}}}\right)$$

Substituting the constants:

$$\boxed{\text{RA} = \text{Alk} - \left(\frac{\text{Ca}^{2+}}{3.5} + \frac{\text{Mg}^{2+}}{7}\right)}$$

**Final Note on Units:** All values must be in **mg/L as CaCO₃** for consistent arithmetic.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Total Hardness from Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺

**Step 1 — Equivalent weight of CaCO₃:**
$$E_{\text{CaCO}_3} = 50 \text{ g/eq}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Ca²⁺ mass to CaCO₃ equivalent:**

$$\text{Ca hardness (as CaCO}_3\text{)} = [\text{Ca}^{2+}]_{\text{mg/L}} \times \frac{50}{20} = 2.5 \times [\text{Ca}^{2+}]$$

**Step 3 — Convert Mg²⁺ mass to CaCO₃ equivalent:**

$$\text{Mg hardness (as CaCO}_3\text{)} = [\text{Mg}^{2+}]_{\text{mg/L}} \times \frac{50}{12.15} = 4.12 \times [\text{Mg}^{2+}]$$

**Step 4 — Sum:**

$$\text{TH} = \text{Ca hardness} + \text{Mg hardness} = 2.5[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.12[\text{Mg}^{2+}]$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — IBU Calculation

**Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]:**
A brewer adds **1.0 oz** of a hop variety with **12 % α-acids** to a boil for **60 minutes**. The expected utilization is **30 %**. The finished beer volume is **5 US gallons**.
Calculate the **IBU** in mg/L.

**Step 1 — Convert weight to grams:**
$$1.0 \text{ oz} = 28.35 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Convert volume to liters:**
$$5 \text{ US gal} = 5 \times 3.785 = 18.93 \text{ L}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the IBU formula:**

$$\text{IBU} = \frac{W_h \times \alpha\% \times U \times 1000}{V_b}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{IBU} = \frac{28.35 \times 0.12 \times 0.30 \times 1000}{18.93}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$28.35 \times 0.12 = 3.402$$

$$3.402 \times 0.30 = 1.0206$$

$$1.0206 \times 1000 = 1020.6$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$\text{IBU} = \frac{1020.6}{18.93} = 53.91 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 6 — Rounding:**
$$\text{IBU} \approx 54 \text{ mg/L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** This beer has 54 IBU — a classic Pale Ale / India Pale Ale bitterness range (40 – 70 IBU).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Residual Alkalinity

**Problem:** A brewing water profile has:
- $\text{Ca}^{2+} = 200$ mg/L as CaCO₃
- $\text{Mg}^{2+} = 40$ mg/L as CaCO₃
- Alkalinity $= 250$ mg/L as CaCO₃

**Compute RA.**

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$\text{RA} = 250 - \left(\frac{200}{3.5} + \frac{40}{7}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate parentheses:**

$$\frac{200}{3.5} = 57.14$$

$$\frac{40}{7} = 5.71$$

$$57.14 + 5.71 = 62.85$$

**Step 3 — Subtract:**

$$\text{RA} = 250 - 62.85 = 187.15 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
RA > 0 → This water is *alkaline* and will **raise mash pH**, suitable for **dark lagers, dunkels, or Märzen** (Munich/Dublin-type profile). The brewer should either acidify with food-grade acid or blend with RO water.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — BU:GU Ratio

**Problem:** A beer has OG = 1.052 and IBU = 35.
**Calculate BU:GU ratio.**

**Step 1 — Compute gravity units:**
$$\text{GU} = (1.052 - 1) \times 1000 = 52$$

**Step 2 — Apply formula:**

$$\text{BU:GU} = \frac{\text{IBU}}{\text{GU}} = \frac{35}{52} = 0.67$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
- 0.5 – 0.8 = balanced Pale Ale
- > 1.0 = hop-forward (IPA)

This beer is a **balanced Pale Ale**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Hop Boil Kinetics & Utilization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Hop Cone Added to Wort] --> B[Alpha-Acids Dissolve into Wort]
    B --> C{Boil Time & Temperature}
    C -->|60 min at 100 deg C| D[Thermal Isomerization n-Humulone to iso-Humulone]
    C -->|15 min| E[Partial Isomerization Plus Flavor Oils Retained]
    C -->|5 min or Whirlpool| F[Aroma Oils Largely Preserved]

    E --> H[Balanced Bitterness plus Flavor]
    F --> I[Aroma Only Minimum Bitterness]
    G --> J[Cooling and Yeast Pitching]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Fermentation and Conditioning]
    K --> L[Final Beer with Target IBU BU GU]
```
