<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the major chemical constituents of hops. Explain the role of α-acids in beer brewing.*

**Model Answer:**

The major chemical constituents of hops are:
1. **α-acids (humulones):** n-Humulone, Ad-Humulone, Co-Humulone (3 – 20 %)
2. **β-acids (lupulones):** 3 – 12 %
3. **Essential oils:** Myrcene, humulene, caryophyllene, farnesene, linalool
4. **Polyphenols/tannins:** Antioxidants contributing astringency and colloidal stability
5. **Waxes, lipids, cellulose:** Structural matrix

**Role of α-acids:** During boiling, α-acids undergo **thermal isomerization** (a stereo-chemical rearrangement) to form **iso-α-acids**, which are the primary bittering compounds in beer. They have a low bitterness threshold (~5 mg/L), provide antimicrobial activity (especially against Gram-positive bacteria), and contribute to foam stability. The degree of utilization (typically 25 – 35 %) depends on boil time, wort gravity, pH, and hop form (pellets > whole cones). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "Residual Alkalinity" of brewing water. State its significance in mash pH control.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Residual Alkalinity (RA) is the net alkalinity of brewing water after accounting for the acidifying power of calcium and magnesium ions. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{RA} = \text{Alkalinity} - \left(\frac{\text{Ca}^{2+}}{3.5} + \frac{\text{Mg}^{2+}}{7}\right) \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Significance:**
1. RA predicts the **mash pH shift** caused by brewing water.
2. A **positive RA** raises mash pH (suitable for dark beers); a **negative RA** lowers it (suitable for pale lagers).
3. It helps brewers decide whether to **add acidulated malt, food-grade acids, or blend with RO water** to hit the target mash pH (5.2 – 5.6).
4. RA controls **enzyme activity, hop utilization, yeast health, and final beer flavor**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

---

### 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **Hops + Adjuncts Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the chemistry of hop resins. With a neat sketch/flowchart, explain the **thermal isomerization of α-acids** and its dependence on boil time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify adjuncts used in brewing. Discuss the **cereal cooker method** of adjunct preparation with its process flow and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Hop Resin Chemistry & Isomerization (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Classification of Hop Resins (2 marks):**
Hop resins are divided into:
- **Hard resins** (CHCl₃ insoluble) — polymerized/oxidized fractions
- **Soft resins** (CHCl₃ soluble) — biologically active fractions, comprising:
  - α-acids (humulones): humulone, cohumulone, adhumulone
  - β-acids (lupulones): lupulone, colupulone, adlupulone

**Step 2 — α-acid to Iso-α-acid Isomerization (3 marks):**
On boiling, humulone undergoes a stereo-specific rearrangement (cis → trans) of the side chain around the C6 carbon. Reaction requires:
- Temperature > 85 °C (typically 100 °C)
- Time: 45 – 90 min
- pH > 5 (mash pH ~5.4 satisfies this)

The reaction yields **iso-humulone**, the principal bittering molecule. Each iso-α-acid is roughly **9 times more bitter** than its parent α-acid.

**Step 3 — Boil Time vs. Utilization (2 marks):**
Utilization follows a curve:
- 60 min: ~30 % (max bitterness, lowest aroma)
- 15 min: ~10 % (balanced)
- 5 min or whirlpool: ~5 % (aroma-driven)
- Dry-hop: 0 % (only aroma, no bitterness)

Reference: *See Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.*

---

**(b) Classification of Adjuncts & Cereal Cooker Method (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Classification (3 marks):**

| Type | Examples | Extract Yield |
|------|----------|---------------|
| Cereals | Corn, rice, sorghum, wheat | 78 – 82 % |
| Sugars | Sucrose, invert syrup | ~100 % |
| Syrups | High-maltose corn syrup | ~80 % |
| Unmalted barley | Raw barley | ~75 % |

**Step 2 — Why Adjuncts Need Special Cooking (2 marks):**
Adjunct starch granules (especially corn, rice) have a high gelatinization temperature (65 – 80 °C) higher than malt amylase optimum (~65 °C). Without pre-gelatinization, raw starch granules would remain insoluble and un-fermentable, lowering extract yield.

**Step 3 — Cereal Cooker Process Flow (2 marks):**
1. Mill adjunct to grist (similar to malt).
2. Mix with water at 35 – 45 °C in cereal cooker (~10 % of total mash volume).
3. Add adjunct amylase or a fraction of malt (5 – 10 %).
4. Heat to **100 °C** (boiling) for 15 – 30 min → starch gelatinizes and liquefies.
5. Transfer cooked adjunct to **main mash** at 65 – 67 °C.
6. Saccharification by malt enzymes → fermentable wort.

**Limitations:** Extra energy, equipment cost, longer brew time, dilution of malt flavor, and risk of Maillard-driven dark color if overheated.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **Water Chemistry Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Residual Alkalinity (RA)** with the Kolbach formula. A brewing water contains: $\text{Ca}^{2+} = 140$ mg/L as CaCO₃, $\text{Mg}^{2+} = 28$ mg/L as CaCO₃, Alkalinity = 180 mg/L as CaCO₃. **Calculate the RA and recommend the beer style** suitable for this water. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the water profiles of **Pilsen, Munich, Dublin, and Burton-on-Trent**. How does each profile influence the resulting beer style? Use a comparative table. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) RA Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — State the formula (2 marks):**

$$\text{RA} = \text{Alkalinity} - \left(\frac{\text{Ca}^{2+}}{3.5} + \frac{\text{Mg}^{2+}}{7}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$\text{RA} = 180 - \left(\frac{140}{3.5} + \frac{28}{7}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute (2 marks):**

$$\frac{140}{3.5} = 40.00$$

$$\frac{28}{7} = 4.00$$

$$40.00 + 4.00 = 44.00$$

$$\text{RA} = 180 - 44 = \mathbf{136 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
RA = +136 mg/L → strongly alkaline water → will raise mash pH above optimum (5.4). Suitable for **dark lagers (Märzen, Bock, Schwarzbier) and stouts**. Not suitable for Pilsners (which need soft, low-RA water). The brewer should blend with RO water or add lactic acid to lower RA before mashing pale beers.

---

**(b) Comparative Water Profiles (7 marks)**

| City | Ca²⁺ (mg/L) | Mg²⁺ (mg/L) | Alkalinity | SO₄²⁻ | Cl⁻ | RA | Beer Style |
|------|--------------|--------------|------------|---------|------|-----|-------------|
| **Pilsen** | 7 | 2 | 15 | 5 | 5 | < 0 | Pilsner Urquell, soft lagers |
| **Munich** | 75 | 18 | 150 | 10 | 2 | +100 | Dunkel, Märzen, Bock |
| **Dublin** | 117 | 4 | 320 | 55 | 19 | +280 | Dry Irish Stout |
| **Burton** | 270 | 24 | 270 | 638 | 36 | +50 | India Pale Ale (IPA) |

**Key Insights (2 marks):**
- **Pilsen** has very low mineral load → crisp, clean, delicate Pilsner.
- **Munich** has carbonate-dominant profile → supports dark malt flavor in Märzen/Dunkel.
- **Dublin's** very high alkalinity (RA +280) creates the iconic **dry-roasted character** of Irish Stout by buffering roast malt acidity.
- **Burton's** famously high **CaSO₄ (gypsum)** drives the SO₄²⁻ : Cl⁻ ratio above 10, accentuating hop bitterness and producing classic English Bitters and IPAs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **DO NOT confuse "Alkalinity" with "Hardness."** Alkalinity = HCO₃⁻ + CO₃²⁻; Hardness = Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this swap.
> 2. **Always include units** (mg/L as CaCO₃) in RA calculations.
> 3. **For IBU problems:** Convert ounces/grams correctly; **US gallon vs Imperial gallon vs Liter** mistakes cost 1 – 2 marks.
> 4. **Hop isomerization is NOT oxidation** — it is a stereo-isomeric rearrangement. Using the wrong term loses ½ mark.
> 5. **RA formula sign convention:** Some textbooks reverse the sign. Stick with the **Kolbach convention** (positive RA = alkaline). Examiners expect this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ⭐ Hops = **Humulus lupulus** female cones; **α-acids isomerize to iso-α-acids** during boiling → bitterness.
- ⭐ α-acid utilization typically **25 – 35 %**; rises with longer boil time, lower wort gravity, lower pH.
- ⭐ **Co-humulone** content dictates harshness of bitterness.
- ⭐ Essential oils (**myrcene, humulene, linalool**) provide aroma — heat-labile → use late additions.
- ⭐ Adjuncts are unmalted fermentables (corn, rice, sorghum, wheat, sugars).
- ⭐ Adjuncts need **pre-gelatinization** (cereal cooker or pre-gelled flakes) because starch granules are insoluble at 65 °C.
- ⭐ Water = **90 – 95 %** of beer; water profile is the **silent architect of beer character**.
- ⭐ **Residual Alkalinity (RA)** formula: $\text{RA} = \text{Alk} - \left(\dfrac{\text{Ca}}{3.5} + \dfrac{\text{Mg}}{7}\right)$ in mg/L CaCO₃.
- ⭐ **Cl⁻ : SO₄²⁻ ratio** decides malt-forward vs hop-forward character.
- ⭐ Famous cities: **Pilsen (soft), Munich (carbonate), Dublin (high alkalinity), Burton (high gypsum)**.
- ⭐ Target **mash pH = 5.2 – 5.6**; IBU calculated by $\dfrac{W_h \times \alpha\% \times U \times 1000}{V_b}$.
- ⭐ **BU:GU ratio** quantifies bitterness balance; 0.5 – 0.8 = balanced Pale Ale; > 1.0 = IPA.
- ⭐ Total hardness $\text{TH} = 2.5[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.12[\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ in mg/L CaCO₃.
- ⭐ Always convert Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ to **CaCO₃ equivalent** before applying formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Yeast. Beer Manufacturing Process - Malting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Yeast. Beer Manufacturing Process - Malting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Yeast — The Microbial Engine of Fermentation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Yeast* is a **unicellular, eukaryotic, facultative anaerobic microorganism** belonging to the *Kingdom Fungi* and the *Phylum Ascomycota*. In brewing, the species of primary industrial significance is ***Saccharomyces cerevisiae*** (top-cropping / ale yeast) and ***Saccharomyces pastorianus*** (formerly *S. carlsbergensis*, bottom-cropping / lager yeast).

Yeasts are the **biological catalysts** of the brewing industry. They convert fermentable sugars — primarily **maltose**, **glucose**, **fructose**, **maltotriose**, and **sucrose** — into **ethanol**, **carbon dioxide**, and a complex bouquet of **congenerers** (higher alcohols, esters, aldehydes, organic acids) that define beer aroma and flavor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shape & Size:**
> - Shape: Spheroidal to ovoidal; some species ellipsoidal.
> - Size: Typically **5 µm – 10 µm** in diameter (about 5× the size of a bacterium).
> - Cell wall: Rigid, composed of **β-glucans**, **mannoproteins**, and small amounts of **chitin**.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Tiny Brewery Worker":**
Imagine a microscopic factory worker who, depending on whether the air-conditioner (oxygen) is on or off, produces different products. When **oxygen is plentiful** (aerobic mode), it eats sugar and produces carbon dioxide + water — gaining lots of energy to grow and multiply. The moment oxygen is cut off (anaerobic mode), it switches to producing **alcohol** instead — like a worker who, when short on time and tools, produces a faster but less efficient output. This "switching behavior" is the **Pasteur Effect**, and it is the heart of beer fermentation.

---

### 1.2 Malting — The Controlled Awakening of the Grain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Malting* is the **controlled, partial germination of a cereal grain (almost exclusively barley, *Hordeum vulgare*, in brewing) under defined environmental conditions of temperature, moisture, and humidity, terminated at the optimal stage by kilning**, for the purpose of developing and stabilizing hydrolytic enzymes (especially α-amylase, β-amylase, limit-dextrinase, proteases, β-glucanases) and modifying the kernel's starchy endosperm into a friable, easily extractable substrate for mashing.

**Intuitive Analogy — "Waking the Sleeping Giant":**
Think of a barley kernel as a **tiny seed carrying a packed lunchbox of starch**, but locked inside a hard suitcase (the cell walls). The lunchbox cannot be opened unless the seed "wakes up" and produces **tiny molecular scissors (enzymes)**. Malting is the farmer's art of:
1. **Soaking** the seed (steeping) to convince it that spring has come,
2. Letting it **sprout just enough** (germination) to manufacture those scissors,
3. Then **roasting it to stop the sprouting** (kilning) at exactly the right moment — preserving the enzymes and creating flavor.

If the seed sprouts too long, the food supply is consumed and the enzymes become unstable. If stopped too early, there are too few enzymes to break down starch later. **Malting is a race against time.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Barley?**
> - Its husk acts as a **natural filter bed** during lautering.
> - It has a **husk-to-kernel ratio** ideal for uniform germination.
> - It contains the highest levels of **β-amylase** among cereals.
> - It produces balanced levels of **enzymatic modification** when malted.

---

### 1.3 Position of These Topics in the Brewing Process Flow

The complete beer production workflow is:
**Barley → Malting → Milling/Mashing → Wort Boiling → Cooling → Fermentation (Yeast) → Maturation → Packaging**.

Malting and Yeast represent the **start and the living heart** of beer manufacturing, respectively.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Yeast — Metabolic Architecture

Yeast is **facultatively anaerobic**, meaning it can survive in both oxygen-rich and oxygen-free environments, but it generates very different amounts of energy from each.

#### 2.1.1 The Pasteur Effect (Energy-Switching Logic)

**Aerobic Respiration (in presence of O₂):**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2 \longrightarrow 6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O + \text{Energy}$$

$$1 \text{ mole glucose} \equiv 28 \text{ ATP (approximately)}$$

**Anaerobic Fermentation (in absence of O₂) — the *Ethanolic* fermentation:**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + \text{Energy}$$

$$1 \text{ mole glucose} \equiv 2 \text{ ATP only}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Pasteur Effect in brewing terms:**
> When wort is pitched (inoculated) with yeast, dissolved oxygen is initially present (~8–10 mg/L) and yeast reproduces rapidly (aerobic phase, ~24 h). Once oxygen is depleted, the **Pasteur switch** flips — yeast begins fermenting sugar to ethanol. **Adding oxygen again mid-fermentation triggers excessive biomass growth at the cost of alcohol yield**, a defect called *over-attenuation*.

#### 2.1.2 The Crabtree Effect — A Brewing Reality

> [!NOTE]
> Even in the presence of oxygen, when the glucose concentration exceeds approximately **9 g/L** (wort contains ~80–100 g/L fermentable sugars), *S. cerevisiae* predominantly performs **ethanol fermentation** rather than respiration. This is the **Crabtree Effect**. It is the *opposite* of the Pasteur Effect and is genetically hard-wired in brewer's yeast.

#### 2.1.3 Top vs Bottom Fermenters

| Feature | *S. cerevisiae* (Ale) | *S. pastorianus* (Lager) |
|---|---|---|
| Optimum Temperature | **18 °C – 24 °C** | **7 °C – 13 °C** |
| Flocculation | Forms clumps at top | Settles to bottom |
| Fermentation Time | 3 – 7 days | 7 – 14 days |
| Beer Type | Ales, Stouts, Porters | Lagers, Pilsners, Bocks |
| Genetic Ploidy | Often polyploid / aneuploid | Hybrid (hybrid genome) |
| Sugar Preference | Maltose > Glucose | Maltotriose metabolized |

#### 2.1.4 Yeast Reproduction

Yeast reproduces by three mechanisms:
1. **Budding** — primary mode; mitotic division forming a daughter cell.
2. **Fission** — in some *Schizosaccharomyces* spp.
3. **Sexual sporulation** — under nutrient starvation, forms **ascospores** (4 spores per ascus), useful for genetic strain improvement.

---

### 2.2 Malting — The Three-Stage Process in Depth

#### 2.2.1 Stage 1 — Barley Reception & Storage

- Barley is received at **12%–14% moisture** and stored dry (<14%) to maintain dormancy.
- Visual inspection, variety identification (2-row vs 6-row), protein content (**9%–11.5%** ideal for brewing; >12% causes haze issues).
- Storage duration: minimum **6 weeks** to break post-harvest dormancy.

#### 2.2.2 Stage 2 — Steeping

- Grain is immersed in water at **12 °C – 18 °C**.
- **Total steeping time:** 36 – 48 hours.
- **Moisture target:** **42% – 46%** (achieves the "chit" — rootlet emergence).
- Cycles alternate: **wet steep → air rest → wet steep → air rest** (typically 2 wet + 2 dry cycles).
- **Air rests** are critical: they supply oxygen for embryo respiration and remove CO₂ and germination-inhibiting hormones (abscisic acid).

> [!WARNING]
> If air rests are omitted, anaerobic conditions cause **embryo death** and produce "steeped-out" grain with no enzymatic activity.

#### 2.2.3 Stage 3 — Germination (Floor Malting or Saladin Box / Drum Malting)

- **Duration:** 4 – 6 days at **13 °C – 18 °C**, RH > 95%.
- The **acrospire** (coleoptile) grows beneath the husk to **½–¾ of the kernel length** — this is the *modification window*.
- Enzymes synthesized: **α-amylase, β-amylase, limit-dextrinase, proteases, β-glucanases, pentosanases, phytase**.
- Grain is turned mechanically every 8–12 h to prevent matting and to ensure even rootlet growth.

#### 2.2.4 Stage 4 — Kilning (Curing)

- **Purpose:** Stop germination by removing moisture; develop color and flavor compounds via **Maillard reactions** and **caramelization**.
- Two-phase drying:
  - **Free drying phase:** 30 °C → 50 °C, brings moisture from ~45% → 12%.
  - **Curing phase:** 60 °C → 85 °C (pale malt) or up to **100 °C – 220 °C** (Munich, Vienna, crystal, chocolate malt).
- Final malt moisture: **3% – 5%**.
- Rootlets ("culms") are removed by **malting cleaners** immediately after kilning.

#### 2.2.5 Stage 5 — Storage / Maturation

- Malt stored 4 – 6 weeks to allow moisture equilibration.
- Enzyme activity remains stable; flavor harshness mellows.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are routinely expected in KTU numerical problems for Module 2.

| # | Concept | Equation / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aerobic ATP yield | $\text{ATP}_{\text{aerobic}} = 28$ | mol ATP / mol glucose | Theoretical max via oxidative phosphorylation |
| 2 | Anaerobic ATP yield | $\text{ATP}_{\text{anaerobic}} = 2$ | mol ATP / mol glucose | Substrate-level phosphorylation only |
| 3 | Pasteur Energy Ratio | $R_P = \dfrac{\text{ATP}_{\text{aerobic}}}{\text{ATP}_{\text{anaerobic}}} = \dfrac{28}{2} = 14$ | — | Brewing implication: aerobic growth consumes more sugar per cell |
| 4 | Theoretical Alcohol Yield (Gay-Lussac) | $Y_{EtOH} = \dfrac{M_{EtOH}}{M_{glucose}} \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times \rho_{EtOH}$ | g/L or % v/v | $M_{EtOH}=46$, $M_{glucose}=180$ |
| 5 | Practical Alcohol Yield | $Y_p \approx 0.46 \text{ to } 0.51$ | g EtOH / g sugar | Accounts for yeast biomass & by-products |
| 6 | CO₂ Mass Balance | $1 \text{ g sugar} \rightarrow 0.51 \text{ g EtOH} + 0.49 \text{ g CO}_2$ | — | Stoichiometric split |
| 7 | Malt Yield | $\eta_{\text{malt}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{malt}}}{m_{\text{barley}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Typical: 78%–82% |
| 8 | Steep Moisture Absorbed | $\theta_{H_2O} = \dfrac{m_{w,\text{abs}}}{m_{\text{dry grain}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Target 42%–46% |
| 9 | Modification Index (Friability) | $F = \dfrac{m_{\text{friable}}}{m_{\text{total malt}}} \times 100\%$ | % | >85% = well-modified |
| 10 | Extract Yield of Malt | $E = \dfrac{m_{\text{dry extract}}}{m_{\text{malt}}} \times 100\%$ | % dry basis | Pale malt: 79%–82% |
| 11 | Kolbach Index (S/T Ratio) | $KI = \dfrac{\text{Soluble Protein}}{\text{Total Protein}} \times 100\%$ | % | Indicator of proteolysis; 38%–45% well-modified |
| 12 | Diastatic Power (DP) | $\text{DP} = \text{Lintner degrees}$ | °L or °WK | Pale malt: 110–160 °L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Gay-Lussac's theoretical yield (4) sets the upper bound (51.1% w/w); practical brewhouse yields reach only **~85% of theoretical** due to yeast metabolism diverted into biomass, glycerol, organic acids, and higher alcohols.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gay-Lussac Theoretical Ethanol Yield

**Problem (typical KTU 4-mark derivation):**
*Starting from the fermentation stoichiometry, derive the maximum theoretical mass of ethanol obtainable from 1 kg of glucose.*

**Step 1 — Write the balanced fermentation equation:**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

**Step 2 — Note the molar relationships:**

$$1 \text{ mol glucose} \rightarrow 2 \text{ mol ethanol} + 2 \text{ mol CO}_2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute molar masses:**

- $M_{\text{glucose}} = 180 \text{ g/mol}$
- $M_{\text{ethanol}} = 46 \text{ g/mol}$
- $M_{CO_2} = 44 \text{ g/mol}$

**Step 4 — Compute mass of ethanol from 1 mol (180 g) of glucose:**

$$m_{EtOH} = 2 \text{ mol} \times 46 \text{ g/mol} = 92 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 — Express as a percentage yield:**

$$Y_{EtOH,\,theoretical} = \frac{92}{180} \times 100\% = 51.11\% \; (\text{w/w})$$

**Step 6 — Scale to 1 kg:**

$$m_{EtOH} = 1.0 \text{ kg glucose} \times 0.5111 = 0.5111 \text{ kg} = 511.1 \text{ g}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to volume using ethanol density $\rho_{EtOH} = 0.789 \text{ g/mL}$:**

$$V_{EtOH} = \frac{511.1}{0.789} = 647.7 \text{ mL} \; (\text{at } 100\% \text{ ABV})$$

> [!NOTE]
> The corresponding CO₂ produced:
> $m_{CO_2} = \dfrac{2 \times 44}{180} = 0.4889 \rightarrow 488.9 \text{ g CO}_2 \text{ per kg glucose}$
> Notice the elegant **51 : 49 split** (EtOH : CO₂).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pasteur Energy Ratio Applied to Yeast Biomass

**Problem:** *If 100 g of glucose is supplied to a yeast culture, how much yeast biomass is produced under (a) aerobic conditions, and (b) anaerobic conditions? Assume biomass yield coefficients $Y_{x/s}^{aer} = 0.5$ g/g and $Y_{x/s}^{anaer} = 0.05$ g/g.*

**Step 1 — Aerobic biomass:**

$$m_{\text{biomass, aer}} = Y_{x/s}^{aer} \times m_{\text{glucose}} = 0.5 \times 100 = 50 \text{ g yeast}$$

**Step 2 — Anaerobic biomass:**

$$m_{\text{biomass, anaer}} = Y_{x/s}^{anaer} \times m_{\text{glucose}} = 0.05 \times 100 = 5 \text{ g yeast}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The aerobic regime produces **10× more yeast cells per gram of sugar**. In brewing terms:
- High oxygen early = rapid cell proliferation (target: 15–20 million cells/mL at peak).
- Anaerobic phase = sugar routed to ethanol, not biomass.

> [!WARNING]
> **Brewing consequence:** A brewer must oxygenate wort **only in the first 6–12 h** (replication phase). Continued aeration mid-fermentation = poor alcohol yield and off-flavors from yeast stress metabolism.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Malt Mass Balance (Steeping Calculation)

**Problem:** *2 tonnes of dry barley (12% moisture, w.b.) is to be steeped to 45% moisture (w.b.). Calculate the water absorbed.*

**Step 1 — Determine dry matter in barley:**

$$m_{\text{dry matter}} = 2000 \text{ kg} \times (1 - 0.12) = 2000 \times 0.88 = 1760 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Express target moisture on wet basis:**
At 45% moisture (w.b.), dry matter fraction = $1 - 0.45 = 0.55$.

**Step 3 — Compute total wet mass after steeping:**

$$m_{\text{wet, total}} = \frac{1760}{0.55} = 3200 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute water added:**

$$m_{\text{water absorbed}} = 3200 - 2000 = 1200 \text{ kg} \; (1.2 \text{ m}^3)$$

**Step 5 — Express as moisture gain on dry basis:**

$$\theta_{H_2O} = \frac{1200}{1760} \times 100\% = 68.18\% \; (\text{d.b.})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** Final moisture 45% (w.b.) is consistent with the standard **42%–46%** target window for full "chit" emergence in barley.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Diastatic Power and Attenuation Limit

**Problem:** *A wort contains 80 g/L of maltose. If the yeast attenuates 70% of the maltose, calculate the final alcohol concentration in % ABV.*

**Step 1 — Maltose molecular weight** $M_{\text{maltose}} = 342 \text{ g/mol}$.

**Step 2 — Convert to molarity:**

$$[\text{maltose}] = \frac{80}{342} = 0.234 \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 3 — Fermentation reaction for maltose (a disaccharide):**

$$C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O \longrightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical ethanol per mole of maltose:**

$$2 \times 46 = 92 \text{ g EtOH per mole maltose}$$

**Step 5 — Attenuated maltose:**

$$m_{\text{maltose, atten}} = 0.7 \times 80 = 56 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 6 — Theoretical EtOH mass:**

$$m_{EtOH} = \frac{56}{342} \times 92 = 15.06 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to % ABV (using ethanol density 0.789 g/mL):**

$$\%\text{ABV} = \frac{15.06}{0.789 \times 10} = \frac{15.06}{7.89} = 1.91\% \; \text{ABV}$$

**Step 8 — Apply practical efficiency (85% of theoretical):**

$$\%\text{ABV}_{\text{actual}} = 1.91 \times 0.85 \approx 1.62\% \; \text{ABV}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary (Yeast × Malting)

| Domain | Process | Key Enzyme / Variable | Temperature | Time | Critical Control Point |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Yeast | Aerobic growth | Oxygen, sugar | 18 °C – 24 °C | 8 – 24 h | Dissolved O₂ > 8 mg/L |
| Yeast | Anaerobic fermentation | ATP from glycolysis | 18 °C – 24 °C | 3 – 7 days | O₂ < 0.5 mg/L |
| Malting | Steeping | Water uptake | 12 °C – 18 °C | 36 – 48 h | Final moisture 42%–46% |
| Malting | Germination | α/β-amylase, proteases | 13 °C – 18 °C | 4 – 6 days | Acrospire ½ – ¾ length |
| Malting | Kilning | Maillard browning | 50 °C → 85 °C | 18 – 24 h | Final moisture < 5% |
| Malting | Malt storage | None (only curing) | Ambient | 4 – 6 weeks | Moisture equilibration |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Malting Process

```mermaid

    A[Barley Reception] --> B[Cleaning and Grading]
    B --> C[Storage 6 weeks for Dormancy Break]
    C --> D[Steeping Tank]

    D1 --> D2[Air Rest 8 to 12 h]
    D2 --> D3[Wet Steep 8 to 12 h]
    D3 --> D4[Air Rest 8 to 12 h]
    D4 --> E[Chit Emergence at 42 to 46 percent Moisture]
    E --> F[Germination Chamber Saladin Box or Drum]
    F --> F1[Day 1 to 2 Rootlet Emergence]
    F1 --> F2[Day 3 to 4 Acrospire Half Length]
    F2 --> F3[Day 5 to 6 Acrospire Three Quarter Length]
    F3 --> G[Kilning Free Drying 30 to 50 C]
    G --> H[Kilning Curing 60 to 85 C Pale]
    H --> I[Rootlet Removal Malt Cleaning]
    I --> J[Malt Storage 4 to 6 weeks]
    J --> K[Finished Malt to Brew House]
```

```mermaid
    q2 -- YES --> pathA
    q2 -- NO --> pathB[Aerobic Respiration]

    pathA --> pathA1[Glucose to 2 Ethanol plus 2 Carbon Dioxide]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Crabtree Effect" with respect to brewer's yeast. Why is it industrially significant?*

**Model Answer:**
The **Crabtree Effect** is the phenomenon by which *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, when exposed to **high glucose concentrations (>9 g/L, as in brewer's wort)**, predominantly produces **ethanol via fermentation even in the presence of oxygen**, suppressing respiratory metabolism. It is genetically regulated by the repression of *AOX1* and related respiratory genes. **Industrial significance:** (i) It ensures alcohol production in wort despite residual oxygen; (ii) it bypasses the regulatory complexity of oxygen control in later fermentation stages; (iii) it permits natural carbonation since CO₂ is produced as a co-product.

**(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 significance points)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List the three major biochemical events that occur during barley germination in malting.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Synthesis and activation of hydrolytic enzymes** — α-amylase, β-amylase, limit-dextrinase, endo-proteases, β-glucanases.
2. **Mobilization of stored reserves** — enzymatic breakdown of starch, protein, and cell-wall β-glucans in the endosperm.
3. **Hormonal signalling** — Gibberellic acid (GA) released by the embryo diffuses to the aleurone layer, triggering enzyme synthesis.

**(3 marks: 1 mark each)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, the student may choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Yeast-Centric

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With neat equations, explain the **Pasteur Effect** in *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*. Compare the ATP yields in aerobic and anaerobic modes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between *S. cerevisiae* (ale) and *S. pastorianus* (lager) yeasts under the headings: morphology, optimum temperature, flocculation behaviour, sugar utilization, and beer types produced. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Pasteur Effect describes the **inhibition of glycolysis (and hence fermentation rate) when oxygen is available to yeast**, since yeast switches from anaerobic fermentation to the energetically superior aerobic respiration.

Aerobic respiration:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2 \longrightarrow 6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O + \sim 28 \,\text{ATP}$$

Anaerobic (ethanolic) fermentation:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + 2\,\text{ATP}$$

| Parameter | Aerobic | Anaerobic |
|---|---|---|
| ATP yield | **~28** | **2** |
| End products | CO₂ + H₂O | Ethanol + CO₂ |
| Yeast activity | Growth, proliferation | Fermentation, alcohol yield |
| Energy efficiency | High (~40%) | Low (~2–4%) |

**Brewing implication:** At pitching, dissolved O₂ must be **8–10 mg/L** to allow rapid cell multiplication (typically 4–6 divisions). Once yeast count reaches **15–20 million cells/mL**, oxygen must be excluded to channel sugar into alcohol rather than biomass.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Parameter | *S. cerevisiae* (Ale) | *S. pastorianus* (Lager) |
|---|---|---|
| Morphology | Round/ovoid, 5–10 µm | Slightly elongated, often larger |
| Optimum Temperature | 18 °C – 24 °C | 7 °C – 13 °C |
| Flocculation | Rises to surface (top-cropping) | Settles to bottom (bottom-cropping) |
| Sugar Utilization | Prefers maltose & glucose | Effectively ferments maltotriose |
| Beer Types | Ales, Stouts, Porters, Wheat | Lagers, Pilsners, Bocks, Märzens |
| Genetic Origin | Pure *cerevisiae* lineage | Hybrid of *cerevisiae × eubayanus* |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Malting-Centric

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe with a flow diagram the **steps involved in malting of barley**, highlighting the biochemical purpose of each stage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A malthouse processes **5 tonnes of dry barley at 12% moisture (w.b.)**. The target post-steep moisture is **45% (w.b.)**. Calculate: (i) the dry matter; (ii) water absorbed; (iii) the mass of green malt before kilning. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

The five sequential stages are:

1. **Cleaning and Grading:** Removal of foreign matter, broken kernels, and weed seeds; grading by size for uniform steeping and germination. *(1 mark)*
2. **Steeping:** Immersion of barley in water at 12–18 °C, alternating with air rests, for 36–48 h, raising moisture from ~12% → **42%–46%**. Activates embryo metabolism, leaches polyphenols, and breaks dormancy. *(2 marks)*
3. **Germination:** Grain is held at 13–18 °C, RH >95% for 4–6 days in Saladin boxes or drums. Embryo releases **gibberellic acid (GA)**, which diffuses to the aleurone layer, triggering synthesis of **α-amylase, β-amylase, proteases, β-glucanases**. Endosperm cell walls and protein matrix are degraded, freeing starch granules. *(2 marks)*
4. **Kilning:** Free drying (30–50 °C) reduces moisture to ~12%; curing (60–85 °C for pale malt, up to 220 °C for specialty malts) reduces moisture to <5%, halts germination, develops Maillard-derived colour and flavour. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Malt Cleaning and Storage:** Rootlets ("culms") removed. Malt stored 4–6 weeks for moisture equilibration and flavour mellowing. *(0.5 mark)*

(Flow diagram — see SECTION 4.1 of these notes for reference.)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Initial mass of barley, $m_1 = 5000 \text{ kg}$ at moisture $M_1 = 12\%$ (w.b.)

**Step (i) — Dry matter content:**

$$m_{\text{dry}} = m_1 \times (1 - M_1) = 5000 \times (1 - 0.12) = 5000 \times 0.88$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{dry}} = 4400 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step (ii) — Water absorbed during steeping:**
Let $m_2$ be the final wet mass at $M_2 = 45\%$ (w.b.).

$$m_{\text{dry}} = m_2 \times (1 - M_2) \Rightarrow m_2 = \frac{4400}{0.55} = 8000 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{\text{water absorbed}} = m_2 - m_1 = 8000 - 5000 = \boxed{3000 \text{ kg}}$$

(equivalent to 3 m³ of water; recommend 3.3 m³ accounting for 10% drain loss).

**Step (iii) — Green malt mass before kilning:**
Green malt moisture is approximately equal to post-steep moisture (≈ 45% w.b.), since germination is conducted under humid conditions without further water addition. Therefore:

$$\boxed{m_{\text{green malt}} \approx 8000 \text{ kg} \; \text{(before moisture drop to 5%)}}$$

During kilning, ~ 3200 kg of water is evaporated, yielding finished malt of:

$$m_{\text{finished malt}} \approx 8000 \times 0.55 = 4400 \text{ kg (dry basis) + 220 kg (5% moisture)} = \boxed{4620 \text{ kg}}$$

This corresponds to a **malt yield of 92.4% on a dry basis**, consistent with industry values of 78–82% on an as-is basis.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 2 Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the molar mass of glucose is 180 g/mol, not 160.** Many students erroneously use 162 (which is the *anhydride* form, not the standard hexose formula used in brewing). The KTU evaluator deducts **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Mixing up wet-basis and dry-basis moisture.** Always state which basis is in use. KTU 2024 papers often phrase the question ambiguously — defaulting to *wet basis* is safer.
> 3. **Omitting the Gay-Lussac stoichiometric factor of ½.** The reaction produces **2 moles** of ethanol per mole of glucose, not 1.
> 4. **Writing *S. carlsbergensis* (deprecated).** The current taxonomically correct name is ***S. pastorianus***. Using the old name may cost a **½ mark** in 2024 scheme valuations.
> 5. **Confusing Pasteur Effect (oxygen suppression of fermentation) with Crabtree Effect (glucose suppression of respiration).** These are *opposite* phenomena and KTU examiners deliberately test this distinction.
> 6. **In malting questions, omitting the role of gibberellic acid** when asked about enzyme synthesis. This single omission typically loses **2 marks**.
> 7. **Dry-matter vs wet-matter mass balance errors** in steeping calculations. Always compute dry matter first; never mix wet masses with wet-basis moisture percentages.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Yeast & Malting**

**Yeast — High-Yield Points:**
- ☐ Yeast is a **facultative anaerobic** fungus (eukaryote), **5–10 µm**.
- ☐ Primary industrial species: ***S. cerevisiae*** (ale) and ***S. pastorianus*** (lager).
- ☐ **Pasteur Effect:** O₂ inhibits fermentation; yeast prefers respiration when O₂ is available.
- ☐ **Crabtree Effect:** High glucose (>9 g/L) represses respiration even with O₂ present → alcohol produced.
- ☐ Aerobic ATP = **28**; Anaerobic ATP = **2**; Ratio = **14**.
- ☐ Gay-Lussac theoretical yield = **51.1% w/w** ethanol from glucose.
- ☐ Practical yield = **~85% of theoretical** → ≈ 0.46–0.51 g EtOH / g sugar.
- ☐ Pitching oxygen target: **8–10 mg/L**; mid-fermentation O₂ should be **< 0.5 mg/L**.
- ☐ Reproduction: **Budding** (main), sporulation (under stress).
- ☐ Ales ferment at **18–24 °C**; Lagers at **7–13 °C**.

**Malting — High-Yield Points:**
- ☐ **Definition:** Controlled, partial germination terminated by kilning.
- ☐ Stages: **Cleaning → Steeping → Germination → Kilning → Cleaning/Storage**.
- ☐ Barley is preferred due to **husk, β-amylase content, and modification behaviour**.
- ☐ Steep moisture target: **42%–46%** (w.b.) in **36–48 h** at **12–18 °C**.
- ☐ **Air rests** during steeping prevent embryo death (avoid anaerobicity).
- ☐ Germination lasts **4–6 days** at **13–18 °C**, RH > 95%.
- ☐ Acrospire should reach **½ to ¾ of kernel length** for optimal modification.
- ☐ **Gibberellic acid (GA)** from the embryo triggers enzyme synthesis at the aleurone.
- ☐ Key enzymes: **α-amylase, β-amylase, limit-dextrinase, proteases, β-glucanases**.
- ☐ Kilning temperatures: pale malt **60–85 °C**, specialty malts up to **220 °C**.
- ☐ Final malt moisture: **3%–5%**.
- ☐ Quality metrics: **Diastatic Power (°L)**, **Kolbach Index (S/T)**, **Friability (%)**, **Extract (%)**.
- ☐ Malt yield: typically **78%–82%** (as-is basis) from dry barley.

**Cross-Linking Insight (Frequently Asked):**
> The enzymes synthesized during **germination** are *not consumed* during malting — they are **preserved by kilning** and become the active catalysts in the **mashing** step. Without proper malting, mashing would fail to extract fermentable sugar, and yeast would have no substrate to convert into ethanol. This is why malting is the **upstream enabler** of the entire brewing process.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Sweet Wort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Preparation of Sweet Wort # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Sweet Wort** is defined as the **unfermented, sugar-rich aqueous extract** obtained from the enzymatic hydrolysis of milled malt (and adjuncts) during the mashing process, subsequently clarified by lautering and boiled with hops, but **prior to yeast pitching and fermentation**. It serves as the nutritional and biochemical substrate that is later converted into beer by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* or *S. pastorianus*. In strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the **Preparation of Sweet Wort** is the upstream brewing unit operation block that converts **starchy raw materials** (malt + adjuncts + brewing water) into a **sterile, hop-balanced, fermentable sugar solution** of defined specific gravity (typically **1.040 – 1.055 g/mL** at 20 °C), ready for fermentation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight — Bloom's Level: L2 (Understand) & L3 (Apply)** > Sweet wort quality is evaluated on three primary parameters: > 1. **Extract Yield** (° Plato / Specific Gravity) > 2. **Wort Clarity** (Nephelometric Turbidity Units, NTU < 50) > 3. **Fermentability** (Ratio of fermentable to total sugars) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Brewing" Intuition > [!NOTE] > Imagine preparing a very strong **sugary herbal tea**, but instead of tea leaves, you use *crushed germinated barley* (malt). You soak the crushed grains in hot water at a controlled temperature for a fixed time, drain the liquid, and you get a sweet, golden syrup — that syrup is your **sweet wort**. - The **barley grains** = starch storage tanks - The **hot water** = solvent + enzyme activator - The **controlled temperature soak (mash)** = the chemical "tea brewing" - The **sweet liquid drained out** = the **sweet wort** In essence, sweet wort preparation is the **controlled aqueous extraction of malt-derived sugars (maltose, maltotriose, glucose) and soluble proteins** under conditions that activate the grain's own enzymes (amylases, proteases, β-glucanases). ## 1.3 Standard Brewing Metrics (Bold Definitions) - **Grist** – The dry, milled mixture of malt and adjuncts. - **Mash** – The aqueous suspension of grist in brewing water during enzymatic conversion. - **Strike Water** – The brewing water added initially to the grist. - **Sparge Water** – The hot water (76–78 °C) used to rinse sugars from the grain bed during lautering. - **Liquor-to-Grist Ratio** – Mass ratio of brewing water to milled grain; typically **2.5 : 1 to 3.5 : 1** (L/kg). - **Plato (°P)** – A brewing density unit; **1 °P ≈ 1 % sucrose (w/w)** in solution. - **Original Gravity (OG)** – Specific gravity of wort before fermentation, measured at **20 °C**. - **Decoction** – Process of removing a portion of the mash, boiling it, and returning it to raise temperature. - **Infusion** – Process of heating the entire mash uniformly without removal. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Unit Operation Sequence (The Six Pillars of Sweet Wort Preparation) The process is conventionally divided into **six sequential, interlinked unit operations**. Each is critical and feeds the next: ### Pillar 1 — Malt Milling (Grinding) - **Objective:** Fracture the endosperm to expose starch granules while keeping the **husk intact** to act as a natural filter bed during lautering. - **Gap Setting:** Typically **0.5 – 0.7 mm** for a 6-roll mill. - **Goal:** Achieve a **grist composition** of ~25 % husk, ~10 % coarse grits, ~35 % fine grits, ~30 % flour. ### Pillar 2 — Mashing (Enzymatic Hydrolysis) - **Objective:** Convert insoluble starch into soluble fermentable sugars using malt enzymes. - The grist is mixed with strike water at a controlled **mash-in temperature of 50 ± 1 °C** (for most lagers and ales). - **Temperature Rests** (a critical KTU high-yield area): | Rest Stage | Temperature (°C) | Duration (min) | Enzyme Active | Function | |:----------:|:----------------:|:--------------:|:-------------:|:---------| | Acid Rest | 35 – 38 | 15 – 30 | Phytase, Glucosidase | Lowers mash pH to 5.4 – 5.6 | | Protein Rest | 45 – 55 | 15 – 30 | Protease, Peptidase | Breaks down proteins → amino acids | | Saccharification Rest | 62 – 67 | 30 – 60 | β-Amylase, α-Amylase | Converts starch → maltose, dextrins | | Mash-out | 76 – 78 | 5 – 10 | Enzyme deactivation | Stops enzymatic activity | > [!NOTE] > **Why Multiple Temperatures?** > Each enzyme has an *optimum temperature and pH range*. The mashing schedule is essentially a **temperature-programmed enzymatic reactor** — like running a multi-step PCR, but with biological enzymes on grain substrates. ### Pillar 3 — Lautering (Solid–Liquid Separation) - **Objective:** Separate the sugar-rich liquid (sweet wort) from the spent grains. - Conducted in a **lauter tun** (a false-bottomed vessel) using a **grain bed ~30 – 50 cm deep** acting as a filter. - **Sub-stages:** 1. **Vorlauf / Recirculation** – First cloudy wort is recirculated until **clarity < 50 NTU** is achieved. 2. **First Wort Run-off** – Sweet wort drawn off. 3. **Sparging** – Hot water (76 – 78 °C) sprayed over the grain bed to extract residual sugars. - **End Point:** Sparging stops when runoff gravity drops below **1.010 (≈ 2.5 °P)**. ### Pillar 4 — Wort Boiling (with Hop Addition) - **Objective:** (a) Sterilize wort; (b) Extract & isomerize hop α-acids into bitter iso-α-acids; (c) Coagulate and precipitate proteins (hot break); (d) Concentrate wort via evaporation. - **Boiling Duration:** **60 – 90 minutes** at atmospheric pressure (~100 °C at sea level, **100/101 = 0.99** pressure compensation at higher altitudes). - **Hop Addition Schedule:** - **Bittering hops** at 0 min (start of boil) - **Flavor hops** at 15 – 30 min - **Aroma hops** at 0 – 5 min (whirlpool addition / hop stand) ### Pillar 5 — Whirlpool Separation (Hot Break Removal) - **Objective:** Centrifugally separate coagulated proteins and hop trub. - Tangential injection of wort into a **whirlpool vessel** at ~7 – 10 m/s for 10 – 15 min creates a vortex. - Trub cone settles at the **geometric center bottom**; clear wort is drawn from the side. ### Pillar 6 — Wort Cooling & Aeration - **Objective:** Cool wort from ~95 °C to **fermentation pitching temperature** (18 – 22 °C for ales, 8 – 12 °C for lagers). - Equipment: **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)**. - Aeration: **Dissolved Oxygen (DO) raised to 6 – 8 mg/L** (essential for yeast cell membrane synthesis in early fermentation). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize These for KTU 2024 Board Exams** | # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Use | |:-:|:--------:|:-------:|:-----------------:|:---------------:| | 1 | Strike Water Volume | $V_s = M_g \times R \times \Delta T \times C_{p,g} \,/\, (C_{p,w} \times \Delta T_w)$ | $M_g$ = grist mass (kg); $R$ = liquor-to-grist ratio; $C_{p,g}$ = grain specific heat (≈ **1.7 kJ/kg·K**); $C_{p,w}$ = water specific heat (**4.19 kJ/kg·K**); $\Delta T$ = target temperature rise | Calculate required strike water quantity | | 2 | Extract Yield (%) | $E \,(\%) = \dfrac{(OG - 1) \times 1000 \times V_w}{M_m}$ | $OG$ = original gravity; $V_w$ = wort volume (L); $M_m$ = malt mass (kg) | Measure brewhouse efficiency | | 3 | Attenuation (Fermentability) | $AF \,(\%) = \dfrac{OG - FG}{OG - 1} \times 100$ | $OG$ = original gravity; $FG$ = final gravity | Predict alcohol & residual sweetness | | 4 | Wort Plato from Specific Gravity | $^\circ P = -616.868 + 1111.14 \times SG - 630.272 \times SG^2 + 135.997 \times SG^3$ | $SG$ = specific gravity at 20 °C | Lab conversion in brewery QC | | 5 | Sugar Conversion (Maltose Equivalent) | $S = C_0 \times (1 - e^{-kt})$ | $C_0$ = initial starch conc.; $k$ = rate constant (1/min); $t$ = mash time (min) | Model mashing kinetics | | 6 | Sparge Water Demand | $V_{sp} = \dfrac{E_{target} - E_{first\,wort}}{C_{sugar\,in\,sparge}}$ | $E$ = extract; $C$ = concentration | Determine sparge volume needed | | 7 | Boil-off Concentration | $SG_{final} = SG_{initial} \times \dfrac{V_i}{V_f}$ | $V_i$, $V_f$ = initial and final volumes (L) | Account for evaporation losses | | 8 | Heat Exchanger Area | $A = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}{U \cdot LMTD}$ | $U$ = overall heat transfer coeff. (≈ **2000 – 3000 W/m²·K** for PHE); $LMTD$ = log-mean temperature difference | Size wort cooler | | 9 | Iso-α-Acid Utilization | $IU \,(\%) = \dfrac{\alpha\text{-acid added (g)} \times 0.30}{Wort\,Volume\,(hL)}$ | Empirical factor 0.30 for kettle utilization | Calculate beer bitterness (IBU) | | 10 | Water-to-Malt Mass Ratio | $R = \dfrac{M_w}{M_m}$ | $M_w$ = water mass (kg); $M_m$ = malt mass (kg) | Standard mashing parameter | > **Note:** Avoid the pipe symbol `|x|` in tables; absolute values use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial Breweries:** A 10 hL brewhouse processes ~**2,500 kg of malt/day**; wort prep energy consumption is ~**8 – 12 %** of total brewery thermal load. - **Process Control:** Modern breweries use **Inline NIR (Near-Infrared) sensors** to monitor °P, pH, and color at the lautering outlet. - **Energy Optimization:** Vapor recompression on boil-off can recover **40 – 60 %** of thermal energy. - **Quality Engineering:** Wort FAN (Free Amino Nitrogen) must be **150 – 220 mg/L** for healthy yeast metabolism. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Strike Water Temperature Calculation The brewhouse must add water hot enough to bring the cold grist (≈ 20 °C) up to the target **mash-in temperature** ($T_m$, typically 50 °C). Let us derive $T_s$ (strike water temperature) from **energy balance**. **System:** Strike water + Grist → Mash at $T_m$. **Energy Balance (assume no heat loss to environment in ideal case):** $$ m_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_s - T_m) = m_g \cdot C_{p,g} \cdot (T_m - T_g) $$ **Rearranging for $T_s$:** $$ T_s = T_m + \dfrac{m_g \cdot C_{p,g} \cdot (T_m - T_g)}{m_w \cdot C_{p,w}} $$ **Substituting the Liquor-to-Grist Ratio** $R = \dfrac{m_w}{m_g}$: $$ T_s = T_m + \dfrac{C_{p,g}}{R \cdot C_{p,w}} \cdot (T_m - T_g) $$ **Numerical Example (Worked Out):** - $T_m = 50 \, ^\circ C$, $T_g = 20 \, ^\circ C$, $R = 2.8$ L/kg, $C_{p,w} = 4.19$ kJ/kg·K, $C_{p,g} = 1.7$ kJ/kg·K $$ \begin{aligned} T_s &= 50 + \dfrac{1.7}{2.8 \times 4.19} \times (50 - 20) \\ &= 50 + \dfrac{1.7}{11.732} \times 30 \\ &= 50 + 0.1449 \times 30 \\ &= 50 + 4.347 \\ T_s &\approx 54.3 \, ^\circ C \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** To achieve a 50 °C mash, the strike water should be heated to **~54.3 °C** when grist is at 20 °C. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Brewhouse Efficiency & Extract Recovery The **brewhouse efficiency** $\eta_B$ is defined as the ratio of **actual extract obtained** to the **theoretical extract** of the malt (provided on malt certificate, typically **78 – 82 %**). $$ \eta_B = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{theoretical}} \times 100 \,(\%) $$ **Where:** $$ E_{actual} = (OG_{wort} - 1) \times 1000 \times V_{wort} \quad \text{[g of extract per brew]} $$ $$ E_{theoretical} = M_{malt} \times E_{malt,cert} \quad \text{[g of extract, from certificate]} $$ **Worked Numerical Example:** - Malt mass $M_{malt} = 100$ kg, certificate extract $E_{cert} = 80$ % - Wort volume $V_{wort} = 280$ L, Wort OG $= 1.048$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{theoretical} &= 100 \times 0.80 = 80 \, \text{kg} \\ E_{actual} &= (1.048 - 1) \times 1000 \times 280 \\ &= 0.048 \times 1000 \times 280 = 13{,}440 \, \text{g} = 13.44 \, \text{kg} \\ \eta_B &= \dfrac{13.44}{80} \times 100 = 16.8 \, \% \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** This unusually low value suggests a calculation error in real practice — let us verify. A typical brewhouse is **70 – 85 %** efficient. The above unit mismatch needs correction using **kg-equivalent** Plato scale. **Corrected Version using °Plato:** For $SG = 1.048$, $^\circ P \approx 12 ^\circ P$ (12 g extract per 100 g solution). $$ E_{actual} = \dfrac{^\circ P \times V_{wort} \times \rho_{wort}}{1000} = \dfrac{12 \times 280 \times 1.048}{1000} = 3.52 \, \text{kg} $$ Wait — this is still off. The correct **malt-based** formula: $$ E_{actual} = (^\circ P) \times V_{wort}\,(\text{L}) \times \rho_{wort} \times 10 \quad \text{[g]} $$ For our case: $12 \times 280 \times 1.048 \times 10 = 35{,}213 \, \text{g} \approx 35.2 \, \text{kg}$. $$ \eta_B = \dfrac{35.2}{80} \times 100 = 44 \, \% \quad \text{(still low — typical)}} $$ For an **efficient** brewhouse targeting **75 % efficiency**, with the same malt: - Required $E_{actual} = 0.75 \times 80 = 60$ kg - Solve for $V_{wort}$: $V_{wort} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{12 \times 1.048 \times 10} = 477$ L - Implying $R = 4.77$ L/kg (high liquor ratio) OR using a **higher-OG mash** at 1.060. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wort Cooling Heat Exchanger Sizing A plate heat exchanger (PHE) cools wort from $T_{in} = 95 \, ^\circ C$ to $T_{out} = 20 \, ^\circ C$ using cooling water flowing counter-currently from $T_{c,in} = 5 \, ^\circ C$ to $T_{c,out} = 75 \, ^\circ C$. **LMTD Calculation (counter-current):** $$ LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} $$ Where: - $\Delta T_1 = T_{in,wort} - T_{c,out} = 95 - 75 = 20 \, ^\circ C$ - $\Delta T_2 = T_{out,wort} - T_{c,in} = 20 - 5 = 15 \, ^\circ C$ $$ \begin{aligned} LMTD &= \dfrac{20 - 15}{\ln(20 / 15)} = \dfrac{5}{\ln(1.333)} \\ &= \dfrac{5}{0.2877} = 17.38 \, ^\circ C \end{aligned} $$ **Heat Load:** $Q = \dot{m}_{wort} \times C_{p,wort} \times \Delta T = 2000 \, \text{kg/h} \times 4.19 \times 75 = 628{,}500 \, \text{kJ/h} = 174.6 \, \text{kW}$. **Required Heat Transfer Area** (using $U = 2500$ W/m²·K for PHE): $$ A = \dfrac{Q}{U \times LMTD} = \dfrac{174{,}600 \, \text{W}}{2500 \times 17.38} = 4.02 \, \text{m}^2 $$ > **Conclusion:** A PHE with **~4 m² plate area** is sufficient to cool 2000 kg/h of wort. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Mash Temperature on Wort Composition | Mash Temp (°C) | α-Amylase Activity | β-Amylase Activity | Resulting Wort Profile | Beer Body | |:--------------:|:------------------:|:------------------:|:----------------------:|:---------:| | 55 – 60 | Low | High | Very fermentable, dry, thin | Light body | | 62 – 66 | Moderate | **Maximum** | Balanced, malty | Medium body | | 67 – 70 | **Maximum** | Deactivating | Less fermentable, dextrins rich | Full body | | 71 – 75 | Fading | Inactive | Starch haze risk, very dextrinous | Heavy body | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic:** "**β Burns at Boiling, α Aces All"** — β-amylase peaks at lower temps (≈ 65 °C), α-amylase dominates at higher mashes (≈ 72 °C). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Complete Sweet Wort Preparation) The following Mermaid diagram maps the **process flow architecture** of sweet wort preparation, showing each unit operation, its input, output, and critical control parameters. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Malt Silos] --> A2[Roll Mill
Gap 0.5-0.7 mm] A3[Adjunct Cooker] --> A2 A2 --> A3a[Milled Grist
Composition 25% Husk 75% Flour] A3a --> B1[Mash Tun
Strike Water 54 C] B1 --> B2[Acid Rest 38 C
15 min pH 5.4] B2 --> B3[Protein Rest 52 C
20 min] B3 --> B4[Saccharification Rest 65 C
45 min] B4 --> B5[Mash-out 78 C
10 min] B5 --> C1[Lauter Tun
Grain Bed 40 cm] C1 --> C2[Vorlauf Recirculation
Turbidity less than 50 NTU] C2 --> C3[First Wort Run-off
OG 1.048 to 1.055] C3 --> C4[Sparging 78 C
to 1.010 endpoint] C4 --> D1[Wort Kettle
Boil 60-90 min] D2[Bittering Hops 0 min] --> D1 D1 --> D3[Flavor Hops 30 min] --> D1 D3 --> D4[Aroma Hops 0 min Whirlpool] --> D1 D1 --> E1[Whirlpool Vessel
Tangential 10 m per s] E1 --> E2[Trub Cone Settling
15 min] E2 --> F1[Plate Heat Exchanger
95 C to 20 C] F1 --> F2[Aeration Static or In-line
DO 6 to 8 mg per L] F2 --> G1[(SWEET WORT READY
FOR FERMENTATION)] style A1 fill:#f9e79f style G1 fill:#a9dfbf style D1 fill:#f5b7b1 style F1 fill:#aed6f1 ``` ## 4.2 Process State Machine — Temperature vs. Time Profile (Mash Schedule) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[0 min
Strike In
54 C] --> S2[15 min
Acid Rest
38 C] S2 --> S3[35 min
Protein Rest
52 C] S3 --> S4[80 min
Saccharification
65 C] S4 --> S5[85 min
Mash-out
78 C] S5 --> S6[Transfer to
Lauter Tun] style S1 fill:#fff3cd style S2 fill:#fde2e4 style S3 fill:#e2f0cb style S4 fill:#fadbd8 style S5 fill:#d6eaf8 style S6 fill:#a9dfbf ``` ## 4.3 Mass & Energy Flow Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD IN1[Malt 100 kg
80% Extract] --> M1 IN2[Water 280 L
20 C] --> M1 IN3[Energy 4.5 kWh
for Heating] --> M1 M1[MASH TUN
Enzymatic Reactor] --> OUT1[Sweet Wort
280 L at 1.048] M1 --> OUT2[Spent Grains
110 kg at 80% Moisture] OUT1 --> M2[KETTLE
+ Hops 0.5 kg] --> OUT3[Hopped Wort
260 L at 1.052
after 8% boil-off] OUT3 --> M3[PHE
+ Cooling Water 1.5 m cubed] --> OUT4[Cold Wort
260 L at 20 C] OUT4 --> M4[AERATOR
+ O2 2 g per hL] --> FINAL((FERMENTER
READY)) style M1 fill:#fff3cd style M2 fill:#f5b7b1 style M3 fill:#aed6f1 style M4 fill:#d5f5e3 style FINAL fill:#a9dfbf ``` ## 4.4 Wort Composition Sankey-Style Resource Map ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Starch 60% of Malt] -->|alpha + beta amylase| P2[Maltose 60%] P1 -->|alpha amylase| P3[Dextrins 25%] P1 -->|Limit Dextrinase| P4[Limit Dextrins 10%] P1 -->|Residual| P5[Unconverted 5%] P6[Proteins 12% of Malt] -->|Protease| P7[Peptides FAN] P6 -->|Coagulation| P8[Hot Break] P9[Cell Wall 8% of Malt] -->|beta Glucanase| P10[Soluble Beta Glucans] P9 -->|Residual| P11[Husks Filter Aid] style P2 fill:#a9dfbf style P7 fill:#f9e79f style P10 fill:#aed6f1 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 Scheme)** > - **Part A (Short Answer):** 3 marks each — direct conceptual > - **Part B (Long Answer):** 14 marks each — internal choice; sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks > - **CO Mapping:** CO2 — *Demonstrate the unit operations in beverage processing* (Bloom: L3–L4) --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model QP]** **Define sweet wort and state any two of its quality parameters.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Sweet wort is the **unfermented, sugar-rich aqueous extract** obtained by enzymatic hydrolysis of malt during mashing, followed by lautering and boiling with hops, prior to yeast inoculation. > **Two quality parameters:** > 1. **Original Gravity (OG):** Typically **1.040 – 1.055** at 20 °C, indicating fermentable sugar concentration. > 2. **Clarity:** Turbidity of the lautered wort should be **less than 50 NTU** (Nephelometric Turbidity Units) to ensure yeast health and fermentation consistency. > *(1 mark definition + 1.5 marks for two parameters + 0.5 mark for unit/specification)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model QP]** **List the four principal temperature rests during mashing with their corresponding temperature ranges and enzymes activated.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Rest | Temperature (°C) | Enzyme | Function | |:----:|:----------------:|:------:|:--------:| | Acid Rest | 35 – 38 | Phytase | Lowers pH | | Protein Rest | 45 – 55 | Protease | Amino acid release | | Saccharification Rest | 62 – 67 | β + α-Amylase | Starch → Sugar | | Mash-out | 76 – 78 | — | Enzyme deactivation | *(0.5 mark per correct row × 4 = 2 marks; 1 mark for formatting/clarity)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]** **(a)** Describe in detail the **mashing process** for the preparation of sweet wort. Explain the importance of temperature rests with a neat flowchart. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A brewery uses **100 kg of malt** with certificate extract of **80 %** and water-to-grist ratio of **3.0 L/kg** at strike water temperature of **55 °C**. Grain is at **22 °C**. Calculate: - (i) The **strike water temperature** required to achieve a mash-in temperature of **50 °C**. - (ii) The **mash volume** in liters. - (iii) The **actual extract** recovered if the OG of the sweet wort is **1.050**. - (iv) The **brewhouse efficiency** in %. Assume $C_{p,w} = 4.19$ kJ/kg·K and $C_{p,g} = 1.7$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Mashing Process (7 marks):** 1. **Definition & Objective:** Mashing is the controlled enzymatic conversion of malt starch into fermentable sugars using brewing water. (1 mark) 2. **Mash-in:** Strike water at calculated $T_s$ mixed with grist to reach $T_m = 50 \, ^\circ C$. (1 mark) 3. **Temperature Rests:** Explanation of acid (38 °C), protein (52 °C), saccharification (65 °C), and mash-out (78 °C) rests with their respective enzyme actions. (3 marks) 4. **Flowchart & End Point:** Mash held until iodine test shows no residual starch; transferred to lauter tun. (1 mark) 5. **Outcome:** Sweet wort with OG 1.040 – 1.055. (1 mark) **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Strike Water Temperature:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_s &= T_m + \dfrac{C_{p,g}}{R \cdot C_{p,w}} \cdot (T_m - T_g) \\ &= 50 + \dfrac{1.7}{3.0 \times 4.19} \times (50 - 22) \\ &= 50 + \dfrac{1.7}{12.57} \times 28 \\ &= 50 + 0.1353 \times 28 \\ &= 50 + 3.79 \\ T_s &\approx 53.8 \, ^\circ C \end{aligned} $$ *(2 marks: formula + calculation)* **(ii) Mash Volume:** $$ V = 100 \times 3.0 = 300 \, \text{L} $$ *(1 mark)* **(iii) Actual Extract Recovered:** For $OG = 1.050$, $^\circ P \approx 12.4 ^\circ P$ (using Plato-SG table). $$ E_{actual} = \dfrac{^\circ P \times V \times \rho}{1000 \times 10} \text{ in kg...} $$ Using simplified: $E_{actual} = (OG - 1) \times 1000 \times V$ in g, but for malt basis: $$ E_{actual} = 12.4 \times \dfrac{300}{100} \times \dfrac{1.050}{1} \approx 39.06 \, \text{kg} $$ *(2 marks)* **(iv) Brewhouse Efficiency:** $$ \eta_B = \dfrac{39.06}{80} \times 100 = 48.8 \, \% $$ *(2 marks)* > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Most Students Lose Marks Here:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** between g and kg in the extract calculation. > 2. **Misapplying the strike water formula** by inverting the ratio. > 3. **Confusing Original Gravity (OG) with Final Gravity (FG)** — FG is post-fermentation, NOT in the mashing stage. > 4. **Skipping the °P ↔ SG conversion** — the polynomial relation is required, not a guess. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** **(a)** Explain the **lautering process** in sweet wort preparation. Discuss the role of the **grain bed, sparging, and vorlauf recirculation** in achieving wort clarity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A brewery operates a **plate heat exchanger (PHE)** to cool **5000 kg/h of wort** from **95 °C to 20 °C** using cooling water entering at **5 °C** and exiting at **70 °C** (counter-current flow). Calculate: - (i) The **Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**. - (ii) The **heat duty (Q)** in kW. - (iii) The **required heat transfer area** if $U = 2500$ W/m²·K. Assume $C_{p,wort} = 4.05$ kJ/kg·K (since wort is slightly less than pure water due to extract). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Lautering (7 marks):** 1. **Definition:** Lautering is the separation of sweet wort from spent grains using a filter bed. (1 mark) 2. **Grain Bed:** Husk-rich bed (~40 cm) acts as a depth filter; correct gap setting on mill is critical. (2 marks) 3. **Vorlauf Recirculation:** First cloudy wort (high in grain particles, fat, protein) is returned until clarity < 50 NTU, ensuring yeast nutrients are preserved. (2 marks) 4. **Sparging:** Hot water (76 – 78 °C) is sprayed over the bed in 2 – 3 cycles to extract residual sugars; endpoint when runoff reaches 1.010. (1.5 marks) 5. **End Criteria:** pH 5.2 – 5.4, OG target achieved. (0.5 mark) **Part (b) — Heat Exchanger Calculation (7 marks):** **(i) LMTD:** - $\Delta T_1 = 95 - 70 = 25 \, ^\circ C$ - $\Delta T_2 = 20 - 5 = 15 \, ^\circ C$ $$ \begin{aligned} LMTD &= \dfrac{25 - 15}{\ln(25 / 15)} = \dfrac{10}{\ln(1.667)} \\ &= \dfrac{10}{0.5108} = 19.58 \, ^\circ C \end{aligned} $$ *(2.5 marks)* **(ii) Heat Duty Q:** $$ Q = \dot{m} \times C_p \times \Delta T = \dfrac{5000}{3600} \times 4.05 \times 75 = 1.389 \times 4.05 \times 75 = 421.9 \, \text{kW} $$ *(2 marks)* **(iii) Heat Transfer Area:** $$ A = \dfrac{Q}{U \times LMTD} = \dfrac{421{,}900 \, \text{W}}{2500 \times 19.58} = \dfrac{421{,}900}{48{,}950} = 8.62 \, \text{m}^2 $$ *(2.5 marks)* > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Heat Exchanger:** > 1. **Sign convention error:** Always use magnitudes for LMTD; never plug in negative ΔT. > 2. **Units trap:** $\dot{m}$ must be in **kg/s**, not kg/h. Divide by 3600. > 3. **Co-current vs Counter-current:** The formula for LMTD is identical, but **ΔT₁ and ΔT₂ assignments differ**; check flow direction carefully. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (for the night before KTU Exam)** ### ✅ Core Process Steps - [x] **Milling** → husk intact, endosperm crushed (gap 0.5 – 0.7 mm) - [x] **Mashing** → enzymatic conversion of starch to sugars - [x] **Lautering** → solid–liquid separation via grain bed filter - [x] **Boiling with hops** → 60 – 90 min, sterilization, isomerization - [x] **Whirlpool** → trub removal via centrifugal settling - [x] **Cooling & Aeration** → PHE from 95 °C to 20 °C, DO 6 – 8 mg/L ### ✅ Critical Temperatures (Mnemonic: "**38-52-65-78**") - 38 °C → Acid Rest - 52 °C → Protein Rest - 65 °C → Saccharification (β-amylase peak) - 78 °C → Mash-out ### ✅ Critical Parameters - Liquor-to-grist ratio: **2.5 : 1 to 3.5 : 1** - Strike water temperature: **~54 °C** (approx. = $T_m$ + 4) - Original Gravity (OG): **1.040 – 1.055** - Wort pH: **5.2 – 5.6** - Boil duration: **60 – 90 min** - Lauter bed depth: **30 – 50 cm** - Wort turbidity (post-Vorlauf): **< 50 NTU** - Iso-α-acid utilization: **~30 %** of added α-acids - FAN (Free Amino Nitrogen): **150 – 220 mg/L** ### ✅ Key Formulas (Top 4 to Memorize) - [x] $T_s = T_m + \dfrac{C_{p,g}}{R \cdot C_{p,w}} \cdot (T_m - T_g)$ - [x] $E_{actual} = (^\circ P) \times V \times \rho \times 10^{-2}$ in kg - [x] $\eta_B = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{theoretical}} \times 100$ - [x] $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \times LMTD}$, with $LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ ### ✅ Common Student Mistakes - ❌ Confusing **liquor** (brewing water) with **wort** (extracted liquid) - ❌ Stating mash-out enzyme activity (it DEACTIVATES enzymes, doesn't activate) - ❌ Using OG = 1.050 without noting it is **dimensionless** (relative to water) - ❌ Forgetting that **α-amylase peaks at higher temps** than β-amylase - ❌ Mixing up **lautering** (filtration) with **mashing** (extraction) ### ✅ Examiner's Quick Tip > Always draw a **neat flowchart** for any 7-mark process question in KTU 2024 scheme. It directly fetches **1.5 – 2 marks** and demonstrates systematic understanding, satisfying the **CO2 → "Apply & Analyze"** descriptor. ---

topics.title: Brewing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Brewing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Brewing** is the biochemical and physico-chemical production of fermented alcoholic beverages — primarily **beer** — from cereal grains (chiefly malted *Hordeum vulgare* L., i.e., barley), through the controlled enzymatic conversion of starch into fermentable sugars, followed by yeast-mediated (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae* or *S. carlsbergensis*) anaerobic fermentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEFTT632):** Brewing is defined as the integrated unit operation sequence comprising *malting → milling → mashing → lautering → wort boiling → hop addition → cooling → fermentation → maturation → packaging*, transforming raw cereal into a finished carbonated alcoholic beverage of standardized ethanol (typically **3.5%–8% v/v**), bitterness (typically **15–50 IBU**), and color (**8–40 EBC**).

The whole process is governed by three core biochemical reactions:
1. **Starch Hydrolysis**: $\text{Starch} \xrightarrow{\text{Amylase}} \text{Maltose + Dextrins}$
2. **Hop Isomerization**: $\alpha\text{-acids} \xrightarrow{90^\circ C,\ \text{time}} \text{iso-}\alpha\text{-acids (bittering compounds)}$
3. **Alcoholic Fermentation**: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}} 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine brewing as **"making tea, but with extra homework."**
> - You start with hard, dry grain seeds (like un-popped popcorn).
> - You wake them up (germinate) so they grow tiny roots and start producing sugar-converting enzymes.
> - You crush and soak them in warm water (the "mash") — this is like steeping tea, but the warm water activates enzymes that **chop long starch chains into sweet sugar molecules**.
> - You drain off the sweet liquid ("wort"), boil it with hops (the bitter, flowery spice), cool it, and pitch yeast.
> - The yeast "eats" the sugar and "exhales" alcohol and bubbles — like tiny bakers working overnight.
> - You then age, carbonate, and bottle the result.

The **four mandatory raw materials** of classical brewing are summarized in a memorable phrase:

> [!TIP]
> **"MASH – HOP – YEAST – WATER"**
> (German purity law *Reinheitsgebot* of 1516 insists only barley malt, hops, water, and yeast are used — a cornerstone of brewing chemistry even today.)

| Raw Material | Classical Role | Quantitative Spec |
|---|---|---|
| **Malted Barley** | Source of starch + enzymes (amylases, proteases) | Moisture **4–6%**, extract **≥ 80%** |
| **Hops (*Humulus lupulus*)** | Bittering, aroma, antiseptic | α-acids **3–15% w/w** |
| **Water** | Solvent, mineral buffer | Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ typically **50–250 ppm** |
| **Yeast** | Fermentation biocatalyst | Pitch rate **0.5–1.0 g/L** |

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Brewing (Industry Norms)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Range | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Original Gravity | OG | **1.040 – 1.060** | Sugar content pre-fermentation |
| Final Gravity | FG | **1.008 – 1.015** | Residual sugar post-fermentation |
| Apparent Attenuation | AA | **65 – 80 %** | Sugar consumed by yeast |
| Alcohol by Volume | ABV | **3.5 – 8.0 %** | Ethanol concentration |
| International Bitterness Units | IBU | **15 – 50** | Perceived bitterness |
| European Brewery Convention Color | EBC | **8 – 40** | Visual beer color |
| pH of Wort | — | **5.2 – 5.6** | Acidic optimum for enzymes |
| Pitching Temperature | — | **18 – 24 °C** | Yeast activity optimum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU exam question on Brewing expects you to convert between **Gravity → Plato → ABV**. Master the formulas in SECTION 2 before attempting numericals.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Sequential Unit Operations of Brewing

#### **Stage 1 — Malting** (controlled germination of barley)
- **Objective:** Mobilize endogenous enzymes (especially α- and β-amylase) inside the grain.
- **Steps:**
  1. **Steeping**: Soak barley in water at **12–18 °C** for 48–72 h (alternating wet/dry cycles) until moisture reaches **42–46%**.
  2. **Germination**: Grain is spread on a malting floor or in a *Saladin box* for 4–6 days at **15–20 °C**; the acrospire grows to **¾ of kernel length**.
  3. **Kilning**: Heat treatment at **50–85 °C** (pale malt) up to **100–220 °C** (roasted/crystal malts) to stop germination and develop color/flavor via the **Maillard reaction**.

#### **Stage 2 — Milling**
- Goal: Crush malt to expose **grist** of optimal husk/grit/flour ratio (typically **25/65/10**).
- Equipment: **Roller mill** (2-roll or 6-roll).

#### **Stage 3 — Mashing** (enzymatic starch conversion)
- Milled grist is mixed with hot water (**mash liquor**) at a **mash-in ratio** of roughly **2.5–3.5 L water per kg grist**.
- Temperature rest program (classical *infusion mash*):
  - **45–50 °C** → β-glucan and protein rest (proteolysis)
  - **62–67 °C** → β-amylase optimum → produces **maltose** (fermentable)
  - **70–75 °C** → α-amylase optimum → produces **dextrins** (unfermentable, body)
  - **78 °C** → mash-out (terminate enzymes)

#### **Stage 4 — Lautering**
- Separation of sweet liquid **wort** from spent grains using a **lauter tun** (false bottom filter) or **mash filter**.
- Sparging with **75–78 °C** water recovers remaining sugars.

#### **Stage 5 — Wort Boiling with Hops**
- Boil wort for **60–90 min** at atmospheric pressure.
- Hop additions follow a **timed schedule**:
  - **Bittering hops**: added at start → **60 min** boil → high isomerization
  - **Flavor hops**: middle of boil → **15–20 min**
  - **Aroma hops**: end of boil → **0–5 min** (or *whirlpool* / *dry hopping*)
- Hop utilization rate **U** is typically **25–35%** in kettle.

#### **Stage 6 — Cooling & Whirlpool**
- Hot wort cooled rapidly to **18–24 °C** via **plate heat exchanger**.
- Trub (protein-polyphenol sludge) removed in a **whirlpool** or *centrifuge*.

#### **Stage 7 — Fermentation**
- Yeast pitched; held **3–7 days**.
- Two metabolic phases: **aerobic respiration (first 6–12 h)** then **anaerobic fermentation**.
- **Top-fermenting** (*S. cerevisiae*) at **18–24 °C** → ales, stouts.
- **Bottom-fermenting** (*S. carlsbergensis / uvarum*) at **8–15 °C** → lagers.

#### **Stage 8 — Maturation, Carbonation & Packaging**
- Lager beer cold-conditioned at **0–2 °C** for **2–8 weeks** (*lagering*).
- Carbonation to **2.5–3.0 v/v CO₂**.
- Packaged in bottles, cans, or kegs after **flash pasteurization (72 °C / 15 s)** or sterile filtration.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-yield numerical formulas** for Module-2 Brewing questions. Memorize these units carefully.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ABV \approx \dfrac{OG - FG}{0.00736}$ | Simplified alcohol estimation from gravity drop | OG, FG dimensionless (e.g., 1.050, 1.012) |
| 2 | $ABV \approx (OG - FG) \times 131.25$ | Classical Balling-style coefficient | % v/v |
| 3 | $\text{Real Extract} \approx 0.1808 \cdot OG + 0.8192 \cdot FG$ | Plato-based residual sugar | °P |
| 4 | $\text{Apparent Attenuation}\ AA = \dfrac{OG - FG}{OG - 1} \times 100$ | % sugar consumed | % |
| 5 | $\text{Plato}^\circ P \approx (OG - 1) \times 1000$ | Specific gravity → Plato degrees | °P (g sucrose / 100 g wort) |
| 6 | $\text{Hop Utilization}\ U = \dfrac{\text{iso-}\alpha\text{-acids in beer}}{\text{Total }\alpha\text{-acids added}}$ | Boil conversion efficiency | typically 25–35% |
| 7 | $\text{Mash Efficiency} = \dfrac{\text{Extract in wort}}{\text{Extract potential of malt}} \times 100$ | Brewhouse yield | typically 75–85% |
| 8 | $\text{Malt Moisture Adj.} = M_{dry} = M_{wet} \times (1 - m)$ | Reporting malt on dry basis | $m$ = fractional moisture |
| 9 | $\text{CO}_2 \text{ from fermentation} = \dfrac{OG - FG}{1.5} \times 100$ | Volumes of CO₂ naturally produced | v/v |
| 10 | $\text{Diastatic Power (DP)} = {}^\circ L$ | Enzyme strength of malt | typically 30–120 °Lintner |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lintner degrees (°L)** — One °Lintner is the amount of enzyme that converts 0.1 mL of 2% soluble starch to dextrin in 1 h at 30 °C. A standard malt should have **DP ≥ 30 °L** for self-conversion; below this, **adjunct enzymes** must be added.

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of Brewing Principles

- **Mass Balance Application**: Every brewer computes extract yield $E = \dfrac{SG_{wort} \times V_{wort} \times \eta}{m_{malt}}$ to predict brewhouse capacity.
- **Heat Integration**: Wort boiling uses **vapor recompression** and **whirlpool** tech to save **30–40%** thermal energy.
- **Quality Control**: ABV, IBU, color, pH, and turbidity are all *in-line* measured sensors for ISO 9001 breweries.
- **By-product Engineering**: Spent grains (~20 kg per 100 L beer) become animal feed; CO₂ recovered for carbonation; surplus yeast → β-glucan extraction.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Alcohol by Volume (ABV) from Gravity Drop

We derive the **classical brewing formula** $ABV \approx (OG - FG) \times 131.25$, as expected in KTU 14-mark numerical questions.

#### Step 1 — Start from stoichiometry
Yeast fermentation of glucose under anaerobic conditions:

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2
$$

Molar masses (g/mol):
- $C_6H_{12}O_6 = 180$
- $C_2H_5OH = 46$
- $CO_2 = 44$

So **180 g glucose** theoretically yields **92 g ethanol** = **51.1% by mass**.

#### Step 2 — Translate gravity to sugar mass
Specific gravity of wort tells us dissolved solids. Approximate:

$$
\text{Sugar mass per litre of wort} \approx 1000 \times (OG - 1) \quad [\text{g/L}]
$$

For OG = 1.050, this gives **50 g/L** dissolved solids (≈5% w/w), close to **5 °Plato**.

#### Step 3 — Convert sugar consumed to ethanol produced
Let $\Delta G = OG - FG$ be the gravity drop. Sugar fermented per litre = $1000 \cdot \Delta G$ g/L. Theoretical ethanol mass = $1000 \cdot \Delta G \times 0.511$ g/L.

#### Step 4 — Convert ethanol mass → volume → % ABV
Ethanol density = **0.789 g/mL**. Volume of ethanol per litre beer:

$$
V_{EtOH} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot \Delta G \times 0.511}{0.789} \quad [\text{mL/L}]
$$

Numerical simplification:

$$
V_{EtOH} = \Delta G \times \dfrac{1000 \times 0.511}{0.789} = \Delta G \times 647.7 \quad [\text{mL/L}]
$$

Since 1 mL/L = 0.1% v/v:

$$
ABV = \Delta G \times 64.77 \quad [\% v/v]
$$

#### Step 5 — Empirical correction
Actual yeast yield is **~50%** of stoichiometric (energy lost as biomass, heat, by-products like higher alcohols). Empirical coefficient:

$$
ABV = \Delta G \times 131.25 \quad [\% v/v]
$$

which absorbs both the stoichiometric factor and the empirical 0.5 efficiency factor.

#### Step 6 — Verification
Take OG = 1.055, FG = 1.013 → ΔG = 0.042

$$
ABV = 0.042 \times 131.25 = 5.51\% \quad \blacksquare
$$

The same beer via simplified formula:

$$
ABV = \dfrac{0.042}{0.00736} = 5.71\%
$$

(Both fall in the realistic pale-ale range **5.0–5.8% ABV**.)

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Apparent Attenuation

Apparent attenuation $AA$ is the fraction of dissolved solids that disappear during fermentation. From definition:

$$
AA = \dfrac{\text{Initial sugar mass} - \text{Final sugar mass}}{\text{Initial sugar mass}}
$$

Substituting the linear gravity ↔ sugar relationship $S \propto (G - 1)$:

$$
AA = \dfrac{(OG - 1) - (FG - 1)}{OG - 1} = \dfrac{OG - FG}{OG - 1}
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
\boxed{AA\ [\%] = \dfrac{OG - FG}{OG - 1} \times 100}
$$

**Verification with OG = 1.050, FG = 1.010**:

$$
AA = \dfrac{1.050 - 1.010}{1.050 - 1} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.040}{0.050} \times 100 = 80\% \quad \blacksquare
$$

This means **80% of dissolved solids were fermented** — typical for a *well-attenuated* pale ale.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style

> **Question:** A brewery produces a lager with **Original Gravity OG = 1.048** and **Final Gravity FG = 1.012**. The mash used **180 kg malt** and produced **900 L wort**. Hops added = **1.8 kg** at **6% α-acids**, with kettle utilization **30%**. Compute:
> (a) Apparent attenuation
> (b) % ABV
> (c) IBU of finished beer
> (d) Brew-house extract efficiency, given malt extract potential **= 80%**

#### (a) Apparent attenuation

$$
AA = \dfrac{1.048 - 1.012}{1.048 - 1} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.036}{0.048} \times 100 = 75\%
$$

#### (b) % ABV

$$
ABV = (1.048 - 1.012) \times 131.25 = 0.036 \times 131.25 = 4.725\%
$$

#### (c) IBU

Iso-α-acids in beer = (α-acids) × utilization = $1.8 \times 0.06 \times 0.30$ kg = **0.0324 kg = 32.4 g** in 900 L → **32.4 / 900 × 1000 = 36 mg/L** → **36 IBU**.

#### (d) Extract efficiency

Wort solids (kg) = $0.048 \times 900 = 43.2$ kg. Malt extract potential = $180 \times 0.80 = 144$ kg.

$$
\text{Efficiency} = \dfrac{43.2}{144} \times 100 = 30\% \quad \text{(note: discrepancy implies under-mashing — investigate lauter losses)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, brewhouse efficiencies of **75–85%** are expected. A computed 30% indicates either measurement error or operational problem — useful learning point for an examiner's follow-up.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ale vs Lager

| Parameter | **Ale** | **Lager** |
|---|---|---|
| Yeast strain | *S. cerevisiae* | *S. carlsbergensis / uvarum* |
| Fermentation temp | **18 – 24 °C** | **8 – 15 °C** |
| Fermentation time | **3 – 7 days** | **7 – 14 days** |
| Maturation | Brief, warm | Cold conditioning **2 – 8 weeks** at **0 – 2 °C** |
| Esters / fusel oils | High (fruity) | Low (clean) |
| Color (EBC) | **15 – 40** | **6 – 20** |
| IBU | **20 – 50** | **15 – 35** |
| Examples | Pale Ale, Stout, Porter | Pilsner, Märzen, Bock |

### 4.2 Process Logic Notes (for Diagram)

- **Solid flow** (yellow nodes): Raw barley progresses through 18 sequential transformations.
- **Blue quality-control nodes**: Critical control points (CCPs) align with **HACCP** standards for ISO-certified breweries.
- **Green by-product nodes**: Zero-discharge philosophy — spent grains, recovered CO₂, and surplus yeast are valorized streams.
- **Critical numbers to remember**:
  - Mash pH optimum **5.2 – 5.5** → achieved via Ca²⁺ adjustment (acid rest).
  - Boil vigor ≈ **8–12% evaporation** to drive off DMS (dimethyl sulfide).
  - Cooling gradient must drop **< 30 min** from 95 °C → 20 °C to avoid *cold break* losses.
