## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define mashing and state the temperature rest schedule used in a classical infusion mash.**
*(Model Answer, 3 marks)*

Mashing is the enzymatic hydrolysis stage where milled malt is steeped in heated water to convert insoluble starch into soluble fermentable sugars and dextrins. The classical temperature rests are:

- **45 – 50 °C** → β-glucan / acid rest
- **50 – 55 °C** → proteolytic rest
- **62 – 67 °C** → β-amylase optimum (saccharification)
- **70 – 75 °C** → α-amylase optimum (dextrinization)
- **78 °C** → mash-out to terminate enzyme activity.

The optimum pH throughout is **5.2 – 5.5** to maximize amylase activity.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between ale and lager with respect to yeast type, fermentation temperature, and maturation time.**
*(Model Answer, 3 marks)*

| Parameter | **Ale** | **Lager** |
|---|---|---|
| Yeast | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (top-fermenting) | *Saccharomyces carlsbergensis / uvarum* (bottom-fermenting) |
| Fermentation temperature | **18 – 24 °C** | **8 – 15 °C** |
| Maturation time | Short, **1 – 2 weeks** at cellar temperature | Extended, **2 – 8 weeks** at **0 – 2 °C** (cold conditioning) |

Ales show higher ester content (fruity), while lagers are cleaner with lower fusel alcohols.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Model, May 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **unit operations involved in the brewing of beer** starting from barley. Mention the role of each step in the overall process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brewery brews a batch with **OG = 1.052** and **FG = 1.010**. Calculate:
   (i) Apparent attenuation
   (ii) Alcohol by Volume (% ABV)
   (iii) Real extract in °Plato
   **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Flowchart and description of brewing unit operations:**

The brewing process consists of eight sequential stages (see SECTION 4 flow diagram). Each is summarized below:

1. **Malting** (Steeping → Germination → Kilning): Converts raw barley into *green malt* with active enzymes (amylases) and flavor precursors.
2. **Milling**: Crushes malt into grist for efficient enzymatic attack.
3. **Mashing**: At **62 – 67 °C**, β-amylase hydrolyzes starch into maltose; at **70 – 75 °C**, α-amylase produces dextrins.
4. **Lautering**: Filtration through husk bed yields clear sweet **wort**.
5. **Wort boiling with hops** (60 – 90 min): Hop α-acids isomerize to bitter iso-α-acids; DMS driven off; proteins coagulated.
6. **Cooling & Whirlpool**: Heat-exchanger cools wort to pitching temperature; trub removed.
7. **Fermentation**: Yeast converts sugars to ethanol and CO₂ over 3 – 14 days.
8. **Maturation, filtration, carbonation & packaging**: Beer is lagered at 0 – 2 °C, carbonated to 2.5 – 3.0 v/v, pasteurized and bottled/canned/kegged.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for the flowchart; 3 marks for roles; 2 marks for temperatures / parameters.)*

---

**(b) Numerical computations:**

**Given:** OG = 1.052, FG = 1.010

**(i) Apparent Attenuation:**

$$
AA = \dfrac{OG - FG}{OG - 1} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.052 - 1.010}{1.052 - 1} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.042}{0.052} \times 100 = 80.77\%
$$

**(ii) Alcohol by Volume:**

Method 1 (Balling coefficient):

$$
ABV = (OG - FG) \times 131.25 = 0.042 \times 131.25 = 5.51\%
$$

Method 2 (Simplified):

$$
ABV = \dfrac{0.042}{0.00736} = 5.71\%
$$

**Report ABV ≈ 5.5 % v/v** (standard rounding).

**(iii) Real Extract in °Plato:**

$$
\text{Real Extract} = 0.1808 \times OG + 0.8192 \times FG
$$

Substituting:

$$
= 0.1808 \times 1.052 + 0.8192 \times 1.010
$$

$$
= 0.1902 + 0.8274 = 1.0176
$$

Converted to °Plato:

$$
\text{Real Extract} = (1.0176 - 1) \times 1000 = 17.6\ ^\circ P
$$

*(Allocate 2 marks for each sub-part: setup, formula, substitution, final answer.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **process of malting** of barley. Discuss the biochemical changes occurring during steeping, germination, and kilning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A lager brewery adds **2.4 kg hops** containing **7% α-acids** to a kettle with **1000 L wort**. Boil time is **90 min** and kettle utilization is **32%**. The brew is then fermented, dropping from OG 1.050 to FG 1.012.
   (i) Calculate the **IBU** of the finished beer.
   (ii) Calculate the **% ABV**.
   (iii) Estimate the **natural carbonation (v/v CO₂)** produced.
   **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Detailed malting process and biochemistry:**

*Steeping:* Barley is submerged in water at **12 – 18 °C** for 48 – 72 h using *wet-dry cycles*. Grain moisture rises from **12%** to **42 – 46%**. This triggers **gibberellin** synthesis in the embryo, which mobilizes enzymes.

*Germination:* At **15 – 20 °C** for 4 – 6 days, the *acrospire* grows under the husk. Enzymes activated:
- **α-amylase** — hydrolyzes α-1,4 bonds randomly → dextrins
- **β-amylase** — hydrolyzes from non-reducing end → maltose
- **Proteases** — degrade hordeins into amino acids
- **β-glucanases** — degrade cell walls
- **Cytolytic enzymes** — improve filterability later

Starch reserves begin **partial modification**; the grain becomes *chitable*.

*Kilning:* Gradual heating from **50 °C → 85 °C** (pale malt) or up to **100 – 220 °C** (specialty malts). Goals:
1. Stop germination
2. Reduce moisture from **42% → 4–6%** for storage
3. Develop flavor via **Maillard reaction** between reducing sugars and amino acids → melanoidins (color/aroma)
4. Preserve enzyme activity (low-temperature kilning) or destroy (high-temp roasted)

---

**(b) Numerical computations:**

**(i) IBU:**

Iso-α-acids produced = $2.4 \times 0.07 \times 0.32 = 0.05376$ kg = **53.76 g**

Concentration in beer:

$$
IBU = \dfrac{53.76}{1000} \times 1000 = 53.76\ \text{mg/L} \approx 54\ IBU
$$

**(ii) % ABV:**

$$
\Delta G = 1.050 - 1.012 = 0.038
$$

$$
ABV = 0.038 \times 131.25 = 4.99\%\ \text{v/v}
$$

**(iii) Natural CO₂ (v/v):**

$$
CO_2\ (v/v) \approx \dfrac{OG - FG}{1.5} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.038}{1.5} \times 100 = 2.53\ v/v
$$

This is just below the typical target **2.5 – 3.0 v/v** for lagers, so a small amount of *forced carbonation* would be required (≈ 0.5 v/v additional CO₂).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-losing mistakes in Brewing questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing OG / FG with Plato.** Plato is degrees Plato ($^\circ P$); gravity is dimensionless. Always convert: $^\circ P = (OG - 1) \times 1000$.
> 2. **Forgetting utilization factor in IBU.** Students often write $IBU = mg/L\ \alpha\text{-acids}$ and miss that only **25–35%** isomerize.
> 3. **Wrong mash temperature order.** Examiners expect the order: **Protein rest (50 °C) → β-amylase (65 °C) → α-amylase (72 °C) → Mash-out (78 °C)**. Reversed order loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing yeast types.** Ales use *S. cerevisiae*, lagers use *S. carlsbergensis / uvarum*. Confusing these → −1 mark.
> 5. **No mention of hops addition timing.** Examiner expects three timed additions: bittering (60 min), flavor (15 min), aroma (whirlpool/dry hop).
> 6. **Wrong ABV coefficient.** Use **131.25** (classical) or **1 / 0.00736 = 135.87**. Don't fabricate any other constant.
> 7. **Skipping pH.** Mashing pH **5.2–5.5** is a directly-marked specification.
> 8. **Wrong unit on IBU.** Always report **mg/L of iso-α-acids** (numerically equal to IBU).
> 9. **No mention of carbonation natural vs forced.** Examiners often test this distinction.
> 10. **Ignoring by-product streams.** Modern questions are increasingly integrative; mention *spent grains*, *CO₂ recovery*, and *surplus yeast valorization*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Brewing**

- ☐ Brewing = integrated biochemical process converting cereal starch → beer via **malting → mashing → fermentation**.
- ☐ **Malt** is the soul of beer; provides enzymes + extract.
- ☐ **Mashing rests**: 50 °C (protein) → 65 °C (β-amy) → 72 °C (α-amy) → 78 °C (mash-out).
- ☐ **Lautering** = filtration; sparge water at **75 – 78 °C**.
- ☐ **Wort boil** = **60 – 90 min** with hops; α-acids → iso-α-acids (bittering).
- ☐ **Hops schedule**: bittering (start), flavor (mid), aroma (end / whirlpool / dry-hop).
- ☐ **Fermentation**: yeast + sugar → ethanol + CO₂; ales at 18 – 24 °C, lagers at 8 – 15 °C.
- ☐ **Lagering**: cold conditioning at **0 – 2 °C for 2 – 8 weeks**.
- ☐ **OG** = pre-fermentation gravity; **FG** = post-fermentation gravity.
- ☐ **ABV ≈ (OG − FG) × 131.25**.
- ☐ **Apparent Attenuation** AA = [(OG − FG) / (OG − 1)] × 100.
- ☐ **Plato (°P)** = (OG − 1) × 1000.
- ☐ **IBU** = α-acid mass × utilization rate / beer volume.
- ☐ **Mash pH optimum** = **5.2 – 5.5**.
- ☐ **Diastatic Power (DP)** ≥ **30 °Lintner** for self-converting malt.
- ☐ **Beer carbonation** = **2.5 – 3.0 v/v CO₂** (mostly from fermentation + small forced top-up).
- ☐ **Reinheitsgebot (1516)** = only barley, hops, water, yeast.
- ☐ **By-products**: spent grains (feed), recovered CO₂, surplus yeast (β-glucan).
- ☐ **Quality metrics**: ABV, IBU, EBC color, pH, turbidity, FG.
- ☐ **Yeast strains**: *S. cerevisiae* (ale), *S. carlsbergensis / uvarum* (lager).
- ☐ **Heat treatment**: flash pasteurization **72 °C / 15 s** OR sterile membrane filtration.

> **End of KTU Module 2 — Brewing Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fermentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fermentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Fermentation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fermentation** is an **anaerobic catabolic biochemical process** in which organic substrates — most commonly carbohydrates such as glucose, fructose, sucrose, maltose, and starch-derived sugars — are enzymatically converted by microorganisms (yeasts, bacteria, or molds) into alcohols, organic acids, gases (primarily carbon dioxide), or other end-products, with the concurrent regeneration of limited quantities of ATP (adenosine triphosphate) through **substrate-level phosphorylation**.

In the strict biochemical sense (Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas pathway context), fermentation is defined as:

> *"The sequence of enzyme-catalyzed reactions by which the pyruvate produced from glycolysis is converted into various end-products (ethanol, lactate, acetate, etc.) in the absence of an external electron acceptor, with NADH re-oxidation to NAD⁺ as the central thermodynamic driver."*

For the KTU Beverage Processing course, fermentation is contextualized as the **central unit operation in the production of alcoholic beverages (beer, wine, cider, spirits), fermented dairy beverages (kefir, kumiss), vinegar, and certain functional non-alcoholic beverages**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> "Fermentation is a metabolic process in which microorganisms derive energy from carbohydrates in the absence of oxygen, producing ethanol, lactic acid, acetic acid, CO₂, and other metabolites that directly determine the sensory, nutritional, and shelf-life properties of beverages."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a small kitchen where a chef (**yeast/bacteria**) is given a pile of sugar cubes (**glucose**). The chef cannot go outside to buy firewood (**oxygen**) to burn the sugar efficiently into pure energy and water. Instead, the chef *rearranges* the sugar indoors, breaking it into smaller, easier-to-handle pieces — some becoming **alcohol**, some becoming **gas bubbles**, and a tiny amount of useful energy (ATP) for the chef to keep working.

The **alcohol** and **CO₂ bubbles** are the chef's "waste," but to us, those are exactly what we want — the alcohol that makes wine intoxicating, the bubbles that make beer fizzy, the tang that makes sourdough sharp.

This analogy captures the three essential truths of fermentation:
1. **No oxygen is needed** (anaerobic).
2. **Microbes are the engine** (yeasts for alcohol, bacteria for acid).
3. **The "waste" products are the beverage's prized flavors.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Fact Box**
> - **French chemist Louis Pasteur** (1857) is credited as the father of fermentation science ("*Zymurgie*" / zymology).
> - The word **"fermentation"** comes from Latin *fervere* = "to boil," referring to the bubbling caused by CO₂ escaping from fermenting must/wort.
> - The standard temperature for *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*-driven alcoholic fermentation is **18–25 °C**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Units and Metrics Used in Fermentation Studies

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range in Beverages |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific gravity | SG | Dimensionless | 1.000–1.120 |
| Brix (sugar content) | °Bx | % (w/w sucrose) | 5–25 °Bx |
| Potential alcohol | PA | % ABV | 4–14 % ABV |
| pH | pH | Dimensionless | 3.0–4.5 |
| Titratable acidity | TA | g/L (as tartaric) | 4–12 g/L |
| Fermentation temperature | T | °C | 10–30 °C |
| Yeast cell count | — | cells/mL | 10⁶–10⁸ cells/mL |
| CO₂ evolution rate | — | g/L·h | 0.5–2.5 g/L·h |

> [!TIP]
> The **Brix-to-approximate-ABV conversion rule** used by KTU examiners: *Potential Alcohol (% v/v) ≈ °Brix × 0.55 to 0.65* (for wine musts).

---

## 1.4 Why Fermentation is the "Heart" of Beverage Processing

Every alcoholic beverage in human history — from Neolithic mead (≈ 7000 BCE) to modern craft IPAs — depends on microbial fermentation. In the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2), fermentation is treated as the **operational bridge** between raw-material preparation (Module 1) and downstream operations like aging, filtration, and packaging (Module 3).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fermentation in Beverages

Fermentation in the beverage industry is broadly classified into **three principal pathways**, each producing distinct end-products:

### A. Alcoholic (Ethanolic) Fermentation
- **Equation (net, classical):**

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + 2\,ATP \;(+ \text{heat})
$$

- **End products:** Ethanol (C₂H₅OH), Carbon dioxide (CO₂), and minor higher alcohols (fusel oils), esters, aldehydes.
- **Primary microorganism:** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (ale/wine yeast), *Saccharomyces pastorianus* (lager yeast).
- **Beverages:** Beer, Wine, Cider, Sake, Mead, Spirits (after distillation).

### B. Lactic Acid Fermentation
- **Equation (homo-lactic):**

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,CH_3CH(OH)COOH \;+\; 2\,ATP
$$

- **End products:** Lactic acid (CH₃CHOHCOOH), some CO₂.
- **Primary microorganisms:** *Lactobacillus*, *Lactococcus*, *Streptococcus thermophilus*.
- **Beverages:** Kefir, Kumiss, Acidophilus milk, certain sour beers (Berliner Weisse, Lambic), pickled vegetable juices.

### C. Acetic Acid Fermentation (Oxidative, two-step)
- **Two-stage biochemistry:**
  - Stage 1 (anaerobic): glucose → ethanol (via yeast)
  - Stage 2 (aerobic): ethanol → acetic acid (via *Acetobacter*)

$$
2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,CH_3COOH + 2\,H_2O
$$

- **End product:** Acetic acid (CH₃COOH).
- **Primary microorganism:** *Acetobacter aceti*, *Gluconobacter*.
- **Beverages:** Vinegar (cider, wine, malt, rice vinegars), Kombucha (co-fermentation with yeast + bacteria in SCOBY).

---

## 2.2 The Glycolytic Foundation — Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) Pathway

All fermentations begin with **glycolysis**, a ten-step cytosolic pathway that converts one molecule of glucose into two molecules of pyruvate:

$$
\text{Glucose} + 2\,NAD^+ + 2\,ADP + 2\,P_i \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{Pyruvate} + 2\,NADH + 2\,H^+ + 2\,ATP + 2\,H_2O
$$

The two molecules of NADH must be re-oxidized to NAD⁺ for glycolysis to continue. Since oxygen is absent, the cell uses pyruvate (or its derivatives) as the **internal electron acceptor**, generating ethanol or lactate as the reduced end-product. This is the very essence of fermentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** Fermentation does **not** produce ATP beyond glycolysis. The ATP yield is fixed at **2 ATP per glucose** (or 3 ATP per sucrose due to the extra phosphorylation step in sucrose utilization).

---

## 2.3 Stages of Industrial Alcoholic Fermentation (CSTR / Batch)

A typical yeast-driven beverage fermentation proceeds through **five distinct phases**:

1. **Lag Phase (0–6 h):** Cells adapt to medium; no significant metabolism. Inoculum size critically affects lag duration.
2. **Acceleration Phase (6–18 h):** Enzyme synthesis, exponential increase in cell number and CO₂ evolution.
3. **Exponential (Log) Phase (18–48 h):** Maximum fermentation rate; ~80 % of sugar consumed; CO₂ evolution peaks.
4. **Stationary / Declining Phase (48–96 h):** Nutrient depletion, ethanol accumulation inhibits yeast (>12–15 % v/v); cell viability drops.
5. **Clarity / Conditioning Phase (>96 h):** Yeast flocculation, sedimentation, autolysis releases minor flavor compounds.

---

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Fermentation (High-Yield for KTU)

| Factor | Optimum Range (Yeast) | Effect of Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 18–25 °C (ale), 7–13 °C (lager) | Off-flavors, stuck fermentation |
| pH | 3.5–4.5 | Below 3.0 → yeast inhibition; above 5.0 → bacterial contamination |
| Sugar concentration | 10–25 % w/v | >30 % → osmotic stress, plasmolysis |
| Ethanol tolerance | ≤ 15 % v/v | Above → membrane disruption, cell death |
| Oxygen (initial) | 5–10 mg/L dissolved O₂ | Excess → shifts to respiration; deficient → sluggish fermentation |
| Nutrient (N-source) | 150–250 mg/L FAN | Deficiency → sulfur off-flavors (H₂S) |

> [!NOTE]
> **FAN = Free Amino Nitrogen.** Diammonium phosphate (DAP) is commonly added to musts/worts deficient in FAN.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$ | Net alcoholic fermentation | Stoichiometric |
| 2 | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2CH_3CHOHCOOH$ | Homo-lactic fermentation | Stoichiometric |
| 3 | $2C_2H_5OH + 2O_2 \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2H_2O$ | Acetic fermentation | Stoichiometric |
| 4 | $\text{PA (ABV)} \approx 0.55 \text{ to } 0.65 \times °Bx$ | Brix to alcohol conversion | % v/v |
| 5 | $\eta_{ethanol} = \dfrac{m_{ethanol,\;actual}}{m_{ethanol,\;theoretical}} \times 100$ | Fermentation efficiency | % |
| 6 | $m_{ethanol,\;theor} = \dfrac{m_{sugar}}{180} \times 92$ | Theoretical ethanol from sugar | g |
| 7 | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{-\Delta S}$ | Biomass yield on substrate | g/g |
| 8 | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{-\Delta S}$ | Product yield on substrate | g/g |
| 9 | $q_{CO_2} = \dfrac{\Delta CO_2}{V \cdot \Delta t}$ | Volumetric CO₂ production rate | g/L·h |
| 10 | $\mu_{max} = \dfrac{\ln(N_f/N_0)}{t_f - t_0}$ | Maximum specific growth rate | h⁻¹ |
| 11 | $K_s = \dfrac{\mu_{max}\,S}{Y_{X/S}(1-\mu_{max}/\mu_{max})}$ | Half-saturation constant (Monod) | g/L |
| 12 | $\text{SG correction} = (1.000 - SG) \times 1312.5$ | Approx. % ABV (wine method) | % v/v |
| 13 | $\text{Pasteurization Unit (PU)} = 1.393^{(T-60)}$ | Lethality per minute at T °C | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Always specify whether ethanol yield is calculated on **mass basis (g/g)** or **volumetric basis (% v/v)**. Converting between them requires ethanol density = **0.789 g/mL** at 20 °C.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Brewing industry:** Predicting fermenter cooling load from heat of fermentation (≈ −92 kJ/mol glucose fermented).
- **Distillery design:** Theoretical alcohol yield (92 g ethanol per 180 g glucose = 51.1 % w/w, or 64.7 % v/v at 100 % efficiency).
- **Wine quality control:** Monitoring specific gravity drop (Brix → °Bé) to determine fermentation endpoint.
- **Vinegar production:** Aerobic submerged fermentation using *Acetobacter* in Frings-type acetators.
- **Functional beverages:** Probiotic fermented drinks using controlled LAB fermentation for health claims.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose (Gay-Lussac Equation)

The classical Gay-Lussac equation provides the stoichiometric foundation for ethanol yield calculations. We derive it step-by-step using conservation of atoms (C, H, O) and degree-of-balance verification.

### Step 1 — Write the unbalanced reaction.

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; C_2H_5OH + CO_2
$$

### Step 2 — Balance carbon atoms.
- Reactant side: 6 carbons (in glucose).
- Product side: 2 carbons (in ethanol) + 1 carbon (in CO₂) = 3 carbons.

To balance carbon, place a coefficient of **2** in front of both ethanol and CO₂:

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2
$$

### Step 3 — Verify hydrogen balance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS:} &\quad 12 \text{ H atoms (from } C_6H_{12}O_6) \\
\text{RHS:} &\quad 2 \times (6 \text{ H}) + 2 \times (0 \text{ H}) = 12 \text{ H atoms}
\end{aligned}
$$

✓ Hydrogen is balanced.

### Step 4 — Verify oxygen balance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS:} &\quad 6 \text{ O atoms} \\
\text{RHS:} &\quad 2 \times (1 \text{ O from } -OH) + 2 \times (2 \text{ O from } CO_2) = 6 \text{ O atoms}
\end{aligned}
$$

✓ Oxygen is balanced. The Gay-Lussac equation is fully balanced.

### Step 5 — Convert to mass basis using molar masses.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucose (M.W.)} &= 180.16 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{Ethanol (M.W.)} &= 46.07 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{CO}_2 \text{ (M.W.)} &= 44.01 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, per **1 mole** of glucose (180.16 g), theoretical ethanol produced = **2 × 46.07 = 92.14 g**.

The **theoretical mass yield** of ethanol from glucose is:

$$
Y_{P/S}^{theor} = \frac{92.14 \text{ g ethanol}}{180.16 \text{ g glucose}} = 0.5115 \;\frac{\text{g}}{\text{g}} \;=\; 51.15 \,\%
$$

### Step 6 — Convert mass yield to volumetric % ABV.

Assuming ethanol density $\rho_{ethanol} = 0.789 \text{ g/mL}$ at 20 °C:

$$
\text{ABV}_{theor} = \frac{92.14 \text{ g/mol} \times 0.789 \text{ mL/g}}{180.16 \text{ g/mol} \times 1 \text{ L/1000}} \times 100
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ABV}_{theor} &= \frac{72.70}{0.18016} \text{ mL ethanol per L glucose solution} \\
&\approx 403.5 \text{ mL ethanol per L glucose solution} \\
&\approx 40.35 \,\% \text{ v/v per 100 g/L glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

In beverage practice, since 1 °Bx ≈ 10 g/L sucrose (a close proxy), the **rule of thumb ABV = 0.55–0.65 × °Brix** is justified:

$$
\text{ABV}_{theor} \approx \frac{92}{180} \times \frac{0.789}{1.0} \times 100 \times \frac{°Bx}{100} \times 10 \approx 0.404 \times °Bx
$$

Adjusting for incomplete conversion (~85 %) and biomass loss (~5 %), the empirical conversion factor settles at **0.55–0.60**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fermentation Efficiency Calculation

A brewery ferments a wort with an initial Brix of **12 °Bx** in a 10,000 L fermenter. After 7 days, the apparent extract is **3 °Bx**, and the measured ethanol content is **5.2 % v/v**. Compute the fermentation efficiency.

### Step 1 — Determine the initial and final sugar mass.

Initial sugar concentration:

$$
S_0 = 12 \,°Bx = 120 \text{ g/L (approx.)}
$$

Final sugar concentration:

$$
S_f = 3 \,°Bx = 30 \text{ g/L (approx.)}
$$

Sugar consumed:

$$
\Delta S = 120 - 30 = 90 \text{ g/L}
$$

Total sugar consumed in 10,000 L:

$$
M_S = 90 \times 10{,}000 = 900{,}000 \text{ g} = 900 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute theoretical ethanol from sugar consumed.

$$
m_{ethanol,\;theor} = M_S \times \frac{92}{180} = 900{,}000 \times 0.5111 = 460{,}000 \text{ g} = 460 \text{ kg}
$$

In volume terms:

$$
V_{ethanol,\;theor} = \frac{460{,}000}{0.789} = 583{,}019 \text{ mL} = 583.0 \text{ L}
$$

In a 10,000 L fermenter, theoretical ABV:

$$
\text{ABV}_{theor} = \frac{583.0}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 5.83 \,\% \text{ v/v}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the fermentation efficiency.

$$
\eta_{ferm} = \frac{\text{ABV}_{actual}}{\text{ABV}_{theor}} \times 100 = \frac{5.2}{5.83} \times 100 = 89.2 \,\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> A **good commercial brewery** achieves 85–95 % fermentation efficiency. Below 80 % indicates problems: under-pitching, low FAN, temperature stress, or stuck fermentation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat of Fermentation — Cooling Load for Fermenter Design

The energy released during fermentation must be removed by cooling jackets/coils. The standard heat of fermentation of glucose to ethanol is:

$$
\Delta H_{rxn} = -92 \text{ kJ/mol glucose}
$$

(negative = exothermic).

For a 10,000 L fermenter consuming 90 g/L of glucose (= 500 mmol/L = 0.5 mol/L):

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{heat} &= \Delta H_{rxn} \times n_{glucose} \\
&= 92 \text{ kJ/mol} \times (0.5 \text{ mol/L} \times 10{,}000 \text{ L}) \\
&= 92 \times 5000 = 460{,}000 \text{ kJ} \\
&= 4.6 \times 10^5 \text{ kJ} = 127.8 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming fermentation completes in 48 h:

$$
\dot{Q}_{cooling} = \frac{4.6 \times 10^5}{48 \times 3600} = 2.66 \text{ kW}
$$

This cooling duty is essential for **fermenter thermal design** in the KTU syllabus.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Fermentation Yields

| Feedstock | Substrate (g/L) | Microorganism | Product | Theoretical Yield (g/g) | Industrial Yield (g/g) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Glucose | 150 | *S. cerevisiae* | Ethanol | 0.511 | 0.45–0.48 |
| Sucrose | 150 | *S. cerevisiae* | Ethanol | 0.538 | 0.46–0.50 |
| Maltose | 150 | *S. cerevisiae* | Ethanol | 0.526 | 0.44–0.48 |
| Glucose | 100 | *L. delbrueckii* | Lactic acid | 1.000 | 0.85–0.95 |
| Ethanol (8 % v/v) | — | *A. aceti* | Acetic acid | 1.304 (mass/mass) | 1.20–1.28 |
| Wort (12 °Bx) | 120 | *S. pastorianus* | Beer (4.5 % ABV) | 0.045 | 0.040–0.043 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Sucrose vs. Glucose:** Sucrose (M.W. = 342) first hydrolyzes into glucose + fructose, yielding **2 moles of hexose per mole of sucrose**. Always use **342 → 184** g ethanol (instead of 180 → 92) when the substrate is sucrose.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Monod Growth Kinetics Applied to Yeast

For exponential phase yeast growth:

$$
\mu = \mu_{max}\cdot\frac{S}{K_s + S}
$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹)
- $\mu_{max}$ = maximum specific growth rate (≈ 0.4 h⁻¹ for *S. cerevisiae*)
- $K_s$ = half-saturation constant (≈ 0.1–0.5 g/L for glucose)
- $S$ = residual sugar concentration (g/L)

For typical brewing wort ($S_0 = 120$ g/L, $S \gg K_s$):

$$
\mu \approx \mu_{max} = 0.4 \text{ h}^{-1}
$$

Doubling time:

$$
t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu_{max}} = \frac{0.693}{0.4} \approx 1.73 \text{ h}
$$

This justifies the **fast exponential phase** of 18–24 h observed in brewery fermentations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Fermentation Process Flow (Beverage-Wide)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Preparation] --> B[Milling or Pressing]
    B --> C[Mashing or Juice Extraction]
    C --> D[Clarification and Sterilization]
    D --> E[Nutrient Adjustment DAP and pH]
    E --> F[Inoculation with Yeast or LAB]
    F --> G[Fermentation Reactor CSTR or Batch]
    G --> H[CO2 Capture or Venting]
    G --> I[Heat Removal via Cooling Jacket]
    G --> J[Monitoring SG Brix pH and Temperature]
    J --> K{Endpoint Reached}
    K -- Yes --> L[Cell Removal Centrifuge or Sedimentation]
    K -- No --> G
    L --> M[Aging Maturation or Conditioning]
    M --> N[Filtration and Stabilization]
    N --> O[Packaging Bottling Canning Kegging]
```

**Caption:** A typical beverage fermentation pipeline, showing the central role of the fermentation reactor and the side-streams (CO₂, heat) that must be managed.

---

## 4.2 Biochemical Pathway Diagram — Three Fermentation Routes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    glucose["Glucose C6H12O6"] --> glycolysis["Glycolysis EMP Pathway"]
    glycolysis --> pyruvate["Pyruvate CH3COCOO minus"]
    pyruvate --> route1["Route 1 Yeast Alcoholic"]
    pyruvate --> route2["Route 2 LAB Lactic"]
    pyruvate --> route3["Route 3 Acetobacter Acetic"]
    route1 --> ethanol["Ethanol C2H5OH plus CO2"]
    route2 --> lactate["Lactic Acid CH3CHOHCOOH"]
    route3 --> acetic["Acetic Acid CH3COOH"]
    ethanol --> bevA["Beer Wine Cider Spirits"]
    lactate --> bevB["Kefir Kumiss Sour Beer"]
    acetic --> bevC["Vinegar Kombucha"]
```

---

## 4.3 Batch Fermentation Time-Course Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p0["Lag Phase 0 to 6 h"] --> p1["Acceleration Phase 6 to 18 h"]
    p1 --> p2["Exponential Phase 18 to 48 h"]
    p2 --> p3["Stationary Phase 48 to 96 h"]
    p3 --> p4["Conditioning Phase 96 h plus"]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of a CSTR Fermenter (Sub-System Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input["Input Side"]
        i1["Wort or Must Inlet"]
        i2["Yeast Pitch Inlet"]
        i3["DAP Nutrient Inlet"]
    end
    subgraph Core["Fermenter Vessel"]
        c1["Temperature Control Jacket Glycol"]
        c2["pH Sensor Probe"]
        c3["Dissolved Oxygen Probe"]
        c4["CO2 Vent with Butterfly Valve"]
        c5["Mechanical Agitator or Pumping"]
        c6["CIP Spray Ball"]
    end
    subgraph Output["Output Side"]
        o1["Green Beer or Young Wine Outlet"]
        o2["Yeast Crop Recovery"]
        o3["CO2 Capture to Scrubber"]
        o4["PLC and SCADA Telemetry"]
    end
    i1 --> Core
    i2 --> Core
    i3 --> Core
    Core --> o1
    Core --> o2
    Core --> o3
    Core --> o4
```

**Engineering Significance:** Modern brewery and winery fermenters integrate **in-line sensors** for SG, pH, DO, and temperature, enabling closed-loop control of fermentation profiles.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - Part A: Short-answer questions × 2 marks each = **3 marks**.
> - Part B: Long-answer questions × 14 marks each (with internal choice) = **14 marks per Q**.

---

## PART A — Two-Mark / Three-mark Questions

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define fermentation. Name the three principal types of fermentation relevant to beverage processing.**

**Model Answer:**

> Fermentation is an anaerobic biochemical process in which microorganisms (yeasts, bacteria, molds) enzymatically convert carbohydrates into alcohols, organic acids, and CO₂, with regeneration of NAD⁺ and a small ATP yield (~2 ATP per glucose).
>
> The three principal types are:
> 1. **Alcoholic (ethanolic) fermentation** — by *Saccharomyces* spp. → ethanol + CO₂.
> 2. **Lactic acid fermentation** — by *Lactobacillus* spp. → lactic acid.
> 3. **Acetic acid fermentation** — by *Acetobacter* spp. → acetic acid (vinegar).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the three types with examples)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Write the Gay-Lussac equation for alcoholic fermentation. Calculate the theoretical mass yield of ethanol from 1 kg of glucose.**

**Model Answer:**

Equation:

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2
$$

Theoretical yield:

$$
m_{ethanol} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{180} \times 92 = 511.1 \text{ g}
$$

So **1 kg glucose → ~511 g ethanol (theoretical, 100 % efficiency).**

*(3 marks: 1 for equation, 2 for calculation with correct substitution)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **biochemistry of alcoholic fermentation** by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*. Include the EMP pathway, the pyruvate decarboxylase and alcohol dehydrogenase reactions, and a stoichiometric mass balance.

**(b) [7 marks]** A wine must has **22 °Brix** and is fermented to dryness (≤ 0 °Brix apparent). If the fermentation efficiency is **88 %**, compute (i) the theoretical alcohol yield in % v/v, (ii) the actual alcohol produced in liters per 1000 L of must, and (iii) the amount of CO₂ evolved (in kg) per 1000 L.

---

#### Solution to (a) — Alcoholic Fermentation Biochemistry

1. **EMP Pathway (Glycolysis):** Glucose is phosphorylated using 2 ATP and split into two triose phosphates, which are then oxidized, producing 2 NADH and 4 ATP (net 2 ATP). The end-product is **pyruvate (CH₃COCOO⁻)**.

2. **Step 11 of glycolysis → Pyruvate Decarboxylase:**

$$
\text{Pyruvate} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Pyruvate decarboxylase, TPP, Mg}^{2+}}\; \text{Acetaldehyde} + CO_2
$$

3. **Step 12 of glycolysis → Alcohol Dehydrogenase:**

$$
\text{Acetaldehyde} + NADH + H^+ \;\xrightarrow{\text{Alcohol dehydrogenase}}\; \text{Ethanol} + NAD^+
$$

4. **Net Reaction:**

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + 2\,ATP
$$

5. **Stoichiometric Mass Balance** (per 180 g glucose):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{In:}  & \quad 180.16 \text{ g glucose} \\
\text{Out:} & \quad 92.14 \text{ g ethanol} + 88.02 \text{ g } CO_2 \\
\text{Carbon balance:} & \quad 6 \times 12 = 72 \text{ g C in} \;;\; 4 \times 12 + 2 \times 12 = 72 \text{ g C out } \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**(Mark allocation:** 2 marks for EMP, 2 for pyruvate decarboxylase + ADH, 2 for net equation, 1 for balance.)

---

#### Solution to (b) — Quantitative Fermentation Analysis

**(i) Theoretical % v/v alcohol from 22 °Bx:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sugar consumed} &= 22 \,°Bx \approx 220 \text{ g/L} \\
\text{Theoretical ethanol (mass)} &= 220 \times 0.5115 = 112.5 \text{ g/L} \\
\text{Theoretical ethanol (volume)} &= \frac{112.5}{0.789} = 142.6 \text{ mL/L} \\
\text{ABV}_{theor} &= 14.26 \,\% \text{ v/v}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Actual alcohol at 88 % efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ABV}_{actual} &= 14.26 \times 0.88 = 12.55 \,\% \text{ v/v} \\
V_{ethanol} \text{ per 1000 L} &= 125.5 \text{ L of pure ethanol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) CO₂ evolved:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2,\;theor} &= \frac{220}{180} \times 88.02 = 107.6 \text{ g/L} \\
m_{CO_2,\;actual} &= 107.6 \times 0.88 = 94.7 \text{ g/L} \\
\text{Total per 1000 L} &= 94.7 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(Mark allocation:** 3 for (i), 2 for (ii), 2 for (iii).)

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare **alcoholic, lactic acid, and acetic acid fermentation** in tabular form. For each, give the chemical equation, the key microorganism, and at least two beverage applications.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **factors affecting alcoholic fermentation** in brewing. Justify each with a one-line mechanism.

---

#### Solution to (a) — Comparative Table

| Parameter | Alcoholic | Lactic Acid | Acetic Acid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Net Equation | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$ | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_3H_6O_3$ | $2C_2H_5OH + 2O_2 \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2H_2O$ |
| Microorganism | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | *Lactobacillus spp.* | *Acetobacter aceti* |
| ATP per glucose | 2 | 2 | 0 (oxidative) |
| Beverage 1 | Beer, Wine | Kefir | Vinegar |
| Beverage 2 | Cider, Sake | Sour beer | Kombucha |
| Oxygen need | Anaerobic | Anaerobic / micro-aerobic | Strictly aerobic |
| Inhibitory end-product | Ethanol (>12 % ABV) | Lactic acid (pH < 3.5) | Acetic acid (>15 %) |

**(Mark allocation:** 1 mark per cell × 6 cells.)

---

#### Solution to (b) — Factors Affecting Brewing Fermentation

1. **Temperature** (optimum 18–25 °C ale / 7–13 °C lager): Controls yeast metabolism; high T produces fruity esters, low T produces clean flavor.
2. **pH** (3.5–4.5): Inhibits contaminants; extreme pH disrupts yeast enzyme function.
3. **Wort Oxygenation** (5–10 mg/L DO initially): Required for sterol and unsaturated fatty acid synthesis in yeast membranes; deficiency causes stuck fermentation.
4. **Sugar Concentration** (10–12 °P typical): High sugar causes osmotic stress; low sugar leads to under-attenuation.
5. **Nutrient (FAN) Availability** (150–250 mg/L): Nitrogen deficiency causes H₂S (rotten egg) off-flavor and poor yeast growth.
6. **Pitching Rate** (10⁶–10⁷ cells/mL·°P): Under-pitching causes excessive ester formation and slow fermentation.
7. **Ethanol Accumulation** (>12–15 % v/v): Disrupts yeast cell membrane; inhibits viability.

**(Mark allocation:** 1 mark per factor × 7 factors.)

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Fermentation questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing mass yield vs. volume yield.** Always show ethanol density (0.789 g/mL) when converting between g and mL.
> 2. **Forgetting to balance CO₂ evolution in stoichiometry.** Examiners deduct 1 mark if CO₂ is missing from the net equation.
> 3. **Wrong molar mass of sucrose (342) vs. glucose (180).** Sucrose hydrolysis releases glucose + fructose — both fermentable.
> 4. **Stating "fermentation produces energy" without quantification.** Always state **2 ATP per glucose**.
> 5. **Missing the difference between apparent extract and real extract.** In beer, alcohol affects SG; use **real extract = apparent extract + 0.001 × ABV** correction.
> 6. **Confusing primary vs. secondary fermentation.** Sparkling wines undergo secondary fermentation *in bottle* (méthode champenoise).
> 7. **Ignoring ethanol inhibition threshold.** State that *S. cerevisiae* typically cannot survive > 15–18 % ABV without specialized strain selection.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Fermentation = anaerobic microbial catabolism of sugars to alcohol/acid + CO₂ with net **2 ATP/glucose**.
- **Gay-Lussac Equation:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$ (theoretical yield = 51.1 % w/w ethanol from glucose).
- **Three types:** Alcoholic (yeast), Lactic (LAB), Acetic (*Acetobacter*).
- **EMP Glycolysis** is the universal first step; **pyruvate decarboxylase** and **alcohol dehydrogenase** are the two committed enzymes for ethanol.
- **Five fermentation phases:** Lag → Acceleration → Exponential → Stationary → Conditioning.
- **Brix-to-ABV rule:** $ABV \approx 0.55–0.65 \times °Bx$ (use 0.55 for wine, 0.6 for beer).
- **Cooling load:** Heat of fermentation ≈ **92 kJ/mol glucose** (used in fermenter jacket sizing).
- **Optimum fermentation conditions:** T = 18–25 °C (ale), pH = 3.5–4.5, DO initial = 5–10 mg/L, FAN = 150–250 mg/L.
- **Pitching rate:** ~0.5–1.0 g dried yeast per hectoliter per °Plato (commercial brewing standard).
- **Industrial alcohol yield:** 85–95 % of theoretical in well-run breweries; < 80 % indicates stuck fermentation.
- **Acetic fermentation** is strictly aerobic (vinegar); the others are anaerobic/micro-aerobic.
- **CO₂ capture:** 1 kg glucose → ~0.49 kg CO₂ — relevant for carbon footprint of breweries.
- **Monod kinetics:** $t_d \approx 1.7$ h for *S. cerevisiae* in exponential phase.
- **Beverage classifications:** Beer (lager yeast, low T), Wine (wine yeast, mid T), Spirits (distilled post-fermentation).
- **Louis Pasteur** = father of fermentation science (1857).
- **Zymology** = scientific study of fermentation.
- **Fusel oils** = higher alcohols (propanol, isobutanol, isoamyl alcohol) produced as minor by-products; important for flavor.
- **Stuck fermentation** = fermentation that stops before all sugar is consumed (causes: nutrient deficiency, temperature shock, oxygen depletion, ethanol toxicity).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pasteurization and Packaging. Beer Defects and Spoilage
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pasteurization and Packaging. Beer Defects and Spoilage

# 🍺 Pasteurization, Packaging & Beer Defects — Module 2 Master Notes

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Pasteurization in Beverages?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Pasteurization):**
> *Pasteurization* is a controlled thermal process in which a beverage (beer, fruit juice, milk) is subjected to a precise time-temperature combination sufficient to inactivate target pathogenic and spoilage microorganisms — particularly the asexual spores of *Saccharomyces* species and vegetative bacteria — **without causing significant sensory (flavour, aroma, colour) damage to the product.**

The classical reference parameters in the brewing industry are anchored to the death kinetics of **ascospores of *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*** (also called "wild yeast" indicators):
- **Reference Temperature ($T_{ref}$)** = **60 °C (140 °F)**
- **z-value ($z$)** = **6.94 °C (7 °C)** — the temperature change required to alter the thermal death rate by a factor of 10.
- **Lethality at 60 °C** = **1 Pasteurization Unit (1 PU) per minute** of holding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Sun-Bathing Yeast":**
> Imagine yeast cells as tiny sunbathers on a beach. A mild sun (warm but not hot beer at 40 °C) does not hurt them — they keep multiplying. A hot noon sun (60 °C) gives them a controlled sunburn that disables their reproduction machinery. An extreme scorcher (boiling, 100 °C) kills them instantly but also ruins the aroma of the beer. Pasteurization is the *engineered sweet spot* — enough UV to sterilize, but not enough to "cook" the flavour compounds (esters, hop oils, malt aromatics). The **PU value is simply the cumulative "dose" of this controlled sunburn.**

---

### 1.2 What is Packaging in the Beverage Industry?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Packaging):**
> *Beverage packaging* is the sequence of unit operations that delivers the finished, pasteurized, stable beverage into a hermetically sealed, light-proof, oxygen-barrier container in a microbiologically clean environment — preserving carbonation, flavour, biological stability, and visual appeal throughout the declared shelf life.

Packaging is **not** a single operation — it is a **7–10 stage automated production line** that converts an empty glass/PET can/keg into a retail-ready carton ready for distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Suitcase for Beer":**
> Think of the finished beer as a valuable liquid perfume. Packaging is the act of bottling the perfume in a dark, airtight, pressure-resistant glass vial, then sealing the vial, polishing it, sticking a designer label on it, putting it in a gift box, and shipping it. A single cracked seal or a single bubble of oxygen would ruin the entire contents. Hence, packaging is the *integrity engineering* of the beverage.

---

### 1.3 What are Beer Defects and Spoilage?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Beer Defects):**
> *Beer defects* are any deviation from the prescribed physico-chemical, sensory, or visual specifications of the finished beer — arising from raw material quality, process deviations, packaging failures, or microbial contamination. *Spoilage* specifically refers to defects caused by **microbial growth** post-packaging (yeast, bacteria, moulds) that render the beer unfit for consumption.

Defects are broadly classified into:
1. **Physical Defects** — turbidity, haze, gushing, foaming problems.
2. **Chemical/Physico-chemical Defects** — oxidation (staling), acetaldehyde, DMS, diacetyl, light-struck (skunky) flavour.
3. **Biological Defects (Spoilage)** — refermentation in bottle, ropiness, sourness, phenolic off-flavours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Sick Patient":**
> A healthy beer is like a healthy person. A *physical defect* is a broken bone (visible). A *chemical defect* is high cholesterol (invisible but detectable). A *biological defect* is a viral infection (caused by an external organism that multiplies inside the host). Pasteurization is the *vaccine* that prevents the infection; packaging is the *skin and immune system* that keeps the patient sealed from the outside world.

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Nomenclature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Brewing Pasteurization Metrics (KTU-Highlighted):**
> - **PU** = Pasteurization Unit = 1 minute of lethality equivalent to 60 °C holding.
> - **F-value** = Total integrated lethality (in minutes, referenced to $z = 6.94\ ^\circ\text{C}$).
> - **P-value** = Equivalent pasteurization time in minutes at 70 °C (used in juice industry).
> - **Recommended PU for beer** = **15 PU** (long-shelf-life, filter-sterilized bottled beer).
> - **Recommended PU for draft/keg beer** = **5–8 PU** (pasteurized in-bulk, shorter shelf life expected).
> - **Fill temperature** of bottle going into tunnel pasteurizer = **≤ 24 °C** (to prevent thermal shock and bottle breakage).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Microbiology of Pasteurization Target Organisms

The purpose of pasteurization is **not sterility** (which would require autoclaving at 121 °C and destroy the beer). It is **commercial stability** — reducing the spoilage load to a level that the beer's own hurdles (low pH ≈ 4.0–4.5, hop bitter compounds, ethanol 4–8 % v/v, low oxygen, CO₂) can suppress.

| Target Organism | Type | Typical Heat Resistance | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| *Saccharomyces* ascospores | Asexual fungal spores | High (D₆₀ ≈ 2–8 min) | Primary pasteurization target |
| *Lactobacillus* spp. | Gram-positive rod bacteria | Moderate | Beer souring, diacetyl |
| *Pediococcus* spp. | Gram-positive cocci | Moderate | Ropiness, sarcina sickness |
| *Acetobacter* spp. | Gram-negative bacteria | Low | Vinegar taint, surface pellicle |
| *Zymomonas* spp. | Gram-negative | Moderate | Over-carbonation, aldehydes |
| Wild yeasts (*Brettanomyces*) | Fungi | High | Phenolic, "horsey" off-flavour |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **z-value of 6.94 °C** is empirically derived from the death kinetics of *Saccharomyces* ascospores. Any temperature 1 °C higher than 60 °C doubles the lethal effect; any temperature 1 °C lower halves it. This logarithmic response is what makes the **PU integral** mathematically tractable.

---

## 2.2 Types of Pasteurizers (KTU Syllabus Focus)

### 2.2.1 Flash Pasteurizer (Plate Heat Exchanger)

- Beer flows through a **plate heat exchanger (PHE)** in turbulent flow.
- Beer is heated to **72–78 °C for 15–30 seconds** (≈ 8–15 PU).
- Hot section → **holding tube** (precisely sized for residence time) → regeneration section → cooling section → CO₂ counter-pressure → filler.
- **Advantage:** in-line, continuous, integrated with aseptic filler.
- **Disadvantage:** does not pasteurize the package; relies on aseptic filling.

### 2.2.2 Tunnel Pasteurizer (In-Bottle)

- Filled, crowned bottles travel on a **mesh belt conveyor** through a long, temperature-zoned tunnel.
- Tunnel has **7–9 distinct temperature zones**:
  1. Preheat zone (entry warming, 30–45 °C)
  2. Heating-up zone (45 → 60 °C)
  3. Pasteurization zone (60–62 °C, 15–20 min)
  4. Holding zone (peak temperature maintained)
  5. Pre-cooling zone
  6. Cooling zone 1 (cold water spray)
  7. Cooling zone 2 (chilled water immersion)
- **Total residence time** = 60–90 minutes.
- **Advantage:** pasteurizes the **filled package** — bottle + crown + beer all treated.
- **Disadvantage:** heat exposure to glass → energy-intensive; risk of bottle breakage.

### 2.2.3 Comparison Table

| Feature | Flash Pasteurizer | Tunnel Pasteurizer |
|---|---|---|
| Beer heated | Yes, in PHE | No, beer in bottle |
| PU achieved | 8–15 PU | 15–25 PU |
| Bottle pasteurized | No (aseptic fill needed) | Yes |
| Time | 15–30 s | 60–90 min |
| Energy | Lower (regeneration > 90 %) | Higher |
| Capital cost | Moderate | High |
| Suitable for | Cans, PET, large volume | Glass bottles, premium brands |
| Operational risk | Heat-flavor damage low | Bottle breakage, label damage |

---

## 2.3 Packaging Line Operations (KTU Sequential Block)

The packaging line is a **strictly ordered sequence** — re-ordering any step causes contamination or product loss:

| Stage | Operation | Critical Control Point (CCP) | Equipment |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bottle Depalletization** | Bottle integrity | Auto depalletizer |
| 2 | **Bottle Washing** | Microbial load, label removal | Soak tank + jet washer (1–2 % caustic, 60–80 °C) |
| 3 | **Bottle Inspection** | Cracks, chips, foreign matter | Electronic optical inspector (EOI) |
| 4 | **Filling** | Fill level, CO₂ retention, foam | Counter-pressure filler |
| 5 | **Crowning (Sealing)** | Hermetic seal, vacuum, O₂ pickup ≤ 0.5 ppm | Rotary crowner |
| 6 | **Pasteurization** | PU control, bottle temperature curve | Tunnel pasteurizer (or flash + aseptic) |
| 7 | **Labeling** | Label alignment, adhesive, legibility | Rotary labeling machine |
| 8 | **Date Coding & Inspection** | Batch traceability, fill level re-check | Inkjet coder + vision system |
| 9 | **Cartoning / Case Packing** | Carton integrity, count accuracy | Carton erector + packer |
| 10 | **Shrink Wrapping & Palletizing** | Stacking stability, transport safety | Shrink tunnel + palletizer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this sequence is sacred:**
> Washing → Inspection → Filling → Crowning → Pasteurization → Labeling is not arbitrary. If you label *before* pasteurization, the heat destroys the label adhesive and ink. If you fill *after* pasteurization, the sterile beer is contaminated by the unsterile bottle. Each step creates the conditions for the next.

---

## 2.4 Beer Defects — Comprehensive Classification (KTU Exam Priority)

### 2.4.1 Physical Defects

| Defect | Cause | Detection | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chill Haze** | Protein-polyphenol complex (haze-active proline-rich proteins + monomeric/polymeric polyphenols) precipitating at < 10 °C | Visual, nephelometric (FTU) | Use silica gel (xerogel) or PVPP to adsorb haze precursors; tannic acid removal |
| **Permanent Haze** | Oxidation of polyphenols → polymerized, irreversible | Visual, particle counter | Reduce O₂ pickup, add ascorbic acid, use low-polyphenol malt |
| **Gushing** | Over-pressurization from nucleation sites (oxalate crystals, metal ions, mouldy barley hordein) | Spontaneous overflow on opening | Avoid mouldy raw material, control CO₂ |
| **Foam Collapse** | Residual detergents, lipids from grains, over-carbonation | Foam stability (Nibem) test | Lipid-free cleaning, proper glass rinsing |
| **Sediment** | Yeast sedimentation in bottle (especially in bottle-conditioned beer) | Visual | Cold conditioning, filtration |

### 2.4.2 Chemical / Sensory Defects

| Defect | Off-Compound | Threshold (approx.) | Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Light-struck (Skunky)** | 3-methyl-2-butene-1-thiol (3-MBT) | 0.000035 ppm (35 ppt) | UV photolysis of hop iso-α-acids → 3-MBT + humulone radicals; catalyzed by riboflavin |
| **Oxidation (Staling)** | (E)-2-nonenal (cardboard), aldehydes, ketones | 0.0001 ppm for nonenal | O₂ exposure post-fermentation; high-temperature storage |
| **DMS (Dimethyl Sulphide)** | DMS, cooked-corn, cooked-vegetable | 0.030 ppm (lager), 0.100 ppm (ale) | Inactive SMM (S-methyl methionine) → DMS on heating |
| **Diacetyl Hang** | Diacetyl (2,3-butanedione) | 0.010–0.020 ppm (clean beer) | Insufficient yeast secondary conditioning; α-acetolactate not converted |
| **Acetaldehyde** | Green apple, paint | 2–4 ppm normal; > 25 ppm defect | Premature yeast flocculation, oxidation of ethanol |
| **Phenolic Off-Flavor** | 4-vinyl guaiacol (clove-like) | Varies | Wild yeast (*Brettanomyces*, *Dekkera*) decarboxylation of ferulic acid |

### 2.4.3 Biological Spoilage (Microbial)

| Spoilage Organism | Symptom | pH/CO₂ Effect |
|---|---|---|
| *Lactobacillus brevis* | Sour, "green apple" lactic acidity | pH drops sharply |
| *Pediococcus damnosus* | Ropiness (ropy/slimy beer), diacetyl | pH drops, viscosity increases 10× |
| *Acetobacter aceti* | Vinegar taint, surface pellicle | Acetic acid (sharp) |
| *Zymomonas mobilis* | Over-carbonation, turbidity, aldehyde | CO₂ explosion, turbid |
| *Brettanomyces bruxellensis* | "Barnyard," horse blanket, phenolic | pH drops, estery |
| *Moulds (Aspergillus, Penicillium)* | Gushing, off-aromas, mycotoxins | Surface growth in cork/wet hop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pasteurization vs. Spoilage Linkage (KTU Critical Concept):**
> The PU level chosen for a beer is directly tied to the *predicted spoilage load* of the packaging line. A 15 PU treatment provides a **5-log (99.999 %) reduction** in *Saccharomyces* ascospores and a **2–3 log reduction** in *Lactobacillus*. Lower PU → shorter shelf life → faster spoilage in distribution chain.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All quantitative problems in KTU Module 2 reduce to the four formulas below. Memorize them.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pasteurization Unit (PU) Definition** | $\text{PU} = t \cdot 10^{\left(\frac{T - 60}{z}\right)}$ | Lethality equivalent minutes at 60 °C | dimensionless (minutes) |
| 2 | **Lethality Rate ($L$) at temperature $T$** | $L = 10^{\left(\frac{T - 60}{z}\right)}$ | Per-minute lethality factor at $T$ | min⁻¹ |
| 3 | **Total Integrated PU (Variable $T$)** | $\text{PU}_{\text{total}} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} 10^{\left(\frac{T(t) - 60}{z}\right)} dt$ | Accumulated lethality over a temperature profile | PU |
| 4 | **D-value Relation** | $\frac{D_{T_1}}{D_{T_2}} = 10^{\left(\frac{T_2 - T_1}{z}\right)}$ | Decimal reduction time at any $T$ | minutes |
| 5 | **Log Reduction** | $n = \frac{t}{D_T}$ | Number of decades of population killed | log |
| 6 | **Survivor Population** | $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D_T}$ | Viable cells after thermal treatment | CFU/mL |
| 7 | **CO₂ Pressure in Package (Henry's Law — ideal)** | $P_{CO_2} = \dfrac{[CO_2]_{dissolved}}{k_H^{CO_2} \cdot T}$ | Pressure required to retain CO₂ | bar / atm |
| 8 | **O₂ Pickup Limit (Bottling)** | $\text{TPO} \leq 0.5\ \text{ppm}$ | Total package oxygen in bottled beer | mg/L |

**Where:**
- $T$ = temperature of beer/bottle (°C)
- $t$ = holding time (minutes)
- $z$ = temperature change for 10× change in D-value (**6.94 °C** in brewing)
- $D_T$ = decimal reduction time at $T$ (time for 90 % kill)
- $N_0$ = initial microbial count (CFU/mL)

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why brewing engineers care about PU:**
> - A craft brewery shipping within the city needs only **5–8 PU** (low heat, preserves hop aroma).
> - A multinational shipping to the tropics for 6 months needs **15–20 PU** (high heat, robust shelf life).
> - The **PU calculation is the design specification for the entire pasteurizer** — it determines holding tube length, tunnel length, water spray flow rate, and zone temperature profiles.
> - In the food industry at large, the same PU framework is the basis of FDA-mandated pasteurization validation for milk, juice, and canned foods.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pasteurization Unit (PU) Equation

### 3.1.1 Starting Postulate (Bigelow Model, 1921)

The thermal death of microorganisms follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to time and an **exponential Arrhenius-type dependence** on temperature:

$$-\frac{dN}{dt} = k(T) \cdot N$$

where $k(T)$ is the specific death rate constant, a strong function of temperature.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Integrate to Get Survivor Curve

Integrating the first-order equation from $t = 0$ to $t$:

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{-k(T) \cdot t}$$

Taking the base-10 logarithm (more convenient for "log reduction"):

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N(t)}\right) = k(T) \cdot t \cdot \log_{10}(e) = \frac{k(T) \cdot t}{2.303}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Define the D-value

By definition, the **D-value** is the time at temperature $T$ that reduces the population by a factor of 10 (i.e., one log reduction, $N/N_0 = 0.1$):

$$D_T = \frac{2.303}{k(T)}$$

Thus the survivor equation becomes the canonical form:

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D_T}$$

> **Conversion logic:** $e^{-k t} = 10^{-k t / 2.303} = 10^{-t/D_T}$ because $D_T = 2.303/k$ by definition.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Empirical z-value Model

Experimentally, $D_T$ decreases **log-linearly** with increasing $T$:

$$\log_{10}(D_T) = \log_{10}(D_{60}) - \frac{(T - 60)}{z}$$

where $D_{60}$ is the D-value at the reference temperature 60 °C, and $z$ is the temperature change for a 10× change in $D_T$. Rearranging:

$$\frac{D_T}{D_{60}} = 10^{-(T - 60)/z} = 10^{(60 - T)/z}$$

Therefore the **lethality rate per minute at temperature $T$** (relative to 60 °C) is:

$$L(T) = \frac{1}{D_T} = \frac{10^{(T - 60)/z}}{D_{60}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Inverting the D-value ratio gives the per-minute "lethal potency" relative to 60 °C.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — The Pasteurization Unit

By **convention** (Del Vecchio, 1951 — American Society of Brewing Chemists), the lethality at 60 °C is defined as exactly 1 PU per minute:

$$\text{PU} = t \cdot L(T) = t \cdot 10^{(T - 60)/z}$$

This is the **master equation** used in every KTU numerical.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example 1 — Constant Temperature Holding

**Problem:** A brewery holds beer at **65 °C for 8 minutes** in a flash pasteurizer's holding tube. What is the PU delivered? Use $z = 6.94\ ^\circ\text{C}$.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1** — Identify the temperature difference from reference:

$$\Delta T = T - 60 = 65 - 60 = 5\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2** — Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{\Delta T}{z} = \frac{5}{6.94} = 0.7205$$

**Step 3** — Compute the lethality factor:

$$10^{0.7205} = 5.260$$

**Step 4** — Multiply by the holding time to obtain PU:

$$\text{PU} = t \cdot 10^{\Delta T / z} = 8 \times 5.260 = 42.08\ \text{PU}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Each minute at 65 °C equals 5.26 minutes at 60 °C. Holding 8 minutes therefore equals 42 PU — **far above the 15 PU needed for premium bottled beer**, indicating the holding tube is oversized (a design inefficiency).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example 2 — Variable Temperature Profile (Tunnel Pasteurizer)

**Problem:** A bottle moves through a tunnel pasteurizer. Its temperature vs. time data (sampled at 5-min intervals) is:

| $t$ (min) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 | 35 | 40 | 45 | 50 | 55 | 60 | 65 | 70 | 75 | 80 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T$ (°C) | 25 | 35 | 45 | 52 | 58 | 61 | 62 | 62 | 62 | 61 | 58 | 52 | 45 | 38 | 30 | 26 | 25 |

**Find the total integrated PU delivered. Use $z = 6.94\ ^\circ\text{C}$.**

**Step-by-step Solution (Trapezoidal Numerical Integration):**

**Step 1** — Compute the lethality factor $L_i = 10^{(T_i - 60)/6.94}$ for each data point.

Using the formula $L = 10^{(T-60)/6.94}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t=0,\ T=25 &: L = 10^{-35/6.94} = 10^{-5.043} = 9.05 \times 10^{-6} \\
t=5,\ T=35 &: L = 10^{-25/6.94} = 10^{-3.602} = 2.50 \times 10^{-4} \\
t=10,\ T=45 &: L = 10^{-15/6.94} = 10^{-2.161} = 6.89 \times 10^{-3} \\
t=15,\ T=52 &: L = 10^{-8/6.94} = 10^{-1.153} = 7.03 \times 10^{-2} \\
t=20,\ T=58 &: L = 10^{-2/6.94} = 10^{-0.288} = 5.15 \times 10^{-1} \\
t=25,\ T=61 &: L = 10^{1/6.94} = 10^{0.144} = 1.393 \\
t=30,\ T=62 &: L = 10^{2/6.94} = 10^{0.288} = 1.940 \\
t=35,\ T=62 &: L = 1.940 \\
t=40,\ T=62 &: L = 1.940 \\
t=45,\ T=61 &: L = 1.393 \\
t=50,\ T=58 &: L = 0.515 \\
t=55,\ T=52 &: L = 0.0703 \\
t=60,\ T=45 &: L = 0.00689 \\
t=65,\ T=38 &: L = 10^{-22/6.94} = 10^{-3.170} = 6.76 \times 10^{-4} \\
t=70,\ T=30 &: L = 10^{-30/6.94} = 10^{-4.323} = 4.75 \times 10^{-5} \\
t=75,\ T=26 &: L = 10^{-34/6.94} = 10^{-4.899} = 1.26 \times 10^{-5} \\
t=80,\ T=25 &: L = 9.05 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2** — Apply the Trapezoidal Rule (interval $h = 5$ min):

$$\text{PU}_{\text{total}} = h \cdot \left[\frac{L_0 + L_n}{2} + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} L_i\right]$$

**Step 3** — Sum the interior terms:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{15} L_i =\ & (2.50 \times 10^{-4}) + (6.89 \times 10^{-3}) + (0.0703) + (0.515) + (1.393) \\
& + (1.940) + (1.940) + (1.940) + (1.393) + (0.515) + (0.0703) \\
& + (0.00689) + (6.76 \times 10^{-4}) + (4.75 \times 10^{-5}) + (1.26 \times 10^{-5})
\end{aligned}$$

Adding:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{15} L_i = 9.793$$

**Step 4** — Apply the trapezoidal formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PU}_{\text{total}} &= 5 \cdot \left[\frac{9.05 \times 10^{-6} + 9.05 \times 10^{-6}}{2} + 9.793\right] \\
&= 5 \cdot \left[9.05 \times 10^{-6} + 9.793\right] \\
&= 5 \cdot 9.793 \\
&= 48.96\ \text{PU}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The trapezoidal rule approximates the area under the lethality-vs-time curve as a series of trapezoids, with each interval's average height multiplied by its width. Summing all trapezoids gives the cumulative PU dose delivered by the tunnel.

> **Engineering Interpretation:** 49 PU is **3.3× the recommended dose** for a bottled beer (15 PU). This means either the tunnel is too long/hot, or the design is over-conservative. The bottleneck is the 15-minute flat-top at 62 °C — most lethality accumulates here.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example 3 — Log Reduction Calculation

**Problem:** A beer initially contains **5,000 ascospores/mL** of *Saccharomyces*. After pasteurization, the count is **2 ascospores/mL**. What is the log reduction? If $D_{60} = 3.5$ minutes, what PU was delivered?

**Step 1** — Compute log reduction:

$$n = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{5000}{2}\right) = \log_{10}(2500) = 3.398\ \text{log}_{10}$$

**Step 2** — Relate log reduction to PU at 60 °C:

$$n = \frac{t}{D_{60}} \implies t = n \cdot D_{60} = 3.398 \times 3.5 = 11.89\ \text{min}$$

**Step 3** — Convert to PU (at 60 °C, 1 min = 1 PU):

$$\text{PU} = t = 11.89\ \text{PU} \approx 12\ \text{PU}$$

> **Conversion logic:** At the reference temperature, time-in-minutes IS PU. So 11.89 minutes at 60 °C delivers 11.89 PU, achieving a ~3.4 log reduction of the most heat-resistant ascospores. This is **close to the 15 PU target** for premium bottled beer.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Defect Diagnosis Quick Reference

| Symptom in Bottle | Probable Cause | Confirmatory Test | Pasteurization Sufficient? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vinegar smell | *Acetobacter* in headspace oxygen | Microscopy, vinegar titer | No — re-infection from cap/line |
| Ropy, viscous beer | *Pediococcus* exopolysaccharide | Viscosity meter, microscopy | No — re-infection |
| Popping bottle (excess CO₂) | *Zymomonas* refermentation | Microscopy, CO₂ pressure | No — refermentation post-pasteurization |
| Skunky, light-struck | Riboflavin + hop iso-α-acids + UV | UV-vis spectrophotometry (3-MBT) | No — chemistry, not biology |
| Cardboard, sherry notes | Stale aldehydes from oxidation | (E)-2-nonenal GC-MS | No — oxidation chain |
| Visible haze at 4 °C, clears at 20 °C | Chill haze (reversible) | Warming test | Cosmetic only |
| White sediment | Yeast sediment (bottle conditioning) | Microscopy | Intended, not defect |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 4 — Tunnel Pasteurizer PU Verification (Steady-State)

**Problem:** In a tunnel pasteurizer, a bottle spends 20 min in the heating zone, 15 min at peak 62 °C, and 25 min in the cooling zone. Temperatures are approximately linear: 25 °C → 62 °C in heating (ramp), constant 62 °C, then 62 °C → 25 °C in cooling (ramp). Use $z = 6.94\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate PU from each phase.

**Phase 1 — Heating (linear 25 → 62 °C over 20 min):**

Temperature: $T(t) = 25 + 1.85 t$ (°C, with $t$ in minutes from 0 to 20).

$$\text{PU}_1 = \frac{1}{20} \int_{0}^{20} 10^{(25 + 1.85t - 60)/6.94}\, dt = \frac{1}{20} \int_{0}^{20} 10^{(1.85t - 35)/6.94}\, dt$$

Substituting $u = (1.85t - 35)/6.94$, $du = (1.85/6.94)\, dt = 0.2666\, dt$:

$$\text{PU}_1 = \frac{1}{20 \cdot 0.2666} \int_{u_0}^{u_f} 10^{u}\, du = \frac{1}{5.333} \cdot \frac{10^{u_f} - 10^{u_0}}{\ln(10)}$$

with $u_0 = -35/6.94 = -5.043$, $u_f = (1.85 \times 20 - 35)/6.94 = 2/6.94 = 0.2882$:

$$\text{PU}_1 = \frac{1}{5.333} \cdot \frac{10^{0.2882} - 10^{-5.043}}{2.3026} = \frac{1.939 - 9.05 \times 10^{-6}}{12.28} = 0.158\ \text{PU}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The exponential grows so fast that the late minutes dominate. Numerically, 95 % of the lethality in this phase comes from the last 5 minutes.

**Phase 2 — Holding (15 min at 62 °C):**

$$\text{PU}_2 = 15 \times 10^{(62-60)/6.94} = 15 \times 10^{0.2882} = 15 \times 1.939 = 29.09\ \text{PU}$$

**Phase 3 — Cooling (linear 62 → 25 °C over 25 min, $T(t) = 62 - 1.48 t$):**

By symmetry (heating and cooling mirror each other around 43.5 °C), and the exponential's dominance, $\text{PU}_3 \approx \text{PU}_1 \approx 0.16\ \text{PU}$.

**Total PU:**

$$\text{PU}_{\text{total}} = 0.158 + 29.09 + 0.158 \approx 29.4\ \text{PU}$$

> **Engineering Verdict:** ~30 PU delivered, of which **99 % comes from the 15-minute holding zone**. The ramps contribute almost nothing — confirming that the **holding zone length, not the ramp zones, is the design lever** for PU control in a tunnel pasteurizer.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flash Pasteurizer System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Feed
        FV[Fermented Bright Beer Tank]
    end

    subgraph FlashUnit["Flash Pasteurizer (PHE)"]
        REG1[Regeneration Section]
        HEAT[Heating Section 72-78 C]
        HOLD[Holding Tube 15-30 s]
        COOL[Cooling Section]
        REG2[Regeneration Section]
    end

    subgraph Utilities
        HW[Hot Water Circuit 78-85 C]
        CW[Chilled Water 2-4 C]
        CO2SYS[CO2 Counter-Pressure System]
    end

    subgraph Output
        AF[Aseptic Buffer Tank]
        FILL[Counter-Pressure Filler]
        CRC[Rotary Crowner]
    end

    FV --> REG1
    REG1 --> HEAT
    HEAT --> HOLD
    HOLD --> COOL
    COOL --> REG2
    REG2 --> AF
    AF --> FILL
    FILL --> CRC
    HW --> HEAT
    CW --> COOL
    CO2SYS --> FILL
    CO2SYS --> AF
    REG1 -.heat recovery.-> REG2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Beer flows in the **upper path** through regeneration, heating, holding, and cooling plates.
- **Regeneration** recovers ~90 % of the heat — cold incoming beer is pre-warmed by hot outgoing beer.
- The **holding tube** is the only section where time-temperature is lethal; everything else is heat transfer.
- CO₂ is added at the buffer tank and filler to prevent O₂ pickup and maintain carbonation.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Tunnel Pasteurizer (In-Bottle)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PreZone[Zone 1 - Pre-Heat 30-45 C]
        Z1A[Entry Warming Spray]
    end

    subgraph HeatZone[Zones 2-3 - Heat Up 45-60 C]
        Z2[Heating Zone A]
        Z3[Heating Zone B]
    end

    subgraph HoldZone[Zones 4-5 - Peak 60-62 C]
        Z4[Peak Holding Zone]
        Z5[Peak Holding Zone]
    end

    subgraph CoolZone[Zones 6-9 - Cool Down]
        Z6[Pre-Cool 50-40 C]
        Z7[Water Spray Cool 40-25 C]
        Z8[Chilled Water Immersion 25-15 C]
        Z9[Final Air Cool 15-20 C]
    end

    subgraph Output
        LAB[Labeling Machine]
        COD[Date Coder]
        OUT[Carton Pack Station]
    end

    BELT[Mesh Belt Conveyor with Filled Crowned Bottles] --> Z1A
    Z1A --> Z2 --> Z3 --> Z4 --> Z5 --> Z6 --> Z7 --> Z8 --> Z9
    Z9 --> LAB --> COD --> OUT
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The 9 zones form a **temperature-time curve** that rises, plateaus at 60–62 °C for 15–20 min, then falls.
- The **plateau zone is the PU-generating region** — anywhere else contributes negligibly.
- The conveyor belt moves continuously; residence time per zone = zone length / belt speed.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Defect Diagnosis in Finished Beer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Finished Beer Complaint Received] --> Q1{Visual Defect?}

    Q1 -->|Yes - Haze| HAZE[Measure Haze Type]
    HAZE --> H1{Clears on Warming?}
    H1 -->|Yes| CHILL[Chill Haze - Protein Polyphenol]
    H1 -->|No| PERM[Permanent Haze - Oxidized Polyphenols or Microbial]

    Q1 -->|Yes - Gushing| GUSH[Check Raw Barley Mold Counts and CO2 Pressure]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Sensory Defect?}

    Q2 -->|Yes - Off-Odor| ODOR{Identify Aroma}
    ODOR -->|Skunky| SKUN[Light-Struck - 3-MBT]
    ODOR -->|Vinegar| VIN[Acetobacter Spoilage]
    ODOR -->|Cardboard| OXID[Oxidative Stale Aldehydes]
    ODOR -->|Sour| SOUR[Lactic or Acetic Acid Bacteria]
    ODOR -->|Diacetyl| DIA[Incomplete Beer Maturation]
    ODOR -->|Barnyard| BARN[Brettanomyces Contamination]

    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Physical Anomaly?}
    Q3 -->|Yes - Burst Bottle| BURST[Over-Carbonation Refermentation]
    Q3 -->|Yes - Ropiness| ROP[Pediococcus Exopolysaccharide]
    Q3 -->|No| UNK[Unknown - Send to QC Lab GC-MS Panel]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- This is a **diagnostic decision tree** used by brewery QC labs.
- Each branch leads to a confirmed cause and a specific corrective action (e.g., skunky → switch to brown glass or use hop extracts that are UV-inert).

---

## 4.4 Packaging Line Block Diagram — Linear 10-Stage Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1: Depalletizer] --> P2[Stage 2: Soak Washer]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Jet Washer 60-80 C Caustic]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4: Bottle Inspector EOI]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5: Counter-Pressure Filler]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6: Crowner Vacuum Seal]
    P6 --> P7[Stage 7: Tunnel Pasteurizer]
    P7 --> P8[Stage 8: Labeler]
    P8 --> P9[Stage 9: Date Coder and Vision]
    P9 --> P10[Stage 10: Carton Pack]
    P10 --> P11[Shrink Wrap and Palletize]
    P11 --> WH[Warehouse - Cold Storage 4-8 C]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **No shortcuts, no reordering**: skipping Stage 4 (inspection) leads to broken bottles in the filler; skipping Stage 6's vacuum causes O₂ pickup and oxidation; pasting labels before Stage 7 (pasteurizer) destroys them.

---

## 4.5 Microbial Spoilage Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RAW[Contaminated Raw Material: Mouldy Barley, Wild Yeast in Air] -->|Inoculation| WORT[Hot Wort 100 C Sterile]
    WORT -->|Cooling| COOLWORT[Cooled Wort 20 C]
    COOLWORT -->|Pitching| YEAST[Pure Yeast Pitch]
    YEAST -->|Fermentation| GREEK[Green Beer]
    GREEK -->|Conditioning| MAT[Maturation - pH 4.0-4.5 Hop 30 IBU]
    MAT -->|Packaging| FILL[Counter-Pressure Filler]
    FILL -->|Past| PAS[15 PU Pasteurization]
    PAS -->|Seal| SEAL[Hermetic Crown Seal]

    SEAL -.->|Re-infection from Line| CONTAM1[Line Contamination]
    CONTAM1 --> SPOIL[Spoilage in Bottle]

    MAT -.->|Survivor Ascospore| SURV[Heat-Resistant Survivor]
    SURV -->|Insufficient PU| SPOIL
    SPOIL --> SYMP{Symptom}
    SYMP -->|Sour| LACTO[Lactobacillus]
    SYMP -->|Ropy| PEDIO[Pediococcus]
    SYMP -->|Vinegar| ACETO[Acetobacter]
    SYMP -->|Phenolic| BRETT[Brettanomyces]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Pasteurization is the **last line of defense**. Everything upstream (wort boiling, fermentation, conditioning, packaging hygiene) must reduce the load to a manageable level for the PU dose to be effective.
- Two spoilage pathways: **re-infection after pasteurization** (line hygiene failure) and **survivor ascospores** (insufficient PU).

---

## 4.6 Cross-Section of a Plate Heat Exchanger (Flash Pasteurizer)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHE[Plate Heat Exchanger Plates Stacked Vertically]
        PL1[Plate 1 - Cold Beer Channel]
        PL2[Plate 2 - Hot Water Channel]
        PL3[Plate 3 - Cold Beer Channel]
        PL4[Plate 4 - Hot Water Channel]
        PL5[Plate 5 - Cold Beer Channel]
    end

    CIN[Cold Beer In 4 C] --> PL1
    PL1 --> PL3
    PL3 --> PL5
    PL5 -->|Regenerated 65 C| REGOUT[To Heater Section]

    HWIN[Hot Water In 78 C] --> PL2
    PL2 --> PL4
    PL4 -->|Cooled 70 C| HWOUT[Hot Water Out - to Boiler Reheat]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Alternating hot and cold channels allow **counter-current heat transfer**.
- Incoming cold beer is warmed by outgoing hot beer — energy recovery of 90 %+ is standard.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Pasteurization Unit (PU)" in the context of brewing. State the reference temperature and z-value used in the brewing industry.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pasteurization Unit (PU)** is defined as the equivalent thermal lethality delivered in one minute at the reference temperature of 60 °C. It is calculated as:

$$\text{PU} = t \cdot 10^{(T - 60)/z}$$

For the brewing industry, the reference temperature is **60 °C** and the z-value used is **6.94 °C**, both anchored to the thermal death kinetics of *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* ascospores. A target dose of **15 PU** is standard for premium bottled beer.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six common beer defects and classify them into physical, chemical, and biological categories.**

**Model Answer:**

| Category | Defects (Examples) |
|---|---|
| **Physical** | Chill haze, permanent haze, gushing, foam collapse, sediment |
| **Chemical** | Light-struck (skunky) flavour, oxidation (cardboard / sherry), DMS (cooked corn), diacetyl, acetaldehyde (green apple) |
| **Biological (Spoilage)** | Ropiness (*Pediococcus*), sourness (*Lactobacillus*), vinegar taint (*Acetobacter*), over-carbonation (*Zymomonas*), phenolic off-flavour (*Brettanomyces*) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **tunnel pasteurizer** with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations compared to a flash pasteurizer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brewery holds beer in the holding tube of a flash pasteurizer at **70 °C for 25 seconds**. Calculate the PU delivered, given $z = 6.94\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Comment on whether this dose is sufficient for premium bottled beer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

A **tunnel pasteurizer** is an in-bottle pasteurization unit consisting of a long, insulated, temperature-zoned tunnel through which filled and crowned bottles are transported on a continuous mesh-belt conveyor. The tunnel is divided into **9 zones**, each maintained at a different temperature by a network of hot-water sprays, steam coils, and chilled-water immersion baths.

**Construction features:**
1. **Mesh-belt conveyor** running at variable speed (typically 1–6 m/min), driven by a variable-frequency drive.
2. **Zone 1 (pre-heat, 30–45 °C):** warm-water sprays to bring bottle temperature up gradually to avoid glass thermal shock.
3. **Zones 2–3 (heat-up, 45–60 °C):** progressive heating to the pasteurization temperature.
4. **Zones 4–5 (peak, 60–62 °C):** hot water at 62–65 °C sprays from above and below; this is the **PU-generating region** where bottles dwell for 15–20 minutes.
5. **Zones 6–7 (pre-cool and water-spray cool, 50–25 °C):** progressive cooling using warm, then cool water.
6. **Zone 8 (chilled water immersion, 25–15 °C):** bottles fully immersed to drop below 20 °C.
7. **Zone 9 (air cool):** ambient air to bring the bottle to room temperature for labeling.

**Working:** The bottle temperature follows a smooth ramp-hold-ramp curve. The integrated area under the lethality curve (PU) is the design target. Typical residence time is 60–90 minutes; typical PU delivered is 15–25.

**Advantages over flash pasteurizer:**
- Pasteurizes the **filled package** (bottle + cap + beer) — no aseptic filling needed.
- Robust to packaging-line micro-leaks.
- Higher PU achievable (more thermal margin for tropical export).
- Long heritage technology, well understood.

**Limitations:**
- **Time-intensive** (60–90 min) — large floor area required.
- **Energy-inefficient** (no heat regeneration section like the PHE).
- **Risk of bottle breakage** (thermal stress).
- **Label and ink damage** if labeling is done before pasteurization — requires post-pasteurization labeling (extra stage).
- Not suitable for cans or PET (deformation risk).

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:** $T = 70\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $t = 25$ seconds, $z = 6.94\ ^\circ\text{C}$.

**Step 1** — Convert time to minutes:

$$t = \frac{25}{60} = 0.4167\ \text{min}$$

**Step 2** — Apply the PU formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PU} &= t \cdot 10^{(T - 60)/z} \\
&= 0.4167 \cdot 10^{(70 - 60)/6.94} \\
&= 0.4167 \cdot 10^{1.4409} \\
&= 0.4167 \cdot 27.61 \\
&= 11.51\ \text{PU}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3** — Interpret:

A dose of 11.5 PU is **below the 15 PU target** for premium bottled beer intended for long shelf life. It is, however, **adequate for short-shelf-life draft/keg beer** (target 5–8 PU) and would suffice for local distribution. For tropical export or 6+ months shelf life, the holding tube length should be increased or the temperature raised slightly.

> **Conversion logic:** The base-10 exponent converts the temperature difference into a per-minute lethality factor (27.61 × at 70 °C), and multiplying by the time-in-minutes yields the cumulative PU dose. Twenty-five seconds is too short at 70 °C alone — to hit 15 PU, the time must be at least $\dfrac{15}{27.61} = 0.543$ min ≈ **32.6 seconds**.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **microbiology of beer spoilage** with reference to *Lactobacillus*, *Pediococcus*, *Acetobacter*, and *Brettanomyces*. For each, state the symptoms, the beer component affected, and one control measure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a flow diagram, describe the **complete packaging line for bottled beer**. Identify the three most critical control points (CCPs) and justify why each is critical. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

| Organism | Type | Symptom in Beer | Component Affected | Critical Control Measure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ***Lactobacillus brevis*** | Gram-positive rod, anaerobic | **Sour, lactic acidity**, pH drop, "green apple" taint | pH, lactic acid | Pre-pasteurization filtration; low pH (3.8) maintenance; hop bitter compounds |
| ***Pediococcus damnosus*** | Gram-positive coccus, anaerobic | **Ropiness** (viscous, slimy beer), diacetyl | Exopolysaccharide (viscosity), vicinal diketones | Sanitation of bright beer tank; eliminate O₂ in headspace |
| ***Acetobacter aceti*** | Gram-negative rod, aerobic | **Vinegar taint**, surface pellicle, acetic acid | Acetic acid (sharp acidity) | Hermetic sealing; minimize O₂ pickup at crowning; avoid headspace air |
| ***Brettanomyces bruxellensis*** | Yeast (fungi), facultative | **Phenolic off-flavour**, "barnyard," "horse blanket," "leather" | 4-vinyl guaiacol, 4-ethyl phenol | Filter-sterilize at 0.45 µm; pasteurize at 15 PU+; maintain pH ≤ 4.0 |

**Summary of spoilage biology:**
- All four are **post-fermentation contaminants** — they survive wort boiling but are kept at bay by competitive *Saccharomyces* during fermentation, hop α-acids, low pH, ethanol, and anaerobic conditions.
- Pasteurization is the **terminal kill step** that prevents these from multiplying in the packaged beer.
- *Brettanomyces* is the most heat-resistant of the four and is the principal reason for the 15 PU specification.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Packaging Line Flow Diagram (text version, to be redrawn in exam):**

$$\text{Depalletizer} \to \text{Bottle Soak} \to \text{Jet Wash} \to \text{EOI Inspection} \to \text{Counter-Pressure Filler} \to \text{Crowner} \to \text{Tunnel Pasteurizer} \to \text{Labeler} \to \text{Date Coder} \to \text{Cartoning} \to \text{Palletizer}$$

**Three Critical Control Points (CCPs):**

1. **CCP-1: Counter-Pressure Filler**
   - **Why critical:** This is where the beer's **carbonation, dissolved O₂, and fill level** are finally fixed. Any CO₂ loss causes flat beer; any O₂ ingress causes oxidation (staling).
