The counter-pressure mechanism (CO₂ head pressure 2.5–3.0 bar) ensures the beer is bottled **without foam formation and without air contact**. Total Package Oxygen (TPO) must be ≤ 0.5 ppm — exceeding this is the single largest cause of premature beer staling.

2. **CCP-2: Crowner (Sealing Station)**
   - **Why critical:** The crown seal is the **only hermetic barrier** between the pasteurized beer and the outside world. A faulty crown (under-crimped, over-crimped, scratched, defective liner) leaks O₂ in and CO₂ out, and provides an entry channel for *Acetobacter* and *Pediococcus*. Vacuum crowning (pulling 0.3–0.6 bar vacuum before sealing) reduces headspace O₂ from ~6000 ppm to < 200 ppm.

3. **CCP-3: Tunnel Pasteurizer**
   - **Why critical:** The **PU delivered** is the final microbial safety net. A temperature probe in the peak zone must continuously log; if PU drops below 12, the batch must be diverted for re-pasteurization. Bottle thermal stress is monitored — if the heating ramp exceeds 1 °C/min, glass breakage probability increases sharply.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Lost by Students:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert seconds to minutes** in PU calculations — losing 2–3 marks per numerical.
> 2. **Using z = 7 °C (rounded) instead of 6.94 °C** — minor, but the precise value gives 1 mark under "correct constants."
> 3. **Confusing flash pasteurizer PU range (8–15) with tunnel pasteurizer PU range (15–25)** — these are different.
> 4. **Listing spoilage organisms without stating the symptom** — half-credit only.
> 5. **Drawing the packaging line but not identifying the CCPS** — the question explicitly asks for them, and they carry 3 of the 7 marks in part (b).
> 6. **Forgetting the role of regeneration in flash pasteurizers** — losing 1 mark on the energy efficiency question.
> 7. **Wrong formula for PU** (e.g., writing $t \cdot e^{(T-60)/z}$ instead of $t \cdot 10^{(T-60)/z}$) — full zero for the numerical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

### Pasteurization Core
- [ ] **PU = $t \cdot 10^{(T-60)/z}$** — master equation, with $z = 6.94\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and reference 60 °C.
- [ ] **Target PU:** 15 PU premium bottled, 5–8 PU keg, 8–15 PU flash pasteurizer output.
- [ ] **Flash pasteurizer** = in-line PHE, 72–78 °C × 15–30 s, aseptic filling required, regeneration ≥ 90 % heat recovery.
- [ ] **Tunnel pasteurizer** = in-bottle, 9 zones, 60–90 min total, ramp–hold–ramp, 15–25 PU delivered.
- [ ] **Holding tube length** $L = v \cdot t$ where $v$ is flow velocity and $t$ is required residence time.
- [ ] **F-value** = integrated lethality, equivalent to PU when referenced to 60 °C.
- [ ] **D-value** = decimal reduction time (time to kill 90 % of population).
- [ ] **z-value** = temperature change for 10× change in D-value (6.94 °C in brewing).
- [ ] **Log reduction** $n = t/D_T$ at constant $T$.

### Packaging Core
- [ ] **10-stage packaging line:** Depalletize → Wash → Inspect → Fill → Crown → Pasteurize → Label → Code → Carton → Palletize.
- [ ] **Fill pressure** = 2.5–3.0 bar CO₂ counter-pressure.
- [ ] **TPO** (Total Package Oxygen) ≤ 0.5 ppm.
- [ ] **Vacuum crowning** reduces headspace O₂ from ~6000 ppm to < 200 ppm.
- [ ] **Three CCPs:** Filler, Crowner, Pasteurizer.
- [ ] **Wash temperature:** 60–80 °C with 1–2 % caustic; rinse with potable water; final rinse with deionized water.

### Defects & Spoilage Core
- [ ] **Chill haze** = protein + polyphenol complex, reversible on warming; remove with silica gel or PVPP.
- [ ] **Light-struck (skunky)** = 3-MBT from hop iso-α-acid UV photolysis; remedy: brown glass, hop extracts, riboflavin removal.
- [ ] **Oxidation** = (E)-2-nonenal, "cardboard" note; remedy: minimize O₂ pickup, use ascorbic acid, low-temperature storage.
- [ ] **Diacetyl hang** = incomplete yeast secondary conditioning; remedy: extended maturation (diacetyl rest at 20 °C for 24–48 h).
- [ ] **DMS** = SMM thermal breakdown to DMS; remedy: low-kilned malt, vigorous boil, cap on evaporation.
- [ ] ***Lactobacillus*** → sourness (lactic acid).
- [ ] ***Pediococcus*** → ropiness (exopolysaccharide) + diacetyl.
- [ ] ***Acetobacter*** → vinegar taint (acetic acid, needs O₂).
- [ ] ***Brettanomyces*** → phenolic, "barnyard" (4-vinyl guaiacol).
- [ ] ***Zymomonas*** → over-carbonation, turbidity.

### Cross-Connect (Frequently Asked)
- [ ] Why is **pasteurization not sterilization**? Because beer is heat-sensitive; 121 °C destroys flavour.
- [ ] Why does the **holding zone dominate PU**? Because of the exponential temperature dependence — below 55 °C, lethality is negligible.
- [ ] Why **label after pasteurize**? Because heat destroys adhesive and ink.
- [ ] Why **fill under counter-pressure**? To retain CO₂ and prevent O₂ pickup.
- [ ] Why **tunnel pasteurizes the package itself**? Eliminates the need for aseptic filling (a higher-risk operation).
- [ ] Why **$z = 6.94\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and not 7 °C exactly**? Empirical fit to *Saccharomyces* ascospore death data — 6.94 is the more accurate published value.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wine Fermentation - Types - Red and White. Wine Defects and Spoilage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wine Fermentation - Types - Red and White. Wine Defects and Spoilage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Wine Fermentation?

> [!NOTE]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> *Wine fermentation* is the **biochemical conversion of grape must sugars (primarily glucose and fructose) into ethanol and carbon dioxide** by the metabolic action of yeasts — predominantly *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* and related species — under controlled anaerobic or microaerobic conditions, accompanied by the secondary transformation of grape-derived phenolic, aromatic, and acidic precursors into the colour, body, flavour, and aroma profile characteristic of the finished wine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Board Significance):**
> In KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation, examiners specifically test the **operational differences between red and white wine fermentation** (contact with skins, temperature regimes, duration, tannin/anthocyanin extraction) and the **microbiological and biochemical causes of wine spoilage**. Treat this topic as a high-yield 14-mark question for Module 2.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sourdough Bread vs Pancake Batter" of Grapes

Imagine two identical bowls of grape juice sitting on a kitchen counter:

- **Bowl A (Red Wine):** You drop the whole *crushed grapes — skins, seeds, stems — into the bowl* and let the wild "sugar-eating microbes" (yeast) do their magic. While the sugar ferments into alcohol, the **skins release red colour and bitter tannin** into the liquid, like *tea leaves infusing colour into hot water*. The longer they sit, the deeper and more bitter the brew becomes.

- **Bowl B (White Wine):** You *strain out the skins and seeds first*, then ferment only the clear, pale juice. The result is a light, fruity, **straw-coloured "white wine"** — even though the grapes were green or red! It is essentially the same fermentation, but without the colour-and-tannin infusion step.

This is the *entire* philosophical difference between red and white wine fermentation: **presence or absence of solid skin contact during the alcoholic fermentation stage.**

### 1.3 Wine Spoilage — The "Rotten Apple in the Fruit Basket" Analogy

Wine spoilage is like a *carefully prepared meal that has been left out too long*. Lactic acid bacteria, wild yeasts (*Brettanomyces*), acetic acid bacteria, and oxidative enzymes (like **laccase** and **polyphenol oxidase**) are the invisible "germs in the kitchen" that, given oxygen, poor hygiene, or residual sugar, transform a beautiful vintage into vinegar, paint-thinner, or wet cardboard. Recognising these defects is a board examiner's favourite question.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Parameters (Bold Highlights)

- **Optimal Red Wine Fermentation Temperature:** **25 – 30 °C**
- **Optimal White Wine Fermentation Temperature:** **12 – 18 °C**
- **Primary Alcoholic Fermentation Duration (Red):** **5 – 14 days**
- **Primary Alcoholic Fermentation Duration (White):** **10 – 30 days**
- **Standard Ethanol Yield (Theoretical):** **0.511 g ethanol per g glucose** (Gay-Lussac stoichiometry)
- **Standard Brix of Grape Must:** **20 – 26 °Brix** (approx. **200 – 260 g/L sugar**)
- **Typical Final Wine Alcohol by Volume (ABV):** **10 – 14 % v/v**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Biochemistry of Alcoholic Fermentation (Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas Pathway)

The conversion of grape sugars to ethanol proceeds through **glycolysis followed by alcoholic fermentation**, catalysed by *Saccharomyces* yeasts:

1. **Inoculation / Lag Phase:** Indigenous or cultured yeast cells (≈ **10⁶ CFU/mL**) acclimatize to the must.
2. **Exponential Growth Phase:** Yeast multiplies rapidly, consuming dissolved oxygen and **pushing the medium into anaerobiosis**.
3. **Stationary / Fermentative Phase:** Pasteur effect triggers fermentation; **> 90 % of sugar is converted**.
4. **Decline Phase:** Nutrient depletion, ethanol toxicity (inhibition begins at **~ 8 % v/v**, lethal at **~ 15 – 18 % v/v**), sedimentation of yeast lees.

### 2.2 Red Wine Fermentation — The Maceration-Fermentation Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Red wines are made by fermenting the juice IN CONTACT with the grape skins (pomace).** This single operational decision drives the **extraction of anthocyanins (red pigments), tannins (proanthocyanidins), and varietal aroma precursors** into the fermenting must.

- **Anthocyanin Extraction:** Maximal at **25 – 30 °C**; occurs primarily during the first **3 – 5 days** of fermentation. Ethanol produced during fermentation acts as a *solvent*, breaking down skin cell walls and releasing pigments.
- **Tannin Extraction:** Occurs throughout fermentation; **seed tannins are more astringent than skin tannins**. Excessive extraction (over-maceration, very high temperatures, excessive pump-overs) yields harsh, bitter wine.
- **Punch-Down (Pigeage) & Pump-Over (Remontage):** Used to wet the floating cap of skins, prevent acetic spoilage, and ensure uniform extraction.
- **Cap Management:** Critical to prevent *Brettanomyces* and *Acetobacter* proliferation on the dry cap.

### 2.3 White Wine Fermentation — The Cleanliness-First Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **White wines are made by fermenting the clarified juice AFTER the skins have been removed.** Pressing precedes fermentation, and the juice is **settled (often with pectinase + SO₂ at 8 – 10 °C for 12 – 24 h)** to remove gross solids and wild microflora.

- **Cool Fermentation (12 – 18 °C):** Preserves the delicate **terpene and ester aromas** (e.g., linalool, isoamyl acetate "banana", ethyl hexanoate "green apple").
- **No Skin Contact:** Prevents extraction of harsh phenolics; yields pale, elegant wines.
- **Settle/Clarify → Rack → Inoculate → Ferment → Bentonite/Fining → Bottle.**
- **Varietal Exception:** Sauternes, Ice Wines, and some late-harvest whites undergo **skin contact ("maceration pelliculaire")** for 12 – 48 h to extract noble-rot (*Botrytis cinerea*) aromatics.

### 2.4 Secondary (Malolactic) Fermentation

After primary alcoholic fermentation, most red wines and many full-bodied whites (e.g., Chardonnay) undergo **malolactic fermentation (MLF)**, catalysed by *Oenococcus oeni*:

L-Malic Acid (dicarboxylic, harsh) → L-Lactic Acid (monocarboxylic, soft, buttery) + CO₂

This **deacidification** and **microbial stabilisation** round out the wine. The sensory signature is **diacetyl (2,3-butanedione)** — the "buttery" or "butterscotch" aroma, typically **1 – 4 mg/L**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| Parameter | Red Wine | White Wine |
|---|---|---|
| Skin Contact | **Yes (during AF)** | **No (pre-press)** |
| Fermentation Temp | **25 – 30 °C** | **12 – 18 °C** |
| Duration of AF | **5 – 14 days** | **10 – 30 days** |
| Cap Management | **Pump-over / Punch-down 2 – 3×/day** | **Not required (juice only)** |
| Colour Source | **Anthocyanins from skins** | **None (or flash détente technique)** |
| Tannin Level | **High (200 – 500 mg/L catechins)** | **Low (< 50 mg/L)** |
| Dominant Yeast | *S. cerevisiae* (often with *S. bayanus* for high Brix) | *S. cerevisiae* (often with *S. bayanus* in cool climates) |
| MLF Practice | **Standard** | **Optional (style-dependent)** |
| Headspace Gas | **CO₂ blanketing (cap protection)** | **N₂ / CO₂ blanketing** |
| SO₂ Use | **Antimicrobial + Antioxidant (10 – 30 ppm free SO₂)** | **Higher (20 – 50 ppm free SO₂) for juice protection** |

### 2.6 Stoichiometric Foundations (KTU Board Favourite)

The classic Gay-Lussac equation for alcoholic fermentation:

C₆H₁₂O₆  →  2 C₂H₅OH  +  2 CO₂  +  Heat (~ 118 kJ per mol glucose)

Additional yeast-by-product reactions (sensory-critical):

1. **Glycerol-Pyruvic Pathway:** Glucose → **Glycerol** + Acetaldehyde → Acetic Acid (residual)
2. **Ehrlich Pathway (Higher Alcohols):** Amino acids → Fusel alcohols (isoamyl, isobutanol)
3. **H₂S / Mercaptan Formation:** Sulphate reduction by yeast under nitrogen deficiency → **rotten-egg, rubber, onion off-odours**
4. **Acetaldehyde (Ethanal) Formation:** Pyruvate → Acetaldehyde → Ethanol; accumulated acetaldehyde = "sherry-like / bruised apple" oxidation defect

### 2.7 Wine Defects and Spoilage — The Master Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Principal Classes of Wine Defects (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Microbiological Spoilage** — Bacteria, wild yeasts
> 2. **Chemical/Oxidative Spoilage** — Oxygen, enzymatic browning
> 3. **Sensory Taints** — Cork, Brett, reduction, geranium tone, mousiness

#### A. Microbiological Defects

| Defect | Causative Agent | Sensory Signature | Threshold (approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volatile Acidity (VA)** | *Acetobacter aceti*, *A. pasteurianus* (acetic acid bacteria) | **Vinegar, nail-polish remover** (acetic acid + ethyl acetate) | > **0.7 g/L acetic acid** (legal limit in many regions) |
| **Brettanomyces (Brett)** | *Brettanomyces bruxellensis*, *B. anomalus* | **Barnyard, horse sweat, leather, smoky, Band-Aid** (4-ethylphenol, 4-ethylguaiacol) | > **0.6 mg/L 4-EP** |
| **Mousiness** | *Brettanomyces*, some *Lactobacillus* spp. | **Mouse-cage, soiled gym clothes** (2-acetyltetrahydropyridine, ATHP) | Detectable at **ng/L** levels |
| **Malolactic Deviation** | *Pediococcus*, rogue *Lactobacillus* | **Ropy / slimy texture** (exopolysaccharide "ropy wine") + excessive diacetyl | Visual |
| **Mannitic Fermentation** | *Lactobacillus brevis* / *L. plantarum* (in high-sugar wines) | **Manzanilla sherry, fizzy, medicinal** (mannitol from fructose) | — |
| **Lactic "Geranium" Taint** | *Lactobacillus* + sorbic acid (added preservative) | **Crushed geranium leaves / potpourri** (2-ethoxyhexa-3,5-diene from sorbate metabolism) | — |

#### B. Chemical / Oxidative Defects

| Defect | Cause | Sensory Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Acetaldehyde Excess (Oxidation)** | O₂ exposure, *A. aceti*, depleted SO₂ | **Sherry, bruised apple, nutty, vinegar-like** |
| **Enzymatic Browning** | Polyphenol oxidase (PPO) on crushed grapes | **Brown / orange discolouration** in whites; loss of red fruit in reds |
| **Non-Enzymatic Browning (Maillard)** | Prolonged ageing, high pH, residual sugar | **Dark amber, caramel, dried-fruit, soy-sauce** |
| **TCA Cork Taint** | 2,4,6-Trichloroanisole from chlorinated cork treatment | **Mouldy cardboard, wet dog, damp basement** (detection threshold **1 – 5 ng/L**) |
| **Mercaptan / Sulphide Off-Odours (Reduction)** | Low SO₂, nutrient deficiency (N, P, vitamins), high reducing environment | **Rotten egg (H₂S), burnt rubber / garlic (mercaptans), onion / cabbage (methyl mercaptan), canned corn (DMS)** |
| **Metallic / Tin Defect** | Contact with corroded metal, low pH | **Blood / iron, metallic, tinny** |
| **UV Light Strike** | Riboflavin (B₂) photo-degradation in clear bottles | **Wet wool, wet dog, struck match** (riboflavin → dimethyl sulphide) |

#### C. Other Commercial Taints

- **Ladybug Taint (Ladybug: Harmonia axyridis):** **Peanut, bell pepper, asparagus** (alkyl-methoxypyrazines); threshold **< 0.01 ng/L**.
- **Methyl Anthranilate (MA):** From Concord-type grapes; sometimes perceived as "foxy."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Stoichiometric Derivation — Theoretical Ethanol Yield

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Gay-Lussac equation is the most frequently asked 3-mark derivation in the KTU Beverage Processing module. Memorise the molecular masses and the yield factor **0.511 g/g**.

**Given:** 1 mole of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) ferments to 2 moles of ethanol + 2 moles of CO₂.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced equation:**

C₆H₁₂O₆  ⟶  2 C₂H₅OH  +  2 CO₂

**Step 2 — Molecular masses (g/mol):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{glucose}} &= (6 \times 12) + (12 \times 1) + (6 \times 16) = 180 \text{ g/mol} \\
M_{\text{ethanol}} &= (2 \times 12) + (6 \times 1) + (1 \times 16) = 46 \text{ g/mol} \\
M_{\text{CO}_2} &= (1 \times 12) + (2 \times 16) = 44 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical mass yield of ethanol per gram of glucose:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{ethanol}} &= \frac{2 \times M_{\text{ethanol}}}{1 \times M_{\text{glucose}}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 46}{180} \\
&= \frac{92}{180} \\
&= 0.5111 \text{ g ethanol / g glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical mass yield of CO₂:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{CO}_2} &= \frac{2 \times M_{\text{CO}_2}}{1 \times M_{\text{glucose}}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 44}{180} \\
&= 0.4889 \text{ g CO}_2 \text{ / g glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Practical ethanol yield (yeast efficiency factor):**

Industrial yeast achieves **90 – 95 %** of the theoretical yield due to biomass, glycerol, and by-product formation.

$$
Y_{\text{practical}} = 0.511 \times 0.92 \approx 0.470 \text{ g ethanol / g glucose}
$$

**Step 6 — Worked Example (KTU Typical):**

A grape must contains **220 g/L** of fermentable sugars. Determine the theoretical and practical ethanol concentration (in % v/v) at completion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical ethanol} &= 220 \times 0.511 = 112.4 \text{ g/L} \\
\text{Practical ethanol} &= 220 \times 0.470 = 103.4 \text{ g/L} \\
\% \text{ v/v} &= \frac{103.4}{789 \text{ (density of ethanol at 20°C, g/L)}} \times 100 \\
&\approx 13.1 \% \text{ v/v}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Energy Yield of Alcoholic Fermentation (ATP Accounting)

> [!NOTE]
> Compared to aerobic respiration (36 – 38 ATP/glucose), alcoholic fermentation yields only **2 ATP/glucose** — this is *why* yeast grows anaerobically to a limited cell density.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Glycolysis (net)} &: \text{Glucose} \rightarrow 2 \text{ Pyruvate} + 2 \text{ ATP} + 2 \text{ NADH} \\
\text{Pyruvate Decarboxylation} &: \text{Pyruvate} \rightarrow \text{Acetaldehyde} + \text{CO}_2 \\
\text{Alcohol Dehydrogenase} &: \text{Acetaldehyde} + \text{NADH} \rightarrow \text{Ethanol} + \text{NAD}^+ \\
\hline
\text{Net yield} &: 2 \text{ ATP per mole of glucose} \\
\text{Heat released} &\approx 118 \text{ kJ per mole of glucose} \text{ (exothermic)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Anthocyanin Extraction Kinetics (Simplified)

For a 1st-order diffusion model describing anthocyanin transfer from skins to fermenting must:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC}{dt} &= k \cdot (C_s - C) \\
C(t) &= C_s \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $C(t)$ = anthocyanin concentration in must at time $t$; $C_s$ = saturation concentration (≈ **500 mg/L** for *Vitis vinifera* reds); $k$ = rate constant (≈ **0.15 – 0.35 day⁻¹** at **25 °C**; doubles roughly per 10 °C rise within range).

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, students should be able to estimate the **time to reach 90 % extraction**: $t_{90} = \frac{\ln(10)}{k} \approx \frac{2.3}{0.25} \approx 9.2$ days.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Defect Diagnosis

| Symptom in Wine | Probable Cause | Confirmatory Test | Corrective / Preventive Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vinegar, sharp acetic smell | *Acetobacter* (VA) | Distillation + titration (volatile acidity) | **Sulphite topping, sterile filtration, CO₂ blanketing** |
| Wet cardboard, mouldy cork | TCA contamination | GC-MS (chloroanisole) | **Replace cork supplier; use synthetic closures** |
| Rotten egg, burnt match | H₂S / mercaptans | Sniff test after copper sulphate addition | **Add CuSO₄ (≤ 0.5 mg/L), aerate briefly, rack off lees** |
| Cloudy + slimy in bottle | *Pediococcus* (ropy) | Microscopic Gram+ cocci in chains | **Filtration, SO₂ adjustment, sterile bottling** |
| Pink-orange hue in white | Oxidation / PPO activity | Spectrophotometry (A420) | **Maintain SO₂ 0.8 ppm molecular; inert gas blanketing** |
| Mouse-cage on back palate | *Brettanomyces* (ATHP) | Sniff after neutralisation; PCR | **Sanitation, SO₂ management, sterile filtration** |
| Geranium on sparkling wine | Sorbic acid + *Lactobacillus* | Confirm sorbate use | **Avoid sorbate in wines undergoing MLF** |
| Bell pepper / peanut | Harmonia axyridis (ladybug) | Visual inspection of clusters at harvest | **Hand-sorting, soft pressing** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Red Wine Fermentation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Harvested Red Grapes] --> B[Sorting and Destemming]
    B --> C[Crushing and Must Preparation]
    C --> D[SO2 Addition 30 to 50 ppm and pH Adjust to 3.3 to 3.6]
    D --> E[Inoculation with S cerevisiae 10 to the 6 CFU per mL]
    E --> F[Primary Alcoholic Fermentation 25 to 30 C 5 to 14 days]
    F --> G[Cap Management Pump Over and Punch Down 2 to 3 times per day]
    G --> H[Pressing and Separation of Pomace]
    H --> I[Malolactic Fermentation Oenococcus oeni 18 to 22 C]
    I --> J[Racking and Lees Removal]
    J --> K[Maturation Oak or Stainless Steel 6 to 24 months]
    K --> L[Fining and Filtration]
    L --> M[Bottling]
    M --> N[Finished Red Wine]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — White Wine Fermentation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Harvested White Grapes Cool Night Harvest] --> B1[Sorting and Gentle Pressing]
    B1 --> C1[Must Clarification Settling 12 to 24 h at 8 to 10 C with Pectinase and SO2]
    C1 --> D1[Racking of Clear Juice]
    D1 --> E1[Inoculation with S cerevisiae Cool Fermentation Strain]
    E1 --> F1[Primary Alcoholic Fermentation 12 to 18 C 10 to 30 days]
    F1 --> G1[Optional Malolactic Fermentation Style Dependent]
    G1 --> H1[Lees Ageing Sur Lie for Body 1 to 6 months]
    H1 --> I1[Bentonite Fining and Protein Stabilisation]
    I1 --> J1[Cold Stabilisation Tartrate]
    J1 --> K1[Filtration 0.45 micron Sterile]
    K1 --> L1[Bottling with Inert Gas Headspace]
    L1 --> M1[Finished White Wine]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Wine Spoilage Causation Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SpoilageAgents[Wine Spoilage Agents]
        AB[Acetobacter]
        BR[Brettanomyces]
        LB[Lactobacillus and Pediococcus]
        PC[Penicillium Cork TCA]
    end
    subgraph Defects[Wine Defects]
        VA[Volatile Acidity Vinegar]
        BT[Brett Taint Barnyard]
        RO[Ropy Texture Mousiness]
        CK[Cork Taint Mouldy Cardboard]
    end
    subgraph Drivers[Contributing Factors]
        OX[Oxygen Exposure]
        HY[Poor Sanitation]
        SU[Low SO2]
        HI[High pH and Residual Sugar]
    end
    AB --> VA
    BR --> BT
    BR --> RO
    LB --> RO
    PC --> CK
    OX --> VA
    HY --> BT
    HY --> RO
    SU --> VA
    SU --> BT
    HI --> VA
    HI --> RO
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Comparative Architecture of Red vs White Fermentation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RedProcess[RED WINE FERMENTATION ARCHITECTURE]
        R1[Grapes] --> R2[Crush with Skins]
        R2 --> R3[Ferment 25 to 30 C with Skins]
        R3 --> R4[Press to Remove Pomace]
        R4 --> R5[MLF Standard]
        R5 --> R6[Oak or Steel Ageing]
        R6 --> R7[Bottle]
    end
    subgraph WhiteProcess[WHITE WINE FERMENTATION ARCHITECTURE]
        W1[Grapes] --> W2[Press First to Remove Skins]
        W2 --> W3[Clarify Juice]
        W3 --> W4[Ferment 12 to 18 C Juice Only]
        W4 --> W5[Optional MLF]
        W5 --> W6[Steel or Light Oak Ageing]
        W6 --> W7[Bottle]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]`**
**Differentiate between red and white wine fermentation in terms of (a) skin contact, (b) temperature regime, and (c) phenolic extraction. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
> (a) In **red wine fermentation**, crushed grape must is fermented **in contact with skins** (pomac fermentation), allowing extraction of anthocyanins and tannins. In **white wine fermentation**, the juice is **pressed and clarified first**, then fermented without skins.
> (b) Red wines ferment at **25 – 30 °C** to optimise anthocyanin extraction, while white wines ferment cool at **12 – 18 °C** to preserve delicate terpene and ester aromas.
> (c) Red wines extract significant **tannins (200 – 500 mg/L catechins) and anthocyanins (red pigments)**, while white wines are deliberately low in phenolics (< 50 mg/L) and remain pale yellow/straw-coloured.

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]`**
**List any three common microbiological spoilage defects in wine along with their causative organisms. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
> 1. **Volatile Acidity (VA):** Caused by *Acetobacter aceti* and *Gluconobacter oxydans*; produces acetic acid and ethyl acetate → vinegar, nail-polish remover aroma.
> 2. **Brettanomyces Taint:** Caused by *Brettanomyces bruxellensis*; produces 4-ethylphenol and 4-ethylguaiacol → barnyard, horse sweat, smoky Band-Aid aroma.
> 3. **Ropy Wine / Mannitic Fermentation:** Caused by *Pediococcus parvulus* and *Lactobacillus brevis*; produces exopolysaccharides or mannitol → slimy texture or manzanilla-like off-odour.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU ESE Module 2 Model - Q Choice A]`**

**(a) Describe in detail the process of red wine fermentation, highlighting cap management, extraction kinetics, and the role of temperature. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**1. Crushing and Must Preparation (1 Mark):**
Harvested red grapes (*Vitis vinifera* cultivars such as Cabernet Sauvignon, Syrah, Pinot Noir) are sorted, destemmed, and gently crushed. **Sulphur dioxide (SO₂) is added at 30 – 50 ppm** for antimicrobial and antioxidant protection; must pH is adjusted to **3.3 – 3.6** using tartaric acid if necessary.

**2. Inoculation (1 Mark):**
Active dry yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, 10⁶ CFU/mL) is rehydrated and pitched into the must. Many wineries also add *Enoferm Syrah* or equivalent selected strains for varietal expression. Some rely on indigenous ("wild") fermentations.

**3. Cap Formation and Management (2 Marks):**
During fermentation, CO₂ evolution pushes the floating **cap of skins, seeds, and stems** to the surface. This cap must be kept wet to:
- Ensure uniform colour/tannin extraction
- Prevent **acetic acid bacteria proliferation** (the dry cap is a hotspot for *Acetobacter*)
- Avoid trapped heat and reductive spoilage

Methods include **Pigeage (punch-down)** — breaking the cap manually 2 – 3 times per day, and **Remontage (pump-over)** — pumping juice from the bottom over the cap. Modern wineries use **automatised pulsed-air or roto-fermenters**.

**4. Temperature Control (1 Mark):**
Red wines ferment at **25 – 30 °C**:
- Lower end (25 °C) favours **colour stability and fruity esters** in delicate varietals (Pinot Noir).
- Higher end (28 – 30 °C) accelerates **anthocyanin extraction** in tannic cultivars (Cabernet, Malbec).
- Exceeding **32 °C** risks **loss of volatile aroma compounds, yeast thermal death, and stuck fermentation**.

**5. Extraction Kinetics (1 Mark):**
Anthocyanin extraction follows **first-order diffusion kinetics** $C(t) = C_s (1 - e^{-kt})$; peak extraction occurs around **5 – 7 days**. Tannin extraction continues beyond alcohol peak (10 – 14 days). Standard maceration period: **7 – 14 days**, followed by pressing.

**6. Pressing and Run-Off (1 Mark):**
The free-run wine is drained; the pressed pomace yields press wine (richer in phenolics, but more astringent; often blended selectively).

**(b) Explain malolactic fermentation. Discuss its biochemical pathway, sensory impact, and conditions favouring *Oenococcus oeni* growth. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
Malolactic fermentation (MLF) is a **secondary bacterial fermentation** that converts **L-malic acid (dicarboxylic, harsh)** into **L-lactic acid (monocarboxylic, soft)** and **CO₂**, performed by lactic acid bacteria (LAB), principally ***Oenococcus oeni***.

**2. Biochemical Pathway (1 Mark):**
$$
\text{L-Malic Acid} \xrightarrow{\text{Malate decarboxylase (MpeA)}} \text{L-Lactic Acid} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow
$$
The reaction proceeds via the **malolactic enzyme**, a biotin-dependent decarboxylase.

**3. Effect on Acidity (1 Mark):**
One mole of dicarboxylic malic acid is replaced by one mole of monocarboxylic lactic acid. Net deacidification effect: **typically 0.1 – 0.3 % reduction in titratable acidity**, raising pH by **0.1 – 0.4 units**. This softens mouthfeel, especially important in cool-climate red wines.

**4. Sensory Impact (1 Mark):**
The most flavour-active by-product is **diacetyl (2,3-butanedione)**, produced from citrate metabolism. It imparts a **"buttery" or "butterscotch" aroma**, with optimal perception at **1 – 4 mg/L**. Above this threshold, it becomes overtly dairy-like (sour cream). The associated CO₂ release is desirable in red wines (improves mouthfeel) but problematic in sparkling wines.

**5. Conditions Favouring O. oeni (2 Marks):**
- **pH:** 3.0 – 3.8 (low pH below 3.0 inhibits growth)
- **Temperature:** 18 – 22 °C (too cold slows growth; too warm favours spoilage LAB)
- **SO₂:** Low free SO₂ (< 10 ppm); SO₂ inhibits LAB
- **Ethanol:** Strains tolerate up to 14 % v/v
- **Nutrients:** Amino acids, vitamins from autolysed yeast lees aid growth

**6. Industrial Practice (1 Mark):**
MLF is triggered after primary AF by **spontaneous growth of indigenous LAB** or, more reliably, by **inoculation with commercial *O. oeni* cultures** (e.g., *Lalvin VP41*, *Enoferm Beta*). Completion is monitored by **paper chromatography, HPLC, or enzymatic assay for L-malic acid** (target: **< 0.2 g/L residual malic acid**).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU ESE Module 2 Model - Q Choice B]`**

**(a) Discuss in detail the microbiological spoilage of wine. Differentiate between the spoilage caused by yeasts (*Brettanomyces*) and that caused by bacteria (*Acetobacter*, *Lactobacillus*). (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**1. Microbial Spoilage — Overview (1 Mark):**
Wine spoilage microorganisms are primarily **yeasts, lactic acid bacteria, and acetic acid bacteria**. They cause off-aromas, haze, deposits, and texture defects, typically triggered by **poor hygiene, residual sugar, low SO₂, oxygen ingress, or improper temperature**.

**2. *Brettanomyces* Spoilage (2 Marks):**
*Brettanomyces bruxellensis* and *B. anomalus* are **slow-growing, ethanol-tolerant ("spoilage") yeasts** that survive in cellar surfaces, barrel staves, and bottling equipment. They produce:
- **4-Ethylphenol (4-EP)** → barnyard, horse sweat, Band-Aid aroma (detection threshold ≈ **0.6 mg/L**)
- **4-Ethylguaiacol (4-EG)** → smoky, spicy, leather
- **Isovaleric acid** → cheesy, sweaty
- **2-Acetyltetrahydropyridine (ATHP)** → mouse-cage taint

**Control:** Sanitation of all equipment, **SO₂ management (0.5 – 0.8 ppm molecular SO₂)**, sterile filtration (< 0.45 µm), and avoiding prolonged barrel ageing of high-pH wines.

**3. *Acetobacter* Spoilage — Volatile Acidity (2 Marks):**
*Acetobacter aceti* and *A. pasteurianus* are **obligately aerobic acetic acid bacteria (AAB)**. They oxidise ethanol into **acetic acid and ethyl acetate** (nail-polish remover, vinegar aroma) via the membrane-bound **alcohol dehydrogenase** and **aldehyde dehydrogenase** enzyme systems. Triggered by:
- **Oxygen exposure** (open vats, leaky barrels, headspace)
- **Low SO₂**
- **Insect vectors** (*Drosophila* fruit flies)
**Control:** Complete CO₂ or N₂ blanketing of headspaces, **SO₂ topping after racking**, clean winery environment, and **legal volatile acidity limits (0.7 – 1.2 g/L depending on region)**.

**4. *Lactobacillus* and *Pediococcus* Spoilage (2 Marks):**
These LAB are more diverse in their spoilage potential:
- **Ropy Wine:** *Pediococcus parvulus* produces exopolysaccharide (β-glucan) capsules → **oily, slimy texture** (visual haze, increased viscosity).
- **Mannitic Fermentation:** *L. brevis*, *L. plantarum* in high-sugar wines reduce fructose to **mannitol** → manzanilla sherry-like off-odour, slight effervescence (CO₂).
- **Geranium Taint:** When *L. plantarum* metabolises **sorbic acid** (added as a yeast inhibitor) → **2-ethoxyhexa-3,5-diene** → geranium-potpourri aroma (problematic in sweet and sparkling wines).

**(b) Enumerate the major chemical and oxidative wine defects, with an emphasis on the role of oxygen, SO₂, and metal ions in their formation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**1. Oxidative Defects (2 Marks):**
- **Acetaldehyde (ethanal) excess:** When ethanol is oxidised by dissolved O₂, especially when SO₂ is depleted. Imparts a **sherry, bruised apple, nutty** off-aroma. Threshold ≈ **100 mg/L**.
- **Enzymatic Browning (PPO/Tyrosinase/Laccase):** Polyphenol oxidase (PPO) catalyses the oxidation of *o*-diphenols (caftaric acid, coutaric acid) into *o*-quinones, which polymerise to **brown polymers** visible as a yellow-brown shift in whites and loss of red fruit in reds.
- **Non-Enzymatic Browning (Maillard / Ascorbic Acid Oxidation):** Slow carbonyl-amine reactions in stored wines, accelerated by **high pH, residual sugar, and warm storage** → amber colour, **caramel, soy-sauce, dried-fruit** off-flavours.

**2. Reduction-Related Defects (2 Marks):**
- **Hydrogen Sulphide (H₂S):** Formed by **sulphate reduction in yeast** under nitrogen, pantothenate, or pyridoxine deficiency. Threshold ≈ **1 – 5 µg/L**. Aroma: **rotten egg, sewage**.
- **Mercaptans (Thiols):** Methyl mercaptan (CH₃SH, cabbage), ethyl mercaptan (CH₃CH₂SH, garlic, onion), and dimethyl sulphide (DMS, canned corn) are degradation products of H₂S in the presence of ethanol/acetaldehyde.
- **Light Strike (Goût de Lumière):** UV light catalyses **riboflavin (vitamin B₂)** to produce **dimethyl sulphide (DMS)** → wet wool, wet dog aroma; avoided by using **amber bottles or non-fluorescent packaging**.

**3. Sulphite-Related and Metal Defects (2 Marks):**
- **Sulphur Dioxide Excess:** At > **50 ppm free SO₂**, wines exhibit a **"burnt match" or pungent sulphur aroma**. A balance is needed between antimicrobial protection and sensory neutrality.
- **Cork Taint (TCA):** **2,4,6-Trichloroanisole** at **1 – 5 ng/L** → musty, mouldy-cardboard aroma. Originates from chlorine-based cork treatment; modern TCA-free cork production uses **peroxide-based sterilisation**.
- **Metallic Defects:** Iron (Fe³⁺ > 10 mg/L) and copper (Cu²⁺ > 0.5 mg/L) catalyses oxidative reactions. **Ferric casse** (iron phosphate haze) is visible in white wines; **cupric casse** (copper-protein complex) appears in red wines. Avoided by using **stainless steel equipment and citric acid additions**.

**4. Preventive Strategies (1 Mark):**
- **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂):** At **0.5 – 0.8 ppm molecular SO₂** for microbial control; **0.3 – 0.5 ppm** as antioxidant; total SO₂ capped at **350 ppm legal limit** in most regions.
- **Inert Gas Blanketing:** CO₂ or N₂ in headspaces; sparging during transfers.
- **Cold Stabilisation / Tartrate Stabilisation** to prevent crystal haze.
- **Appropriate Closure Selection** (screwcap, synthetic cork, TCA-free natural cork).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing malolactic fermentation (MLF) with primary alcoholic fermentation (AF).** MLF is **bacterial**, not yeast-driven; do NOT write "MLF converts sugar to lactic acid." It converts **L-malic acid → L-lactic acid + CO₂**.
> 2. **Mixing up *Acetobacter* and *Brettanomyces* symptoms.** Acetobacter → vinegar (acetic acid); Brett → barnyard (4-ethylphenol). Examiners deduct 1 mark per confusion.
> 3. **Omitting the temperature parameter.** Every wine question must specify **°C values** (red: 25 – 30; white: 12 – 18). Writing "warm" or "cool" without numerical values costs ½ mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the causative volatile compound.** Brettanomyces → 4-EP / 4-EG; Acetobacter → acetic acid + ethyl acetate; Cork taint → TCA. Generic answers lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Saying "yeast fermentation makes red wine red."** Colour comes from **anthocyanins extracted from grape skins** during fermentation, not from the yeast metabolism itself.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Module 2 Recap — Wine Fermentation, Defects, and Spoilage**

- ☐ **Wine fermentation = yeast (*S. cerevisiae*) converts grape sugars → ethanol + CO₂**, predominantly via the EMP (glycolysis) pathway.
- ☐ **Stoichiometry:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂; **theoretical ethanol yield = 0.511 g/g glucose**; practical yield ≈ **0.470 g/g**.
- ☐ **Red wine** = *fermentation with skins*; **25 – 30 °C**; anthocyanin and tannin extraction; **7 – 14 days**; cap management essential.
- ☐ **White wine** = *fermentation of clarified juice only*; **12 – 18 °C**; preserves delicate aroma esters; **10 – 30 days**.
- ☐ **Malolactic fermentation (MLF)** = bacterial; L-malic → L-lactic + CO₂; *Oenococcus oeni*; **18 – 22 °C, pH 3.0 – 3.8, SO₂ < 10 ppm**; produces diacetyl (buttery).
- ☐ **Microbiological spoilage** = *Acetobacter* (VA/vinegar), *Brettanomyces* (barnyard, 4-EP/4-EG), *Lactobacillus* (ropy, mousiness, geranium, mannitic), *Pediococcus* (ropy).
- ☐ **Chemical/oxidative defects** = acetaldehyde (sherry), enzymatic browning (PPO), non-enzymatic browning (Maillard), reduction (H₂S, mercaptans), TCA (cork taint), light strike (DMS).
- ☐ **SO₂** is the master preservative: **0.5 – 0.8 ppm molecular SO₂** antimicrobial; **0.3 – 0.5 ppm** antioxidant; total SO₂ ≤ **350 ppm legal**.
- ☐ **Volatile acidity legal limit ≈ 0.7 – 1.2 g/L** (region-dependent); 4-EP Brett threshold ≈ **0.6 mg/L**; TCA threshold ≈ **1 – 5 ng/L**.
- ☐ **Always cite temperature, time, and the specific volatile compound** responsible for any defect in exam answers — examiners reward numerical precision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Malt and Grain, Whisky
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Malt and Grain, Whisky

# 🍾 Malt, Grain, and Whisky — The Foundation of Distilled Spirits

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Malt?

In the context of beverage processing, **malt** refers to cereal grain (most commonly **barley** for whisky, though rye, wheat, and corn can also be malted) that has been subjected to a controlled partial germination process. This process activates and develops the grain's native enzymes—primarily **amylase**, **protease**, and **dexttrinase**—which are essential for converting starches into fermentable sugars.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Malt is germinated cereal grain that has been dried (and often kilned) under controlled conditions. The malting process converts insoluble starch reserves into soluble sugars and amino acids, making the grain biologically active for the subsequent mashing and fermentation stages in beverage production.

#### Conceptual Analogy 🧠
Think of a seed in soil. When a barley kernel absorbs water and begins to sprout, it is essentially "waking up" and preparing to feed the new plant. To do this, it produces enzymes that break down its starchy food reserve into sugar. The distiller hijacks this natural biological process—just enough to produce lots of sugar, but stopping the growth before the seedling actually uses that sugar up. The result is a sweet, enzymatically-active grain ready to be mashed.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Big Roles of Malt:**
> 1. **Enzyme Source:** Provides amylases to convert starch → sugar.
> 2. **Sugar Source:** The starch itself becomes the feedstock.
> 3. **Flavor Source:** Kilning/roasting develops color and flavor compounds (Maillard reactions).

### 1.2 What is Whisky?

**Whisky (or whiskey)** is a distilled spirit manufactured from a fermented mash of cereal grains, matured in wooden casks (typically oak) for a legally defined minimum period. The grain is mashed, fermented with yeast, distilled, and aged.

#### Key Terminology Variations

| Spelling | Origin | Typical Aging |
|----------|--------|---------------|
| **Whisky** | Scotland, Canada, Japan | $\geq$ **3 years** (Scotch) |
| **Whiskey** | Ireland, USA | $\geq$ **2 years** (Bourbon) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard ABV of Commercial Whisky:** Typically **40% ABV** (Alcohol By Volume) or **80 US Proof**, which is the minimum legal bottling strength in most jurisdictions.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Grains Used in Whisky Production

Whisky classification is fundamentally tied to the grain bill used:

| Whisky Class | Primary Grain | Region |
|--------------|---------------|--------|
| **Malt Whisky** | 100% malted barley | Scotland, Ireland |
| **Grain Whisky** | Wheat/maize + small malt % | Scotland |
| **Bourbon** | $\geq$ 51% corn | USA |
| **Rye Whiskey** | $\geq$ 51% rye | USA, Canada |
| **Single Pot Still** | Malted + unmalted barley | Ireland |

> [!NOTE]
> The grain used is not just a flavor choice—**legal definitions in most whisky-producing countries mandate the exact grain composition**.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Malting Process — Step-by-Step Science

Malting is fundamentally an exercise in **controlling biological activity**. There are three classical phases:

#### Phase 1: Steeping 🌊
- Barley is immersed in water at **10–16°C** for **2–3 days**.
- Moisture content rises from **12% → 42–46%**.
- Goal: Trigger germination by hydrating the embryo.

#### Phase 2: Germination 🌱
- Drained barley is spread on a malting floor or in a Saladin box.
- Lasts **4–6 days**, temperature held at **15–20°C**.
- **Chitting** (rootlet emergence) is the visual marker of progress.
- The aleurone layer releases **gibberellic acid**, signaling the endosperm to synthesize **α-amylase** and **β-amylase**.

#### Phase 3: Kilning 🔥
- Green malt is dried using hot air.
- Temperature profile determines final malt character:
  - **Low-temperature kilning** (60–70°C): Pale malt → light color, neutral flavor → used for gin/vodka.
  - **High-temperature kilning** (120–160°C): Heavily peated malt → smoky, phenolic → used for Islay Scotch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Green Malt" Window:** Distillers take malt **before** all moisture is driven off because over-drying destroys enzymes. Most distillers therefore use **air-dried malt** and may even add the malt to the mash unmalted-style after partial kilning.

### 2.2 The Mashing Process

After malting, the grain is milled (grist) and mixed with hot water in a **mash tun**.

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Mash Temperature | **63–65°C** (starch saccharification optimum) |
| Mash pH | **5.0–5.5** |
| Water-to-Grain Ratio | **2.5:1 to 3:1** |
| Mash Time | **45–90 minutes** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 65°C?** This is the optimum temperature for α-amylase activity, which aggressively breaks down starch into dextrins and maltose. At higher temperatures (~70°C), β-amylase becomes dominant, producing more maltose.

### 2.3 Fermentation (Washing)

The cooled wort (~20°C) is transferred to a **washback** where yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) ferments sugars into ethanol and CO₂.

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast}} 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2 + \text{Heat}$$

Fermentation lasts **48–96 hours**, yielding **wash** with **~8–10% ABV**.

### 2.4 Distillation

Two main still types are relevant:

| Still Type | Spirit Type | Typical Number of Distillations |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pot Still** (batch) | Malt whisky | 2 (Scotland), 3 (Ireland) |
| **Patent/Column Still** (continuous) | Grain whisky | 1 (continuous) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heads, Hearts, and Tails:** The distillate is split into three fractions. Only the middle "hearts" fraction (containing the desired ethanol concentration, typically **65–70% ABV**) is retained for maturation.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| $Y_{ethanol} = 0.51 \times S \times \eta_{ferment}$ | Theoretical ethanol yield (Y) from starch (S) | Litres ethanol / tonne grain |
| $\eta_{ferment} \approx 0.85 - 0.95$ | Fermentation efficiency | dimensionless |
| $\%ABV = \frac{V_{ethanol}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | Alcohol by volume | % |
| $E_{activation} \approx 65°C$ | Optimum α-amylase activation temp | °C |
| $\text{Proof}_{US} = 2 \times \%ABV$ | US Proof conversion | dimensionless |
| $M_{moisture}^{malt} \approx 4-5\%$ | Final malt moisture after kilning | % |
| $T_{maturation} \geq 3 \, \text{years}$ | Legal Scotch maturation | years |
| $T_{maturation} \geq 2 \, \text{years}$ | Legal Bourbon/Rye maturation | years |
| $\text{Angel's Share} \approx 2\%/\text{year}$ | Evaporation loss during aging | % volume/year |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the **theoretical** yield (0.51 L/kg starch) with the **actual** yield. Real-world yields are **~0.40 L pure ethanol per kg grain** after losses in mashing, fermentation, and distillation.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Starch

The stoichiometric basis comes from the Gay-Lussac equation of alcoholic fermentation:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$$

**Step 1: Compute molar masses.**
- Glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$): M = **180 g/mol**
- Ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$): M = **46 g/mol**

**Step 2: Calculate mass conversion ratio.**

$$\frac{m_{ethanol}}{m_{glucose}} = \frac{2 \times 46}{180} = \frac{92}{180} \approx 0.511$$

Therefore, per kg of glucose, the maximum theoretical ethanol is **511 grams**.

**Step 3: Convert mass to volume using ethanol density.**

$$\rho_{ethanol} \approx 789 \, \text{kg/m}^3 = 0.789 \, \text{kg/L}$$

$$V_{ethanol} = \frac{0.511 \, \text{kg}}{0.789 \, \text{kg/L}} \approx 0.648 \, \text{L} / \text{kg glucose}$$

**Step 4: Account for starch-to-glucose hydrolysis.**

Since starch is a polymer of glucose, the yield must be adjusted for hydrolysis efficiency. The starch content of malted barley is roughly **60–65%**, and conversion efficiency $\eta$ is typically **85–95%**:

$$Y_{ethanol}^{real} = 0.648 \times S_{starch\%} \times \eta_{conversion} \times \eta_{fermentation}$$

For a typical malt with 62% starch, 90% conversion, and 90% fermentation:

$$Y = 0.648 \times 0.62 \times 0.90 \times 0.90 \approx 0.325 \, \text{L ethanol} / \text{kg malt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Distillers typically use **~3 kg of malt to produce 1 L of pure alcohol** at the distillation stage, before maturation losses.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Distillation Cuts Comparison

| Cut Fraction | Composition | ABV Range | Use |
|--------------|------------|-----------|-----|
| **Foreshots (Heads)** | Methanol, acetone, aldehydes | > 80% ABV | Re-distilled or discarded |
| **Hearts** | Ethanol, esters, congeners | 65–80% ABV | **Retained for maturation** |
| **Feints/Tails** | Fusel oils, fatty acids | < 65% ABV | Re-distilled |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **congener profile** (esters, aldehydes, higher alcohols) defines the whisky's flavor signature. This is why pot stills with rectification produce heavier, more flavorful malt whisky compared to continuous-still grain whisky.

### 3.3 Maturation Chemistry — The "Angel's Share"

During oak aging, three simultaneous processes occur:

1. **Extraction:** Compounds from wood (vanillin, lactones, tannins) leach into spirit.
2. **Oxidation:** Slow reaction with O₂ through wood pores creates esters and aldehydes.
3. **Evaporation:** Loss of ethanol and water to atmosphere.

$$V_{final} = V_{initial} \times (1 - r)^{t}$$

Where $r$ is the annual loss rate (~2%) and $t$ is years of aging.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Whisky Production Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Barley Grain Intake] --> B[Steeping Tank - Water 10 to 16 C - 2 to 3 Days]
    B --> C[Germination Floor - 4 to 6 Days at 15 to 20 C]
    C --> D[Kilning - Heat Drying at 60 to 160 C]
    D --> E[Milled Grist]
    E --> F[Mash Tun - Mix with Hot Water at 65 C]
    F --> G[Conversion of Starch to Sugar by Amylase]
    G --> H[Washback - Fermentation with Yeast 48 to 96 Hours]
    H --> I[Wash - 8 to 10 percent ABV]
    I --> J[Pot Still 1 - First Distillation Low Wines]
    J --> K[Pot Still 2 - Second Distillation Spirit]
    K --> L[Heart Cut Collection - 65 to 70 percent ABV]
    L --> M[Oak Cask Maturation - Minimum 3 Years for Scotch]
    M --> N[Vatting and Blending]
    N --> O[Bottling at Minimum 40 percent ABV]
```

### 4.2 Malting Process Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Barley - 12 percent Moisture] --> B[Steeping Stage - 36 to 48 Hours]
    B --> C[Germination - 4 to 6 Days]
    C --> D[Green Malt - 42 to 46 percent Moisture]
    D --> E[Kilning Stage]
    E --> F[Low Temp Malt - 60 to 70 C Pale Color]
    E --> G[High Temp Malt - 120 to 160 C Peated Smoky]
    F --> H[Finished Malt - 4 to 5 percent Moisture]
    G --> H
```

### 4.3 Scotch Whisky Classification Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SW[Scotch Whisky] --> SM[Single Malt - 100 percent Malted Barley One Distillery]
    SW --> SG[Single Grain - Wheat or Maize with Malt One Distillery]
    SW --> BL[Blended Whisky - Mix of Malt and Grain Whiskies]
    SM --> MS[Malt Scotch]
    SG --> GS[Grain Scotch]
```

### 4.4 Grain-to-Bottle Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Raw Material Selection] --> P1[Pre Treatment Malting]
    P1 --> P2[Milling]
    P2 --> P3[Mashing and Saccharification]
    P3 --> P4[Fermentation]
    P4 --> P5[Distillation]
    P5 --> P6[Maturation in Oak]
    P6 --> P7[Quality Control and Bottling]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the term "malt" and briefly explain its role in whisky production.

**Model Answer:**
Malt is germinated cereal grain—typically barley—that has been dried under controlled conditions after a partial germination process. In whisky production, malt serves three critical roles: **(i)** it provides the enzymes (amylases) required to convert starch into fermentable sugars, **(ii)** it supplies the starch substrate itself, and **(iii)** it contributes flavor and color compounds developed during kilning. Without malting, the starch in raw grain would be inaccessible to yeast, making fermentation impossible.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** State the legal minimum maturation period for Scotch whisky and mention any two changes that occur in the spirit during aging.

**Model Answer:**
The legal minimum maturation period for Scotch whisky is **three years** (as per the Scotch Whisky Regulations 2009). During aging in oak casks, two major changes occur: **(i)** **Extraction** of wood compounds such as vanillin, tannins, and lactones from the cask into the spirit, contributing color, aroma, and flavor; and **(ii)** **Evaporation loss** known as the "Angel's Share," which concentrates remaining congeners.

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe the malting of barley with a neat flow diagram. Highlight the biochemical changes occurring during germination. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of malt and grain in whisky production. Compare single malt, grain whisky, and blended whisky. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q1(a):**

The malting of barley is a controlled three-stage biological process:

**Stage 1 — Steeping (Day 0–2):**
Cleaned barley is immersed in water at **10–16°C** for **36–72 hours**, with periodic air rests. Moisture content rises from **12% → 45%**. The embryo becomes metabolically active.

**Stage 2 — Germination (Day 2–7):**
Drained barley is spread on malting floors or in pneumatic Saladin boxes at **15–20°C**. Over **4–6 days**, the grain "chits" (rootlets emerge). Key biochemical changes:
- The aleurone layer releases **gibberellic acid (GA3)**, which diffuses to the starchy endosperm.
- GA3 induces synthesis of **α-amylase**, **β-amylase**, **protease**, and **β-glucanase**.
- **α-amylase** randomly cleaves starch → dextrins.
- **β-amylase** cleaves maltose units from starch chains → maltose.
- **Proteases** hydrolyze proteins → amino acids (yeast nutrition).
- Cell walls (β-glucans) are partially degraded, improving mash viscosity.

**Stage 3 — Kilning (Day 7–9):**
Germination is halted by drying with hot air. A typical Scotch malt is kilned at **60–70°C** (final 2 hours up to 80°C), while peated malts may reach **120–160°C**. Kilning reduces moisture to **~4–5%**, locks in enzymes, and develops color/flavor through **Maillard reactions** between reducing sugars and amino acids, producing melanoidins. Peat smoke introduces **phenols** (e.g., guaiacol, cresol) for smoky character.

The final malt is screened to remove rootlets (culms) and stored.

---

**Model Solution for Q1(b):**

**Role of Malt and Grain:**
Malt is the enzymatic and flavor backbone of whisky. Grain (typically wheat, maize, or unmalted barley in grain whisky) provides a cheaper starch source. The combination determines whisky classification:

| Type | Composition | Flavor Profile |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| **Single Malt** | 100% malted barley, one distillery | Complex, rich, full-bodied |
| **Single Grain** | Mainly wheat/maize with ~10% malt as enzyme source | Lighter, smoother, less peaty |
| **Blended Whisky** | Mix of single malts + grain whiskies | Balanced, consistent, popular globally |

**Comparison:**
- **Single Malt:** Produced in pot stills, double-distilled, retaining more congeners. Premium, expensive, varies by distillery.
- **Single Grain:** Produced in continuous column (Coffey) stills → lighter, higher yield, ~94% ABV off still.
- **Blended Whisky:** ~80–90% grain + 10–20% malt. The category dominates global Scotch sales (~90%).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete manufacturing process of Scotch whisky from grain to bottle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain maturation of whisky in oak casks. What is the "Angel's Share" and how does it impact economics of whisky production? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q2(a):**

**Block Diagram — Scotch Whisky Manufacture:**

```
Barley → Steeping → Germination → Kilning → Milling 
→ Mashing (65°C, with hot water) → Wort 
→ Cooling → Fermentation (yeast, 48–96 hrs) → Wash (~8% ABV) 
→ Distillation (2 pot stills) → New Make Spirit (~70% ABV) 
→ Oak Cask Maturation (≥3 yrs) → Vatting/Blending 
→ Bottling (≥40% ABV)
```

**Detailed Stages:**

1. **Malting and Milling:** Barley is steeped, germinated, kilned, and ground into grist (husk + grist + flour).

2. **Mashing:** Grist is mixed with hot water (3:1 water:grain) in a mash tun. Temperature ramps through 64–90°C. At 64°C, amylases convert starch → fermentable sugars (mostly maltose). Liquid "wort" is drained; "sparge water" extracts residual sugars.

3. **Fermentation:** Cooled wort (20°C) is pitched with yeast in a washback. After 48–96 hours, the wash contains ~8% ABV plus hundreds of congeners (esters, higher alcohols, acids).

4. **Distillation:** Two copper pot stills (wash still + spirit still). The first distillation produces "low wines" (~20% ABV); the second produces new-make spirit (~70% ABV) after separating foreshots, hearts, and feints.

5. **Maturation:** Spirit is filled into oak casks (often ex-bourbon or ex-sherry) and aged ≥3 years.

6. **Vatting and Bottling:** Multiple casks are blended for consistency, diluted to bottling strength (typically 40–46% ABV), and bottled.

---

**Model Solution for Q2(b):**

**Oak Cask Maturation:**

During aging, three phenomena occur simultaneously:
- **Extraction of wood compounds:** Vanillin (vanilla), syringaldehyde (spice), lactones (coconut/wood), tannins (astringency), and color (from lignin oxidation).
- **Oxidation:** Slow ingress of oxygen through wood pores produces aldehydes and esters.
- **Evaporation:** Loss of ethanol and water ("Angel's Share"), increasing relative congener concentration.

**The Angel's Share:**

The Angel's Share refers to the **~2% volume of whisky lost per year** to evaporation through the oak cask walls. Over a 12-year maturation, this can mean a **~22% reduction in cask volume**.

**Economic Impact:**
- **Direct volume loss:** A distillery filling 10,000 L in year 0 will recover only ~7,800 L after 12 years.
- **Inventory carrying cost:** Capital is locked in the cask for years, with zero revenue generation during aging.
- **Compensating factors:** The aged product commands a premium price (often 5–10× the new-make spirit price), justifying the loss.
- **Warehouse climate control:** Scotland's cool, humid climate reduces loss rate vs. hotter Kentucky warehouses, where bourbon can lose 5–8% per year (though climate adds unique character).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mistake 1:** Students often forget that maturation MUST occur in oak and CANNOT be accelerated by artificial methods under Scotch law. Reusing a cask more than 2× is illegal in Scotch whisky.
> 2. **Mistake 2:** Confusing the **theoretical yield** (~0.65 L/kg glucose) with the **practical yield** (~0.32–0.40 L/kg malt) is a common error. Always show the conversion efficiency chain.
> 3. **Mistake 3:** Forgetting to mention the **role of peat** when discussing Islay malts. Peat is partially decomposed vegetable matter burned during kilning, depositing phenolic compounds.
> 4. **Mistake 4:** Writing "ethyl alcohol" or "C₂H₆O" instead of "ethanol (C₂H₅OH)" — KTU examiners penalize incorrect chemistry notation.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Malt** = germinated + dried cereal grain, enzymatically active, used for starch-to-sugar conversion.
- **Steeping** raises barley moisture to **~45%**; **germination** lasts **4–6 days at 15–20°C**; **kilning** at **60–160°C** determines flavor.
- The three enzymes critical to mashing are **α-amylase**, **β-amylase**, and **protease**.
- **Mashing temperature** sweet spot is **~65°C** for α-amylase optimum.
- **Yeast fermentation** follows $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$.
- **Theoretical ethanol yield** = 0.51 g ethanol per g glucose; **practical yield** = ~0.32–0.40 L/kg malt.
- **Scotch Whisky** = aged ≥ **3 years** in oak, ≥ **40% ABV** at bottling, made in Scotland.
- **Bourbon** = ≥ 51% corn, aged ≥ **2 years** in new charred American white oak.
- **Pot stills** (batch) make malt whisky; **column stills** (continuous) make grain whisky.
- **Foreshots, hearts, feints** = distillation cuts; only hearts mature.
- **Angel's Share** = ~2% volume lost per year through cask evaporation.
- **Single Malt** = one distillery, 100% malted barley; **Blended** = malt + grain mix; **Single Grain** = one distillery, mainly wheat/maize.
- **Peat smoke** = source of phenolic compounds responsible for smoky character in Islay Scotch.
- **Maturation** contributes ~**60–70% of final whisky flavor** through extraction, oxidation, and concentration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types, Raw Materials and Processing. Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types, Raw Materials and Processing. Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: TYPES, RAW MATERIALS, PROCESSING & DESIGN OF BEVERAGES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of KTU's **PEFTT632 – Beverage Processing** (2024 Scheme), Module 3 addresses the **engineering classification, raw material specifications, unit operations, processing sequences, and plant/factory design considerations** of non-alcoholic stimulant beverages — primarily **Tea, Coffee, and Cocoa**. These three commodities share a common biochemical lineage: they are all **plant-derived alkaloid-bearing products** containing **methylxanthines** (caffeine, theobromine, theophylline) and **polyphenolic compounds (tannins)** that undergo distinct post-harvest biochemical transformations to develop characteristic flavor, color, and aroma profiles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus Areas:**
> 1. **Types & Classification** – Botanical varieties, grades, and commercial grades
> 2. **Raw Materials** – Chemical composition, maturity indices, harvest specifications
> 3. **Processing** – Primary (on-farm) and secondary (factory) processing
> 4. **Design** – Plant layout, equipment sizing, mass-energy balance, HACCP

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Alchemy of the Leaf, Bean, and Pod"

Imagine three siblings from the same family — the **leaf (tea)**, the **seed (coffee)**, and the **pod (cocoa)** — each born with a bitter, inedible, and chemically aggressive constitution. **Processing is essentially controlled "alchemy"** that converts these raw botanical tissues into smooth, aromatic, consumer-acceptable beverages.

- **Tea** = A leaf that is *withered, rolled, oxidized, and dried* — analogous to **drying a herb to preserve its essential oils**.
- **Coffee** = A seed inside a fruit that is *pulped, fermented, dried, and roasted* — analogous to **malting barley for beer**, where enzymatic and Maillard chemistry creates flavor.
- **Cocoa** = A fermented seed from a pod that is *fermented, dried, roasted, and ground* — analogous to **cheese-making**, where microbial fermentation is essential.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Processing Principle (KTU High-Yield Concept):**
> All three beverages share a **5-stage backbone**: *Harvest → Primary Processing (Biochemical Conditioning) → Drying/Preservation → Secondary Processing (Roasting/Blending) → Extraction/Brewing*. The Design aspect deals with the **factory layout, equipment selection, and process flow optimization** for these stages.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Value / Standard | Unit |
|-----------|------------------|------|
| **Global Tea Production** | ≈ **6.5 million tonnes/year** | Mt/yr |
| **Global Coffee Production** | ≈ **10.5 million tonnes/year** | Mt/yr |
| **Global Cocoa Production** | ≈ **5.0 million tonnes/year** | Mt/yr |
| **Tea : Coffee : Cocoa (Beverage Volume Ratio)** | ≈ 1 : 0.8 : 0.05 | — |
| **Standard Brewing Temperature** | **90 – 96 °C** | °C |
| **Optimal Moisture for Storage** | **< 6 % (w/w)** | % |
| **KTU Reference Standard** | **IS 3633 (Tea), IS 3077 (Coffee), IS 7836 (Cocoa)** | — |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Beverages — Engineering Taxonomy

### 2.1.1 Tea (*Camellia sinensis*)

Tea is classified by **post-harvest oxidation level (fermentation degree)**, which is the most important industrial classification in KTU 2024 Module 3.

- **White Tea** — Unwilted, unoxidized; buds only; **0–5 % oxidation**; delicate flavor.
- **Green Tea** — Withered, *un*-oxidized (enzymes deactivated by pan-firing or steaming); **0–2 % oxidation**; high in catechins (EGCG).
- **Oolong Tea** — Partially oxidized; **15–80 % oxidation**; complex floral-fruity profile.
- **Black Tea** — Fully oxidized; **80–100 % oxidation**; theaflavins + thearubigins dominate.
- **Pu-erh Tea** — Microbially fermented post-drying; aged; **0 % chemical oxidation, but microbial fermentation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The term *"fermentation"* in tea processing is a **misnomer**. It is **enzymatic oxidation by polyphenol oxidase (PPO) and peroxidase (POD)**, NOT microbial fermentation. This is a common 3-mark question trap.

### 2.1.2 Coffee (*Coffea* spp.)

- **Arabica (*Coffea arabica*)** — **60–70 %** of global production; grown at **600–2000 m** altitude; **1.0–1.5 % caffeine**; superior flavor.
- **Robusta (*Coffea canephora*)** — **30–40 %** of global production; **0–600 m** altitude; **2.0–2.5 % caffeine** (twice arabica); stronger, more bitter; used in espresso blends and instant coffee.
- **Liberica & Excelsa** — Minor varieties; **< 1 %** of production.

**Process Classification:**
- **Washed (Wet-Processed)** Coffee — Pulped, fermented, washed; clean, bright acidity.
- **Natural (Dry-Processed)** Coffee — Whole cherries sun-dried; fruity, heavy body.
- **Honey (Pulped Natural)** Coffee — Pulped but not washed; mucilage left on bean.

### 2.1.3 Cocoa (*Theobroma cacao*)

Three main varietal groups:
- **Criollo** — **1–5 %** of production; finest flavor; fragile; low yield.
- **Forastero** — **80–90 %** of production; hardy; bulk chocolate.
- **Trinitario** — Hybrid of Criollo × Forastero; **10–15 %**; premium quality.

## 2.2 Raw Materials — Chemical Composition

### 2.2.1 Tea Leaf Composition

The chemical composition of fresh tea leaf determines the quality of finished black tea after processing.

| Component | Fresh Leaf (% w/w) | Black Tea (% w/w) | Functional Role |
|-----------|-------------------:|------------------:|-----------------|
| Water | **75–80** | **3–5** | Solvent for enzymes |
| Polyphenols (catechins) | **20–30** | **5–10** (as theaflavins/thearubigins) | Astringency, color |
| Caffeine | **3–4** | **2.5–3.5** | Stimulant, bitterness |
| Amino acids (theanine) | **1–3** | **1–2** | Umami (L-theanine) |
| Proteins | **15–20** | — | Enzymes (PPO) |
| Cellulose/hemicellulose | **20–25** | — | Structural |
| Pectins | **4–6** | — | Gelation |
| Volatiles (terpenes, etc.) | **0.05–0.1** | Trace | Aroma precursors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Theanine (γ-glutamylethylamide)** is unique to tea and is responsible for the **umami/savory taste** and the "calm alertness" effect of L-theanine + caffeine synergy.

### 2.2.2 Coffee Bean Composition (Green vs. Roasted)

| Component | Green Bean (% w/w) | Roasted Bean (% w/w) | Role |
|-----------|-------------------:|---------------------:|------|
| Water | **10–12** | **1–5** | — |
| Carbohydrates | **50–60** | **30–40** | Maillard precursors |
| Lipids | **10–16** | **10–16** | Aroma, crema |
| Proteins | **10–13** | **10–13** | Maillard reactants |
| Caffeine | **1.0–2.5** | **1.0–2.5** | Stimulant |
| Chlorogenic acids | **5–10** | **1–3** | Bitterness, antioxidant |
| Trigonelline | **0.5–1.5** | **0.1–0.5** | Roasting → Niacin (vit. B3) + aroma |

### 2.2.3 Cocoa Bean Composition (Fermented vs. Roasted)

| Component | Fresh Bean (% w/w) | Fermented/Roasted (% w/w) | Role |
|-----------|------------------:|---------------------------:|------|
| Water | **60–65** | **2–4** | — |
| Lipids (cocoa butter) | **3–5** (in pulp) / **45–55** (in nib) | **50–55** | Mouthfeel, melting |
| Proteins | **10–15** | **10–15** | Maillard substrates |
| Polyphenols | **10–15** | **5–8** (after fermentation) | Bitterness, color |
| Theobromine | **1.0–1.5** | **1.0–1.5** | Stimulant |
| Starch | **5–10** | **5–10** | Body |

## 2.3 Processing Stages — Unit Operations Map

### 2.3.1 Tea Processing Flow

**Primary (on-farm) processing — for Black Tea:**

1. **Plucking** — Two leaves + bud (standard); or one leaf + bud (fine grade).
2. **Withering** — **18–24 h** at **25–30 °C**; air velocity **0.5–1.0 m/s**; moisture **75% → 55–60%**.
3. **Rolling (CTC or Orthodox)** — Cell disruption; juice release; initiates enzymatic oxidation.
   - **CTC** (Crush, Tear, Curl) — 3 roller pairs; faster; granular tea.
   - **Orthodox** — Rolling table; whole-leaf appearance.
4. **Fermentation (Oxidation)** — **45–90 min** at **25–28 °C**, RH > 95%; PPO converts catechins → theaflavins (yellow-orange) → thearubigins (red-brown).
5. **Drying (Firing)** — **120–130 °C** inlet; moisture → **3–5%**; stops enzyme activity.

**Secondary (factory) processing:**
6. **Sorting & Grading** — Fiber extractors, vibratory sieves, color sorters.
7. **Blending & Packaging** — Multi-origin blends for consistency.

### 2.3.2 Coffee Processing Flow

1. **Harvesting** — Selective (hand-picking) or strip (mechanical).
2. **Wet Processing (Washed) Method:**
   - Pulping → Fermentation (12–72 h, microbial) → Washing → Drying (sun/mechanical to 10–12% moisture).
3. **Dry Processing:** Whole cherries sun-dried to 10–12% moisture.
4. **Hulling** — Removal of parchment (wet-processed) or entire dried husk (dry-processed).
5. **Storage** — Green beans, jute/burlap bags, 10–12% moisture.
6. **Roasting** — **180–240 °C** for **10–20 min**; **1st crack at ~196 °C**; color development **Cie-Lab** values; moisture → 1–5%.
7. **Grinding** — Particle size distribution: **< 500 μm** (Turkish), **500–750 μm** (Espresso), **750–1000 μm** (Filter), **> 1000 μm** (French press).
8. **Extraction (Brewing)** — Solid-liquid extraction; grind size, water temperature, pressure, time, and **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids)** control the cup quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept — Maillard Reaction in Coffee Roasting:**
> During roasting, **Maillard non-enzymatic browning** between reducing sugars (arabinose, galactose) and amino acids (alanine, glycine) produces **> 800 volatile aroma compounds**, including pyrazines, furans, and aldehydes. This is **not** enzymatic. Coffee roasting is essentially **controlled Maillard chemistry**.

### 2.3.3 Cocoa Processing Flow

1. **Harvesting** — Pods cut from trunk (cauliflory).
2. **Pod Breaking & Fermentation** — Beans with pulp in boxes/banana leaves for **5–7 days** at ambient temperature (rising to **45–50 °C** due to microbial activity). Yeasts → Lactic acid bacteria → Acetic acid bacteria.
3. **Drying** — Sun-dried or mechanical to **6–7%** moisture.
4. **Roasting** — **120–150 °C** for **15–30 min**; Maillard reactions; develops chocolate aroma.
5. **Winnowing** — Removal of husks; separation of **nibs** (cotyledons).
6. **Grinding (Nib grinding)** — Production of **cocoa mass (cocoa liquor)**.
7. **Pressing** — Hydraulic press separates **cocoa butter** from **cocoa cake** (powder after grinding).
8. **Conching** — Refining chocolate mass; shear mixing for hours-days; smooth texture.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Engineering Calculations

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU high-yield for Part A 3-mark and Part B 14-mark numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\theta = \dfrac{M_{\text{in}} - M_{\text{out}}}{M_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Moisture removal efficiency in drying | % |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{M_{\text{final}}}{M_{\text{initial}}} \times 100$ | Mass yield / Retention | % |
| 3 | $M_{\text{water removed}} = M_{\text{in}}\left(1 - \dfrac{1 - \theta_i}{1 - \theta_f}\right)$ | Water to be evaporated | kg |
| 4 | $Q = M_{\text{water}} \cdot \lambda$ | Heat for evaporation ($\lambda$ = latent heat ≈ 2260 kJ/kg) | kJ |
| 5 | $Y = \dfrac{Y_r}{1 + (\text{BPR})}$ | Mass balance with By-Product Ratio (BPR) | kg |
| 6 | $E_{\text{ext}} = 1 - e^{-k \cdot t}$ | Solid-liquid extraction kinetics (1st order) | — |
| 7 | $T_{\text{brewing}} \approx T_{\text{sat}} - \Delta T_{\text{loss}}$ | Brewing temperature (loss factor) | °C |
| 8 | $Q_{\text{roaster}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m_{\text{evap}} \cdot \lambda$ | Total energy in roaster | kJ |
| 9 | $C_{\text{caffeine}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{beverage}} \cdot V_{\text{cup}}}{m_{\text{grounds}}}$ | Caffeine extraction yield | mg/g |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{plant}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{product}}}{m_{\text{raw}} + m_{\text{additives}}}$ | Plant material efficiency | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Constraint:** Use `\vert` for absolute value: e.g., $c = \vert v \vert$. Avoid raw `\vert` inside markdown table cells when it conflicts with column separators.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The biochemical engineering principles underlying beverage processing directly inform:
- **Phytochemical extraction industries** (phytopharmaceuticals, nutraceuticals)
- **Plant-based beverage analogs** (oat milk, almond milk — similar extraction kinetics)
- **Specialty coffee science** (SCA cupping scores, brew ratio control)
- **Cocoa butter equivalents (CBE)** in chocolate manufacturing
- **Decaffeination processes** (supercritical CO₂ extraction, Swiss Water Process)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Moisture Removal and Heat Requirement in Tea Drying

### Problem Setup
A tea factory processes **M₁ = 1000 kg** of fermented tea leaf (after rolling) with an initial moisture content of **θᵢ = 60 % (w.b.)** to a final moisture of **θ_f = 3 % (w.b.)** using a fluidized bed dryer. Calculate the **water to be removed** and the **heat energy required** for drying.

### Step 1: Express the Initial and Final Moisture on Dry Basis

The dry basis moisture is defined as:

$$X_i = \dfrac{\theta_i}{1 - \theta_i} = \dfrac{0.60}{1 - 0.60} = \dfrac{0.60}{0.40} = 1.50 \quad \text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$

The final dry basis moisture is:

$$X_f = \dfrac{\theta_f}{1 - \theta_f} = \dfrac{0.03}{1 - 0.03} = \dfrac{0.03}{0.97} \approx 0.0309 \quad \text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Textual Conversion Logic:** The wet basis moisture represents the mass of water divided by the total mass. The dry basis represents water per unit mass of dry solid, which is more convenient for mass balance because the dry solid mass is invariant during drying.

### Step 2: Determine the Mass of Dry Solid in the Feed

The mass of dry solid (bone-dry) is the non-water portion of the initial feed:

$$m_s = M_1 \cdot (1 - \theta_i) = 1000 \times 0.40 = 400 \text{ kg dry solid}$$

**Textual Conversion Logic:** Since 60 % of the initial 1000 kg is water, the remaining 40 % is dry matter, which remains constant throughout drying.

### Step 3: Calculate the Initial and Final Mass of Water

The initial water mass is:

$$m_{w,i} = M_1 \cdot \theta_i = 1000 \times 0.60 = 600 \text{ kg water}$$

The final water mass is:

$$m_{w,f} = m_s \cdot X_f = 400 \times 0.0309 \approx 12.37 \text{ kg water}$$

**Textual Conversion Logic:** The final water mass is the dry solid mass multiplied by the final dry basis moisture ratio.

### Step 4: Calculate the Water to Be Removed

The water removed during drying is the difference between the initial and final water mass:

$$\Delta m_w = m_{w,i} - m_{w,f} = 600 - 12.37 = 587.63 \text{ kg water}$$

**Textual Conversion Logic:** The dryer must evaporate 587.63 kg of water from 1000 kg of wet leaf to obtain the final dried tea.

### Step 5: Verify the Final Product Mass

The final mass of dried tea is:

$$M_2 = m_s + m_{w,f} = 400 + 12.37 = 412.37 \text{ kg dried tea}$$

**Textual Conversion Logic:** The final mass equals the sum of the dry solid (unchanged) and the residual water.

### Step 6: Calculate the Heat Energy Required

The total heat required includes sensible heating of the wet leaf and the latent heat of vaporization. The latent heat of vaporization of water at drying temperatures is approximately:

$$\lambda \approx 2260 \text{ kJ/kg (at 100 °C; slightly higher at lower temps)}$$

For this simplified calculation, the dominant term is the latent heat of vaporization:

$$Q_{\text{evap}} = \Delta m_w \cdot \lambda = 587.63 \times 2260 = 1{,}328{,}043.8 \text{ kJ} \approx 1328 \text{ MJ}$$

If the initial leaf temperature is **T₁ = 25 °C** and the dryer exit (final) is approximately **T₂ = 90 °C** (typical post-drying leaf temperature), the sensible heat for the wet feed is:

$$Q_{\text{sensible,feed}} = M_1 \cdot c_{p,\text{wet}} \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$

With $c_{p,\text{wet}} \approx 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K):

$$Q_{\text{sensible,feed}} = 1000 \times 3.5 \times (90 - 25) = 227{,}500 \text{ kJ} = 227.5 \text{ MJ}$$

Sensible heat for the dry product:

$$Q_{\text{sensible,product}} = M_2 \cdot c_{p,\text{dry}} \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = 412.37 \times 1.7 \times 65 \approx 45{,}567 \text{ kJ} \approx 45.6 \text{ MJ}$$

Total heat requirement:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{evap}} + Q_{\text{sensible,feed}} + Q_{\text{sensible,product}} = 1328 + 227.5 + 45.6 \approx 1601 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 7: Convert to Useful Energy Units

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 1601 \text{ MJ} \approx \dfrac{1601}{3.6} = 444.7 \text{ kWh}$$

**Textual Conversion Logic:** Converting MJ to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ) gives an intuitive industrial electricity equivalent. In practice, an additional **20–30 % heat loss** to exhaust gases must be considered, so the actual furnace input is approximately **1800–2000 MJ**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Beverages

| Process Variable | Tea (Black) | Coffee (Arabica) | Cocoa |
|------------------|-------------|-------------------|--------|
| **Raw Material State** | Two leaves + bud | Seeds in pulp | Seeds in pulp, in pod |
| **Initial Moisture** | 75–80 % | 60–70 % (pulp) | 60–65 % (pulp) |
| **Primary Processing** | Withering + Rolling + Oxidation | Pulping + Fermentation | Pod-breaking + Box fermentation |
| **Key Enzyme/Microbe** | PPO (endogenous) | Pectinolytic microbes | Yeast → LAB → AAB |
| **Optimal Process T** | 25–28 °C (fermentation) | 20–30 °C (wet fermentation) | 30–50 °C (peak fermentation) |
| **Drying T** | 120–130 °C inlet | Sun / 40–60 °C | Sun / 50–70 °C |
| **Final Moisture** | 3–5 % | 10–12 % (green) | 6–7 % |
| **Roasting T** | Not roasted | 180–240 °C | 120–150 °C |
| **Roasting Chemistry** | None | Maillard dominant | Maillard + Strecker |
| **Mass Yield (raw → beverage-ready)** | ≈ 22–25 % | ≈ 18–20 % (green) → 84 % (roasted) | ≈ 30–35 % (fermented) |
| **Caffeine (beverage)** | 25–50 mg/cup | 80–120 mg/cup | 5–10 mg/cup (theobromine) |
| **Plant Design Critical Aspect** | Continuous withering troughs; CTC machines; fluidized bed dryers | Wet mills; fermentation tanks; drum roasters; coolers | Fermentation boxes; sun-drying patios; roasters; hydraulic presses; conches |

## 3.3 KTU Plant Design Principles (Brief)

**Plant layout for a beverage processing facility follows the 5S and HACCP framework:**

$$\text{Linear Flow: Receiving} \rightarrow \text{Pre-processing} \rightarrow \text{Primary} \rightarrow \text{Secondary} \rightarrow \text{Packaging} \rightarrow \text{Storage} \rightarrow \text{Dispatch}$$

- **Capacity design (kg/h)** — derived from annual target divided by operating hours × yield factor.
