- **Equipment sizing** — Use the largest peak load + 15 % design margin (engineering standard).
- **CIP (Clean-in-Place)** loops for piping; SS-304/SS-316 stainless steel for all food contact surfaces.
- **HACCP Critical Control Points (CCPs):**
  - Tea: Final moisture (CCP-1), metal detection (CCP-2)
  - Coffee: Roast color (CCP-1), grind size (CCP-2)
  - Cocoa: Pod sorting (CCP-1), final moisture (CCP-2)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between the three main varieties of tea based on the degree of enzymatic oxidation during processing. State the percentage oxidation for each and the dominant pigment class responsible for the color.**

**Model Answer:**
- **White Tea:** 0–5 % oxidation; buds only; minimal processing; light yellow-green liquor.
- **Green Tea:** 0–2 % oxidation; oxidation is prevented by pan-firing (Chinese) or steaming (Japanese); retains **catechins** (especially EGCG); greenish-yellow liquor.
- **Oolong Tea:** 15–80 % oxidation; partially oxidized; intermediate flavor profile; combines qualities of green and black tea; brownish-orange liquor.
- **Black Tea:** 80–100 % oxidation; fully oxidized; **theaflavins** (yellow-orange) and **thearubigins** (red-brown) are the dominant pigments formed from catechins via polyphenol oxidase (PPO).

> **Key Note:** The degree of oxidation directly determines the **color, flavor, and chemical composition** of the final tea.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Explain the term "wet processing" of coffee. How does it differ from dry (natural) processing, and what is the role of fermentation?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Wet Processing (Washed Coffee):** After harvesting, ripe cherries are mechanically depulped to remove the outer skin. The beans, still coated in mucilage (a sticky pectin layer), are placed in **fermentation tanks for 12–72 hours**. Indigenous microorganisms (yeasts, lactic acid bacteria) produce **pectinolytic enzymes** that degrade the mucilage. After fermentation, the beans are **washed in clean water channels** and then **sun- or mechanically-dried** to 10–12 % moisture.
- **Dry (Natural) Processing:** Whole cherries are spread on **drying patios** and sun-dried for 2–4 weeks without depulping. The entire fruit dries around the bean.
- **Role of Fermentation:** Removes mucilage → produces clean, bright, acidic cup profile; eliminates off-flavors; standardizes quality.

> **Comparison Table (for board answer):**

| Aspect | Wet Process | Dry Process |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Cup Profile | Clean, bright, acidic | Fruity, heavy body |
| Risk | Fermentation defects | Over-fermentation, mold |
| Water Use | High (50–100 L/kg) | Low (rain-fed) |
| Quality Consistency | High | Variable |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 MARKS (Module 3 Focus: Tea Processing & Design)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model – 14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat flow diagram, describe the orthodox method of black tea processing on a commercial scale. Explain the biochemical changes that occur during the "fermentation" (oxidation) stage.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Plucking (1 mark):**
- Standard: Two leaves + one bud (TGB) for fine grade.
- Plucking interval: 7–14 days (flush cycle).

**2. Withering (1 mark):**
- **Physical Withering:** Moisture loss from **75–80 % → 55–60 %** (w.b.) over **18–24 hours**.
- **Trough withering:** Leaf spread 5–10 cm deep; air at **25–30 °C** with velocity **0.5–1.0 m/s**; RH 60–70 %.
- **Chemical Withering:** Increase in cell permeability, concentration of cell sap, partial breakdown of proteins to amino acids (precursors of aroma).

**3. Rolling (1 mark):**
- **Orthodox rolling** on a rolling table or **CTC (Crush, Tear, Curl)** machine.
- **Objective:** Cell disruption → release of cytoplasmic enzymes (PPO, POD) and substrates (catechins) → intimate mixing for enzymatic oxidation.

**4. Fermentation (Oxidation) — 2 marks:**
- **Conditions:** Rolled leaf spread in thin layers (2–4 cm) on the fermentation floor; **25–28 °C**, RH > 95 %, time **45–90 minutes**.
- **Biochemistry:**
  - PPO (polyphenol oxidase) catalyzes: **Catechins → Quinones → Theaflavins → Thearubigins**.
  - Theaflavins (≈ 1–2 % of dry tea): bright, brisk, yellow-orange color.
  - Thearubigins (≈ 10–15 % of dry tea): deep red-brown color, body.
  - **Ratio TF:TR ≈ 1:10** is considered optimal.
- Other changes: Caffeine binds to theaflavins; amino acids contribute to aroma; lipids converted to volatile flavor compounds.

**5. Drying (Firing) (1 mark):**
- **Fluidized bed dryer** or **endless chain pressure dryer (ECPD)**.
- Inlet air: **120–130 °C**; leaf temperature held below 90 °C to avoid scorching.
- **Objective:** Stop enzymatic activity rapidly; reduce moisture to **3–5 %**; develop "made tea" aroma.

**6. Sorting and Grading (0.5 mark):**
- Fiber extractors → vibratory sieves → color sorters → grades: OP, BOP, BOPF, FBOP, Dust, Fannings.

**7. Flow Diagram (0.5 mark):** (As shown in Section 4.2 of these notes)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A tea factory receives 5000 kg of fresh green leaf per day (8-hour shift) at 78 % moisture (w.b.). The withering trough reduces moisture to 58 % (w.b.) and the final black tea product has 4 % moisture. Calculate: (i) Withered leaf mass; (ii) Fermented rolled leaf mass (assume no moisture loss during rolling); (iii) Final black tea mass; (iv) Overall yield.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- M₁ = 5000 kg fresh leaf
- θ₁ = 78 % = 0.78 (initial moisture, w.b.)
- θ₂ = 58 % = 0.58 (after withering, w.b.)
- θ₃ = 4 % = 0.04 (final black tea moisture, w.b.)

**Step 1: Mass of dry solid (constant throughout processing) (1 mark)**

$$m_s = M_1 \times (1 - \theta_1) = 5000 \times (1 - 0.78) = 5000 \times 0.22 = 1100 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Withered leaf mass (1.5 marks)**

The withered leaf has the same dry solid but different moisture:

$$M_2 = \dfrac{m_s}{1 - \theta_2} = \dfrac{1100}{1 - 0.58} = \dfrac{1100}{0.42} \approx 2619.05 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Fermented rolled leaf mass (1.5 marks)**

Rolling does not change moisture (assumption), so:

$$M_3 = M_2 \approx 2619.05 \text{ kg}$$

(But note: rolling may add ~2 % extra moisture from leaf juices, depending on assumption; the question states no change, so 2619.05 kg.)

**Step 4: Final black tea mass (2 marks)**

The final tea has 4 % moisture, so:

$$M_4 = \dfrac{m_s}{1 - \theta_3} = \dfrac{1100}{1 - 0.04} = \dfrac{1100}{0.96} \approx 1145.83 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5: Overall yield (1 mark)**

$$Y = \dfrac{M_4}{M_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{1145.83}{5000} \times 100 \approx 22.92 \text{ %}$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **23 %** of fresh green leaf is recovered as finished black tea — consistent with industry norms of **22–25 %**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly:
> - Forget that **dry solid mass is invariant**; instead, they mistakenly treat each stage's total mass as the reference (loses 2–3 marks).
> - Confuse **wet basis (w.b.)** and **dry basis (d.b.)** moisture notation. KTU 2024 scheme specifies w.b. unless stated otherwise.
> - Fail to show the final **yield interpretation** — a 1-mark courtesy point.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (Module 3 Focus: Coffee & Cocoa Processing & Design)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model – 14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the wet processing of coffee from harvest to green bean storage. With a flow diagram, explain each unit operation and the biochemical/microbiological changes involved.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Harvesting (1 mark):**
- Selective hand-picking of **ripe red cherries** (preferred for quality) OR mechanical stripping (large scale).
- Float tank separation: ripe cherries **sink**; overripe/underripe/defective cherries **float** and are discarded.

**2. Pulping (1 mark):**
- Mechanical **disc pulper** or **drum pulper** removes the outer exocarp (skin).
- Beans remain coated in **mucilage** (sticky pectin layer, 0.5–2 mm thick).

**3. Mucilage Fermentation (2 marks):**
- **Duration:** 12–72 hours depending on temperature, pectinolytic activity, and ambient conditions.
- **Microbial succession:**
  - **Hours 0–18:** Yeasts (Saccharomyces spp.) → consume sugars → ethanol, CO₂.
  - **Hours 18–36:** Lactic acid bacteria (Lactobacillus, Leuconostoc) → lactic acid, acetic acid.
  - **Hours 36–72:** Acetic acid bacteria → raise temperature; lower pH to 3.5–4.0.
- **Enzymatic action:** Pectinolytic enzymes (pectin lyase, polygalacturonase) **degrade the pectin** in the mucilage, making it water-soluble.

**4. Washing (0.5 mark):**
- Beans are channeled through **fresh water channels** with agitation; mucilage is completely removed.
- **Critical:** Under-fermentation leaves mucilage → defective beans ("stinkers"); over-fermentation → "soured" off-flavors.

**5. Drying (1 mark):**
- **Sun drying** on patios (8–14 days, layer 2–3 cm, raked hourly).
- **Mechanical drying** in **Guardiola dryers** (perforated drum, 45–50 °C air, 24–48 h).
- **Final moisture: 10–12 % (w.b.)** — critical for safe storage and prevention of mold (Aspergillus ochraceus → ochratoxin A risk if > 13 %).

**6. Hulling (0.5 mark):**
- Removal of the **parchment (endocarp)** and **silverskin** via **friction hullers**.
- Produces **green coffee beans** ready for storage or export.

**7. Storage (0.5 mark):**
- Green beans in **jute bags (60–70 kg)** or **GrainPro hermetic bags**; humidity-controlled warehouses (RH 60–65 %).
- Green beans can be stored for 6–12 months with minimal quality loss.

**8. Flow Diagram (0.5 mark):** (As shown in Section 4.3 of these notes)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A coffee roasting plant roasts 200 kg of green beans (moisture 11 %) to a roast loss of 15 %. If the green beans contain 1.2 % caffeine, what is the caffeine content in the roasted beans? Comment on the role of Maillard reaction in coffee roasting.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- M_green = 200 kg
- Initial moisture θ_i = 11 % (w.b.)
- Roast loss R_L = 15 % (mass loss during roasting)
- Caffeine in green beans = 1.2 % (w/w)

**Step 1: Calculate the mass of roasted beans (1.5 marks)**

Roasting loss includes both moisture loss and volatile loss (CO₂, water from Maillard, VOCs). The roasted bean mass is:

$$M_{\text{roast}} = M_{\text{green}} \times (1 - R_L) = 200 \times 0.85 = 170 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Caffeine balance during roasting (1.5 marks)**

Caffeine is **thermally stable** (sublimation point ≈ 178 °C; bulk of caffeine remains in the bean). Assume **negligible caffeine loss** during roasting:

$$m_{\text{caffeine,green}} = 200 \times 0.012 = 2.4 \text{ kg caffeine}$$

**Step 3: Caffeine content in roasted beans (2 marks)**

$$C_{\text{caffeine,roast}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{caffeine,green}}}{M_{\text{roast}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.4}{170} \times 100 \approx 1.41 \text{ %}$$

**Interpretation:** Caffeine content **increases** in roasted beans (from 1.2 % to 1.41 %) because the total mass decreases due to moisture and volatile loss, but the caffeine mass remains essentially constant.

**Step 4: Role of Maillard Reaction in Coffee Roasting (2 marks):**

The **Maillard reaction** is a non-enzymatic browning reaction between **reducing sugars** (arabinose, galactose, glucose) and **amino groups** of proteins/amino acids. It is the **dominant flavor-generating reaction** in coffee roasting, producing:

- **Pyrazines** — earthy, roasted, nutty aromas.
- **Furans** — caramel, bready notes (e.g., furfural, hydroxymethylfurfural).
- **Aldehydes** — fruity, malty (e.g., acetaldehyde, benzaldehyde).
- **Thiophenes and Thiazoles** — meaty, sulfurous notes (e.g., 2-furfurylthiol — the characteristic "fresh coffee" aroma compound).

The reaction occurs in three overlapping stages:
1. **Initial:** Amadori rearrangement products form.
2. **Intermediate:** Dehydration, fragmentation; reactive intermediates form.
3. **Final:** Melanoidins (high-MW brown polymers) form — contribute to **body** and **color** of brewed coffee.

Roast color is monitored by **Agtron discs** or **colorimeters (Cie-Lab)**: L* value drops from ~70 (green) to ~25 (very dark roast).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Coffee/Cocoa):**
> - Conflating **caffeine** with **chlorogenic acid (CGA)**: CGA degrades during roasting (5–10 % → 1–3 %) and contributes bitterness; caffeine remains stable.
> - Forgetting to state the **Maillard reaction** explicitly when asked about coffee flavor.
> - Not stating the **loss of chlorogenic acid and trigonelline** during roasting (a 1-mark KTU-favorite).
> - Confusing **cacao (raw pod)** with **cocoa (processed bean)** in the spelling.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 High-Yield Revision Checklist — Beverage Types, Raw Materials, Processing & Design**

### A. Types — Quick Recall

- **Tea:** White, Green, Oolong, Black, Pu-erh (oxidative classification).
- **Coffee:** Arabica, Robusta, Liberica; Washed, Natural, Honey (process classification).
- **Cocoa:** Criollo, Forastero, Trinitario.

### B. Raw Materials — Key Chemicals to Memorize

- **Tea:** Catechins → Theaflavins (yellow) + Thearubigins (red-brown); L-Theanine; Caffeine.
- **Coffee:** Caffeine (stable); Chlorogenic acid (degrades); Trigonelline → Niacin; Sucrose → Maillard.
- **Cocoa:** Theobromine (not caffeine — minor); Polyphenols (flavan-3-ols); Cocoa butter (lipids); Methylxanthines.

### C. Processing — Critical Stages

- **Tea:** Withering → Rolling → Fermentation (Oxidation, PPO enzyme) → Drying → Sorting.
- **Coffee:** Pulping → Fermentation (microbial) → Washing → Drying → Hulling → Roasting → Grinding.
- **Cocoa:** Pod-breaking → Box Fermentation (microbial) → Drying → Roasting → Winnowing → Grinding → Pressing → Conching.

### D. Design — Plant Layout Principles

- **Linear unidirectional flow:** Receiving → Processing → Packaging → Storage → Dispatch.
- **Material:** SS-304/SS-316 stainless steel for food contact.
- **Sanitation:** CIP (Clean-in-Place) loops, HACCP plan, ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000 compliance.
- **Capacity:** Annual target ÷ Operating hours ÷ Yield factor = Hourly throughput.
- **Utilities:** Steam, hot water, compressed air, refrigeration, effluent treatment (ETP).

### E. KTU 2024 Most-Likely 14-Mark Question Topics

1. **Tea processing** flow with biochemistry of oxidation.
2. **Mass balance** of moisture removal (drying calculations).
3. **Coffee wet vs. dry processing** comparison.
4. **Roasting chemistry** (Maillard reaction) in coffee and cocoa.
5. **Cocoa fermentation** with microbial succession.
6. **Plant layout** for any of the three beverage factories.
7. **Caffeine / theobromine balance** in roasted beans.

### F. Common Spelling/Pitfall Traps

- **"Fermentation"** in tea = oxidation (NOT microbial).
- **"Fermentation"** in coffee/cocoa = microbial (NOT enzymatic).
- **"Cacao"** = tree or raw pod; **"Cocoa"** = processed bean/liquor/butter.
- **"Moisture w.b. vs. d.b."** — always clarify in numerical answers.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** In the board exam, always include a **labeled flow diagram** (≥1–2 marks reserved for it). Use **SI units** consistently. State all **assumptions** clearly. Conclude with a **practical industrial interpretation** — examiners reward applied understanding.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — PEFTT632 Beverage Processing**

topics.title: Wash Still Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wash Still Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wash Still (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *wash still* is a large batch-operated copper pot distillation vessel used in the production of potable spirits (notably Scotch whisky, Irish whiskey, rum, and grain-neutral spirits). Its primary function is to perform the **first of two (or three) distillation passes**, converting the fermented *wash* — a low-alcohol aqueous beer of roughly **7–10 % ABV** — into a distillate known as ***low wines***, typically concentrated to **20–25 % ABV**, while simultaneously separating volatile congeners into distinct fractions (*foreshots, heart, feints*).

The wash still operates on the fundamental principle of **differential volatility**: ethanol (boiling point **78.37 °C**) vaporizes at a lower temperature than water (**100 °C**), allowing selective enrichment of the vapor phase when the wash is heated to boiling.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Giant Kettle of Selective Boiling":**
> Imagine boiling a pot of salty seawater vs. a pot of spiced wine. Salt cannot be boiled away (it stays behind), while alcohol — being more volatile than water — escapes as vapor first, carrying distinct aromatic "notes" with it. A wash still is precisely this principle, scaled up to industrial proportions, but with surgical precision: the vapors are captured, condensed, and the distiller *cuts* the run into fractions like a chef slicing a roast into prime, choice, and braising portions. The "prime cut" is the *heart* — the clean spirit that becomes whisky.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Wash charge volume | **10,000 – 25,000 L** | Per batch |
| Initial wash ABV | **7 – 10 % v/v** | Fermented beer strength |
| Low wines ABV (outlet) | **20 – 25 % v/v** | First distillate strength |
| Wash charge temperature | **20 – 30 °C** | Pre-heating ambient |
| Still pot operating T | **90 – 100 °C** | Boiling regime |
| Ethanol boiling point | **78.37 °C** | Pure component reference |
| Water boiling point | **100 °C** | Pure component reference |
| Batch cycle time | **5 – 8 hours** | Total distillation time |
| Heating source | **Steam coil / gas fire** | Energy delivery |

## 1.4 Position in the Distillation Cascade

> [!NOTE]
> **Wash → Low Wines → (Spirit Still) → New Make Spirit**
>
> The wash still is *never* the final still in malt whisky production. Its output (*low wines*) is collected, diluted to ~25–30 % ABV, and recharged into a smaller **spirit still** for the second (and final) distillation that produces *new make spirit*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Sequence (Bulleted Logic)

The wash still follows a strictly choreographed batch sequence. Each step has a clear "why" rooted in either mass-transfer efficiency, energy economy, or regulatory compliance.

1. **Charging the Still**
   - *Why:* Introduce fermented wash (with solids → hence the need for a wash still with a *wash charger* and *rummager*).
   - *How:* Pumped in via the *man-door* or *swan-neck* charging port; volume typically **70 – 80 % of pot capacity** to prevent boil-over.

2. **Sealing & Safety Check**
   - *Why:* Positive pressure build-up during heating; ethanol vapor is flammable (**LEL = 3.3 % v/v in air**).
   - *How:* Close all valves, verify the **dome safety valve** set at **≤ 0.2 bar gauge**.

3. **Heating Initiation**
   - *Why:* Raise wash to boiling; ethanol preferentially vaporizes due to higher vapor pressure.
   - *How:* Steam coils (internal) or external gas-fired bowser; ramp at **≤ 2 °C/min** to prevent thermal shock to copper.

4. **Vapor Rise & Partial Condensation**
   - *Why:* Heavy congeners and water condense on the **lyne arm** walls, refluxing back → improves separation.
   - *How:* Vapor ascends through the **swan neck** and **lyne arm**; partial condensation controlled by lyne-arm cooling water.

5. **Total Condensation in Worm Tub / Condenser**
   - *Why:* Convert vapor back to liquid (low wines).
   - *How:* Shell-and-tube or coil condenser; coolant at **< 20 °C**; output temperature **15 – 25 °C**.

6. **Fraction Collection (The "Cut")**
   - *Why:* Separate *foreshots* (methanol, acetone, aldehydes) and *feints* (fusel oils, late-run tails) from the *clean heart* low wines.
   - *How:* Based on spirit hydrometer readings, time, or temperature thresholds; typically **foreshots cut at ABV > 75 %**, **feints cut at ABV < 70 %** (varies by distillery).

7. **Drainage of Spent Lees**
   - *Why:* Recover residual heat; remove solids (dead yeast, grain) for reprocessing as draff.
   - *How:* Pump out through the *bottom man-door*; can be hot (~95 °C) and used for **COP (Combined Heat & Power)** recovery.

8. **Cleaning Cycle (Back-to-Back Distillation)**
   - *Why:* Remove copper-sulphide scale (regulatory hygiene + prevents flavor carry-over).
   - *How:* Caustic wash, rinse, then often re-distilled with next wash (the "sacrificial first run" technique).

## 2.2 Governing Physical Laws

### Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures
The total vapor pressure of an ideal mixture equals the sum of partial pressures:

$$P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \cdot P$$

where $P_i$ is the partial pressure of component $i$, $y_i$ is its vapor mole fraction, and $P$ is total pressure.

### Raoult's Law (Ideal Solution Behavior)
For component $i$ in an ideal liquid mixture:

$$P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)$$

where $x_i$ is the liquid mole fraction and $P_i^{sat}(T)$ is the saturation vapor pressure at temperature $T$ (often computed via the **Antoine equation**):

$$\log_{10} P_i^{sat}(T) = A - \frac{B}{C + T}$$

with $T$ in °C and $P_i^{sat}$ in mmHg.

### Vapor–Liquid Equilibrium (VLE) Enrichment
Combining Dalton + Raoult yields the **relative volatility** $\alpha_{ij}$ between ethanol (i) and water (j):

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{K_i}{K_j} = \frac{(y_i / x_i)}{(y_j / x_j)} = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P_j^{sat}(T)}$$

For ethanol-water at the azeotropic point (**95.6 % v/v ethanol, 78.2 °C**), $\alpha \to 1$, marking the theoretical limit of simple batch distillation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All high-yield formulas a student must memorize for wash still problems:**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Typical Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass balance (overall) | $F = D + W$ | $F$=feed (kg), $D$=distillate (kg), $W$=waste (kg) | Steady-state or per batch |
| Mass balance (ethanol) | $F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_D + W \cdot x_W$ | $x$ = mass or mole fraction ethanol | Closure check |
| ABV mass-to-mole conversion | $x_{EtOH} = \dfrac{M_W \cdot \rho_{EtOH} \cdot V\%}{M_{EtOH} \cdot \rho_{EtOH} \cdot V\% + M_W \cdot (100 - V\%)}$ | $M_W = 18.015$ g/mol, $M_{EtOH} = 46.07$ g/mol | Required for VLE math |
| Ethanol enrichment ratio | $E = \dfrac{x_{D,EtOH}}{x_{F,EtOH}}$ | Dimensionless | Wash still: **E ≈ 2.5 – 3.5** |
| Energy demand (steam) | $Q = m \cdot (c_p \Delta T + \lambda_v)$ | $m$ = wash mass, $c_p \approx 4.0$ kJ/kg·K, $\lambda_v \approx 2260$ kJ/kg | Per batch |
| Reflux ratio | $R = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | $L$ = liquid returned, $D$ = distillate | Wash still: $R \approx 0.5 – 1.0$ |
| Theoretical plate number (Fenske) | $N_{min} + 1 = \dfrac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\left(\dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | $x_B$ = bottoms composition | Used for column sizing |
| Boiling-point elevation correction | $T_{mix} = \sum x_i T_i^{sat}$ | Linear ideal mixing approximation | Approximate; use Dühring chart for accuracy |

> **Critical Note on `|` symbol:** KTU answer scripts use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX contexts; this cheat-sheet uses $\vert x \vert$ notation inline but **never** uses the unescaped pipe in markdown table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillery Design:** Wash still sizing (10,000–25,000 L) sets the throughput of the entire distillery; it is the *bottleneck equipment*.
- **Energy Auditing:** Wash still energy input accounts for **~60 – 70 %** of total distillery thermal load → prime target for **heat integration** (e.g., spent-heat recovery to pre-heat next wash charge).
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Copper contact is *legally mandated* in Scotch whisky production (UK Excise regulations) → prevents formation of **ethyl carbamate** and removes sulfur compounds.
- **Sustainability:** Modern wash stills integrate **mechanical vapor recompression (MVR)** or **thermal vapor recompression (TVR)** to reduce steam demand by up to **40 %**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Balance for a Wash Still Batch

**Problem Statement:** A wash still is charged with **15,000 L** of wash at **8.0 % ABV** (density $\rho_{wash} = 985 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The distillation yields **5,800 L** of low wines at **22 % ABV** ($\rho_{LW} = 962 \text{ kg/m}^3$) and the remaining *pot ale/spent lees* at **0.1 % ABV** ($\rho_{W} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, treat as water).

**Verify the ethanol mass balance and determine the volumetric recovery ratio.**

### Step A — Convert all volumes to mass

**Row 1 explanation:** Mass = volume × density. Each stream must first be converted from litres to m³ by dividing by 1000.

$$m_{F} = 15000 \text{ L} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ m}^3}{1000 \text{ L}} \times 985 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 14{,}775 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{D} = 5800 \text{ L} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ m}^3}{1000 \text{ L}} \times 962 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 5579.6 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{W} = ? \quad \text{(unknown — to be solved)}$$

### Step B — Apply overall mass balance

**Row 2 explanation:** Conservation of mass dictates that feed mass equals distillate mass plus waste mass.

$$F = D + W \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = F - D$$

$$W = 14{,}775 - 5579.6 = 9195.4 \text{ kg}$$

**Sanity check the implied spent-lees volume:**

$$V_{W} = \dfrac{9195.4 \text{ kg}}{1000 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 9.195 \text{ m}^3 = 9195 \text{ L}$$

This matches expectations (≈ 9200 L left in pot after distillation).

### Step C — Compute ethanol mass in each stream

**Row 3 explanation:** Ethanol mass in each stream = total mass × mass fraction. Convert ABV (vol %) to mass fraction using:

$$\text{mass fraction} = \dfrac{\rho_{EtOH} \cdot V\%}{\rho_{EtOH} \cdot V\% + \rho_W \cdot (1 - V\%) / 100}$$

For **8.0 % ABV** with $\rho_{EtOH} = 789 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_W = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$w_{EtOH,F} = \dfrac{789 \times 8.0}{789 \times 8.0 + 1000 \times 92.0} = \dfrac{6312}{6312 + 92{,}000} = \dfrac{6312}{98{,}312} = 0.0642$$

$$m_{EtOH,F} = 14{,}775 \times 0.0642 = 948.55 \text{ kg}$$

For **22 % ABV low wines**:

$$w_{EtOH,D} = \dfrac{789 \times 22}{789 \times 22 + 1000 \times 78} = \dfrac{17{,}358}{17{,}358 + 78{,}000} = \dfrac{17{,}358}{95{,}358} = 0.1821$$

$$m_{EtOH,D} = 5579.6 \times 0.1821 = 1015.94 \text{ kg}$$

For **0.1 % ABV spent lees** (treated as dilute ethanol in water):

$$w_{EtOH,W} \approx \dfrac{789 \times 0.1}{1000 \times 99.9} = 7.90 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$m_{EtOH,W} = 9195.4 \times 7.90 \times 10^{-4} = 7.26 \text{ kg}$$

### Step D — Verify component balance

**Row 4 explanation:** Ethanol in feed must equal ethanol in distillate + ethanol in waste.

$$m_{EtOH,F} = 948.55 \text{ kg (computed)}$$

$$m_{EtOH,D} + m_{EtOH,W} = 1015.94 + 7.26 = 1023.20 \text{ kg}$$

**The discrepancy of ~74.6 kg** indicates measurement / rounding error and shows the *importance of using exact compositions in real distillery accounting*. In a real audit, this would prompt re-sampling.

> [!NOTE]
> **The discrepancy arises because we assumed the spent lees density = 1000 kg/m³**, but actually the spent lees contain dissolved solids (yeast, protein) raising density to ~1010 kg/m³. KTU examiners may give students the *option* to neglect this for simplicity, but flagging it in the answer earns full marks.

### Step E — Compute the volumetric recovery ratio

**Row 5 explanation:** Volumetric recovery of low wines relative to wash charge.

$$\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{D}}{V_{F}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{5800}{15{,}000} \times 100\% = 38.67\%$$

**Ethanol recovery efficiency:**

$$\eta_{EtOH} = \dfrac{m_{EtOH,D}}{m_{EtOH,F}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1015.94}{948.55} \times 100\% = 107.1\%$$

The fact that this exceeds 100% is a clear red flag for measurement error — the conclusion is that one or more input compositions is slightly off; in reality, ethanol recovery is **93 – 97 %** in a well-operated wash still.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Required to Heat Wash to Boiling

**Problem Statement:** Determine the thermal energy $Q$ required to heat **15,000 kg** of wash from **25 °C** to its boiling point (assume **95 °C** at start of vapor rise, accounting for ethanol content).

**Step F — Identify the energy components:**

1. Sensible heat: $Q_{sens} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$
2. Latent heat of vaporization: $Q_{lat} = m_{vap} \cdot \lambda_v$

**Step G — Compute sensible heat (heating from 25 °C to 95 °C):**

Use $c_p \approx 4.0 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ for dilute ethanol-water:

$$Q_{sens} = 15{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 4.0 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \times (95 - 25) \text{ K}$$

$$Q_{sens} = 15{,}000 \times 4.0 \times 70 = 4{,}200{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 4200 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step H — Compute latent heat of vaporization for the distillate:**

The mass of distillate (low wines) from Step B was $m_D = 5579.6 \text{ kg}$. Latent heat of vaporization of dilute ethanol ≈ **$\lambda_v = 2200 \text{ kJ/kg}$** (slightly less than water's 2260 kJ/kg):

$$Q_{lat} = 5579.6 \times 2200 = 12{,}275{,}120 \text{ kJ} \approx 12{,}275 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step I — Total energy demand per batch:**

$$Q_{total} = Q_{sens} + Q_{lat} = 4200 + 12{,}275 = 16{,}475 \text{ MJ}$$

Converting to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$Q_{total} = \dfrac{16{,}475}{3.6} \approx 4576 \text{ kWh per batch}$$

**Step J — Steam requirement:**

Assuming saturated steam at **3 bar gauge** ($T_{sat} \approx 134 °C$, latent heat ≈ 2140 kJ/kg):

$$m_{steam} = \dfrac{16{,}475{,}000 \text{ kJ}}{2140 \text{ kJ/kg}} \approx 7699 \text{ kg steam per batch}$$

**Interpretation:** At ~6 hours per batch, this is a sustained steam demand of ~**1280 kg/h** — confirming why steam-boiler sizing is critical in distillery design.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Operating Variable Sensitivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners love sensitivity questions.** The matrix below maps input variables to output quality — memorize the arrows.

| Operating Variable | ↑ Increase Effect on Spirit Yield | ↑ Increase Effect on Congener Loss | ↑ Increase Effect on Energy Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wash charge ABV | ↑↑ Strongly increases | ↓ Slightly reduces | ↑ Increases |
| Heating rate (steam flow) | ↑ Slightly increases | ↑↑ Increases (less reflux time) | ↑↑ Strongly increases |
| Reflux ratio (R = L/D) | ↓ Slightly decreases | ↑↑ Strongly reduces heavy congeners | ↑ Increases |
| Lyne-arm cooling water T | ↑ (warmer) → ↑ Slightly | ↑ Increases light congener carry-over | ↓ Slightly decreases |
| Pot fill level | ↑ (more wash) → ↑ Slightly | ↑ Increases (longer contact) | ↑ Increases |
| Copper surface area exposed | ↓ Slightly decreases | ↑↑ Strongly removes sulfur | Neutral |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Define "wash still" and state its function in spirit production.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A wash still is a **copper pot distillation vessel** used in the first stage of malt spirit (e.g., Scotch whisky) production. Its primary function is to **distill the fermented wash (7–10 % ABV) into low wines (20–25 % ABV)** by exploiting the differential volatility of ethanol (BP 78.37 °C) and water (BP 100 °C). It operates as a **batch process**, separating volatile congeners into foreshots, heart (clean low wines), and feints, while leaving spent lees (residue) behind. **[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for function, 1 for fractionation concept]**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**List any three fractions collected during wash still distillation and state what each represents.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Foreshots** — First ~30 minutes of distillate, rich in low-boiling volatiles like methanol, acetone, acetaldehyde; toxic, must be discarded or reprocessed. **[1 mark]**
2. **Heart (low wines)** — Middle, clean fraction with ABV typically 20–25 %; this is the desired distillate that is forwarded to the spirit still. **[1 mark]**
3. **Feints (tails)** — Late fraction containing fusel oils (higher alcohols) and water-rich condensate; recycled into the next wash still charge. **[1 mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, ESE Pattern]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat flow diagram the **step-by-step operation of a wash still** including charging, heating, vapor handling, condensation, and fraction collection.

**(b) [7 marks]** A wash still is charged with **18,000 kg** of wash containing **9 % ABV** by volume. The distillation produces low wines at **24 % ABV** and spent lees containing **0.05 % ABV**. Calculate the **mass of low wines produced**, the **mass of spent lees**, and the **ethanol recovery efficiency**. Given: density of wash = 985 kg/m³, density of low wines = 960 kg/m³, density of water = 1000 kg/m³, density of ethanol = 789 kg/m³.

#### (a) Model Solution — Wash Still Operation Sequence

**Step 1 – Charging [1 mark]:** The wash (fermented beer) is pumped into the copper pot still via the charging port, filling to **70–80 %** of capacity (~13,000–14,000 L in a 18,000 L still). This headspace prevents boil-over during vigorous boiling.

**Step 2 – Sealing [1 mark]:** All valves including the man-door are sealed; safety pressure valve is set at **≤ 0.2 bar** gauge. The dome is checked for gasket integrity.

**Step 3 – Heating [1 mark]:** Steam is admitted to internal heating coils (or the external gas burner is ignited). Heating proceeds at a controlled rate (~**2 °C/min**) to prevent thermal shock to copper and to allow stable boiling.

**Step 4 – Vapor Rise [1 mark]:** As wash reaches ~78–95 °C, ethanol-rich vapors rise through the swan neck. The vapor phase is enriched in ethanol (typically 55–65 % ethanol by volume).

**Step 5 – Partial Condensation [1 mark]:** Vapors entering the **lyne arm** encounter cooling; heavier congeners and water condense and reflux back into the pot (reflux ratio R ≈ 0.5–1.0). This improves separation.

**Step 6 – Total Condensation [1 mark]:** Remaining vapor enters the **worm tub condenser** (shell-and-tube heat exchanger cooled by water < 20 °C), where it is fully condensed to liquid low wines at 15–25 °C.

**Step 7 – Fraction Collection [1 mark]:** The distillate is directed sequentially to:
- **Foreshots receiver** (first ~30 min, ABV > 75 %) — discarded/reprocessed
- **Heart receiver** (mid-run, ABV 70–75 %) → stored as low wines for spirit still
- **Feints receiver** (late run, ABV < 70 %) → recycled with next wash charge

#### (b) Model Solution — Mass Balance Calculation

**Step 1 — Compute feed volume and mass of ethanol:**

$$V_F = \dfrac{18{,}000}{985} = 18.274 \text{ m}^3 = 18{,}274 \text{ L}$$

Mass fraction of ethanol in 9 % ABV wash:

$$w_F = \dfrac{789 \times 9}{789 \times 9 + 1000 \times 91} = \dfrac{7101}{7101 + 91{,}000} = \dfrac{7101}{98{,}101} = 0.0724$$

$$m_{EtOH,F} = 18{,}000 \times 0.0724 = 1303.2 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Set up mass balance equations [2 marks]:**

Let $m_D$ = mass of low wines, $m_W$ = mass of spent lees.

Overall: $18{,}000 = m_D + m_W$

Ethanol: $1303.2 = m_D \cdot w_D + m_W \cdot w_W$

Mass fractions of low wines (24 % ABV) and spent lees (0.05 % ABV):

$$w_D = \dfrac{789 \times 24}{789 \times 24 + 1000 \times 76} = \dfrac{18{,}936}{18{,}936 + 76{,}000} = \dfrac{18{,}936}{94{,}936} = 0.1995$$

$$w_W \approx \dfrac{789 \times 0.05}{1000} = 3.945 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system [2 marks]:**

Substitute $m_W = 18{,}000 - m_D$ into ethanol balance:

$$1303.2 = 0.1995 \cdot m_D + 3.945 \times 10^{-4} \cdot (18{,}000 - m_D)$$

$$1303.2 = 0.1995 \cdot m_D + 7.101 - 3.945 \times 10^{-4} \cdot m_D$$

$$1303.2 - 7.101 = (0.1995 - 3.945 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot m_D$$

$$1296.10 = 0.19911 \cdot m_D$$

$$m_D = \dfrac{1296.10}{0.19911} = 6509.4 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_W = 18{,}000 - 6509.4 = 11{,}490.6 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ethanol recovery efficiency [1 mark]:**

$$m_{EtOH,D} = 6509.4 \times 0.1995 = 1298.6 \text{ kg}$$

$$\eta_{EtOH} = \dfrac{1298.6}{1303.2} \times 100\% = 99.65\%$$

**Step 5 — Convert volumes for verification:**

$$V_D = \dfrac{6509.4}{960} = 6.781 \text{ m}^3 = 6781 \text{ L} \quad \checkmark$$

$$V_W = \dfrac{11{,}490.6}{1000} = 11.491 \text{ m}^3 = 11{,}491 \text{ L} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Error 1:** Students forget to convert ABV (volume %) to mass fraction using densities. This causes ~10–15 % error in all downstream calculations.
> - **Common Error 2:** Students assume $m_W = $ mass of "water remaining" only, ignoring dissolved solids (~5 % by mass in real spent lees).
> - **Common Error 3:** Sign errors when substituting $m_W = F - m_D$; always re-check by summing back.
> - **Mark Loss Zone:** Forgetting units (kg vs. L) in the final answer typically costs 1 mark per occurrence.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, ESE Pattern]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **role of copper** in a wash still. Why is it mandatory in Scotch whisky production, and how does it interact with sulfur compounds?

**(b) [7 marks]** A continuous-feed wash still receives **2000 kg/h** of wash at **7.5 % ABV**. It produces **700 kg/h** of low wines at **22 % ABV** and the rest exits as spent lees. Determine the spent lees ABV and the steam consumption (kg/h) if **3.5 kg steam** is required per kg of low wines produced. Use $\rho_{EtOH} = 789$ kg/m³, $\rho_W = 1000$ kg/m³.

#### (a) Model Solution — Role of Copper

**Step 1 — Thermal Conductivity [2 marks]:** Copper has a thermal conductivity of **~400 W/m·K**, far exceeding stainless steel (~16 W/m·K) or glass (~1 W/m·K). This allows efficient, uniform heat transfer across the pot wall and prevents localized hot spots that would scorch the wash and produce off-flavors.

**Step 2 — Catalytic Removal of Sulfur Compounds [3 marks]:** Copper reacts with volatile sulfur compounds (e.g., hydrogen sulfide H₂S, dimethyl sulfide DMS, mercaptans) to form **copper sulfide (CuS)** deposits on the still interior:

$$Cu_{(s)} + H_2S_{(g)} \rightarrow CuS_{(s)} + H_{2(g)}$$

This reaction is **catalytic and self-renewing** because CuS decomposes back to Cu at high temperatures, allowing continuous sulfur scavenging. Without copper, the spirit would exhibit **rubbery, vegetal off-notes**.

**Step 3 — Regulatory Mandate [1 mark]:** The Scotch Whisky Regulations 2009 (UK) explicitly require distillation in **copper pot stills** (with a minimum copper contact specification). Other spirits (e.g., vodka, neutral grain spirit) may use stainless steel columns because congener removal is less critical.

**Step 4 — Ester and Aldehyde Modulation [1 mark]:** Copper surfaces also catalyze the **esterification of fatty acids** with ethanol, producing fruity aroma esters (e.g., ethyl hexanoate → apple note), enhancing spirit complexity.

#### (b) Model Solution — Continuous Wash Still

**Step 1 — Mass fractions [1 mark]:**

For 7.5 % ABV wash:
$$w_F = \dfrac{789 \times 7.5}{789 \times 7.5 + 1000 \times 92.5} = \dfrac{5917.5}{5917.5 + 92{,}500} = \dfrac{5917.5}{98{,}417.5} = 0.0601$$

For 22 % ABV low wines:
$$w_D = \dfrac{789 \times 22}{789 \times 22 + 1000 \times 78} = \dfrac{17{,}358}{95{,}358} = 0.1821$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance to find $m_W$ [1 mark]:**

$$m_W = 2000 - 700 = 1300 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Ethanol balance to find spent lees ABV [2 marks]:**

$$m_{EtOH,F} = 2000 \times 0.0601 = 120.2 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$m_{EtOH,D} = 700 \times 0.1821 = 127.47 \text{ kg/h}$$

**The fact that $m_{EtOH,D} > m_{EtOH,F}$** indicates that the 22 % ABV assumption is too high (or 7.5 % ABV input is too low) — a realistic distillation would not produce more ethanol than input. **In practice**, we solve for $w_W$ assuming the stated values are correct:

$$m_{EtOH,W} = m_{EtOH,F} - m_{EtOH,D} = 120.2 - 127.47 = -7.27 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Negative → input data inconsistency.** For exam purposes, the correct interpretation is: the ethanol in low wines **must equal** the ethanol input minus ethanol in spent lees. Assuming zero carry-over for ideal textbook:

$$m_{EtOH,W} \approx 0 \Rightarrow w_W \approx 0 \Rightarrow \text{ABV}_{W} \approx 0.05–0.10 \%$$

A safer textbook answer is **spent lees ABV ≈ 0.1 % v/v** (typical industrial value).

**Step 4 — Steam consumption [2 marks]:**

$$m_{steam} = 3.5 \times 700 = 2450 \text{ kg/h}$$

**At 700 kg/h low wines, this is a steam rate of ~41 kg/min** — within typical distillery boiler capacity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Confusing *mass fraction* with *volume fraction*. KTU expects ABV as volume %; mass balance MUST use mass fractions.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Negative ethanol — students often blindly write the result. The correct engineering response is to flag the inconsistency and provide a realistic industrial value.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Forgetting that foreshots and feints are *not* counted in "low wines" heart fraction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Wash Still Operation**

- ✅ **Wash still** = first pot still in malt spirit production; converts **wash (7–10 % ABV)** → **low wines (20–25 % ABV)**.
- ✅ Operates as a **batch process** with charging, heating, vapor handling, condensation, and fraction collection.
- ✅ **Copper** is mandatory (Scotch regulation) — removes sulfur compounds via **CuS formation**.
- ✅ Key fractions: **foreshots** (discard), **heart** (low wines, forward to spirit still), **feints** (recycle).
- ✅ Governing laws: **Dalton's Law** (vapor pressure summation), **Raoult's Law** (VLE), **Antoine equation** (saturation pressure).
- ✅ Reflux ratio in wash still is **low (R = 0.5 – 1.0)** compared to spirit still (R = 1.5 – 3.0).
- ✅ Energy demand ≈ **4200 MJ sensible** + **12,275 MJ latent** ≈ **16,500 MJ per 15,000 kg batch**.
- ✅ Spent lees are **hot (~95 °C)** — recovered for **pre-heating next batch** via heat exchangers.
- ✅ **Ethanol boiling point = 78.37 °C**; **water = 100 °C**; azeotrope at **95.6 % v/v, 78.2 °C** limits simple batch distillation.
- ✅ Material balance closure: $F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_D + W \cdot x_W$ — must convert ABV → mass fraction using densities.
- ✅ Ethanol mass fraction formula: $w = \dfrac{\rho_{EtOH} \cdot V\%}{\rho_{EtOH} \cdot V\% + \rho_W (1 - V\%)}$.
- ✅ Typical **pot fill = 70–80 %** to prevent boil-over.
- ✅ Steam consumption in continuous operation: ~**3.5 kg steam / kg low wines**.
- ✅ Wash still is the **bottleneck equipment** in distillery throughput — sets the entire production rate.
- ✅ Safety: ethanol vapor LEL = **3.3 % v/v**; safety valves set at **≤ 0.2 bar**; cooling water < **20 °C**.
- ✅ Copper contact also **enhances ester formation** (fruity notes) — secondary benefit beyond sulfur removal.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spirit Still Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spirit Still Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spirit Still Operation** refers to the controlled **distillation** of a fermented wash (or low-proof beer) to concentrate **ethanol** and selectively separate the volatile aroma congeners into defined fractions: **foreshots**, **heads**, **hearts**, and **feints (tails)**. The device in which this operation is performed is called a **spirit still**, and its operating regime dictates the organoleptic profile, purity, and yield of the final beverage-grade spirit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The course treats the spirit still as a **mass-transfer separation unit** governed by **vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE)**. Students must be able to distinguish between **batch (pot still)** and **continuous (patent / Coffey still)** operation, and apply **reflux-based plate calculations** for design.

There are two principal classes of spirit stills:

| Still Type | Operation Mode | Typical Products |
|---|---|---|
| **Pot Still** | Batch | Scotch malt whisky, Irish whiskey, Cognac, Armagnac, pot-still rum |
| **Patent (Coffey) Still** | Continuous | Grain whisky, vodka, neutral spirit, grain-neutral rum |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are boiling a pot of spiced tea. The steam rising carries the volatile aromatic oils (cardamom, ginger) upward first because they vaporize at the lowest temperatures, while the heavier bitter compounds stay dissolved in the water. If you condense the steam in stages — catching the first wisps separately, the middle separately, and the lingering after-steam separately — you obtain three different "fractions" of flavor. A **spirit still works on exactly this principle**, except the "tea" is a fermented wash containing water, ethanol (boiling point **78.37 °C**), and hundreds of flavor congeners such as **fusel oils**, **aldehydes**, **esters**, and **methanol** (boiling point **64.7 °C**).

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the still as a **"fractional perfume collector"**: the distiller is intentionally splitting a complex vapor stream into cuts based on volatility, so that undesirable compounds (e.g., methanol in foreshots, fusel oils in tails) are rejected while the desirable heart of the spirit is retained.

### 1.3 Key Operational Metrics (Bolded)

- **Reflux Ratio (R):** Ratio of condensed liquid returned to the column to the liquid withdrawn as distillate; dimensionless.
- **Pot Charge:** Typically **8–15 % alcohol by volume (ABV)** wash loaded into the pot.
- **Low Wines:** First distillation output (~**20–30 % ABV**).
- **Feints:** Tails fraction combined with foreshots for redistillation.
- **Hearts Cut:** Final spirit strength typically **65–70 % ABV** for whisky, **~96 % ABV** for vodka.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Logic of a Pot Still (Batch)

1. **Charging:** The pot (copper vessel) is filled to ~**70–80 %** capacity with wash to prevent boil-over.
2. **Heating:** Steam is injected through internal coils or an external fire heats the pot wall. The wash temperature rises toward the bubble point.
3. **Vapor Generation:** As boiling begins, the more volatile components (ethyl acetate, acetaldehyde, methanol) vaporize first → these constitute **foreshots** (collected separately, always discarded).
4. **Main Distillation:** Ethanol and fruity esters dominate the vapor stream → this is the **heads**, transitioning into the **heart** (the desirable fraction).
5. **Hearts Collection:** The distiller separates heart from heads/tails by **taste and ABV measurement** (using a hydrometer or ebulliometer).
6. **Tails Onset:** As ethanol depletes, **fusel oils (1-propanol, isoamyl alcohol, 2-methyl-1-butanol)** and heavier acids co-distill. The condenser output ABV drops below the cut point (commonly **~58–65 % ABV**).
7. **End of Run:** Tails (feints) are collected and stored separately for re-distillation in the next batch.
8. **Cleaning:** Copper reacts with sulfur compounds to remove off-flavors — a critical reason pot stills use **copper**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic of a Patent (Coffey) Still (Continuous)

1. **Wash enters** the analyzer (rectifying) column near the top, where it descends against a rising steam/vapor stream.
2. **Steam** is injected at the base of the analyzer, stripping ethanol from the descending wash.
3. The **stripped water (spent lees)** exits at the bottom of the analyzer.
4. Vapor rich in ethanol rises to the **rectifier column**, where it passes through perforated plates.
5. Each plate acts as a **single equilibrium stage** — partially condensing the vapor and re-evaporating the liquid.
6. **Reflux** is generated by a **condenser** at the top; a portion (reflux ratio) returns to the column, the rest is withdrawn as **distillate** at high proof (~**94–96 % ABV**).
7. The **doubler (or spirit heater)** partially re-evaporates the distillate, allowing final congener stripping and heads/tails separation in a smaller secondary column.

### 2.3 Governing Principles (the "Why" and "How")

- **How does separation occur?** Boiling creates a vapor phase enriched in the more volatile component. On each plate (or each "theoretical stage"), the rising vapor contacts descending liquid; ethanol preferentially transfers to the vapor. This stage-wise contact approaches equilibrium, and many plates ≈ high purity.
- **Why reflux?** Returning some condensed distillate to the top increases the number of effective stages. Higher reflux ratio → sharper separation but more energy.
- **Why copper?** It catalyzes the removal of foul-smelling **sulfur compounds** (e.g., dimethyl sulfide) and precipitates fatty acids.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The number of plates above the feed (rectifying section) and below the feed (stripping section) determines the sharpness of the ethanol-water separation. Most patent stills operate with **15–25 actual plates**, achieving ~**95 % ABV** distillate.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y_A = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P}$ | Dalton's Law — vapor mole fraction = partial pressure / total pressure | dimensionless |
| 2 | $P_A = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}(T)$ | Raoult's Law — partial pressure of component A over ideal liquid | kPa |
| 3 | $\alpha_{A,B} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$ | Relative volatility (ethanol vs water); at 78.37 °C, $\alpha \approx 12.4$ (ideal) but ~**3.5–4.0** effective at distillation column conditions | dimensionless |
| 4 | $y = \dfrac{\alpha \, x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)\, x}$ | VLE relationship for a binary ideal system | dimensionless |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | Reflux Ratio = molar liquid returned / molar distillate | dimensionless |
| 6 | $q$-line: $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}\, x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | Feed condition line (McCabe–Thiele); for saturated liquid $q = 1$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $R_{\min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_F^{*}}{y_F^{*} - x_F}$ | Minimum reflux for binary separation | dimensionless |
| 8 | $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1}\, x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Operating line — Rectifying section (above feed) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $y_{m+1} = \dfrac{L'}{V'}\, x_m - \dfrac{W \, x_W}{V'}$ | Operating line — Stripping section (below feed) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_{ABV} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \rho_D \cdot ABV_D}{V_W \cdot \rho_W \cdot ABV_W} \times 100$ | Alcohol Yield / Recovery (%) | % |
| 11 | $Q_{steam} = \dot{m}_F \, c_{p,F} \, \Delta T + \Delta H_{vap}$ | Steam requirement per batch | kJ |
| 12 | $\text{Proof} = 2 \times \text{ABV (US)}$; $\text{British Proof} = 1.75 \times \text{ABV}$ | Strength conversion | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all exam derivations, students must explicitly state the assumption: **constant molar overflow** (CMO) for McCabe–Thiele analysis and **constant relative volatility** across plate temperatures unless otherwise noted.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Spirit stills form the heart of multi-billion-dollar global industries: Scotch whisky (£**7+ billion** exports), Irish whiskey, bourbon, vodka (~$**45 billion** global market), and brandy. The same engineering principles — VLE, reflux, plate efficiency — apply directly to **petroleum refining columns**, **ethanol bio-refineries**, and **flavor/fragrance extractors**. Mastering spirit-still mathematics gives a B.Tech student a transferable mass-transfer toolkit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Binary VLE for Ethanol–Water

**Given:** Raoult's law holds for an ideal liquid solution; total pressure $P = 101.325$ kPa; component A = ethanol, component B = water.

**Step 1:** Apply Raoult's Law to both components.

$$P = P_A + P_B = x_A \, P_A^{sat}(T) + (1 - x_A) \, P_B^{sat}(T)$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the bubble-point temperature given $x_A$ (or for $x_A$ given $T$).

$$x_A = \dfrac{P - P_B^{sat}(T)}{P_A^{sat}(T) - P_B^{sat}(T)}$$

**Step 3:** Express vapor-phase composition using Dalton's Law.

$$y_A = \dfrac{P_A}{P} = \dfrac{x_A \, P_A^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $x_A$ from Step 2 into Step 3 to get $y_A$ directly as a function of $T$.

$$y_A = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P} \cdot \dfrac{P - P_B^{sat}(T)}{P_A^{sat}(T) - P_B^{sat}(T)}$$

**Step 5:** Introduce relative volatility $\alpha = P_A^{sat} / P_B^{sat}$ (function of $T$).

$$y_A = \dfrac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1)\, x_A}$$

This is the canonical **binary VLE equation** plotted on the McCabe–Thiele diagram.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mass Balance on the Rectifying Section of a Patent Still

**Assumption:** Steady-state, constant molar overflow, no reaction.

**Step 1:** Draw the control volume around the top of the column above the feed. Total molar flows: vapor $V$ rising, liquid $L$ descending, distillate $D$ withdrawn.

**Step 2:** Total material balance.

$$V = L + D$$

**Step 3:** Component (ethanol) material balance.

$$V \, y_{n+1} = L \, x_n + D \, x_D$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $L = V - D$ and $R = L/D \Rightarrow L = R \, D$.

$$V \, y_{n+1} = R \, D \, x_n + D \, x_D$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by $V$ and use $V = D(R + 1)$.

$$y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R + 1}\, x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R + 1}$$

This is the **rectifying operating line**, intersecting the diagonal $y = x$ at $x = x_D$ with slope $R/(R+1)$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Reflux Ratio ($R_{\min}$)

**Step 1:** At minimum reflux, an infinite number of stages is required and the operating line intersects the **feed line** at the **q-line–VLE intersection point** $(x_F, y_F^{*})$.

**Step 2:** Write the rectifying line passing through $(x_D, x_D)$ and $(x_F, y_F^{*})$.

$$\dfrac{x_D - y_F^{*}}{x_D - x_F} = \dfrac{R_{\min}}{R_{\min} + 1}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $R_{\min}$.

$$R_{\min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_F^{*}}{y_F^{*} - x_F}$$

This is the boundary condition used in the **Gilliland correlation** to estimate the actual number of plates for a chosen operating reflux ratio $R = (1.2 \text{ to } 2.0) \, R_{\min}$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Alcohol Recovery / Yield for a Pot Still

**Given:** Wash volume $V_W$ (L) at $ABV_W$ (%), distillate volume $V_D$ (L) at $ABV_D$ (%).

**Step 1:** Volume of pure alcohol in wash.

$$A_{in} = V_W \cdot \dfrac{ABV_W}{100}$$

**Step 2:** Volume of pure alcohol in distillate.

$$A_{out} = V_D \cdot \dfrac{ABV_D}{100}$$

**Step 3:** Recovery efficiency.

$$\eta = \dfrac{A_{out}}{A_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{V_D \cdot ABV_D}{V_W \cdot ABV_W} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often supply $V_W$, $ABV_W$, and $V_D$ and ask for $ABV_D$ (or vice versa). Always retain units — a common 1-mark loss comes from missing the %/100 conversion.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A patent still receives **1000 L/h** of wash at **7 % ABV** and produces **70 L/h** of distillate at **94 % ABV**. Calculate (a) alcohol recovery, (b) mass of spent lees per hour at 0.05 % ABV.

**Step (a):** Apply the recovery formula.

$$\eta = \dfrac{70 \times 0.94}{1000 \times 0.07} \times 100 = \dfrac{65.8}{70} \times 100 = 94\,\%$$

**Step (b):** Volumetric balance.

$$V_{lees} = V_W - V_D = 1000 - 70 = 930 \text{ L/h}$$

Mass balance on water (neglecting density differences): water in ≈ water out at 93 % mass balance.

**Ethanol check:**
- In: $1000 \times 0.07 = 70$ L ethanol/h
- Out: $70 \times 0.94 + 930 \times 0.0005 = 65.8 + 0.465 = 66.265$ L ethanol/h
- Loss: $70 - 66.265 = 3.735$ L ethanol/h → vented/entrained.

The ~**5 % unaccounted ethanol** represents the small losses in tails and vapor leaks, which is realistic for industrial operation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pot Still Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["WASH HOLDING VESSEL"] --> nodeB["POT STILL COPPER CHARGE 70 PERCENT FILL"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["HEATING BY STEAM COIL OR DIRECT FIRE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["VAPOR RISES THROUGH SWAN NECK LYNE ARM"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["CONDENSER SHELL AND TUBE OR WORM TUB"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["FORESHOTS FRACTION COLLECTED DISCARDED"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["HEADS FRACTION OPTIONAL SEPARATION"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["HEARTS FRACTION MAIN PRODUCT 65 TO 70 ABV"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["TAILS FEINTS COLLECTED FOR REDISTILLATION"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["SPENT LEES POT ALE REMOVED FROM BOTTOM"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["COOLING AND WASHING CYCLE REPEAT"]
```

### 4.2 Patent Coffey Still Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1["WASH FEED TANK PREHEATED"] --> nodeP2["ANALYZER COLUMN STRIPPING SECTION 20 PLATES"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3["STEAM INJECTION BOTTOM"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4["SPENT LEES EXIT NEAR 0 PERCENT ABV"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP5["RECTIFIER COLUMN ENRICHING SECTION 15 PLATES"]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6["TOP CONDENSER TOTAL CONDENSER"]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7["REFUX RETURN TO RECTIFIER TOP"]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP8["DISTILLATE WITHDRAWAL 94 TO 96 ABV"]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP9["DOUBLER OR SPIRIT HEATER AUX COLUMN"]
    nodeP9 --> nodeP10["HEADS AND TAILS SIDE DRAWS"]
    nodeP10 --> nodeP11["FINAL HEART PRODUCT TO MATURATION"]
```

### 4.3 Fractional Cut Sequence (Heads–Hearts–Tails)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1["DISTILLATION BEGINS"] --> nodeQ2["VAPOR TEMPERATURE 78 TO 80 C"]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3["FORESHOTS METHANOL ACETALDEHYDE DISCARDED"]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ4["HEADS LIGHT ESTERS COLLECTED SEPARATELY"]
    nodeQ4 --> nodeQ5["HEARTS DESIRED FRACTION MAIN PRODUCT"]
    nodeQ5 --> nodeQ6["ABV DROPS BELOW CUTOFF APPROX 60 PERCENT"]
    nodeQ6 --> nodeQ7["TAILS FUSEL OILS HEAVY ESTERS STORED FOR REDISTILLATION"]
    nodeQ7 --> nodeQ8["RUN ENDS POT DRAINED AND CLEANED"]
```

### 4.4 Plate-by-Plate McCabe–Thiele Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR1["REFLUX L RETURNS TO TOP PLATE N"] --> nodeR2["PLATE N VAPOR YN RISES"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3["PLATE N MINUS 1 LIQUID DESCENDS"]
    nodeR3 --> nodeR4["EACH PLATE ONE EQUILIBRIUM STEP"]
    nodeR4 --> nodeR5["FEED PLATE XF ZONE OF TRANSITION"]
    nodeR5 --> nodeR6["STRIPPING SECTION BELOW FEED"]
    nodeR6 --> nodeR7["STEAM INJECTED AT REBOILER"]
    nodeR7 --> nodeR8["SPENT LEES WITHDRAWN XW NEAR 0"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR9["CONDENSER VAPOR TO LIQUID"]
    nodeR9 --> nodeR10["SPLIT REFLUX L AND DISTILLATE D XD"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The number of **rectifying plates** above the feed is graphically obtained by stepping up from $(x_F, y_F)$ to $(x_D, x_D)$ using alternating operating-line and equilibrium-curve segments on the McCabe–Thiele diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
*Distinguish between a pot still and a patent still. Mention two products each made using them.*

**Model Answer:**
- A **pot still** operates in **batch mode**, distilling a fixed charge of wash with no reflux. It retains many flavor congeners, producing heavy-bodied spirits such as **Scotch malt whisky** and **Cognac**.
- A **patent (Coffey) still** operates **continuously** with multiple plates and reflux, producing high-purity neutral spirit at ~**94–96 % ABV** such as **vodka** and **grain whisky**.
- Key differences: batch vs continuous, low vs high reflux, heavy congener vs neutral spirit, single vs multi-plate separation.

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*What are the four fractions collected during spirit still operation? Briefly state their characteristic compounds.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Foreshots** — highly toxic and volatile; methanol, acetaldehyde; always discarded.
2. **Heads** — light esters (ethyl acetate) and aldehydes; pungent; often re-distilled.
3. **Hearts** — ethanol + desirable fruity esters; the marketed spirit cut.
4. **Tails (feints)** — fusel oils (isoamyl alcohol), heavier acids; collected for redistillation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the **rectifying operating line equation** for a continuous distillation column operating under the constant molar overflow assumption. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wash containing **8 % ethanol (by mass)** is fed at **2000 kg/h** to a patent still. The distillate is collected at **95 % ethanol by mass** and the spent lees at **0.1 % ethanol by mass**. Calculate (i) distillate flow rate, (ii) spent lees flow rate, (iii) alcohol recovery percentage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** 
- Assumption list: steady-state, binary system (ethanol-water), constant molar overflow, equilibrium at each plate, negligible heat losses.
- Total balance: $V = L + D$
- Component balance on plate $n$: $V y_{n+1} = L x_n + D x_D$
- Substituting $R = L/D$, divide through by $V = D(R+1)$:

$$y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1}\, x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$$

**(b)**
- Let $D$ = distillate (kg/h), $W$ = spent lees (kg/h).
- Total balance: $2000 = D + W$ ⟹ $W = 2000 - D$
- Ethanol balance: $2000 \times 0.08 = D \times 0.95 + W \times 0.001$
- Substitute: $160 = 0.95 D + 0.001(2000 - D) = 0.95 D + 2 - 0.001 D = 0.949 D + 2$
- Solve: $0.949 D = 158$ ⟹ $D = 166.49$ kg/h
- $W = 2000 - 166.49 = 1833.51$ kg/h
- Recovery: $\eta = (166.49 \times 0.95) / (2000 \times 0.08) \times 100 = 158.17 / 160 \times 100 = 98.85\,\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A part (b):** Students often forget to **convert mass % to fractional form** before substitution. Writing $0.95 D$ instead of $0.95$ (already fractional) is fine, but treating **8 % as "8"** without dividing by 100 is the most common 1-mark loss.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the working of a **Coffey (patent) still**. Identify the role of the **analyzer**, **rectifier**, and **doubler**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the **McCabe–Thiele method**, determine the **minimum reflux ratio** for separating a feed containing **40 mol % ethanol** to obtain a distillate of **90 mol % ethanol** and a bottoms product of **1 mol % ethanol**. The relative volatility $\alpha = 2.0$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** 
- **Diagram**: Two stacked columns — the analyzer below, the rectifier above. Feed enters the top of the analyzer; steam enters the bottom of the analyzer; vapor rises to the rectifier; a condenser at the top of the rectifier provides reflux; the doubler receives a side-draw of distillate for final rectification.
- **Analyzer**: acts as the stripping section — removes ethanol from the descending wash using live steam.
- **Rectifier**: enriching section — concentrates ethanol vapor against descending reflux.
- **Doubler**: small auxiliary column providing an additional equilibrium stage to sharpen the cut between heads, hearts, and tails.

**(b)**
- At minimum reflux, the operating line passes through the **q-line intersection with the VLE curve**. For a saturated liquid feed ($q = 1$), the q-line is vertical at $x_F = 0.40$.
- Find $y_F^{*}$ from the VLE relation $y = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha - 1) x]$:

$$y_F^{*} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 0.40}{1 + (2.0 - 1)(0.40)} = \dfrac{0.80}{1 + 0.40} = \dfrac{0.80}{1.40} = 0.5714$$

- Apply $R_{\min}$ formula:

$$R_{\min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_F^{*}}{y_F^{*} - x_F} = \dfrac{0.90 - 0.5714}{0.5714 - 0.40} = \dfrac{0.3286}{0.1714} = 1.917$$

- Practical operating reflux ratio: $R = (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \times R_{\min} \approx 2.3 \text{ to } 2.9$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B part (b):** Students frequently compute $y_F^{*}$ by linearly interpolating between $(x, y)$ data points instead of using the closed-form VLE formula. While interpolation may be acceptable if data is provided, the **closed-form VLE expression** (assuming constant $\alpha$) is preferred for full 7-mark credit. Also, remember to **state the q-line assumption** ($q = 1$ for saturated liquid feed) — losing 1 mark here is common.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Spirit still** = distillation device that fractionates fermented wash into ethanol-rich fractions with characteristic flavor profiles.
- **Two still types:** Pot (batch, low reflux, heavy congeners) and Patent/Coffey (continuous, multi-plate, high reflux, neutral spirit).
- **Four fractions:** Foreshots (discard), Heads (often re-distilled), Hearts (product), Tails/Feints (re-distilled).
- **Governing laws:** Raoult's Law + Dalton's Law → binary VLE: $y = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha - 1)x]$.
- **Reflux ratio $R = L/D$** directly controls separation sharpness; energy vs purity trade-off.
- **Rectifying operating line:** $y_{n+1} = [R/(R+1)] x_n + [x_D/(R+1)]$.
- **Minimum reflux:** $R_{\min} = (x_D - y_F^{*}) / (y_F^{*} - x_F)$.
- **McCabe–Thiele step-off** gives the number of theoretical plates graphically.
- **Alcohol recovery:** $\eta = (V_D \cdot ABV_D) / (V_W \cdot ABV_W) \times 100\,\%$.
- **Copper contact** is critical in pot stills for sulfur removal and flavor modification.
- **Steam requirement** for batch pot still: $Q_{steam} = \dot{m}_F c_{p,F} \Delta T + \Delta H_{vap}$.
- **Doubler** in a patent still provides final congener stripping and heads/tails separation.
- **Proof conversion:** US Proof $= 2 \times$ ABV; British Proof $= 1.75 \times$ ABV.
- **Always retain units and state assumptions** (constant molar overflow, constant $\alpha$, steady-state) for full credit in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Distillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Continuous Distillation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Continuous Distillation** is an industrial steady-state separation process in which a liquid mixture (typically a binary or multi-component beverage system such as ethanol–water, methanol–ethanol, or essential oil blends) is continuously fed into a vertical column equipped with either trays (plates) or structured/random packing, where it is repeatedly vaporized and condensed under carefully controlled thermal conditions to achieve separation based on the relative volatilities (α) of its constituent components.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Continuous distillation is a mass-transfer operation in which a feed mixture enters the column continuously, the more volatile component is preferentially concentrated in the vapor phase ascending the column, and is recovered as the **distillate (D)** at the top, while the less volatile component descends as the **bottom product (W or B)** and is recovered from the **reboiler (kettle)**.

The fundamental governing principle is the establishment of a **vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE)** between the rising vapor and the descending liquid on each stage, described quantitatively by the relative volatility:

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{(y_A/x_A)}{(y_B/x_B)}$$

where $K_i$ is the **K-value (equilibrium constant)** of component *i*, $y_i$ is the vapor-phase mole fraction, and $x_i$ is the liquid-phase mole fraction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling-Pot Waterfall"

Imagine a tall, vertical **waterfall inside a heated chimney**:
- A **mixture of two perfumes** (light "Citrus" and heavy "Sandalwood") is poured in at the middle.
- The chimney is gently heated at the bottom. The lighter Citrus perfume evaporates first and **rises upward as vapor**, while the heavier Sandalwood liquid **drips downward**.
- At each level of the chimney, the rising vapor meets the descending liquid and partially exchanges molecules (just like two waterfalls colliding and exchanging droplets).
- At the **very top**, a cold plate condenses the Citrus vapor into liquid. Part of it is **drawn off as the pure product (distillate)** and the rest is **sent back down the column as reflux**, like an internal recycling waterfall.
- At the **very bottom**, the heavy Sandalwood collects in the kettle and is drawn off as the bottoms product.

This continuous recycling (reflux) and counter-current contact is what makes continuous distillation **far more efficient** than boiling a pot once and collecting all the vapor — the same molecules get "re-distilled" hundreds of times internally.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Industrial Operating Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------|
| **Reflux Ratio** | R | **1.2 – 5.0** |
| **Number of Theoretical Plates** | $N_{th}$ | **10 – 80** |
| **Column Pressure** | P | **1 – 10 atm** |
| **Feed Composition** | $z_F$ | **0.05 – 0.60** (mole fraction) |
| **Relative Volatility** | α | **1.1 – 10** |
| **Murphree Tray Efficiency** | $E_{Mv}$ | **0.5 – 0.8** |
| **Feed Thermal Condition** | q | **0 to 1+** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** When answering theory questions, students must explicitly mention the **Reflux Ratio (R = L/D)** as the single most important operating handle, because it directly controls **purity vs. throughput** trade-off.

### 1.4 Advantages of Continuous over Batch Distillation (Beverage Industry Perspective)

1. **Steady-state product quality** — uniform ethanol concentration in every drop of distillate.
2. **Higher energy efficiency** — heat integration via feed pre-heating reduces utility cost.
3. **Larger throughput** — required for industrial-scale whiskey, vodka, rum, and bioethanol plants.
4. **Better automation** — DCS/PLC control of R, T, P, F makes consistent product specification easier.
5. **Lower labor and supervision cost** per litre of finished beverage alcohol.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Stage-by-Stage Logic

A continuous distillation column operates on the principle of **counter-current multi-stage mass transfer** between a vapor phase rising upward and a liquid phase flowing downward. The logic unfolds in structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Feed Introduction:** The pre-heated feed (composition $z_F$) is introduced onto the **feed plate**, which is selected to minimize the total number of stages required.
- **Step 2 — Vapor Generation:** The reboiler at the base of the column vaporizes a portion of the bottom liquid, generating vapor of composition $y_W$ (enriched in the more volatile component).
- **Step 3 — Vapor Ascent & Equilibrium:** Vapor rises through each tray, contacting the descending liquid. On every tray, partial equilibrium is established between the two phases (governed by Murphree efficiency $E_{Mv}$).
- **Step 4 — Liquid Descent & Reflux:** The condensed overhead vapor is split into two streams in the **reflux drum**: (i) Distillate product D (composition $x_D$), and (ii) Reflux L (returned to the top plate).
- **Step 5 — Bottom Withdrawal:** The heavy-key-enriched liquid leaves the reboiler as bottoms W (composition $x_W$).
- **Step 6 — Steady-State Material Balance:** Under constant operation, $F = D + W$ and $F \cdot z_F = D \cdot x_D + W \cdot x_W$.

### 2.2 The Two Sections of the Column

| Section | Location | Function | Operating Line |
|---------|----------|----------|----------------|
| **Rectifying (Enriching)** | Above feed plate | Enriches vapor in light component | Uses $x_D$ and R |
| **Stripping (Exhausting)** | Below feed plate | Strips light component from liquid | Uses $x_W$ and $\bar{R}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind two operating lines:** Above the feed, the liquid flow is L (only reflux), so the operating line is "steeper" and tied to the distillate purity. Below the feed, additional liquid enters from the feed (qF) plus reflux, so the liquid flow becomes $\bar{L} = L + qF$ and the operating line tilts differently.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Overall Material Balance** | $F = D + W$ | Feed equals sum of products |
| 2 | **Component Material Balance** | $F \cdot z_F = D \cdot x_D + W \cdot x_W$ | Ethanol (or key component) balance |
| 3 | **Reflux Ratio** | $R = L / D$ | Reflux flow per unit distillate |
| 4 | **Vapor Flow in Rectifying Section** | $V = L + D = (R+1)D$ | From total material balance |
| 5 | **Rectifying Operating Line (ROL)** | $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1} x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | y-intercept = $x_D/(R+1)$; slope = $R/(R+1)$ |
| 6 | **Stripping Operating Line (SOL)** | $y = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{\bar{R}-1} x - \dfrac{x_W}{\bar{R}-1}$ | Slope = $\bar{R}/(\bar{R}-1)$ |
| 7 | **q-line Equation** | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | Locus of feed-stage intersection |
| 8 | **Feed Condition Parameter** | $q = \dfrac{\text{moles of saturated liquid formed}}{\text{moles of feed}}$ | Defines feed thermal state |
| 9 | **Relative Volatility** | $\alpha_{AB} = (y_A/x_A)/(y_B/x_B)$ | Ease of separation |
| 10 | **Fenske Equation (Min. Stages)** | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_D}{x_W}\bigg/\dfrac{1-x_D}{1-x_W}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | Total reflux, no feed, no product |
| 11 | **Underwood Equation (Min. Reflux)** | $\sum \dfrac{\alpha_i \, z_{F,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1 - q$ | Implicit equation for θ |
| 12 | **Underwood R$_{\text{min}}$ Formula** | $R_{min} = \sum \dfrac{\alpha_i \, x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1$ | After solving θ |
| 13 | **Gilliland Correlation (Approx.)** | $Y = 0.75\,(1 - X^{0.5668})$ | $X = (N - N_{min})/(N+1)$, $Y = (N-N_{min})/(N+1)$ |
| 14 | **Murphree Vapor Efficiency** | $E_{Mv} = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$ | Real vs. ideal tray |
| 15 | **Overall Column Efficiency** | $E_o = N_{th}/N_{actual}$ | Uses empirical O'Connell correlation |
| 16 | **Minimum Reflux (Geometric)** | $R_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_F^*}{y_F^* - x_F}$ | From intersection of q-line with equilibrium |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When writing the operating line equations, students often write the **q-line as a single equation** valid for any q. Always substitute the correct q-value of the given feed (e.g., q = 1 for saturated liquid gives a **vertical q-line** with infinite slope).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Whiskey / Brandy plants:** Continuous column stills (e.g., Coffey still) separate ethanol from wash with hundreds of stages.
- **Vodka production:** Multi-column continuous systems achieve **95+ % ethanol purity** before the final charcoal filtration.
- **Bioethanol refineries:** Continuous distillation coupled with molecular sieves achieves azeotrope-breaking.
- **Essential oil recovery:** Citrus, menthol, and lavender oils are purified via continuous steam-distillation variants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rectifying Operating Line (ROL)

**Starting Point — Total Material Balance on Rectifying Section Envelope (top of column down to, but not including, the feed plate):**

$$V = L + D$$

where:
- $V$ = vapor flow rate leaving stage $n+1$ (mol/s)
- $L$ = liquid flow rate descending from stage $n$ (mol/s)
- $D$ = distillate product flow rate (mol/s)

**Component (Light Key) Material Balance on the Same Envelope:**

$$V \cdot y_{n+1} = L \cdot x_n + D \cdot x_D$$

**Step 1 — Divide throughout by V:**

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{V} x_n + \frac{D}{V} x_D$$

**Step 2 — Express L/V in terms of R using $R = L/D$:**

Since $V = L + D = (R+1)D$, we get:

$$\frac{L}{V} = \frac{RD}{(R+1)D} = \frac{R}{R+1}$$

and:

$$\frac{D}{V} = \frac{1}{R+1}$$

**Step 3 — Drop subscripts since the relationship holds for any stage $n$ in the rectifying section:**

$$\boxed{\,y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1}\,}$$

> **Conversion Logic Explained:** The intercept $x_D/(R+1)$ on the y-axis is the value of $y$ when $x = 0$ (pure heavy key entering the top). The slope $R/(R+1)$ is always less than 1 and increases toward unity as $R \to \infty$ (total reflux limit).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Stripping Operating Line (SOL)

**Total Material Balance on the Stripping Section Envelope (feed plate down to the reboiler):**

$$\bar{L} = \bar{V} + W$$

**Component (Light Key) Material Balance on the Same Envelope:**

$$\bar{L} \, x_m = \bar{V} \, y_{m+1} + W \, x_W$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $y_{m+1}$:**

$$y_{m+1} = \frac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} x_m - \frac{W}{\bar{V}} x_W$$

**Step 2 — Define Stripping Reflux Ratio $\bar{R} = \bar{L}/W$:**

Since $\bar{V} = \bar{L} - W = W(\bar{R} - 1)$:

$$\frac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{\bar{R}-1}, \qquad \frac{W}{\bar{V}} = \frac{1}{\bar{R}-1}$$

**Step 3 — Final Stripping Operating Line:**

$$\boxed{\,y = \frac{\bar{R}}{\bar{R}-1} x - \frac{x_W}{\bar{R}-1}\,}$$

> **Conversion Logic Explained:** The slope is greater than 1 (typical for stripping sections). As $\bar{R} \to \infty$ (large boil-up), the SOL approaches the 45° diagonal — same limit as the ROL at total reflux.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the q-line Equation

The q-line represents the locus of all possible intersection points between the ROL and SOL, parameterized by the feed condition.

**Feed Plate Material Balance:**

$$\bar{L} = L + qF$$
$$\bar{V} = V - (1-q)F$$

**Step 1 — Start from ROL and SOL set equal at intersection:**

$$\frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{\bar{R}}{\bar{R}-1} x - \frac{x_W}{\bar{R}-1}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the feed plate relationships and use overall balances** ($D = F(1-q)+W$... this becomes algebraically intensive). A more elegant shortcut is to write the q-line as the locus where:

$$\bar{L} x - L x = q F \, x$$
$$V y - \bar{V} y = (1-q) F \, y$$

**Step 3 — After algebraic reduction (standard textbook result):**

$$\boxed{\,y = \frac{q}{q-1} x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}\,}$$

> **Conversion Logic Explained:** The q-line passes through the feed composition point $(x = z_F, \, y = z_F)$. The slope $q/(q-1)$ depends entirely on the feed thermal state — saturated liquid → vertical line, saturated vapor → horizontal line.

---

### 3.4 Fenske Equation for Minimum Number of Theoretical Stages ($N_{min}$)

**Starting from Stage-wise Equilibrium at Total Reflux (R → ∞, F = D = W = 0):**

At total reflux, the operating line is the 45° diagonal: $y_{n+1} = x_n$.

**Step 1 — Write the equilibrium relationship across any stage n for a binary system:**

$$y_n = \frac{\alpha \, x_n}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_n}$$

**Step 2 — Define α-relative volatility and rearrange using Kremser formulation:**

$$\frac{x_D}{x_W} = \alpha^N \cdot \frac{1 - x_W}{1 - x_D}$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$N_{min} \cdot \ln \alpha = \ln \left[\frac{x_D}{x_W} \cdot \frac{1 - x_W}{1 - x_D}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Final Fenske Equation:**
