| 5 | Mass of CO₂ Required | $m_{CO_2} = V_{bev} \times V_{CO_2} \times \rho_{CO_2,STP}$ | grams | CO₂ cylinder sizing |
| 6 | Density of CO₂ at STP | $\rho_{CO_2,STP} = 1.977$ | g/L | Used in mass calculation |
| 7 | Mass of CO₂ at STP | $1$ V/V $= 1.977$ g/L of beverage | g/L | Quick reference |
| 8 | Carbonation Tower Hold-up | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{tower}}{\dot{V}}$ | seconds | Contact time check |
| 9 | Brix Acid Ratio | BAR $= \dfrac{\text{Brix}}{\text{Titratable Acid (as \% citric)}}$ | ratio | Cola balance index |
| 10 | pH of Acidified Beverage | pH $= 2.5$–$3.5$ (typical) | log scale | Quality specification |

---

## ⚙️ Engineering Utility of the System

| Application Domain | Use of Carbonation Principles |
|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Soft Drinks Industry** | Producing colas, lemonades, sparkling waters |
| **Breweries** | Beer (natural carbonation via fermentation + forced) |
| **Dairy Beverages** | Carbonated flavored milk, kefir-style drinks |
| **Functional Drinks** | Probiotic sparkling drinks, energy drinks |
| **Food Preservation** | Modified atmosphere packaging (CO₂ flush) |
| **Wine & Champagne** | Sparkling wine secondary fermentation |

---

## 🔍 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

1. **Step 1 — Identify target V/V** (e.g., 3.5 for cola).
2. **Step 2 — Determine chilled water temperature** (typically 4 °C).
3. **Step 3 — Read required pressure from carbonation chart** (≈ 60 psi at 4 °C).
4. **Step 4 — Set carbonator pressure regulator** to that value.
5. **Step 5 — Inject food-grade CO₂** (purity ≥ 99.9 %) through diffuser/sparger.
6. **Step 6 — Allow contact time** (3–10 seconds in modern inline systems).
7. **Step 7 — Verify V/V** using in-line **Zahm & Nagel piercing device** or **CarboQC**.
8. **Step 8 — Proceed to filler** maintaining back-pressure to prevent de-gassing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📘 Derivation 1: Volumes of CO₂ from First Principles

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We begin with the **ideal gas law** applied to CO₂ dissolved at STP:

$$PV = nRT$$

Rearranging to express moles of gas:

$$n = \dfrac{PV}{RT}$$

At **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP: 273.15 K, 1 atm)**:

$$V_{CO_2, STP} = \dfrac{nRT}{P_{atm}} = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{M_{CO_2}} \cdot \dfrac{RT}{P_{atm}}$$

The number of **volumes of CO₂** is defined as:

$$V_{CO_2} = \dfrac{V_{CO_2, STP}}{V_{liquid}}$$

Substituting the mass of CO₂ dissolved:

$$V_{CO_2} = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{V_{liquid} \cdot \rho_{CO_2, STP}}$$

Since $m_{CO_2}$ is determined by **Henry's Law** $m_{CO_2} = k_H \cdot P_{CO_2} \cdot V_{liquid}$, we get:

$$V_{CO_2} = \dfrac{k_H \cdot P_{CO_2}}{\rho_{CO_2, STP}}$$

For **CO₂ in water at 4 °C**, $k_H$ is calibrated such that:

$$V_{CO_2} = \dfrac{P_{abs}}{P_{atm}} \cdot f(T)$$

Where $f(T)$ is the empirical temperature correction factor:

$$f(T) = 1 - 0.018 \times (T - 4)$$

For $T = 4$ °C: $f(4) = 1.00$ (reference).

---

## 📘 Derivation 2: Mass of CO₂ Required for a Batch

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Given:**
- Beverage volume $V_{bev} = 10{,}000$ L
- Target carbonation $V_{CO_2} = 3.5$ V/V
- Density of CO₂ at STP: $\rho_{CO_2, STP} = 1.977$ g/L

**Step 1:** Calculate volume of CO₂ needed at STP:

$$V_{CO_2, STP} = V_{CO_2} \times V_{bev} = 3.5 \times 10{,}000 = 35{,}000 \text{ L (at STP)}$$

**Step 2:** Convert STP volume to mass:

$$m_{CO_2} = V_{CO_2, STP} \times \rho_{CO_2, STP}$$

$$m_{CO_2} = 35{,}000 \times 1.977 = 69{,}195 \text{ g} \approx 69.2 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3:** Account for actual CO₂ cylinder pressure (≈ 850 psi or 58.6 bar at 21 °C):

$$V_{CO_2, cylinder} = \dfrac{35{,}000 \text{ L (STP)}}{58.6} \approx 597 \text{ L of liquid CO}_2$$

**Step 4:** Convert liquid CO₂ to cylinder volume (standard cylinder holds ≈ 50 L water equivalent):

$$\text{Number of cylinders} = \dfrac{597}{50} \approx 12 \text{ cylinders}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Always oversize the CO₂ supply by **10–15 %** to handle startup losses, line purges, and pressure drops. For our 10,000 L batch, **13–14 cylinders** would be recommended in practice.

---

## 📘 Derivation 3: Syrup-to-Water Ratio for Final Brix

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Given:**
- Syrup concentrate Brix: $B_{syrup} = 67$ °Bx (typical for cola)
- Final beverage Brix target: $B_{final} = 11$ °Bx (typical for cola)
- Total beverage volume: $V_{total} = 10{,}000$ L

**Step 1:** Apply the dilution principle:

$$B_{final} \times V_{total} = B_{syrup} \times V_{syrup}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for syrup volume:

$$V_{syrup} = \dfrac{B_{final} \times V_{total}}{B_{syrup}} = \dfrac{11 \times 10{,}000}{67}$$

$$V_{syrup} = \dfrac{110{,}000}{67} \approx 1{,}642 \text{ L of concentrate}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate water volume:

$$V_{water} = V_{total} - V_{syrup} = 10{,}000 - 1{,}642 = 8{,}358 \text{ L}$$

**Step 4:** Check the ratio:

$$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{V_{syrup}}{V_{water}} = \dfrac{1{,}642}{8{,}358} \approx 1 : 5.09$$

This is called a **"5 + 1" dilution** — a standard industry benchmark.

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Procedure vs Equipment vs Ingredient

| Procedure Step | Equipment Used | Key Ingredient | Critical Parameter |
|----------------|----------------|----------------|--------------------|
| 1. Raw water reception | Storage tanks, sand filters | Raw water | TDS < 500 ppm |
| 2. Water softening | Ion-exchange columns | Salt (NaCl) for regeneration | Hardness < 50 ppm |
| 3. Water dechlorination | Activated carbon + UV | — | Cl₂ < 0.1 ppm |
| 4. Water chilling | Plate heat exchanger | Refrigerant (R-404A) | 0–4 °C |
| 5. Syrup preparation | Mixing tanks, agitators | Sugar, acid, color, flavor | 60–67 °Bx |
| 6. Syrup filtration | Plate filters | Filter aid (diatomite) | Clarity < 5 NTU |
| 7. Carbonation | Inline carbonator | **CO₂ (food grade, 99.9 %)** | 60 psi @ 4 °C = 3.5 V/V |
| 8. Proportioning | Volumetric proportioner | Treated water + syrup | 5:1 ratio |
| 9. Filler pressurization | Counter-pressure filler | — | Back-pressure ≈ 35 psi |
| 10. Bottle crowning | Crown corker | Crown caps (metal) | Torque 18–22 in-lb |
| 11. Pasteurization (optional) | Tunnel pasteurizer | — | 71 °C / 30 s (HTST) |
| 12. Labeling & packaging | Labeler, shrink wrapper | Labels, shrink film | — |

---

## 📐 Sample Numerical Problem (KTU Board Style)

### Problem:
A beverage plant produces **5,000 L/h of carbonated cola** at **3.8 V/V** and **4 °C**. Calculate:
1. Mass of CO₂ required per hour.
2. Pressure set-point on the carbonator.
3. CO₂ cylinder consumption per shift (8 h).

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Mass of CO₂ per hour:**

$$m_{CO_2} = V_{bev} \times V_{CO_2} \times \rho_{CO_2,STP}$$

$$m_{CO_2} = 5{,}000 \times 3.8 \times 1.977$$

$$m_{CO_2} = 37{,}563 \text{ g/h} \approx 37.6 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Carbonator pressure:**

From the table, at 4 °C for 3.8 V/V:

$$P_{abs} = 3.8 \times 14.7 \times 1.0 = 55.86 \text{ psi (abs)}$$

$$P_{gauge} = 55.86 - 14.7 = 41.16 \text{ psi}$$

**Step 3 — Cylinder consumption per shift:**

$$\text{Total CO}_2 = 37.6 \times 8 = 300.8 \text{ kg per shift}$$

$$\text{Liquid CO}_2 = \dfrac{300{,}800}{1.977} \approx 152{,}150 \text{ L (STP)}$$

$$\text{At 850 psi cylinder: } \dfrac{152{,}150}{58.6} \approx 2{,}596 \text{ L liquid CO}_2$$

$$\text{Cylinders (50 L each): } \dfrac{2{,}596}{50} \approx 52 \text{ cylinders per 8-h shift}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 🧭 Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Carbonated Beverage Manufacture

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define carbonation. List any two carbonation procedures used in the beverage industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Carbonation is the unit operation of dissolving food-grade carbon dioxide (CO₂) gas into a chilled, treated liquid (water + syrup) under controlled pressure (typically 30–80 psi) and low temperature (0–4 °C) to achieve a desired carbonation level of 2.5–4.5 volumes of CO₂ per volume of beverage, as governed by Henry's Law.

**Two Procedures (1 mark):**
1. **Inline Carbonation** — Treated chilled water and syrup are metered separately, mixed in the carbonator, and CO₂ is injected directly. Used in modern continuous systems.
2. **Pre-mix Carbonation** — Syrup is pre-diluted with water, and the entire mixture is carbonated together in a batch or continuous saturator. Used in older plants.

---

### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Name any three essential ingredients used in carbonated beverages and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Ingredient | Function |
|------------|----------|
| **Water** (treated, demineralized) | Bulk medium (85–95 %); must be low in minerals, chlorine-free, microbiologically safe |
| **CO₂** (food grade, 99.9 % purity) | Provides fizz, mouthfeel, slight tang, and acts as antimicrobial preservative |
| **Sugar / HFCS** | Provides sweetness, mouthfeel, and fermentation-resistant caloric load |

*(Optional additional ingredients: Acidulants for tartness, Flavors for sensory identity, Colors for visual appeal, Preservatives for shelf life.)*

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1):** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Describe in detail the various procedures adopted for carbonation of beverages.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The carbonation of beverages involves a series of systematic procedures:

**1. Water Treatment Procedure (1 mark)**
Raw water undergoes sand filtration, activated carbon filtration (to remove chlorine), ion-exchange softening (to reduce hardness below 50 ppm as CaCO₃), and UV sterilization. Treated water is then chilled in a plate heat exchanger to **0–4 °C** — critical for high CO₂ solubility.

**2. Syrup Preparation Procedure (2 marks)**
Sugar (or HFCS 55) is dissolved in treated water at 60–80 °C in a high-shear mixer to form a **concentrate of 60–67 °Bx**. Acidulants, colors, flavors, and preservatives are added sequentially. The syrup is filtered through plate filters (clarity < 5 NTU) and cooled.

**3. Inline Carbonation Procedure (2 marks)**
Treated chilled water and syrup are metered through a **volumetric proportioner** (typically 5:1 water:syrup). This mixture enters the carbonation tower where CO₂ is injected through a **sparger** at 60 psi and 4 °C. Contact time is **3–10 seconds**. The carbonated beverage is verified at **3.5 V/V** using a Zahm & Nagel piercing device.

**4. Filling and Crowning Procedure (1 mark)**
The carbonated beverage is bottled using a **counter-pressure filler** (which maintains back-pressure ≈ 35 psi to prevent CO₂ loss). Crown caps are applied immediately and torqued to **18–22 in-lb**.

**5. Quality Assurance Procedure (1 mark)**
In-process checks include Brix, acidity, pH, CO₂ content, and microbiological testing.

**(b) Explain the construction and working of an inline carbonator with a neat sketch.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
- **Cylindrical Pressure Vessel** — stainless steel SS-304, designed for 150 psi working pressure, ASME-coded.
- **CO₂ Sparger** — a perforated pipe or sintered metal diffuser at the base that releases CO₂ as fine bubbles (1–3 mm diameter).
- **Baffle Plates / Packing** — promotes bubble-liquid contact and prevents channeling.
- **Inlet Ports** — one for water/syrup mixture, one for CO₂.
- **Outlet Port** — at the top, leading to the filler.
- **Pressure Gauge & Safety Relief Valve** — to monitor and protect against overpressure.
- **Level Sensor & Sight Glass** — for operational visibility.

**Working (3.5 marks):**
1. Chilled water (0–4 °C) enters the carbonator at the top.
2. CO₂ from a cylinder bank, regulated to **60 psi**, enters via the sparger at the bottom.
3. CO₂ bubbles rise counter-current to the descending liquid, maximizing contact.
4. Per Henry's Law, CO₂ dissolves into the chilled liquid until equilibrium at the set pressure.
5. The carbonated beverage exits at the top, passing through a **static mixer** for homogenization.
6. A **Zahm & Nagel test** at the outlet confirms the V/V level before forwarding to the filler.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2):** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Discuss the different ingredients used in carbonated beverages and their functions.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Ingredient | Typical % | Function |
|------------|-----------|----------|
| **Treated Water** | 85–95 % | Bulk solvent; must be demineralized, dechlorinated, and microbiologically pure |
| **Sugar / HFCS** | 8–13 % | Sweetness, body, mouthfeel, caloric value |
| **CO₂ (food grade)** | 0.3–0.6 % by mass | Carbonation, effervescence, mild preservation |
| **Acidulants** | 0.1–0.5 % | Tartness, pH control (2.5–3.5), preservative action |
| **Flavors** | 0.05–0.3 % | Sensory identity (cola, lemon, orange) |
| **Colors** | 0.001–0.05 % | Visual identity (caramel for cola, tartrazine for lemon) |
| **Preservatives** | 0.01–0.1 % | Shelf-life extension (sodium benzoate, potassium sorbate) |
| **Emulsifiers** | 0.01–0.05 % | Cloud stability in citrus drinks (gum arabic) |
| **Antioxidants** | Trace | Prevent oxidation (ascorbic acid) |

**Functions in detail:**
1. **Water** — provides the liquid matrix and must be free of off-flavors.
2. **Sweeteners** — sucrose gives clean sweetness; HFCS 55 is cheaper and more soluble.
3. **CO₂** — provides carbonation level (3–4 V/V), slight acidity (carbonic acid), and inhibits microbial growth.
4. **Acidulants** — citric acid (lemon-lime), phosphoric acid (cola), malic acid (apple).
5. **Flavors** — natural essential oils (orange, lemon) or artificial molecules.
6. **Colors** — caramel (cola), β-carotene (orange), anthocyanins (grape).
7. **Preservatives** — sodium benzoate (pH < 3.5), potassium sorbate.

**(b) Describe the construction and working of (i) a CO₂ pressure regulator and (ii) a counter-pressure bottle filler.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) CO₂ Pressure Regulator (3.5 marks):**

**Construction:**
- **Body** — brass or stainless steel, with two pressure chambers separated by a **diaphragm**.
- **Adjustment Spring** — sets the desired outlet pressure.
- **Inlet Valve** — connects to the CO₂ cylinder (high-pressure side, ≈ 850 psi).
- **Outlet Port** — delivers CO₂ at reduced pressure (30–80 psi) to the carbonator.
- **Pressure Gauges** — two gauges (inlet and outlet).

**Working:**
1. High-pressure CO₂ enters the regulator.
2. The adjustment spring presses against the diaphragm, controlling the opening of the inlet valve.
3. As outlet pressure drops (due to consumption), the diaphragm opens the valve wider, allowing more CO₂ through.
4. Once set outlet pressure is reached, the diaphragm balances and the valve partially closes.
5. The result is a **constant outlet pressure** regardless of cylinder pressure variations.

**(ii) Counter-Pressure Bottle Filler (3.5 marks):**

**Construction:**
- **Rotary Bowl** — stainless steel rotating carousel with multiple filling heads.
- **Filling Nozzles** — each with a gas tube and a liquid tube.
- **Pressure Equalization Chamber** — purges air from the bottle with CO₂ before filling.
- **Sealing Rings** — create an airtight seal against the bottle mouth.

**Working:**
1. Empty bottle is conveyed and lifted to the filling nozzle.
2. The nozzle seals the bottle mouth against the sealing ring.
3. **Step 1 — Pre-evacuation:** Air is evacuated and replaced with CO₂ (≈ 35 psi).
4. **Step 2 — Pressure equalization:** Bottle interior pressure equals tank pressure.
5. **Step 3 — Filling:** Liquid valve opens; beverage flows in under gravity + pressure.
6. **Step 4 — Snift:** A small vent releases headspace pressure to release any foam.
7. The bottle is released, conveyed to the crowner, and sealed.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Brix with specific gravity** — Brix is mass % of soluble solids, NOT density.
> 2. **Forgetting that CO₂ cylinders hold liquid, not gas** — calculations must convert liquid → gas volume.
> 3. **Using gauge pressure instead of absolute pressure** in Henry's Law — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Missing the temperature correction factor** $f(T)$ — students often quote "60 psi = 3.5 V/V" without mentioning this is valid only at 4 °C.
> 5. **Skipping the counter-pressure principle** when describing fillers — examiners expect explicit mention of pre-evacuation and pressure equalization.
> 6. **Omitting ingredient functions** — merely listing ingredients without stating their *role* in the beverage will cost 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Wrong units** — mixing psi, bar, kPa in the same numerical problem is a common error.
> 8. **Not labeling diagrams** — every diagram must have titled blocks/arrows with operating conditions (e.g., "60 psi, 4 °C").

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Carbonation** = controlled dissolution of food-grade **CO₂** in chilled, treated liquid under **pressure**, governed by **Henry's Law**.
- [x] **Volumes of CO₂** = industry-standard carbonation metric (soft drinks: 2.5–4.5 V/V).
- [x] **3.5 V/V at 4 °C ≈ 60 psi (4.14 bar)** — the benchmark setting for cola-style drinks.
- [x] **Three pillars**: Procedures (steps) + Equipment (machines) + Ingredients (inputs).
- [x] **Inline carbonation** is modern and preferred; **pre-mix** is older.
- [x] **Counter-pressure fillers** prevent CO₂ loss during bottling.
- [x] **Treated water** must be < 50 ppm hardness, < 0.1 ppm chlorine, chilled to 0–4 °C.
- [x] **Syrup concentrate** is 60–67 °Bx, diluted ≈ 5:1 with water for ≈ 11 °Bx final.
- [x] **CO₂ cylinders** store liquid CO₂; convert using **1 V/V = 1.977 g/L** at STP.
- [x] **Acidulants** (citric, phosphoric, malic) drop pH to 2.5–3.5, enhancing preservation.
- [x] **Preservatives** (sodium benzoate, potassium sorbate) are effective only at pH < 3.5.
- [x] **Caramel color (E150d)** gives cola its signature dark brown hue.
- [x] **Quality control** uses **Zahm & Nagel piercing device** to verify V/V.
- [x] **Mandatory equipment trio**: carbonator + filler + crowner.
- [x] **Henry's Law constant** $k_H$ for CO₂ **increases as temperature decreases** — colder = more CO₂.
- [x] **Always use absolute pressure** $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + 14.7$ psi in carbonation calculations.
- [x] **Crown torque** specification: 18–22 in-lb (too loose → leaks; too tight → cap damage).
- [x] **Pasteurization** (if applied): **71 °C for 30 s** — preserves carbonation by killing yeast/mold.
- [x] **Mass balance principle**: $Brix_{syrup} \times V_{syrup} = Brix_{final} \times V_{total}$.
- [x] **CO₂ purity**: food grade ≥ 99.9 %; impurities (H₂S, SO₂) cause off-flavors.
- [x] **Diagrams must be labeled** with operating pressure and temperature for full marks.

---

> **🎯 Final KTU 2024 Tip:**
> When answering questions on this module, always **structure your answer in three sections** — (1) What is done, (2) With what equipment, (3) Using what ingredients. This **3-pillar framework** is exactly how KTU examiners mark these questions. Combine it with **Henry's Law reference**, a **labeled diagram**, and **at least one numerical example** for guaranteed high marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Syrups - Filling Systems - Packaging - Containers and Closures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Syrups - Filling Systems - Packaging - Containers and Closures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Syrups — Technical Definition

A **syrup** in beverage processing is a concentrated, viscous, sweetened aqueous solution containing dissolved sucrose (or other saccharides) along with functional ingredients such as acids, flavors, colorants, preservatives, and nutraceuticals. Syrups serve as the **primary flavor-carrier matrix** in carbonated soft drinks (CSDs), ready-to-serve (RTS) beverages, and as a **base stock** for dilution in post-mix dispensing systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A syrup is defined by its **Brix value** (°Bx), which represents the percentage by mass of soluble solids (predominantly sugar) in the solution, measured via refractometer at **20 °C**. Standard beverage syrups are formulated to a **65 ± 1 °Bx** for end-product use, or **40–50 °Bx** for use in post-mix carbonated systems.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a syrup as the **"concentrated soul" of a beverage**:
- Just as a stock cube holds the entire essence of a soup in a tiny cube, a syrup holds the entire flavor, sweetness, and color of a soft drink in a thick, sugar-rich liquid.
- At the bottling plant, this syrup is either filled directly (in CSDs) or diluted with chilled, carbonated water (in fountain/post-mix systems) to make the final drink.
- The sugar is not just for sweetness — it acts as a **preservative** (by osmotic pressure), a **mouthfeel enhancer**, and a **flavor carrier**.

### 1.3 Filling Systems — Technical Definition

A **filling system** (or filler) is the engineered assembly of mechanical, pneumatic, and electro-mechanical components responsible for dispensing a precise, metered volume of beverage into a container at high throughput while maintaining hygienic conditions, minimizing oxygen pickup, and preserving product integrity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The selection of a filling system is dictated by the **product's physical properties** (viscosity, carbonation level, particulates, temperature) and the **container type** (glass, PET, can, pouch). A mismatch results in foam, splashing, underfills, or container damage.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy for Filling

A filling system is like a **precision water tap with a brain**: imagine a tap that opens for exactly 0.45 seconds to deliver exactly 250 mL — every single time, thousands of times an hour, without spilling a drop and without letting the drink go flat. That's what a beverage filler does.

### 1.5 Packaging — Technical Definition

**Packaging** in beverage processing refers to the **science, engineering, and art** of enclosing the product in a protective, functional, and marketable container system that preserves quality, extends shelf life, communicates brand identity, and ensures safe distribution from producer to consumer.

### 1.6 Containers and Closures — Technical Definition

A **container** is the primary rigid or semi-rigid vessel that holds the beverage (bottle, can, carton, pouch). A **closure** is the secondary component that seals the container to prevent leakage, microbial ingress, and gas exchange (CO₂ loss / O₂ ingress).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** The container is the **"house"** of the beverage, and the closure is the **"front door with a lock"**. The door must seal perfectly to keep intruders (microbes, oxygen) out, and to keep the prized resident (CO₂, flavor) safely inside.

### 1.7 Why This Module Matters in the Beverage Industry

- Syrup preparation is the **heart of flavor consistency** — every bottle of a global brand must taste identical regardless of which country it is bottled in.
- Filling systems are typically the **bottleneck (rate-limiting step)** of the entire packaging line, running at **200 to 80,000 containers per hour (CPH)**.
- Packaging represents **30–40 %** of the total product cost and is the single largest factor in shelf-life determination.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Syrup Preparation — Operational Logic

The preparation of syrups follows a strict, scientifically controlled sequence. Each step is engineered to maximize **flavor extraction, microbial stability, and process efficiency**.

#### Step-by-Step Logic of Syrup Preparation:

1. **Water Quality Pre-treatment**
   - Use deionized, demineralized, or treated water with **TDS < 50 ppm**, free of chlorine (off-flavor), and microbiologically pure (**TVC < 10 CFU/100 mL**).

2. **Sugar Dissolution (The "Cooking" Step)**
   - Sugar is dissolved in hot water (**70–80 °C**) under continuous agitation.
   - Heat accelerates dissolution, reduces viscosity, and provides **partial pasteurization** (destroys vegetative microorganisms).

3. **Addition of Acidulants**
   - Citric acid (or malic/phosphoric acid) is added **after** the sugar has dissolved and the solution has cooled to **≤ 50 °C**.
   - Reason: Inverting sucrose at low pH and high temperature produces HMF (hydroxymethylfurfural), which causes off-flavors and browning.

4. **Addition of Heat-Labile Components**
   - Flavors, colors, and vitamins are added at **≤ 30 °C** to prevent volatilization and thermal degradation.

5. **Filtration and Polishing**
   - The syrup is passed through a **plate-and-frame filter** or **bag filter** (typically **5–10 µm** rating) to remove undissolved particles.

6. **Cooling to Filling Temperature**
   - Syrup is cooled to **20–25 °C** for filling to prevent thermal expansion of containers and CO₂ loss in CSDs.

7. **Storage in Service Tanks**
   - Cooled syrup is held in **stainless steel (SS 304) service tanks** under sanitary conditions until the filler calls for it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Point:** The order of ingredient addition is **not arbitrary**. The pH–temperature–time (pHT) relationship governs the chemistry. Add acid to hot sugar and you create an inverted syrup that browns the product and alters sweetness perception.

### 2.2 Types of Syrups (Industry Classification)

| Type | Brix (°Bx) | Application | Key Property |
|------|------------|-------------|--------------|
| Simple syrup (sugar syrup) | 50–67 | Base stock | Only sugar + water |
| Flavored syrup | 65 | RTS beverages, cordials | Contains natural/artificial flavor |
| Glucose syrup | 70–80 | Brewing, confectionery | Uses glucose/dextrose |
| High-fructose corn syrup (HFCS-55 / HFCS-42) | 71 | CSDs (USA) | Liquid, no dissolution needed |
| Invert syrup | 65 | Premium beverages | Sucrose split into glucose + fructose |
| Functional syrup | 50–65 | Energy drinks, fortified drinks | Contains vitamins, taurine, caffeine |

### 2.3 Filling Systems — Detailed Engineering Classification

Filling systems are classified by the **mechanism used to meter the product** into the container.

#### 2.3.1 Gravity Fillers
- **Principle:** Product flows into the container by gravity.
- **Use:** Still water, non-carbonated, low-viscosity liquids.
- **Speed:** 30–60 containers/min (small-scale).
- **Pros:** Simple, cheap, no foaming.
- **Cons:** Not suitable for carbonated or viscous products.

#### 2.3.2 Pressure (Counter-Pressure) Fillers
- **Principle:** The container is pressurized with CO₂ to match the line pressure of the carbonated product, then the product flows in by **isobaric** pressure difference (≈ 0).
- **Use:** **Carbonated soft drinks, beer, sparkling water** (the industry standard for CSDs).
- **Speed:** 800–80,000 CPH.
- **Pros:** No foam, no CO₂ loss, no oxygen pickup.
- **Cons:** Complex, requires CO₂ supply, expensive.

#### 2.3.3 Vacuum Fillers
- **Principle:** A vacuum is drawn in the container; atmospheric pressure pushes the product in.
- **Use:** Still beverages, juices, oils.
- **Pros:** Accurate fill, no dripping, hygienic.
- **Cons:** Not for carbonated products.

#### 2.3.4 Volumetric Fillers (Piston / Metering)
- **Principle:** A piston of fixed volume meters the product and discharges it into the container.
- **Use:** Viscous products, concentrates, fruit pulps.
- **Pros:** Highly accurate (± 0.5 %), handles particulates.
- **Cons:** Mechanical wear, lower speed.

#### 2.3.5 Net-Weight Fillers
- **Principle:** A load cell measures the mass of product dispensed; cutoff when target mass is reached.
- **Use:** Premium juices, hot-fill products.
- **Pros:** Highest accuracy independent of viscosity.
- **Cons:** Slow, expensive.

#### 2.3.6 Aseptic Fillers
- **Principle:** Container and product are both sterilized; filling occurs in a **sterile enclosure** with HEPA-filtered air.
- **Use:** Milk, dairy beverages, low-acid RTD teas.
- **Pros:** Shelf-stable without preservatives.
- **Cons:** Very expensive, requires sterile packaging.

### 2.4 Packaging Materials — Comparative Analysis

| Material | CO₂ Barrier | O₂ Barrier | Weight | Recyclability | Cost | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------|------------|--------|---------------|------|-------------|
| Glass | Excellent | Excellent | Heavy | 100 % infinite | High | Premium beer, soft drinks |
| PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate) | Moderate | Moderate | Light | 100 % | Low | CSD, water, juice |
| Aluminum can | Perfect (laminated liner) | Perfect | Very light | 100 % infinite | Medium | Beer, CSD, energy drinks |
| Tetra Pak (paperboard + PE + Al foil) | Perfect | Perfect | Light | Limited | High | Juice, milk, RTD tea |
| HDPE | Poor | Poor | Light | 100 % | Low | Milk, water (economy) |
| Aseptic pouch | Good | Good | Very light | Limited | Low | Wine, water, juice |

### 2.5 Closures — Engineering Taxonomy

| Closure Type | Application | Seal Mechanism |
|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| Crown cap (pry-off, twist-off) | Glass bottles (CSD, beer) | Crimped metal skirt on crown |
| Roll-on pilfer-proof (ROPP) | Spirits, wine, premium beverages | Aluminum cap rolled onto thread |
| Plastic screw cap (PE/PP) | PET bottles (water, juice) | Threaded engagement with torque control |
| Sport cap / push-pull | Sports drinks, water | Spring-loaded valve |
| Lug cap (twist-off) | Jars, hot-fill bottles | Tamper-evident break |
| Crown cork with PVC liner | Carbonated beverages | Crown pressed on bottle mouth |
| Aluminum easy-open end (EOE) | Cans | Riveted aluminum tab |
| Heat-sealed foil | Aseptic cartons | Induction or conduction sealing |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Symbols | Units | Application |
|---------|---------|---------|-------|-------------|
| Brix correction (temperature) | $B_{20} = B_T + 0.0001 \cdot (T - 20) \cdot (74.5 + B_T)$ | $B_{20}$: Brix at 20 °C; $B_T$: observed Brix; $T$: temperature | °C, °Bx | Syrup QC lab |
| Dilution formula (Pearson's square) | $V_c = \dfrac{B_s - B_f}{B_s - B_c} \cdot V_f$ | $V_c$: vol of concentrate; $B_s$: Brix syrup; $B_f$: Brix final; $B_c$: Brix concentrate; $V_f$: vol of final drink | mL, °Bx | Diluting concentrate to RTS |
| Sugar mass required | $m_s = V_f \cdot \rho \cdot \dfrac{B_f}{100}$ | $m_s$: sugar mass; $V_f$: final volume; $\rho$: density of syrup | g, mL, g/mL, °Bx | Batch preparation |
| Fill volume (thermal) | $V_{25} = V_T \cdot [1 + \beta \cdot (25 - T)]$ | $V_{25}$: vol at 25 °C; $V_T$: hot fill vol; $\beta$: cubic expansion coefficient | mL, °C | Hot-fill validation |
| CO₂ pressure in CSD | $P = \dfrac{k_H \cdot m_{CO_2}}{V - V_{liquid}}$ | $k_H$: Henry's constant; $m_{CO_2}$: CO₂ mass; $V$: headspace + liquid | bar, g, mL | Carbonation design |
| Pasteurization units | $PU = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{(T(t)-T_{ref})/z} \, dt$ | $T_{ref}=60\,°C$; $z=10\,°C$ | minutes | Microbial inactivation |
| Torque for screw cap | $T_q = k \cdot D \cdot F$ | $k$: friction coeff; $D$: cap diameter; $F$: axial force | N·m | Closure integrity |
| Line efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{actual}}{N_{rated}} \cdot 100\%$ | $N_{actual}$: filled; $N_{rated}$: rated capacity | CPH, % | Bottling KPI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pearson's Square** is the single most-tested dilution formula in KTU exams. Memorize it. It is the engineer's "alligation method" for blending two streams of different concentrations to obtain a target.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Brix-temperature correction** is performed every hour in every beverage QC lab globally — the formula above is the **ICUMSA** (International Commission for Uniform Methods of Sugar Analysis) standard.
- **Pearson's Square** is used by every fountain-syrup manufacturer (Coca-Cola, Pepsi) to instruct bottlers on dilution ratios in restaurants.
- **CO₂ Henry's Law** governs the design of carbonators: a 2.5 vol CO₂ beverage needs **~1.21 bar at 4 °C** to stay in solution.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Syrup Dilution (Pearson's Square) Formula

**Problem Statement:** A bottler has a concentrate syrup of **65 °Bx** and wishes to dilute it with water (**0 °Bx**) to produce **10,000 L** of a **12 °Bx** ready-to-serve beverage. Calculate the volume of concentrate and water required.

**Given:**
- $B_s = 65\,°Bx$ (concentrate Brix)
- $B_c = 0\,°Bx$ (water Brix)
- $B_f = 12\,°Bx$ (final Brix)
- $V_f = 10{,}000$ L (final total volume)

**Step 1 — Set up the mass balance on soluble solids.**

$$
\text{Soluble solids in} = \text{Soluble solids out}
$$

$$
V_c \cdot B_s + V_w \cdot B_c = V_f \cdot B_f
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$$
V_c \cdot 65 + V_w \cdot 0 = 10{,}000 \cdot 12
$$

$$
65 \cdot V_c = 120{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_c$ (concentrate volume).**

$$
V_c = \dfrac{120{,}000}{65} = 1846.15 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_w$ (water volume) using the volume balance.**

$$
V_w = V_f - V_c
$$

$$
V_w = 10{,}000 - 1846.15 = 8153.85 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification (re-compute final Brix).**

$$
B_f = \dfrac{V_c \cdot B_s + V_w \cdot B_c}{V_f} = \dfrac{1846.15 \cdot 65 + 8153.85 \cdot 0}{10{,}000} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{10{,}000} = 12\,°Bx \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Generalization (Pearson's Square).**

For any two-stream dilution, the general formula is:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{B_f - B_w}{B_s - B_w} \cdot V_f
$$

**Substitution in this problem:**

$$
V_c = \dfrac{12 - 0}{65 - 0} \cdot 10{,}000 = \dfrac{12}{65} \cdot 10{,}000 = 1846.15 \text{ L}
$$

$$
V_w = \dfrac{B_s - B_f}{B_s - B_w} \cdot V_f = \dfrac{65 - 12}{65 - 0} \cdot 10{,}000 = \dfrac{53}{65} \cdot 10{,}000 = 8153.85 \text{ L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Generalization:** The two numerator terms $(B_f - B_w)$ and $(B_s - B_f)$ must **sum to $(B_s - B_w)$** — this is the geometric property of the Pearson's Square that allows rapid mental computation.

### 3.2 Derivation of Brix Temperature Correction (ICUMSA)

**Problem Statement:** A syrup sample reads **64.8 °Bx** on a refractometer at **28 °C**. What is the corrected Brix at the standard 20 °C?

**Given:**
- $B_T = 64.8\,°Bx$ (observed at 28 °C)
- $T = 28\,°C$
- Standard reference: $T_{ref} = 20\,°C$

**Step 1 — Identify the correction direction.**

Since the sample is **warmer** than 20 °C, the refractive index is lower than at 20 °C, so the Brix reading is **lower than the true value** at 20 °C. We need to **add** a positive correction.

**Step 2 — Apply the ICUMSA correction formula.**

$$
B_{20} = B_T + 0.0001 \cdot (T - 20) \cdot (74.5 + B_T)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature term.**

$$
T - 20 = 28 - 20 = 8\,°C
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Brix-dependent term.**

$$
74.5 + B_T = 74.5 + 64.8 = 139.3
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the correction factor.**

$$
0.0001 \cdot 8 \cdot 139.3 = 0.1114\,°Bx
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the correction.**

$$
B_{20} = 64.8 + 0.1114 = 64.91\,°Bx
$$

**Step 7 — Validation.**

The corrected Brix is slightly **higher** than the observed Brix, which is consistent with the physical fact that a warmer solution has a lower refractive index (and thus lower Brix reading). The correction has restored the value to the standard reference temperature. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation of Hot-Fill Volume Correction

**Problem Statement:** A glass bottle is filled with juice at **85 °C**. The target fill volume at **25 °C** is **250 mL**. The cubic expansion coefficient of juice is $\beta = 4 \times 10^{-4}\,°C^{-1}$. Calculate the required hot-fill volume.

**Given:**
- $V_{25} = 250$ mL
- $T_{fill} = 85\,°C$
- $T_{ref} = 25\,°C$
- $\beta = 4 \times 10^{-4}\,°C^{-1}$

**Step 1 — Recall the volumetric expansion equation.**

$$
V_{T} = V_{25} \cdot [1 + \beta \cdot (T - 25)]
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature difference.**

$$
\Delta T = 85 - 25 = 60\,°C
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the expansion factor.**

$$
\beta \cdot \Delta T = 4 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 60 = 0.024
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the hot-fill volume.**

$$
V_{85} = 250 \cdot [1 + 0.024] = 250 \cdot 1.024 = 256 \text{ mL}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

If the filler dispenses **256 mL at 85 °C**, the product will contract to exactly **250 mL at 25 °C** as it cools — meeting the label declaration. Underfilling leads to legal issues (consumer protection laws); overfilling wastes product and increases cost.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Filler Selection

| Product | Viscosity (cP) | CO₂ Content | Filler Type | Speed (CPH) | Cost (₹ Lakhs) |
|---------|----------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| Still water | 1 | None | Gravity / vacuum | 36,000 | 25–50 |
| CSD | 1–2 | 2.5–4.2 vol | Counter-pressure | 60,000 | 200–800 |
| Beer | 2 | 2.5–3.0 vol | Counter-pressure | 50,000 | 300–900 |
| Fruit juice | 5–50 | None | Gravity / volumetric | 12,000 | 50–150 |
| Energy drink | 1–3 | 1.5–2.5 vol | Counter-pressure | 40,000 | 250–600 |
| Tomato sauce | 1000–5000 | None | Piston volumetric | 6,000 | 80–200 |
| Hot-fill juice | 5–50 | None | Hot-fill gravity (85 °C) | 24,000 | 100–250 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The **counter-pressure filler** is the workhorse of the global beverage industry — over **70 %** of all carbonated drinks in the world are filled using this principle.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a syrup used in beverage processing. List the major ingredients in a typical carbonated soft drink syrup.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A syrup in beverage processing is a **concentrated, viscous, sweetened aqueous solution** containing dissolved sugars and other functional ingredients, characterized by its Brix value (°Bx). It serves as the **flavor-carrier base** for carbonated soft drinks (CSDs) and ready-to-serve (RTS) beverages.

**Major ingredients of a typical CSD syrup:**
1. **High-purity sucrose** (or HFCS) — 50–65 % w/w (sweetener, body, preservative).
2. **Water** — demineralized, chlorine-free, low-TDS.
3. **Acidulant** — citric acid (pH 2.5–3.5) for tartness, microbial stability, and CO₂ solubility enhancement.
4. **Flavor** — natural or nature-identical (e.g., cola, lemon-lime).
5. **Color** — caramel, anthocyanin, or approved synthetic (e.g., Sunset Yellow FCF).
6. **Preservative** — sodium benzoate (≤ 150 ppm) for shelf-life extension.

*(Full 3 marks for definition + categorized list.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between a counter-pressure filler and a gravity filler. State one product that uses each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Gravity Filler | Counter-Pressure Filler |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|
| Operating principle | Product flows by gravity | Product flows under isobaric CO₂ pressure |
| Product use | Still water, non-carbonated juice | Carbonated soft drinks, beer |
| Foam generation | Possible at high flow | No foam (CO₂ pre-fills headspace) |
| CO₂ loss | Not applicable | Negligible |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Speed | Lower | Very high (up to 80,000 CPH) |

**Examples:**
- Gravity filler — **Still mineral water** (Bisleri,Kinley).
- Counter-pressure filler — **Coca-Cola, Pepsi, Kingfisher beer**.

*(Full 3 marks for tabular comparison + one example each.)*

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step industrial preparation of a carbonated soft drink syrup**, with emphasis on ingredient addition order, temperature control, and filtration. Justify the engineering rationale behind each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bottler receives a concentrate syrup of **67 °Bx** and wishes to prepare **15,000 L** of a **12 °Bx** finished beverage using water (0 °Bx) and the concentrate. Calculate the volumes of concentrate and water required using **Pearson's Square method**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The industrial preparation of CSD syrup follows a sequence engineered to maximize **flavor stability, microbial safety, and process efficiency**.

**Step 1 — Water Treatment (1 mark):**
Raw water is passed through activated carbon (removes chlorine and organics), softening (removes hardness), UV sterilization (destroys microbes), and deaeration (reduces dissolved O₂ to prevent oxidation of flavors).

**Step 2 — Sugar Dissolution (2 marks):**
Granular sucrose is added to **pre-heated water at 70–80 °C** under high-shear agitation. The high temperature accelerates dissolution (reduces processing time from hours to ~20 minutes) and provides **partial pasteurization** by inactivating vegetative microorganisms. The solution is held at this temperature until complete dissolution (visual clarity).

**Step 3 — Acid Addition (1 mark):**
The syrup is cooled to **≤ 50 °C**, and **citric acid** is added slowly with continuous mixing. The temperature restriction is critical: at high temperature and low pH, sucrose undergoes **acid hydrolysis (inversion)** into glucose and fructose, generating HMF that causes off-flavors and darkening.

**Step 4 — Addition of Heat-Labile Ingredients (1 mark):**
When the syrup has cooled to **≤ 30 °C**, flavors, colors, and preservatives (sodium benzoate) are added. These compounds are volatile or thermally labile and would degrade if added earlier.

**Step 5 — Filtration (1 mark):**
The syrup is passed through a **plate-and-frame filter (5–10 µm)** to remove undissolved sugar crystals, foreign matter, and any precipitated material. This step ensures **visual clarity** and protects downstream filler valves from clogging.

**Step 6 — Cooling and Storage (1 mark):**
The finished syrup is cooled to **20–25 °C** and stored in **SS 304 service tanks** with sanitary sealing. Filling at this temperature prevents thermal expansion of containers, CO₂ loss in CSDs, and supports accurate fill volume.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $B_s = 67\,°Bx$ (concentrate Brix)
- $B_w = 0\,°Bx$ (water Brix)
- $B_f = 12\,°Bx$ (final Brix)
- $V_f = 15{,}000$ L

**Pearson's Square Setup (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \dfrac{B_f - B_w}{B_s - B_w} \cdot V_f \\
V_c &= \dfrac{12 - 0}{67 - 0} \cdot 15{,}000 \\
V_c &= \dfrac{12}{67} \cdot 15{,}000 \\
V_c &= 2686.57 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Water Volume (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_w &= V_f - V_c \\
V_w &= 15{,}000 - 2686.57 \\
V_w &= 12{,}313.43 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{check} &= \dfrac{V_c \cdot B_s}{V_f} = \dfrac{2686.57 \cdot 67}{15{,}000} \\
B_{check} &= \dfrac{180{,}000}{15{,}000} = 12.00\,°Bx \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):**
- Volume of concentrate required: **2686.57 L**
- Volume of water required: **12,313.43 L**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **operation of a counter-pressure (isobaric) filling system** used for carbonated beverages. Explain why it is preferred over a simple gravity filler for CSDs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A glass bottle is hot-filled with mango juice at **88 °C**. The target fill volume at **25 °C** is **200 mL**. The cubic expansion coefficient of juice is $\beta = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}\,°C^{-1}$. **(7 marks)**

- (i) Calculate the required hot-fill volume at 88 °C.
- (ii) If the filler dispenses 207 mL at 25 °C by mistake, what is the percentage overfill?

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A **counter-pressure filler** (also called an isobaric filler) is the industry standard for carbonated soft drinks, beer, and sparkling water.

**Block Diagram Description (4 marks):**

The system consists of the following sequence:
1. **Container entry** — Empty bottle enters the filler carousel and is lifted onto a **filling valve** by a centripetal or linear motion.
2. **Sealing** — The bottle mouth is sealed against the filling valve gasket, creating a **pressure-tight chamber**.
3. **Pre-evacuation** — A vacuum briefly evacuates air from the bottle (optional, to reduce O₂ pickup).
4. **CO₂ pressurization** — The bottle headspace is pressurized with CO₂ to a pressure **equal to the product tank pressure** (typically 2.0–3.5 bar for a 2.5–3.5 vol CSD).
5. **Isobaric filling** — The product valve opens, and the beverage flows into the bottle by **gravity**, with zero net pressure differential (true isobaric condition). No bubbling, no foam.
6. **Snift** — The fill valve closes; the bottle is vented back to atmospheric pressure via a "snift" cycle that returns residual CO₂ to the product tank.
7. **Discharge** — The bottle is lowered and exits to the capper.

**Why Preferred over Gravity Filler (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Counter-Pressure | Gravity |
|--------|------------------|---------|
| Foam | No foam (CO₂ is pre-dissolved) | Foaming for any carbonated product |
| CO₂ retention | 100 % (no desorption) | Major loss |
| Fill accuracy | ± 0.5 % | ± 2 % |
| O₂ pickup | < 50 ppb | 500–2000 ppb |
| Speed | Up to 80,000 CPH | 36,000 CPH max |
| Cost | High | Low |

The counter-pressure filler preserves **carbonation, flavor, and shelf life**, and runs at high speeds without foam-related downtime — making it the only economically viable option for high-volume CSD production.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $V_{25} = 200$ mL (target at 25 °C)
- $T_{fill} = 88\,°C$
- $\beta = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}\,°C^{-1}$

**(i) Hot-fill volume calculation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{88} &= V_{25} \cdot [1 + \beta \cdot (T_{fill} - 25)] \\
V_{88} &= 200 \cdot [1 + 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot (88 - 25)] \\
V_{88} &= 200 \cdot [1 + 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 63] \\
V_{88} &= 200 \cdot [1 + 0.02205] \\
V_{88} &= 200 \cdot 1.02205 \\
V_{88} &= 204.41 \text{ mL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The filler must dispense 204.41 mL at 88 °C** to deliver exactly 200 mL at 25 °C after cooling.

**(ii) Percentage overfill calculation (3 marks):**

If the filler dispenses **207 mL at 25 °C** (assuming this is the final cooled volume), the overfill is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overfill} &= 207 - 200 = 7 \text{ mL} \\
\text{Overfill \%} &= \dfrac{7}{200} \cdot 100\% = 3.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 3.5 % overfill exceeds the typical industry tolerance of **± 1.0 %** and would result in:
- Direct product giveaway cost (₹ lakhs/year).
- Possible consumer-protection violation if detected in regulatory audit.
- Indicates filler calibration error requiring immediate corrective action (CIP + recalibration).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Order of ingredient addition in syrup** — Many students add acid first or flavors first. This is the #1 reason for losing 2–3 marks in syrup questions. **Memorize the rule: Sugar first (hot), acid second (warm), flavor last (cool).**
>
> 2. **Pearson's Square sign error** — Students often write $(B_s - B_f)$ as the numerator for $V_c$ instead of $(B_f - B_w)$. The numerator is always **"final minus diluent"**, NOT "stock minus final."
>
> 3. **Hot-fill volume direction** — Students confuse whether to add or subtract $\beta \Delta T$. **Rule of thumb: hot fill is ALWAYS more than cold fill.** Always add the correction when going from cold → hot.
>
> 4. **Counter-pressure filler confusion with vacuum filler** — Vacuum fillers are for STILL beverages, counter-pressure is for CARBONATED. Examiners deduct full marks if confused.
>
> 5. **Units in Brix correction** — Writing °Bx without °C for temperature terms loses the unit mark.
>
> 6. **Closure terminology** — Writing "cap" generically when "crown cap," "ROPP," or "lug cap" is expected. Be specific; engineers do not generalize.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4):**

**Syrups:**
- ✅ Syrup = sugar + water + acid + flavor + color + preservative.
- ✅ Brix (°Bx) = % soluble solids, measured at 20 °C, ICUMSA correction applied.
- ✅ Standard CSD syrup: **65 ± 1 °Bx**.
- ✅ Sugar dissolves at 70–80 °C; acid added ≤ 50 °C; flavor ≤ 30 °C.
- ✅ Acid inversion at high T + low pH → HMF → off-flavor.
- ✅ Filtration at 5–10 µm via plate-and-frame filter.
- ✅ Storage in **SS 304 service tanks** at 20–25 °C.

**Filling Systems:**
- ✅ Gravity filler → still water, no foam risk.
- ✅ Counter-pressure (isobaric) filler → CSD, beer, sparkling water.
- ✅ Vacuum filler → still juice, oils.
- ✅ Volumetric (piston) filler → viscous pulps, concentrates.
- ✅ Net-weight (mass) filler → highest accuracy, slowest.
- ✅ Aseptic filler → milk, dairy, low-acid RTD teas.
- ✅ Counter-pressure is the **industry standard** for CSD.

**Packaging:**
- ✅ PET dominates water, CSD, juice.
- ✅ Glass dominates beer, premium CSD.
- ✅ Aluminum can dominates beer, energy drinks.
- ✅ Tetra Pak dominates milk, juice (aseptic).
- ✅ CO₂ barrier ranking: Al can ≈ Tetra Pak > Glass > PET > HDPE.
- ✅ Cost: Glass > Tetra Pak > Al can > PET > HDPE.

**Containers & Closures:**
- ✅ Crown cap → glass CSD, beer.
- ✅ ROPP → spirits, wine.
- ✅ Plastic screw cap → PET water, juice.
- ✅ Sport cap → sports drinks.
- ✅ Aluminum EOE → cans.
- ✅ Heat-sealed foil → aseptic cartons.
- ✅ Torque-controlled screw caps ensure seal integrity.
- ✅ CO₂ loss in CSD is governed by **Henry's Law**: $P = k_H \cdot c$.

**Key Formulas to Memorize:**
- ✅ Pearson's Square: $V_c = \dfrac{B_f - B_w}{B_s - B_w} \cdot V_f$.
- ✅ Brix temperature correction: $B_{20} = B_T + 0.0001 \cdot (T - 20) \cdot (74.5 + B_T)$.
- ✅ Hot-fill volume: $V_{T} = V_{25} \cdot [1 + \beta \cdot (T - 25)]$.
- ✅ Pasteurization units: $PU = \int 10^{(T - 60)/10} \, dt$.
- ✅ Line efficiency: $\eta = (N_{actual} / N_{rated}) \cdot 100\%$.

**Industry Numbers Worth Remembering:**
- ✅ Counter-pressure filler speed: 800 – 80,000 CPH.
- ✅ Typical CSD carbonation: 2.5–4.2 vol CO₂.
- ✅ Standard hot-fill temperature: 85–95 °C.
- ✅ Glass bottle sizes: 200 mL, 250 mL, 330 mL, 500 mL, 750 mL, 1 L, 1.25 L.
- ✅ PET bottle sizes: 200 mL – 2.5 L.
- ✅ Aluminum can sizes: 200 mL (slim), 250 mL (sleek), 330 mL (standard), 500 mL (tall).

**Process Engineering Principles:**
- ✅ The filler is the **bottleneck** of a packaging line.
- ✅ The order of ingredient addition is governed by **pH–temperature–time (pHT)** chemistry.
- ✅ The choice of filler is governed by **product viscosity, CO₂ content, and particulate load**.
- ✅ The choice of container is governed by **CO₂/O₂ barrier, cost, weight, and recyclability**.
- ✅ The choice of closure is governed by **tamper evidence, seal integrity, and consumer convenience**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coffee Bean Preparation - Processing - Brewing - Decaffeination - Instant Coffee - Tea - Types - Black

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ☕ Module 4: Coffee & Tea Processing — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Coffee** is a brewed beverage prepared from the roasted seeds (commonly referred to as **beans**) of the *Coffea* plant family, predominantly *Coffea arabica* and *Coffea canephora* (Robusta). Beverage processing encompasses the entire chain of **post-harvest unit operations** that transform the agricultural commodity — fresh red coffee cherries — into a stable, consumable extract or soluble powder.

**Tea** is an aromatic beverage prepared by infusing the cured leaves of the *Camellia sinensis* plant in hot or boiling water. The biochemical transformations occurring during **withering, rolling, fermentation (oxidation), and drying/firing** dictate the final category — Black, Green, Oolong, White, or Yellow tea.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The module explicitly emphasizes the **unit operations** in coffee cherry processing (wet vs. dry method), **roasting chemistry** (Maillard reaction, Strecker degradation), **brewing kinetics** (extraction yield vs. grind size), **decaffeination** (solvent, water process, supercritical CO₂), **instant coffee** (spray drying, freeze drying, agglomeration), and **tea processing** with focus on **Black tea** (orthodox vs. CTC methods).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of coffee and tea processing as a **"from-field-to-cup" pipeline of controlled decay and stabilization**:

- **Analogy 1 — The Coffee Cherry is a "Time Capsule":** A ripe red coffee cherry protects two green seeds inside a mucilaginous husk. Post-harvest processing is essentially opening the capsule, removing the protective layers, drying the seeds to a stable moisture content (~10–12 %), and then **roasting** — which is a controlled pyrolysis reaction that unlocks ~800–1000 aromatic compounds.

- **Analogy 2 — Tea Leaves are "Living Chemistry Labs":** Once plucked, tea leaves are biologically active. The wither-and-roll process **ruptures cell walls**, allowing polyphenol oxidase (PPO) and peroxidase enzymes to contact catechins and convert them into **theaflavins and thearubigins** — the pigments responsible for the red-brown liquor of black tea. Stopping this enzymatic clock (by firing/drying) determines the tea type.

- **Analogy 3 — Brewing is "Solid–Liquid Extraction in a Cup":** Brewing is governed by Fick's laws of diffusion. Grind size, water temperature (~92–96 °C for coffee), contact time, and turbulence are the **four dials** the barista (or engineer) adjusts to extract the right balance of solubles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Beverage Metrics (Industry Norms):**
> - **Coffee extraction yield:** 18–22 % (soluble solids extracted ÷ dry coffee mass)
> - **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids):** 1.15–1.35 % for espresso; 1.25–1.45 % for filter coffee
> - **Tea leaf to water ratio:** 2 g per 200 mL (≈1 % w/v)
> - **Caffeine content:** Arabica ~1.2 %, Robusta ~2.2 %, Black tea ~3.5 %

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Coffee Bean Preparation — Sequential Unit Operations

The journey from **coffee cherry** to **green bean** involves these sequential unit operations:

### 2.1.1 Harvesting
- **Selective/Picking:** Hand-picked ripe cherries (highest quality, labour-intensive).
- **Stripping:** All cherries stripped from the branch (mechanical/hand).
- **Mechanical harvesting:** Tractor-mounted vibrating or rotating fingers.

### 2.1.2 Processing Methods (Two Principal Routes)

| Method | Sub-type | Steps | End Product | Region |
|--------|----------|-------|-------------|--------|
| **Dry / Natural** | — | Sun-drying whole cherries on raised beds (~12–25 days) | Fruity, heavy-bodied, sweet | Brazil, Ethiopia, Yemen |
| **Wet / Washed** | Fully Washed | Pulping → Mucilage removal (fermentation 12–72 h) → Washing → Drying | Clean, bright, acidic | Colombia, Kenya, Guatemala |
| **Wet / Washed** | Semi-Washed (Honey) | Pulping → Partial mucilage retention → Drying | Sweet, syrupy, balanced | Costa Rica, El Salvador |
| **Mechanical / Mucilaging** | — | Pulping → Mechanical mucilage removal → Drying | Cleaner than natural, less complex than fully washed | Brazil (some) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Critical Concept:** The **fermentation step in wet processing** is *not* a microbial alcoholic fermentation (despite the name) — it is a **pectinolytic enzymatic hydrolysis** of the mucilage layer by native pectinases, pectate lyases, and polygalacturonases present in the cherry and ambient microflora.

### 2.1.3 Drying Mechanics
- **Sun Drying:** Spread on patios/raised African beds; target moisture **10–12 % w.b.**
- **Mechanical Drying:** Guardiola dryers, rotary drum dryers (~45–60 °C).
- Drying rate follows a **falling-rate period** (diffusion-controlled) — analogous to food dehydration.

### 2.1.4 Hulling, Polishing, Grading, Sorting
- **Hulling:** Mechanical removal of dried parchment (endocarp) from wet-processed beans or the entire dried husk from dry-processed beans.
- **Polishing:** Optional; removes silverskin for aesthetic appeal.
- **Grading:** By **screen size** (10–20), by **defects** (primary/secondary), by **density** (gravity table).
- **Sorting:** Optical/electronic colour sorters remove defective beans.

## 2.2 Roasting — The Pyrolytic Heart of Coffee

Roasting is a complex **endothermic-then-exothermic** thermochemical transformation:

### 2.2.1 Stages of Roasting (Endothermic → Exothermic Curve)

| Stage | Bean Temp (°C) | Physical/Chemical Event | Color |
|-------|----------------|--------------------------|-------|
| Drying | 50–150 | Free and bound water evaporates | Green → Yellow |
| Maillard Reaction | 150–200 | Amino acids + reducing sugars → melanoidins, pyrazines | Yellow → Tan |
| First Crack (FC) | ~196–205 | Pyrolysis, CO₂ release, expansion, audible crack | Brown |
| Development Phase | 205–225 | Strecker degradation, lipid breakdown, ~700 aromas | Brown → Dark Brown |
| Second Crack (SC) | ~225–235 | Cell wall rupture, oil surfacing, smoke | Very Dark / French / Italian |

> [!NOTE]
> **Roast Loss = Mass Loss during roasting (typically 12–20 % w.b.)** — comprises moisture loss + volatile matter (CO₂, water vapour, VOCs). Used as a quality/QC metric.

### 2.2.2 Key Chemical Transformations
- **Maillard Reaction:** Non-enzymatic browning; produces melanoidins, pyrazines (roasty/nutty), furans, thiophenes.
- **Strecker Degradation:** Amino acid decomposition → aldehydes + α-keto acids → contributes to aroma.
- **Caramelization:** Sugar pyrolysis at >160 °C → diacetyl, furaneol, maltol.
- **Caffeine:** Largely **thermally stable** — remains mostly intact even in dark roasts.
- **Chlorogenic Acids (CGAs):** Degrade by 50–80 % depending on roast level → quinic acid + caffeic acid.
- **Lipid Migration:** Internal coffee lipids migrate to the bean surface (especially >225 °C).

## 2.3 Brewing — Solid–Liquid Extraction Kinetics

Brewing is fundamentally a **batch, unsteady-state, solid–liquid extraction** governed by mass transfer.

### 2.3.1 Key Parameters (The "Brewing Tetrahedron")

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Extraction |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Grind Size** | 200–1000 μm | Finer = faster extraction (higher surface area) |
| **Water Temperature** | 88–96 °C | Higher = faster, more complete |
| **Contact Time** | 0:25 – 4:00 min | Longer = more extraction (risk of over-extraction/bitterness) |
| **Turbulence / Agitation** | Low – High | Higher = faster equilibrium |

### 2.3.2 Soluble Extraction Phases (in order of extraction rate)
1. **Ions and Salts** (potassium, sodium, chlorides) — fastest
2. **Acids** (citric, malic, chlorogenic) — fast
3. **Caffeine** — intermediate
4. **Lipids** (triglycerides, diterpenes) — slow, requires emulsification
5. **Maillard products / melanoidins** — slow, large MW

### 2.3.3 Under- vs. Over-Extraction
- **Under-extraction:** Sour, thin, salty — not enough solubles.
- **Balanced:** Sweet, acidic, bitter, full-bodied.
- **Over-extraction:** Bitter, astringent, hollow — excess tannins, phenylindanes.

## 2.4 Decaffeination — Engineering Out the Stimulant

Caffeine (1,3,7-trimethylxanthine, C₈H₁₀N₄O₂) is selectively removed from green coffee beans **before roasting** because:
- Roasted beans have an impermeable glassy/cellular structure, making post-roast extraction inefficient.
- The process targets **>97 % caffeine removal** (regulatory "decaffeinated" threshold) while preserving flavor precursors.

### 2.4.1 Industrial Decaffeination Methods (KTU High-Yield)

| Method | Solvent/Agent | Temp (°C) | Time (h) | Mechanism | Pros / Cons |
|--------|---------------|-----------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Direct Solvent (Ethyl Acetate / DCM)** | EA or DCM | 60–100 | 8–12 | Beans steamed → solvent percolates → dissolves caffeine | Inexpensive; solvent residue concerns |
| **Indirect Solvent (Water Process)** | EA or DCM on water extract | 60–100 | 8–12 | Soaked beans → aqueous caffeine extract → solvent extraction → caffeine-stripped water returned to beans | Gentler, less flavor loss |
| **Swiss Water Process®** | Water only (no chemical solvent) | 90–100 | 8–10 | Green coffee extract (GCE) with controlled <0.001 % caffeine selectively leaches caffeine from new batch | 100 % chemical-free; certified organic; slow |
| **Supercritical CO₂ (SCCO₂)** | CO₂ at 31 °C, 73 atm | 35–80 | 3–5 | CO₂ above critical point → gas-like diffusivity + liquid-like solvating power → selective caffeine extraction | Premium, clean, recyclable CO₂; high capex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supercritical CO₂ Chemistry (KTU-Favorite Question):** Above its critical point (**T_c = 31.1 °C, P_c = 73.8 bar**), CO₂ exhibits **zero surface tension, liquid-like density (~700–900 kg/m³), and gas-like diffusivity (~10⁻⁷ m²/s)**, making it an ideal tunable solvent. Caffeine has high solubility in SCCO₂; flavor compounds (sugars, amino acids, CGAs) have low solubility → **selective extraction**.

### 2.4.2 Caffeine Recovery
- From solvent processes: evaporation/crystallization → pure caffeine (sold to pharma/cola industries).
- From Swiss Water: caffeine adsorbed onto activated carbon/ion-exchange resin.
- From SCCO₂: pressure reduction → CO₂ evaporates → caffeine precipitates.

## 2.5 Instant Coffee — From Liquid Extract to Soluble Powder

**Instant (soluble) coffee** is the dehydrated, water-soluble solid extract of roasted coffee, designed to reconstitute in hot water with no brewing equipment.

### 2.5.1 Manufacturing Process (Block Diagram Logic)

1. **Roasting** of green beans (industrial scale, dark roast preferred).
2. **Grinding** to coarse/fine depending on extractor type.
3. **Extraction** in multi-stage **counter-current battery** of percolators (diffusion batteries) or in continuous columns.
4. **Concentration** of the dilute extract (~15–30 % solids) via:
   - **Evaporation** (multi-effect vacuum evaporators, falling film).
   - **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** / **Freeze concentration**.
5. **Drying** → critical step (see below).
6. **Aromatization & Agglomeration** (optional).
7. **Packaging** under nitrogen to prevent oxidation.

### 2.5.2 Drying Technologies

| Method | Mechanism | Particle Form | Quality |
|--------|-----------|---------------|---------|
| **Spray Drying** | Extract atomized → hot air (150–250 °C inlet) → rapid evaporation | Hollow spherical microparticles (50–300 μm) | High throughput, lower cost |
| **Freeze Drying (Lyophilization)** | Extract frozen → sublimation under vacuum | Irregular, porous, fast-reconstituting granules | Premium, retains aromatics |
| **Agglomeration** | Steam jets fuse fine particles into granules (1–3 mm) | Granular clusters | Improved wettability, "cafetiere look" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reconstitution Kinetics:** Instant coffee is judged by **wettability** (sinks vs. floats), **dispersibility** (no clumps), **solubility** (dissolves fully in <30 s in hot water). Freeze-dried granules outperform spray-dried in all three.

## 2.6 Tea — Botany, Chemistry, and the Six Processing Types

### 2.6.1 Botanical Origin
All true teas come from ***Camellia sinensis***:
- **C. sinensis var. sinensis** (China type) — small leaves, cold-hardy, low altitude.
- **C. sinensis var. assamica** (Assam type) — large leaves, tropical, high yield.

### 2.6.2 Key Biochemical Constituents of Fresh Leaf

| Component | Content (% dry weight) | Role |
|-----------|------------------------|------|
| **Catechins (Polyphenols)** | 20–30 | Astringency, oxidize to theaflavins/thearubigins |
| **Caffeine** | 3–4 | Stimulant, bitterness |
| **Amino Acids (Theanine)** | 1–5 | Umami, sweetness (esp. in shade-grown) |
| **Volatiles (Terpenes, Aldehydes)** | 0.01–0.02 | Aroma |
| **Carbohydrates, Proteins, Minerals** | Balance | Mouthfeel, body |

### 2.6.3 Six Types of Tea — Defined by Processing (KTU Critical Question)

| Type | Wither | Roll | Ferment (Oxidize) | Fire/Dry | Key Feature |
|------|--------|------|-------------------|----------|-------------|
| **Black Tea (Red Tea in China)** | Heavy | Yes (Orthodox/CTC) | **Fully (100 %)** | Hot air | Dark liquor, bold, malty |
| **Oolong Tea** | Light–Medium | Yes (partial) | **Partial (20–80 %)** | Hot air | Floral/fruity, semi-oxidized |
| **Green Tea** | None/Brief | Light | **None (0 %)** | Pan-fired (kill-green) or Steamed | Grassy, vegetal, vegetal catechins intact |
| **White Tea** | Sun-wither only | None | **Minimal (<10 %)** | Sun-dry/low heat | Delicate, silvery, low caffeine |
| **Yellow Tea** | Wither | Light | Slight | **"Men-huang" (sealed yellowing)** | Mellow, less astringent than green |
| **Dark / Post-Fermented Tea (Pu-erh)** | Wither | Yes | Microbial (microbial fermentation, not enzymatic) | Sun-dry + aging | Earthy, woody, aged |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Fermentation" in Tea Processing is a Misnomer:** It is **enzymatic oxidation** by **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** and **peroxidase (POD)** — not microbial fermentation. Pu-erh's "fermentation" is genuinely microbial (*Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, *Blastobotrys*) and is properly called **post-fermentation**.

## 2.7 Black Tea — Detailed Process Flow (KTU Module 4 Focus)

### 2.7.1 Manufacturing Stages of Black Tea (Orthodox Method)

1. **Plucking:** Two leaves + bud (TFB) standard.
2. **Withering:** 12–18 h, 25–35 °C, RH 60–70 %. Moisture reduces from 75–80 % to 55–65 %. Cell membranes become permeable → PPO substrates released.
3. **Rolling:** Mechanical or hand rolling; **cell rupture** exposes PPO to catechins → enzymatic browning begins. Orthodox → whole-leaf appearance; CTC (Crush, Tear, Curl) → granular, high-fanning yield.
4. **Fermentation (Oxidation):** Rolled leaf spread on racks; 20–150 min, 20–30 °C, high RH. Catechins → **theaflavins (TFs, yellow-red)** and **thearubigins (TRs, brown-red)**.
5. **Drying (Firing):** Hot air 90–120 °C; moisture → 2–3 %; **enzyme denaturation** halts oxidation.
6. **Sorting & Grading:** By leaf size (Orange Pekoe, Pekoe, Fannings, Dust).
7. **Storage:** Hermetic, moisture-proof, away from sunlight.

### 2.7.2 Quality Indices

| Index | Formula / Standard | Meaning |
|-------|---------------------|---------|
| **TF/TR Ratio** | TF (μmol/g) ÷ TR (μmol/g) | Higher = brighter, more "brisk" liquor |
| **Total Color** | Spectrophotometric absorbance at 460 nm | Reflects TR content |
| **Brightness** | Visual & sensoric | Desirable in Darjeeling, Assam |
| **Theaflavin Content** | 0.3–2.0 % of dry weight | Premium: >1 % |

### 2.7.3 Black Tea Grades (Sri Lanka / India Standard)

| Grade | Leaf Size (mm) | Use |
|-------|----------------|-----|
| **Orange Pekoe (OP)** | 7–11 | Premium loose-leaf |
| **Pekoe (P)** | 5–7 | Mid-tier |
| **Broken Orange Pekoe (BOP)** | 2–4 | Teabags |
| **Fannings (F)** | 1–2 | Tea bags |
| **Dust (D)** | <1 | Single-serve sachets |

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Concept/Formula Sheet

| # | Concept / Equation | Formula / Statement | Units / Typical Value |
|---|--------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Roast Loss (RL)** | $RL = \dfrac{m_{\text{green}} - m_{\text{roasted}}}{m_{\text{green}}} \times 100$ | % (typ. 12–20) |
| 2 | **Extraction Yield (EY)** | $EY = \dfrac{\text{solubles extracted}}{\text{dry coffee used}} \times 100$ | % (typ. 18–22) |
| 3 | **Moisture Loss (Drying)** | $X_t = X_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ (1st-order decay, falling rate) | dimensionless |
| 4 | **SCCO₂ Critical Point** | $T_c = 31.1\,°C, \; P_c = 73.8\,\text{bar}$ | CO₂ |
| 5 | **Coffee Yield (Green-to-Roasted)** | $Y_{\text{roast}} = 1 - RL$ | 0.80–0.88 |
| 6 | **Brewing Solubility (TDS)** | $TDS = \dfrac{\text{mass of dissolved solids}}{\text{mass of brew}} \times 100$ | 1.15–1.45 % |
| 7 | **Decaffeination Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{decaf}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | >97 % (regulatory) |
| 8 | **Drying Rate (Falling Rate)** | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = -k \cdot (X - X_e)$ | X = moisture content (d.b.) |
| 9 | **Theaflavin/Tea Ratio** | $R_{TF/TR} = \dfrac{[TF]}{[TR]}$ | Premium ≥ 0.05 |
| 10 | **Mass Balance on Extraction Battery** | $C_n = C_0 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-n\beta}\right)$ (ideal plug flow approx.) | n = number of stages |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Application Insight:** Beverage processing engineers use these formulas to **scale up** lab recipes to industrial continuous lines — for example, designing a 5-stage diffusion battery for instant coffee requires solving the counter-current mass balance iteratively.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Extraction Yield in a Counter-Current Diffusion Battery

**Problem (KTU-Type Analytical Question):** Derive the relationship between extraction yield and the number of stages in a counter-current diffusion battery for instant coffee.

### 3.1.1 Setup
Let:
- $S$ = mass of dry coffee solids entering fresh (kg)
- $L$ = mass of water entering fresh (kg)
- $C_n$ = concentration of solubles in liquid leaving stage $n$ (kg solubles/kg solution)
- $y_n$ = concentration in solid phase leaving stage $n$ (kg solubles/kg insoluble solid)
- $n$ = total number of stages (1, 2, ... N)
- $\beta$ = stage efficiency factor (0 < β ≤ 1)

### 3.1.2 Material Balance Across One Stage (Stage i)

The mass of solute transferred from solid to liquid in stage $i$:

$$
L \cdot (C_{i-1} - C_i) = S \cdot (y_i - y_{i+1})
$$

### 3.1.3 Equilibrium Assumption (Linear Equilibrium)

Assuming local equilibrium with stage efficiency $\beta$:

$$
C_i = \beta \cdot C_i^{*}
$$

where $C_i^{*}$ is the equilibrium liquid concentration. For a linear partition:

$$
y_i = m \cdot C_i
$$

where $m$ is the partition coefficient (kg solubles/kg insoluble per kg/kg solution).

### 3.1.4 Iterative Solution

Substituting and rearranging:

$$
C_i = C_{i-1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{S \cdot m}{L \cdot \beta}}\right)
$$

Let $\alpha = \dfrac{S \cdot m}{L \cdot \beta}$ (extraction parameter).

Then:

$$
C_i = C_{i-1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \alpha}
$$

### 3.1.5 Closed-Form Solution After n Stages

$$
C_n = C_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + \alpha}\right)^{n}
$$

The **cumulative extraction yield** is:

$$
\eta_{\text{ext}} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + \alpha}\right)^{n}
$$

### 3.1.6 Numerical Example (Worked)

**Given:** $S = 100\,\text{kg}$ coffee, $L = 1000\,\text{kg}$ water, $m = 0.5$, $\beta = 0.9$, $C_0 = 0$ (fresh water).

**Step 1:** Calculate $\alpha$:

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{100 \times 0.5}{1000 \times 0.9} = \dfrac{50}{900} = 0.0556
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate yield for $n = 5$ stages:

$$
\eta_{\text{ext}} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1.0556}\right)^5 = 1 - (0.9471)^5
$$

$$
= 1 - 0.7615 = 0.2385
$$

**Step 3:** For $n = 10$:

$$
\eta_{\text{ext}} = 1 - (0.9471)^{10} = 1 - 0.5800 = 0.4200
$$

**Step 4:** For $n = 15$:

$$
\eta_{\text{ext}} = 1 - (0.9471)^{15} = 1 - 0.4417 = 0.5583
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to extraction yield (assuming initial soluble content of coffee is 28 % of green bean mass):

$$
EY = 0.5583 \times 28 = 15.63\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Beyond ~15 stages, the marginal yield gain per stage becomes economically unattractive. Industrial instant coffee plants therefore operate **5–10 stage diffusion batteries** at elevated temperature (160–180 °C) and pressure (4–20 bar) to accelerate kinetics.

## 3.2 Derivation: Caffeine Mass Balance for SCCO₂ Decaffeination

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
A batch of $M = 1000\,\text{kg}$ green coffee beans (initial caffeine $C_0 = 1.2\,\%\,\text{w/w}$) is decaffeinated using SCCO₂ at $T = 40\,°C$, $P = 250\,\text{bar}$. The solubility of caffeine in SCCO₂ at these conditions is $S_{\text{CAF}} = 0.0012\,\text{kg}_{\text{CAF}}/\text{kg}_{\text{CO}_2}$. Calculate the minimum mass of CO₂ required to reduce caffeine content below 0.1 % (decaffeinated threshold).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Initial caffeine mass**

$$
m_{\text{CAF},0} = 1000 \times 0.012 = 12.0\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 2: Final (target) caffeine mass**

$$
m_{\text{CAF},f} = 1000 \times 0.001 = 1.0\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3: Caffeine to be removed**

$$
\Delta m_{\text{CAF}} = 12.0 - 1.0 = 11.0\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4: Minimum CO₂ required (theoretical, single-pass, equilibrium saturation)**

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{\Delta m_{\text{CAF}}}{S_{\text{CAF}}} = \dfrac{11.0}{0.0012} = 9166.7\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5: Realistic recycling factor (industrial: CO₂ recycled with 5 % fresh make-up)**

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,\text{circulating}} = 9166.7 / 0.95 \approx 9650\,\text{kg}\;\text{(loop mass)}
$$

**Step 6: Convert to volume at operating conditions (CO₂ density at 40 °C, 250 bar ≈ 870 kg/m³)**

$$
V_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{9650}{870} = 11.09\,\text{m}^3
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Coffee vs. Tea Processing Comparison

| Parameter | Coffee Processing | Tea Processing |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Raw material** | Coffee cherry (fruit, seed) | Tea leaf (vegetative) |
| **Primary biochemical event** | Pyrolysis (roasting) | Enzymatic oxidation (fermentation) |
| **Drying endpoint** | Green bean: 10–12 % moisture | Made tea: 2–3 % moisture |
| **Decaffeination target** | <0.1 % caffeine (EU) / <0.3 % (US) | <0.4 % (rarely done) |
| **Caffeine retention** | 95 % in green → ~70–80 % in roasted | ~85 % in made black tea |
| **Brew temperature** | 88–96 °C | 85–95 °C (lower for green) |
| **Steep time** | 25 s – 4 min | 2 – 5 min |
| **Yield per kg raw** | ~0.21 kg roasted (Arabica) | ~0.22 kg made tea |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Coffee Cherry → Green Bean Processing Routes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Harvested Coffee Cherries] --> B{Quality Check}
    B --> C[Sorted Ripe Cherries]
    C --> D{Processing Method}
    D --> E1[Wet Method Route]
    D --> E2[Dry Method Route]
    D --> E3[Mechanical Method]
    
    E1 --> F1[Pulping]
    F1 --> G1[Fermentation Tank 12 to 72 h]
    G1 --> H1[Wash to remove mucilage]
    H1 --> I1[Sun or Mechanical Drying]
    
    E2 --> F2[Spread on raised African beds]
    F2 --> G2[Sun drying 12 to 25 days]
    
    E3 --> F3[Pulping]
    F3 --> G3[Mechanical mucilage removal]
    G3 --> H3[Drying]
    
    I1 --> J1[Moisture 10 to 12 percent]
    G2 --> J1
    H3 --> J1
    
    J1 --> K1[Hulling]
    K1 --> L1[Polishing Optional]
    L1 --> M1[Grading by Screen Size]
    M1 --> N1[Sorting Optical Color]
    N1 --> O1[Green Beans]
    O1 --> P1[Storage in Jute Sacks GrainPro]
    
    stepA1[P1 Storage] --> stepA2[Roasting 196 to 235 C]
    stepA2 --> stepA3[Grinding]
    stepA3 --> stepA4[Brewing or Extraction]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Tea (Black Tea) Manufacturing Line

```mermaid

    L2 --> L3[Withering Trough 12 to 18 h]

    M3 --> N1
    
    N1 --> O1[PPO Oxidation Catechins to TF and TR]
    O1 --> P1[Drying Fluid Bed Dryer 90 to 120 C]
    P1 --> Q1[Sorting by Size OP BOP Fannings Dust]

```
