# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
**"Differentiate between the wet and dry methods of coffee bean processing. List two key quality differences observed in the cup."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Wet (Washed) Method | Dry (Natural) Method |
|--------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Mucilage removal** | Mechanical + fermentation (12–72 h) | Retained, absorbed during drying |
| **Drying** | Parchment + bean, post-fermentation | Whole cherry |
| **Time** | Shorter (5–10 days total) | Longer (12–25 days) |
| **Flavor profile** | Clean, bright, acidic, complex | Fruity, sweet, heavy-bodied |
| **Risk** | Defective fermentation → "stinker" beans | Uncontrolled drying → mold |

> **Key Cup Differences:**
> 1. **Acidity:** Washed coffees display higher perceived acidity (citric/malic); naturals are softer.
> 2. **Body:** Naturals have a heavier, syrupy mouthfeel due to retained fruit sugars.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]**
**"What is supercritical carbon dioxide (SCCO₂) decaffeination? Mention the critical constants of CO₂."**

**Model Answer:**
SCCO₂ decaffeination is a non-toxic, residue-free process where CO₂, when held above its **critical point ($T_c = 31.1\,°C, P_c = 73.8\,\text{bar}$)**, acquires **liquid-like density** and **gas-like diffusivity**, making it an excellent selective solvent for caffeine. Green coffee beans are exposed to circulating SCCO₂; caffeine dissolves into the supercritical fluid, which is then depressurized to precipitate the caffeine for recovery. The CO₂ is recycled.

**Critical Constants:**
$$
T_c = 31.1\,°C, \quad P_c = 73.8\,\text{bar} \approx 73\,\text{atm}
$$

> **Why Supercritical?** Below the critical point, CO₂ exists as gas (no solvent power) or liquid (poor mass transfer). Above it, density ≈ 700–900 kg/m³ → effective solvent; diffusivity ≈ $10^{-7}$ m²/s → rapid mass transfer.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks) — **OPTION A**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model, Module 4]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe in detail the **unit operations involved in the manufacture of instant (soluble) coffee**, starting from green coffee beans. Include the role of diffusion batteries, concentration methods, and drying techniques.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare **spray drying** and **freeze drying** for instant coffee. Discuss the effect of each method on the **aroma retention, reconstitution behavior, and shelf-life** of the final product.

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (Question A)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The manufacture of instant coffee follows a sequence of well-defined unit operations:

**Step 1 — Roasting (1 mark)**
Green beans are roasted to a **dark roast (Agtron ~30–45)** at 210–230 °C to maximize soluble yield. Light roasts are avoided because they generate excess fines and reduce extraction efficiency.

**Step 2 — Grinding (0.5 mark)**
Roasted beans are ground to a **coarse grind** to facilitate packing in extractor cells while minimizing fines that cause channeling.

**Step 3 — Counter-Current Diffusion Battery Extraction (2.5 marks)**
The ground coffee is charged into a series of **5–10 stainless steel extractor cells** arranged in series. Hot water (160–180 °C) under pressure (4–20 bar) flows **counter-current** to the coffee mass:
- Fresh water enters the cell with the most-extracted coffee.
- Concentrated extract leaves the cell with fresh coffee.
- Total contact time: ~3–5 hours.
- Final extract concentration: 15–30 % total solids.

**Step 4 — Concentration (1 mark)**
The dilute extract is concentrated to **40–50 % solids** using:
- **Multi-effect vacuum evaporators** (3-effect, 60–70 °C) — common.
- **Reverse osmosis (RO)** — preserves heat-sensitive volatiles.
- **Freeze concentration** — premium-grade only.

**Step 5 — Drying & Aromatization (1.5 marks)**
The concentrated extract is dried to **2–4 % moisture** by:
- **Spray drying** (atomization + hot air 200–250 °C inlet, 90–110 °C outlet) → fine powder.
- **Freeze drying** (frozen to −40 °C, sublimation at 50–100 Pa) → porous granules.
- Volatile aroma compounds stripped during evaporation are **re-incorporated** as an oil layer on the powder surface (aromatization).

**Step 6 — Packing (0.5 mark)**
Under nitrogen flush, in airtight glass jars or laminated foil pouches, to prevent oxidative staling.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

| Property | Spray Drying | Freeze Drying |
|----------|--------------|----------------|
| **Process** | Atomization in hot air (150–250 °C) | Sublimation from frozen state (vacuum) |
| **Particle shape** | Hollow, spherical, 50–300 μm | Irregular, porous, granular clusters |
| **Bulk density** | 0.15–0.25 g/cm³ | 0.20–0.30 g/cm³ |
| **Aroma retention** | **Poor** — high temperatures drive off volatiles; mitigated by post-aromatization | **Excellent** — cold process preserves native aroma |
| **Reconstitution** | Slower; needs stirring; may float | Fast; wets and dissolves in <10 s |
| **Shelf life** | Good; lower aroma initially; stable if packed in N₂ | Excellent; high initial aroma; slightly hygroscopic |
| **Cost** | Low (~$1–2/kg) | High (~$4–8/kg) |
| **Energy** | High thermal (gas/steam) | High electrical (vacuum + refrigeration) |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Spray drying is the **workhorse** for mass-market instant coffee; freeze-drying is reserved for **premium segments** (e.g., Nescafé Gold, Starbucks VIA).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **OPTION B**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model, Module 4]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain with a flow diagram the **manufacture of black tea by the Orthodox method**. Mention the key biochemical changes during the **fermentation (oxidation) stage** and their impact on liquor quality.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **role of withering in black tea processing**. How does withering time and temperature influence the quality parameters — namely, **brightness, briskness, and theaflavin content**?

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (Question B)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Orthodox Black Tea Manufacture (Flow Description, 4 marks):**

1. **Plucking:** Two leaves + bud (TFB) hand-plucked; 1.5 mark.
2. **Withering:** 12–18 h at 25–35 °C, RH 60–70 %; moisture falls from 75–80 % → 55–65 %; 0.5 mark.
3. **Rolling:** Mechanical rollers rupture cells; 0.5 mark.
4. **Fermentation (Oxidation):** Spread on racks/floor, 20–150 min at 20–30 °C, high RH; 0.5 mark.
5. **Drying (Firing):** Hot air 90–120 °C; moisture ↓ to 2–3 %; enzymes denatured; 0.5 mark.
6. **Sorting & Grading:** By leaf size into OP, BOP, Fannings, Dust; 0.5 mark.

**Biochemical Changes during Fermentation (2.5 marks):**
- **Polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** + **Peroxidase (POD)** catalyze oxidation of **catechins** (epigallocatechin gallate, epicatechin gallate).
- Catechins → **Quinones** → **Theaflavins (TFs)** — yellow-red pigments → **Thearubigins (TRs)** — brown-red polymers.
- TF/TR ratio determines **brightness and briskness** of liquor.
- Amino acid L-**theanine** + sugars contribute **umami and sweetness**.

**Impact on Quality (0.5 mark):**
- Over-fermentation → excessive TRs → dull, flat liquor.
- Under-fermentation → residual catechins → harsh, grassy/astringent liquor.
- Optimal: TF/TR ≈ 0.05–0.10; TF content 0.8–1.5 %.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Role of Withering (2 marks):**
- Reduces moisture to make leaves flaccid and non-brittle — essential for clean rolling without breakage.
- Initiates **chemical wither** — slow enzymatic hydrolysis of starches to sugars, breakdown of proteins to amino acids, partial oxidation of catechins.
- Concentrates cell sap → enhances enzyme-substrate contact.

**Effect of Wither Time (2.5 marks):**
- **Under-withering (10–12 h):** Leaves still turgid → poor rolling → coarse, flaky leaf → under-fermented, grassy liquor.
- **Optimal wither (14–18 h, 55–65 % moisture):** Flaccid leaf rolls into twisted, well-broken form → uniform fermentation → high TF content, bright liquor.
- **Over-withering (>20 h):** Excessive moisture loss + early enzymatic activity → dull leaf, low TF/TR → flat, lifeless liquor.

**Effect of Wither Temperature (2.5 marks):**
- **Low temp (20–25 °C):** Slow wither; extended time may allow microbial growth → "mouldy" taint.
- **Optimal (28–35 °C):** Balanced chemical + physical wither → best TF development.
- **High temp (>40 °C):** Rapid moisture loss → "hard wither"; leaves roll poorly → harsh liquor, low aroma.

> **Key Quality Correlation (KTU Insight):**
> - **Brightness** ∝ Theaflavin (TF) content
> - **Briskness** ∝ TF + Caffeine complex → "creamy" astringency
> - **Body/Color** ∝ Thearubigin (TR) content
> - **Aroma** ∝ Terpenes + Aldehydes preserved by gentle withering

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "fermentation" in tea vs. coffee:** Tea "fermentation" is **enzymatic oxidation (PPO/POD)** — *not* microbial. Stating it as microbial = −1 mark.
> 2. **Missing the critical constants of CO₂:** Forgetting $T_c = 31.1\,°C$ and $P_c = 73.8$ bar in a SCCO₂ question = up to −1.5 marks.
> 3. **Writing "roasting decreases caffeine":** Caffeine is **largely thermally stable**. Caffeine is *developed* (chlorogenic acid loss) but the absolute caffeine content decreases by only 5–10 %, not "destroyed." Wrong = −1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing spray drying vs. freeze drying:** Spray = hot air atomization (fast, cheap, hollow spheres); freeze = sublimation (slow, premium, porous granules). Vague = −1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the final moisture target of made tea (2–3 %) and roasted coffee (2–4 % for instant, 1.5–2.5 % for ground):** Examiner expects specific numbers.
> 6. **Drawing a flow diagram without arrows or with crossed lines:** Lose 0.5 mark for poor schematic clarity.
> 7. **Writing "decaffeination removes all caffeine":** It must state the **regulatory threshold** (<0.1 % in EU, <0.3 % in US for "decaffeinated" label).
> 8. **Skipping the role of PPO enzyme in black tea:** Naming "fermentation" without naming **polyphenol oxidase** = incomplete answer (−0.5 mark).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### ☕ COFFEE PROCESSING
- ✅ Coffee cherry → Green bean via **wet (washed), dry (natural), or mechanical** methods
- ✅ Wet method: Pulping → **pectinolytic fermentation** (12–72 h) → Washing → Drying
- ✅ Dry method: Whole cherry sun-dried 12–25 days
- ✅ Target moisture: Green bean = **10–12 % w.b.**
- ✅ **Roasting** is endothermic (drying) → exothermic (Maillard, caramelization, pyrolysis)
- ✅ **First Crack** ≈ 196–205 °C; **Second Crack** ≈ 225–235 °C
- ✅ **Roast loss** = 12–20 %; **chlorogenic acids** degrade 50–80 %
- ✅ **Brewing** = solid–liquid extraction governed by grind, temperature, time, turbulence
- ✅ **TDS** for espresso = 1.15–1.35 %; filter = 1.25–1.45 %
- ✅ **Decaffeination** target: <0.1 % caffeine (EU), <0.3 % (US)
- ✅ **SCCO₂** = CO₂ at $T > 31.1\,°C$ and $P > 73.8$ bar; selective caffeine extraction
- ✅ **Swiss Water Process** = chemical-free; uses green coffee extract
- ✅ **Instant coffee** = extract + concentration + drying (spray or freeze)
- ✅ **Spray drying** → hollow 50–300 μm spheres; **Freeze drying** → porous granules
- ✅ **5–10 stage counter-current diffusion batteries** for industrial extraction

### 🍵 TEA PROCESSING
- ✅ All true teas from ***Camellia sinensis*** (var. *sinensis* or *assamica*)
- ✅ **Catechins** (20–30 %) are oxidized by **PPO/POD** during "fermentation"
- ✅ Six tea types defined by **degree of oxidation**:
  - Black (100 %), Oolong (20–80 %), Green (0 %), White (<10 %), Yellow (slight), Pu-erh (microbial, post-fermented)
- ✅ **Black tea** process: Pluck → Wither (12–18 h) → Roll → Ferment (20–150 min) → Fire (90–120 °C) → Sort
- ✅ **Withering** reduces moisture from 75–80 % → 55–65 %
- ✅ **Theaflavins (TFs)** = yellow-red; **Thearubigins (TRs)** = brown-red
- ✅ **TF/TR ratio** = brightness/briskness indicator
- ✅ Premium black tea TF content: **0.8–1.5 %** of dry weight
- ✅ **L-theanine** = umami amino acid; **caffeine** = 3–4 % in made black tea
- ✅ **Grades:** Orange Pekoe (OP), Pekoe (P), BOP, Fannings (F), Dust (D)
- ✅ **CTC** method = Crush, Tear, Curl → granular, high-fanning yield (tea-bag grade)
- ✅ **Orthodox** method = whole-leaf, premium appearance

### 🎯 KTU HIGH-WEIGHTAGE POINTS (Must Memorize)
1. SCCO₂ critical constants: **$T_c = 31.1\,°C, P_c = 73.8$ bar**
2. Roast loss formula & range (12–20 %)
3. Black tea TF/TR ratio and color/brightness correlation
4. Decaffeination regulatory thresholds
5. Diffusion battery principle (counter-current, 5–10 stages)
6. Spray vs. freeze drying — mechanism, particle form, cost

> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 examiners award partial credit for **labeled flow diagrams**. Always include a clear, sequential flow chart in any 7-mark descriptive answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green and Oolong. Dairy Based Beverages. Fruit Juices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Green Tea — Formal Definition

**Green Tea** is a non-fermented (unoxidised) tea manufactured from the tender shoots (*two leaves and a bud*) of *Camellia sinensis* (L.) O. Kuntze, in which the endogenous enzyme **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)** is inactivated immediately after plucking through thermal fixation ("kill-green" step). This preserves the native catechins and prevents the enzymatic conversion of catechins → theaflavins/thearubigins (which is what defines black tea).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Green tea belongs to the **unfermented** category, Oolong to the **semi-fermented** category, and black tea to the **fully fermented** category — all from the *same* plant. The difference is **process control**, not raw material.

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of green tea like a **freshly cut apple left in lemon juice**: the lemon juice (heat) instantly stops the browning (oxidation). The apple stays pale and crisp because the enzyme that causes browning (PPO) is destroyed. In green tea, the heat "freezes" the leaf chemistry at its fresh, green state — preserving its grassy, vegetal, slightly sweet character.

### Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Fixation (Kill-Green) Temperature: 250 °C – 300 °C** (pan-fired/Chinese method) **OR steam at 100 °C for 30–60 s** (Japanese method)
- **Brewing Temperature: 70 °C – 80 °C** (to prevent bitterness extraction)
- **Steeping Time: 1 – 3 minutes**
- **Caffeine Content: 25 – 35 mg per 200 mL cup**
- **Total Catechins: 80 – 140 mg/g dry weight** (with **EGCG** ~50–80 mg/g)
- **Moisture in finished tea: < 7 % (w/w)**
- **Production Share: ~20 % of global tea production** (China, Japan, Vietnam)

### Major Sub-Types

| Sub-Type | Origin | Method | Character |
|----------|--------|--------|-----------|
| **Sencha** | Japan | Steamed (Japanese method) | Grassy, vegetal |
| **Matcha** | Japan | Shade-grown, stone-milled powder | Umami, intense |
| **Longjing (Dragonwell)** | China | Pan-fired (Chinese method) | Toasty, chestnut |
| **Gunpowder** | China | Rolled into pellets | Smoky, strong |
| **Bi-luo-chun** | China | Spiral-rolled, hand-rolled | Fruity, floral |

---

## 1.2 Oolong Tea — Formal Definition

**Oolong Tea** is a *semi-fermented* (semi-oxidised) tea in which the harvested leaves undergo **partial enzymatic oxidation** (typically **30 % – 60 %**, although the range can extend from 10 % to 80 %) before thermal fixation halts the reaction. It therefore occupies a **biochemical and sensory intermediate** between green and black tea.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Degree of Fermentation (DoF)** is the single most important processing variable in oolong manufacture. It dictates aroma profile, leaf appearance, and liquor colour.

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine an avocado: cut it open and leave it on the counter. If you eat it immediately → *green-tea stage* (no oxidation). If you wait 4 hours until half the flesh has browned → *oolong stage* (partial oxidation). If you wait 24 hours until fully brown → *black-tea stage* (full oxidation). The chef's decision of *when to "freeze"* the avocado by refrigeration = the oolong master's decision of *when to fire* the leaves.

### Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Degree of Oxidation: 10 % – 80 %** (light, medium, dark oolong)
- **Fixation Temperature: 200 °C – 260 °C** (tumble-fired in a rolling oven)
- **Oolong Withering Duration: 6 – 12 hours** (longer than green, shorter than black)
- **Bruising/Tossing Stage: 3 – 5 cycles** of 5–10 minutes each in a bamboo drum
- **Moisture in finished tea: < 6 % (w/w)**
- **Global Production Share: < 3 %** (mainly Fujian, Guangdong, Taiwan)

### Famous Sub-Types

| Sub-Type | Region | DoF | Character |
|----------|--------|-----|-----------|
| **Tieguanyin (Iron Goddess)** | Fujian, China | 30 – 40 % | Floral, orchid-like |
| **Da Hong Pao (Big Red Robe)** | Wuyi Mountains, China | 50 – 70 % | Roasted, mineral |
| **Dong Ding (Frozen Summit)** | Taiwan | 30 – 40 % | Buttery, orchid |
| **Oriental Beauty (Bai Hao)** | Taiwan | 60 – 80 % | Honey, fruity |
| **Anxi Tieguanyin** | Fujian | 30 % | Light, floral |

---

## 1.3 Dairy-Based Beverages — Formal Definition

**Dairy-based beverages** are liquid or semi-liquid potable products in which milk or milk-derived components (skim milk, whey, cream, milk solids) form the continuous aqueous phase, optionally combined with permitted flavouring, sugar, fruit, cocoa, coffee, probiotic cultures, or hydrocolloids, and processed to achieve microbiological safety and shelf stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Regulations, 2011 – Dairy Products)**, the *minimum* milk solids-not-fat (SNF) requirement for flavoured milk is **8.5 %**, and total milk solids must be at least **12 %** unless specifically defined otherwise (e.g., khoa, chhana).

### Sub-Categories (Quick Reference)

1. **Plain pasteurised / UHT milk**
2. **Flavoured milk** (chocolate, strawberry, vanilla)
3. **Fermented dairy beverages** — *yogurt drink, lassi, kefir, buttermilk, kumis*
4. **Whey-based beverages**
5. **Cream-based beverages** (e.g., Irish cream liqueur, coffee creamers)
6. **Sterilised and ESL (Extended Shelf Life) milk**
7. **Plant–dairy hybrids** (oat-milk, almond-milk — strictly plant-based but processed on dairy lines)

### Standard Quality Metrics (Bolded)

- **Pasteurisation LTLT: 63 °C × 30 min** (Low-Temperature-Long-Time)
- **Pasteurisation HTST: 72 °C × 15 s** (High-Temperature-Short-Time)
- **UHT Sterilisation: 135 °C – 150 °C × 2 – 5 s**
- **Acidity (as lactic acid): 0.10 – 0.15 %** for pasteurised milk; < 0.20 % for fermented
- **Fat %:** Toned 3.0 % | Double-toned 1.5 % | Skimmed < 0.5 %
- **SNF %:** Cow 8.5 | Buffalo 9.0

---

## 1.4 Fruit Juices — Formal Definition

**Fruit Juice** is the unfermented but fermentable liquid obtained from the edible part of sound, ripe, fresh fruit by mechanical extraction, possessing the characteristic colour, flavour, and aroma of the fruit. It is intended for direct consumption and may be either **NFC (Not From Concentrate)** or **reconstituted from concentrate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Codex Alimentarius Definition (CODEX STAN 247-2005):** "100 % fruit juice" must contain **no added sugars, salt, spices, or other substances**, but may have **restored volatiles** and **pulp/cells from the same fruit** that were lost during processing.

### Sub-Categories (Codex Classification)

| Codex Category | Definition |
|----------------|------------|
| **Fruit Juice** | 100 % mechanically extracted |
| **Fruit Juice from Concentrate** | Reconstituted to original soluble solids |
| **Concentrated Fruit Juice** | Brix ≥ concentration factor × original |
| **Nectar** | Juice + water + sugar + acid (40 – 50 % juice minimum) |
| **Fruit Beverage/Drink** | < 20 % juice + additives |

### Conceptual Analogy — Why Pasteurisation?

Think of fruit juice as a freshly squeezed orange on a kitchen counter: by **hour 4**, wild yeasts and moulds (which were always present on the peel) have fermented the sugars, producing CO₂ and off-flavours. **Pasteurisation** is the equivalent of immediately refrigerating the juice: it kills 99.9 % of the microbes, "freezing" the freshness for weeks or months.

### Standard Quality Metrics (Bolded)

- **Brix (°Bx): total soluble solids (% w/w)** — orange juice ≥ 10.5 °Bx
- **Brix-Acid Ratio: 14 – 18** for orange juice (quality indicator)
- **pH: 2.5 – 4.5** (most fruit juices are acidic)
- **Pasteurisation HTST: 90 – 95 °C × 15 – 60 s** (pH < 4.0, "acid foods")
- **Yeast/Mould limit: < 100 CFU/mL** (pasteurised)
- **Total Plate Count: < 1000 CFU/mL**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Fruit juices with pH < 4.5 are termed **acid foods** by FDA/USDA. They can be pasteurised at lower temperatures (90–95 °C) than low-acid foods (pH > 4.5) because *Clostridium botulinum* cannot grow below pH 4.6. This is the **botulinum safety boundary** at **pH 4.6**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Green Tea — Process Engineering Logic

The **logic of green tea manufacture** is to *inhibit oxidation at t = 0*:

1. **Plucking** — only *two leaves and a bud*, hand-plucked at dawn to avoid enzymatic pre-oxidation in the field.
2. **Withering (Optional, 30 – 60 min)** — soft breeze at 25 – 30 °C, reduces moisture from 75 – 80 % → 70 %. *Optional for green tea; in Japan, withering is skipped to retain grassy character.*
3. **Fixation / "Kill-Green"** — the **critical step**. Two industrial methods:
   - **Pan-firing (Chinese method):** leaves in a hot iron pan at **250 – 300 °C** for 2 – 5 min, with continuous hand or mechanical tossing.
   - **Steaming (Japanese method):** leaves pass through a steam chamber at **100 °C for 30 – 60 s** on a conveyorised steamer.
4. **Rolling** — hot-rolled to break cells, shape the leaf, and release intracellular enzymes (already denatured, so no further oxidation occurs).
5. **Drying** — final moisture to < 5 % in a chain or fluidised-bed dryer at **100 – 130 °C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why kill PPO so fast?** PPO catalyses: **Catechins + O₂ → Quinones → Theaflavins → Thearubigins** (brown pigments). By denaturing PPO at the first moment, **catechins are preserved**, giving green tea its characteristic *non-browning, light-yellow-green, astringent* liquor and *higher antioxidant capacity* than black tea.

---

## 2.2 Oolong Tea — Process Engineering Logic

Oolong is a **controlled partial oxidation** — the *art lies in the timing* of fixation:

1. **Plucking** — older, more mature leaves are preferred (higher L-theanine content).
2. **Solar Withering (5 – 20 min in sunlight)** — initiates slow moisture loss; some Japanese cultivars are skipped.
3. **Indoor Withering (1 – 4 hours, 20 – 25 °C, 60 – 70 % RH)** — water content falls to 55 – 60 %.
4. **Bruising / Tossing (3 – 5 cycles in a bamboo drum or rotary tumbler)** — mechanical damage at leaf edges ruptures cells, exposing PPO to oxygen. This produces the **characteristic 30 – 60 % oxidation** at *leaf margins only* (giving the trademark red-brown edge with green centre).
5. **Panning / Fixation (200 – 260 °C in a rolling oven)** — terminates the oxidation.
6. **Rolling (manual or roller)** — imparts twisted, balled, or strip shape.
7. **Drying (90 – 110 °C)** — final moisture < 6 %.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Toss" Stage** is unique to oolong. The leaves are repeatedly tossed against a bamboo screen, mechanically rupturing cell walls at edges. The fix is then carried out before uniform oxidation — producing the *partially* oxidised chemical gradient that defines oolong's flavour complexity.

---

## 2.3 Dairy-Based Beverages — Process Logic

### Pasteurised Fluid Milk (The Reference Process)

1. **Reception and Chilling (4 °C)** — within 2 hours of milking.
2. **Standardisation** — fat and SNF adjusted to target % using a **Pearson square**.
3. **Pre-heating (40 – 50 °C)** — to enable separation/clarification.
4. **Homogenisation (15 – 20 MPa at 60 – 70 °C)** — reduces fat globules to < 2 µm (prevents creaming).
5. **Pasteurisation (HTST: 72 °C × 15 s)** — destroys pathogens (*M. tuberculosis*, *Brucella*, *E. coli*, *Salmonella*).
6. **Cooling (4 °C)** — to inhibit surviving spore-formers.
7. **Packaging** — opaque, food-grade.

### UHT Milk

**1 – 5 s at 135 – 150 °C** in a plate or tubular heat exchanger, then aseptically packed. Shelf life: **6 months at ambient temperature**. The high temperature causes **Maillard browning** (lysine + lactose) and a slightly cooked flavour — controlled by indirect vs direct heating.

### Fermented Dairy Beverages

Common starter cultures:
- **Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus**
- **Streptococcus thermophilus** (yogurt co-culture)
- **Lactobacillus acidophilus, Bifidobacterium** (probiotic)

The pH drops from 6.7 → 4.5 due to **lactic acid production** (~1.0 – 1.5 %), gelling the casein and producing a smooth, viscous, tart drinkable yogurt.

---

## 2.4 Fruit Juice — Process Logic

### Generic NFC (Not From Concentrate) Process

1. **Receiving, Sorting, Washing** — removes dirt, surface microbes, defective fruit.
2. **Size Reduction** — crushing, milling, or pulping.
3. **Enzymatic Treatment (Pectinase 0.05 – 0.1 % at 40 – 50 °C, 1 – 2 h)** — breaks pectin, increases yield, decreases viscosity.
4. **Extraction** — screw press, basket press, decanter centrifuge, or belt press. Yields 70 – 85 %.
5. **Filtration / Clarification** — for clear juices; use diatomaceous earth or ultrafiltration (5 – 50 kDa cut-off).
6. **De-aeration (Vacuum at 40 °C)** — removes dissolved O₂, preventing browning.
7. **Pasteurisation (HTST 90 – 95 °C × 15 – 60 s)**.
8. **Hot-filling at 85 – 90 °C** into pre-sterilised glass/PET — OR — **Aseptic cold-fill** after UHT treatment.
9. **Cooling & Storage** (4 °C or ambient for aseptic).

### Reconstituted Juice from Concentrate

1. Concentrate (TSS ≈ 65 – 70 °Bx) → dilutes with treated water to original Brix.
2. **Fortification with "essence" / restored volatiles** to recover lost aroma.
3. Pasteurise, pack.

### Concentration Methods

- **Multi-Effect Evaporation (MEE):** 3 – 7 effect, low temperature (45 – 70 °C) falling film; inexpensive but volatile losses are high.
- **Freeze Concentration:** preserves aroma, expensive.
- **Reverse Osmosis (RO):** 10 – 30 bar; minimal thermal damage; membrane-limited to ~30 °Bx.
- **Osmotic Distillation / Membrane Distillation:** newer, hybrid processes.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following engineering formulas are **recurrent in KTU numerical questions on juice/dairy processing**. Memorise them with units.

| Equation (No Pipes — Use "vert" or "over") | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pearson Square for Standardisation** | $\dfrac{Fat_{skim} - F_{target}}{F_{target} - F_{cream}} = \dfrac{Mass_{cream}}{Mass_{skim}}$ | F = fat % | Dairy fat standardisation |
| **Dilution (Concentrate) Formula** | $V_{1} \times C_{1} = V_{2} \times C_{2}$ | V = volume (L), C = concentration (°Bx or %) | Reconstituting juice concentrate |
| **Brix-Acid Ratio** | $BAR = \dfrac{\text{°Bx}}{\text{Titratable Acidity} \, (g/100g \text{ citric})}$ | Dimensionless | Orange juice flavour quality |
| **Titratable Acidity (Citric)** | $\% Acid = \dfrac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 0.064 \times 100}{V_{sample}}$ | V in mL, N in eq/L | Quality testing of juice |
| **HTST Equivalent (D, z)** | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right) = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{z}$ | D = decimal reduction time (min), T = °C, z = °C | Pasteurisation design |
| **First-Order Microbial Death** | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\dfrac{t}{D}$ | N = survivors (CFU/mL) | Pasteurisation lethality |
| **Mass Balance on Brix** | $M_1 \times Bx_1 = M_2 \times Bx_2$ | M = mass, Bx = Brix | Concentration, dilution |
| **Energy for Pasteurisation** | $Q = m \times c_p \times \Delta T$ | Q in kJ, $c_p \approx 3.93 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ for milk | Heat exchanger sizing |
| **Refractometer Brix Correction** | $Bx_{corr} = Bx_{meas} \times \left[1 + 0.0001 \times (T - 20)\right]$ | T in °C | Lab measurement |
| **% Claret / Lambert-Beer (Concentration)** | $A = \varepsilon \times c \times l$ | A = absorbance | Spectrophotometry (color) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on table formatting:** Per the strict protocol, the vertical pipe symbol "$\vert$" or "$\mid$" is intentionally avoided inside table rows. The dilution equation uses $\times$ for multiplication and division is expressed as a fraction.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Process | Industry Use |
|---|---|
| **Green tea fixation (steam/pan)** | Industrial: drum-type or tower steamers in Japan; pan-firing in China; direct-fired heated rotating drum (60 – 80 kg/batch) |
| **Oolong bruising** | Rotary "jie" tumbler (Taiwan) or bamboo-tossing (Fujian) — mechanised at 6 – 8 rpm, 5 min cycles |
| **HTST plate heat exchanger (milk)** | Tetra Pak, GEA, SPX — 30,000 L/h flow rates, energy recovery > 90 % |
| **Multi-effect evaporator (juice)** | GEA, Fenix — 6-effect falling-film; energy saving ~80 % vs single-effect |
| **Aseptic cold-fill** | Tetra Brik aseptic lines — 1 billion packs/day globally |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pearson Square for Milk Standardisation

> **Problem:** A dairy plant has **2000 L of milk with 3.5 % fat** and wants to standardise to **3.0 % fat** by adding **skim milk (0.5 % fat)**. How much skim milk must be added?

### Step 1 — State the Mass Balance (in terms of fat)

Let $V_s$ be the volume of skim milk to add (L). Total fat must be conserved.

$$ \text{Fat}_{in} = \text{Fat}_{out} $$

### Step 2 — Write the Equation in Variables

$$ 2000 \times 3.5\% + V_s \times 0.5\% = (2000 + V_s) \times 3.0\% $$

### Step 3 — Convert Percentages to Decimals (for clarity)

$$ 2000 \times 0.035 + V_s \times 0.005 = (2000 + V_s) \times 0.030 $$

### Step 4 — Expand the Right-Hand Side

$$ 70 + 0.005\,V_s = 60 + 0.030\,V_s $$

### Step 5 — Rearrange Terms

$$ 70 - 60 = 0.030\,V_s - 0.005\,V_s $$

$$ 10 = 0.025\,V_s $$

### Step 6 — Solve for $V_s$

$$ V_s = \dfrac{10}{0.025} = 400 \text{ L} $$

### Step 7 — Verify by Mass Balance

$$ \text{Fat in} = 2000 \times 0.035 + 400 \times 0.005 = 70 + 2 = 72 \text{ kg} $$

$$ \text{Fat out} = (2000 + 400) \times 0.030 = 2400 \times 0.030 = 72 \text{ kg} \;\;\checkmark $$

### Step 8 — Generalised Pearson Square

If the "low" stream has fat $F_L$ (skim) and the "high" stream has fat $F_H$ (cream or whole), and the target is $F_T$:

$$ \text{Mass of High} = (F_T - F_L) \times M_{total} $$

$$ \text{Mass of Low} = (F_H - F_T) \times M_{total} $$

For our problem: $F_H = 3.5$, $F_L = 0.5$, $F_T = 3.0$, $M_{total} = 2000$ L.

$$ \text{Proportion Skim} = \dfrac{3.5 - 3.0}{(3.5 - 3.0) + (3.0 - 0.5)} = \dfrac{0.5}{3.0} = 0.167 $$

$$ \text{Skim Volume} = 0.167 \times 2000 = 333 \text{ L (target ratio basis)} $$

*Note: This gives the ratio for blending with a third stream; the algebraic 400 L is the unique value that brings 2000 L of 3.5 % to 3.0 % using pure skim.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Reconstitution of Orange Concentrate

> **Problem:** An orange juice concentrate has **65 °Bx**. You need to prepare **1000 L of single-strength juice at 11.5 °Bx**. How much water must be added to the concentrate?

### Step 1 — Identify the Mass-Balance Equation

$$ M_c \times Bx_c = M_{final} \times Bx_{final} $$

where $M_c$ = mass of concentrate, $M_{final}$ = mass of reconstituted juice, $B_x$ = Brix. Masses are conserved, but volumes change (density varies); we therefore use **mass** for accuracy. However, because Bx is in w/w, we can equivalently use **volumes at unit density** as an approximation.

### Step 2 — Apply the Dilution Principle

$$ V_c \times 65 = 1000 \times 11.5 $$

### Step 3 — Solve for $V_c$

$$ V_c = \dfrac{1000 \times 11.5}{65} = \dfrac{11500}{65} = 176.92 \text{ L} $$

### Step 4 — Compute Volume of Water to Add

$$ V_{water} = V_{final} - V_c = 1000 - 176.92 = 823.08 \text{ L} $$

### Step 5 — Density Correction (if more accurate required)

For orange juice at 65 °Bx, $\rho \approx 1.317 \text{ kg/L}$; at 11.5 °Bx, $\rho \approx 1.045 \text{ kg/L}$.

$$ M_c = 176.92 \times 1.317 = 232.99 \text{ kg} $$

$$ M_{final} = 1000 \times 1.045 = 1045 \text{ kg} $$

$$ M_{water} = 1045 - 232.99 = 812.0 \text{ kg} \approx 812 \text{ L} $$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU problems at 100 % Bx accuracy, the simple dilution equation is sufficient. Density correction is **only required for > 30 % Bx systems or for engineering process design**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Brix-Acid Ratio Calculation

> **Problem:** An orange juice sample has Brix 12.0 °Bx and titratable acidity 0.85 g/100 g (as citric acid). Compute the Brix-Acid Ratio (BAR).

### Step 1 — Define the Formula

$$ BAR = \dfrac{\text{°Bx}}{\text{Titratable Acidity}} $$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$ BAR = \dfrac{12.0}{0.85} = 14.12 $$

### Step 3 — Quality Interpretation

| BAR Value | Sensory Implication |
|---|---|
| < 12 | Tart, acidic, immature fruit |
| 12 – 16 | Balanced, "table-grade" orange juice |
| 16 – 20 | Sweet, less acidic |
| > 20 | Insipid, sugary, possibly immature or over-mature fruit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brazilian/Florida processing standard:** Target BAR = **14.0 – 16.0** for NFC orange juice export. A BAR below 12 indicates the fruit was harvested too early; a BAR above 18 indicates low-acid late-season fruit.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Pasteurisation Lethality (D, z Model)

> **Problem:** A juice pasteuriser is to achieve a **5-log reduction** of a target microorganism. The reference D-value of the target organism is $D_{72} = 0.20$ min at 72 °C, and $z = 8$ °C. The juice will be held at **78 °C**. What is the required holding time?

### Step 1 — State the Log-Reduction Equation

The required number of decimal reductions:

$$ n = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = \log_{10}(10^5) = 5 $$

### Step 2 — Determine D at the Process Temperature

Using the thermal death time relationship:

$$ \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D_{T}}{D_{ref}}\right) = \dfrac{T_{ref} - T}{z} $$

$$ \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D_{78}}{D_{72}}\right) = \dfrac{72 - 78}{8} = \dfrac{-6}{8} = -0.75 $$

$$ D_{78} = D_{72} \times 10^{-0.75} = 0.20 \times 0.178 = 0.0356 \text{ min} $$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Required Holding Time

The relationship between log-reduction and D-value:

$$ t = n \times D_T $$

$$ t = 5 \times 0.0356 = 0.178 \text{ min} \approx 10.7 \text{ seconds} $$

### Step 4 — Verify and Add Safety Factor

Industry practice applies a **safety factor of 1.5 – 2×**:

$$ t_{design} = 0.178 \times 2 = 0.356 \text{ min} \approx 21 \text{ s} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Design Note:** Aseptic juice lines typically hold at **93 – 95 °C for 15 – 30 s** to deliver a 5-log reduction of *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, and *yeasts*, with the safety margin incorporating both the "come-up time" and the "cool-down time" of the holding tube.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Across Beverage Categories

| Parameter | Green Tea | Oolong Tea | Pasteurised Milk | Fruit Juice (NFC) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat treatment** | 250 – 300 °C pan / 100 °C steam | 200 – 260 °C pan | 72 °C × 15 s | 90 – 95 °C × 15 – 60 s |
| **Key enzyme/inactivation** | PPO denatured | PPO partially active | Lipase, phosphatase | Pectin methyl esterase |
| **pH** | 5.5 – 7.0 | 5.0 – 6.0 | 6.6 – 6.8 | 2.5 – 4.5 |
| **Shelf life** | 1 – 2 yr (dry) | 1 – 2 yr (dry) | 7 – 14 d (4 °C) | 21 – 60 d (4 °C) |
| **Storage temp** | Ambient | Ambient | 4 °C | 4 °C (or 25 °C aseptic) |
| **Brew/mixing ratio** | 1 g : 100 mL | 1 g : 30 mL (gongfu) | Ready-to-drink | Ready-to-drink |
| **Active compounds** | Catechins (EGCG) | Catechins + theaflavins | Casein, whey, Ca | Vitamin C, polyphenols |
| **Caffeine** | 25 – 35 mg/cup | 30 – 50 mg/cup | 0 | 0 (most juices) |
| **Microbial load post-process** | Sterile (low moisture) | Sterile | < 1000 CFU/mL | < 1000 CFU/mL |
| **Critical control point (HACCP)** | Enzyme inactivation | Oxidation control | Pasteurisation | Pasteurisation + pH |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** A common student error is to claim that **all "fermented" teas contain alcohol**. This is false. *Enzymatic* oxidation in tea is a **non-microbial** process. The term "fermentation" in tea is a misnomer (Hessel t'in, 1904). True *microbial* fermentation is reserved for kombucha, kefir, and yogurt.


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

Differentiate between **green tea** and **oolong tea** in terms of processing. Mention the **Degree of Oxidation (DoF)** in each case and the typical brewing temperature.

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Criterion | Green Tea | Oolong Tea |
|---|---|---|
| **Enzymatic oxidation** | Nil (PPO denatured at t = 0) | Partial (30 – 60 %) |
| **Fixation method** | Pan-firing (250 – 300 °C) or steaming (100 °C) | Tumble-firing (200 – 260 °C) |
| **DoF** | 0 % | 10 – 80 % (typically 30 – 60 %) |
| **Brewing temperature** | 70 – 80 °C | 90 – 95 °C |
| **Aroma** | Grassy, vegetal | Floral, complex, between green and black |
| **Catechin content** | Highest | Intermediate |
| **Caffeine** | 25 – 35 mg/cup | 30 – 50 mg/cup |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation key:** Marks will be awarded for *both* biochemical (DoF, PPO) and *processing* (fixation) details. A purely *sensory* answer receives at most 1 mark.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

Define **Brix-Acid Ratio (BAR)** for fruit juices. A sample of orange juice has Brix 11.8 °Bx and titratable acidity 0.7 g/100 g (as citric acid). Compute the BAR and state the quality category it falls in.

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (1 mark):** The Brix-Acid Ratio is the quotient of **Total Soluble Solids (°Bx)** to **Titratable Acidity (g/100 g)**, expressed as a dimensionless number. It is a key indicator of juice flavour balance.

**Calculation (1 mark):**

$$ BAR = \dfrac{11.8}{0.7} = 16.86 $$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** A BAR of 16.86 falls in the **16 – 20 range**, indicating a **sweet, less acidic juice** typical of late-season or over-mature orange fruit. The industry target is **14 – 16** for NFC orange juice export.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper – Module 4]**

> (a) **[7 marks]** Describe with a flow diagram the manufacturing process of **green tea**. Compare the **Chinese (pan-firing)** and **Japanese (steaming)** methods of fixation in terms of biochemical effect on catechins and final sensory quality.
>
> (b) **[7 marks]** A fruit juice concentrate has Brix 60 °Bx. Calculate the volume of concentrate and water required to produce 1500 L of reconstituted juice at 11 °Bx. Also compute the Brix-Acid Ratio if the titratable acidity of the single-strength juice is 0.8 g/100 g (as citric acid).

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]:

**1. Definition and Significance (1 mark):** Green tea is a non-fermented tea in which the polyphenol oxidase (PPO) enzyme is inactivated immediately after plucking by thermal "kill-green" fixation. The catechin content is preserved at 80 – 140 mg/g dry weight.

**2. Process Flow (3 marks):**

| Step | Process | Time / Temperature | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plucking (two leaves + bud) | Dawn | Quality raw material |
| 2 | Withering (optional) | 30 – 60 min, 25 – 30 °C | Softening, 5 % moisture loss |
| 3 | Fixation (kill-green) | 250 – 300 °C pan or 100 °C steam | PPO denaturation |
| 4 | Rolling | 10 – 30 min | Cell rupture, shape formation |
| 5 | Drying | 100 – 130 °C, 20 – 30 min | Moisture < 5 % |
| 6 | Sorting / Packaging | — | Commercial presentation |

**3. Comparison of Fixation Methods (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pan-Firing (Chinese) | Steaming (Japanese) |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat transfer** | Conduction + convection | Latent heat of vapour |
| **Temperature** | 250 – 300 °C | 100 °C |
| **Exposure time** | 2 – 5 min | 30 – 60 s |
| **Moisture removal** | Partial during firing | Minimal during steaming (post-drying required) |
| **Catechin retention** | 70 – 85 % | 85 – 95 % (gentler thermal load) |
| **Flavour** | Toasty, chestnut, sweet | Grassy, marine, vegetal, umami |
| **Leaf colour** | Dull olive-green | Bright emerald-green |
| **Examples** | Longjing, Gunpowder | Sencha, Matcha, Gyokuro |

**Valuation key:** 1 mark for the table; 1 mark each for the biochemical, sensory, and visual distinctions. A flow diagram earns 1 mark.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**Step 1 — Apply Dilution Formula (2 marks):**

$$ V_c \times Bx_c = V_f \times Bx_f $$

$$ V_c \times 60 = 1500 \times 11 $$

$$ V_c = \dfrac{16500}{60} = 275 \text{ L} $$

**Step 2 — Volume of Water (1 mark):**

$$ V_{water} = 1500 - 275 = 1225 \text{ L} $$

**Step 3 — Compute Brix-Acid Ratio (2 marks):**

$$ BAR = \dfrac{11.0}{0.8} = 13.75 $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

A BAR of 13.75 lies within the **target Brazilian/Florida range of 12 – 16**, indicating a **well-balanced, table-grade orange juice** with characteristic flavour and acid balance.

**Step 5 — Density-Corrected (Bonus – 1 mark):** At 60 °Bx, $\rho \approx 1.293$; at 11 °Bx, $\rho \approx 1.044$.

$$ M_c = 275 \times 1.293 = 355.6 \text{ kg} $$

$$ M_f = 1500 \times 1.044 = 1566 \text{ kg} $$

$$ M_{water} = 1566 - 355.6 = 1210.4 \text{ kg} \approx 1210 \text{ L} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Marks will be **deducted** if students use the simple dilution equation for concentrates > 30 °Bx without mentioning density correction.
> 2. Marks will be **deducted** if the Brix-Acid Ratio is computed without stating the **target range** for interpretation.
> 3. Marks will be **deducted** if the difference between *titratable acidity* and *pH* is not distinguished.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper – Module 4]**

> (a) **[7 marks]** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **manufacturing process of Oolong tea**. Explain the **biochemistry of partial oxidation** and its effect on the flavour profile. Mention the **role of the bruising/tossing stage**.
>
> (b) **[7 marks]** A dairy plant receives **5000 L of cow milk at 4.0 % fat**. It is to be standardised to **2.0 % fat** by adding **skim milk at 0.1 % fat**. Calculate the volume of skim milk required. If the standardised milk is pasteurised by HTST at 72 °C for 15 s, with $D_{72} = 0.6$ min for the target organism and $z = 7$ °C, calculate the **log reduction** achieved. Standardise the milk to a **final fat of 3.0 %** if the cream to be added has **40 % fat**. How much cream is required?

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]:

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Oolong is a **semi-fermented tea** with **10 – 80 % enzymatic oxidation** (typically 30 – 60 %). It is biochemically and sensorially intermediate between green and black tea.

**2. Process Flow (3 marks):**

| Step | Operation | Time / Temp | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plucking (mature leaves) | Daytime | Higher L-theanine |
| 2 | Solar withering | 5 – 20 min | Initial moisture loss |
| 3 | Indoor withering | 1 – 4 h, 20 – 25 °C | Brings moisture to 55 – 60 % |
| 4 | Bruising / tossing | 3 – 5 cycles × 5 – 10 min | Cell rupture at edges, exposing PPO |
| 5 | Partial oxidation | 1 – 3 h, controlled RH | Produces 30 – 60 % oxidation gradient |
| 6 | Fixation (pan-firing) | 200 – 260 °C | PPO deactivation, stops oxidation |
| 7 | Rolling / Balling | 10 – 30 min | Leaf shaping |
| 8 | Drying | 90 – 110 °C, 20 – 30 min | Final moisture < 6 % |
| 9 | Optional re-roasting | — | Develops roasted notes (e.g., Da Hong Pao) |

**3. Biochemistry of Partial Oxidation (2 marks):** Polyphenol oxidase catalyses the conversion of catechins to theaflavins and thearubigins in a *controlled* manner. At 30 – 60 % oxidation, only a portion of catechins is converted, leading to a **dual character** — freshness of green + body of black. The result is the **oolong aroma precursors (indole, jasmone, linalool oxides)** that yield floral, fruity, and honey notes.

**4. Role of Bruising / Tossing (1 mark):** The repeated mechanical tumbling ruptures cells at the **leaf margins** while leaving the **central vein and lamina** intact. This produces the trademark **red-brown edge with green centre**, *uneven* oxidation that distinguishes oolong from uniformly oxidised black tea.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**Step 1 — Pearson Square for 4.0 % → 2.0 % (using skim) (2 marks):**

$$ 5000 \times 4.0 + V_s \times 0.1 = (5000 + V_s) \times 2.0 $$

$$ 20000 + 0.1 V_s = 10000 + 2.0 V_s $$

$$ 10000 = 1.9 V_s $$

$$ V_s = 5263.16 \text{ L skim} $$

**Step 2 — Log-Reduction Calculation (2 marks):**

First, D at 72 °C = 0.6 min; z = 7 °C; t = 15 s = 0.25 min.

$$ n = \dfrac{t}{D_T} = \dfrac{0.25}{0.6} = 0.417 $$

$$ \log \text{Reduction} = 0.417 \text{ log} $$

*This is unrealistically low* — in industry, the process is designed for **5-log reduction**. Therefore, the process is **inadequate** and would need higher T or longer time.

**Step 3 — Re-Design at 80 °C (1 mark):**

$$ \log\!\left(\dfrac{D_{80}}{D_{72}}\right) = \dfrac{72 - 80}{7} = -1.143 $$

$$ D_{80} = 0.6 \times 10^{-1.143} = 0.6 \times 0.0719 = 0.0431 \text{ min} $$

$$ n = \dfrac{0.25}{0.0431} = 5.79 \log \text{ reduction} \;\;\checkmark $$

**Step 4 — Pearson Square for 4.0 % → 3.0 % using 40 % cream (2 marks):**

$$ 5000 \times 4.0 + V_{cr} \times 40 = (5000 + V_{cr}) \times 3.0 $$

$$ 20000 + 40 V_{cr} = 15000 + 3.0 V_{cr} $$

$$ 5000 = -37 V_{cr} \;\;\text{(NEGATIVE — impossible)} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Logical error caught:** If you *add* 40 % cream to 4.0 % milk, the fat content *increases* above 3.0 %; you cannot *lower* fat with 40 % cream. The student should re-read the question or add **skim milk** instead. This is a typical *trick question* in KTU valuation to test conceptual understanding.

**Correct alternative** (using skim, 0.1 % fat):

$$ 5000 \times 4.0 + V_s \times 0.1 = (5000 + V_s) \times 3.0 $$

$$ 20000 + 0.1 V_s = 15000 + 3.0 V_s $$

$$ 5000 = 2.9 V_s \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_s = 1724.1 \text{ L skim milk} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Step 1** of part (b): 1 mark is reserved for the *correct set-up* of the mass-balance equation. Marks will be **deducted** if the student uses a volume of 5000 in the right-hand side after adding $V_s$.
> 2. **Step 3** of part (b): If a student does not notice the **negative-result error** in the cream calculation, full marks will be deducted for "not verifying the physical feasibility of the result."
> 3. **Pasteurisation calculation:** Marks are awarded for *identifying* the inadequacy of 0.25 min holding at 72 °C (only 0.4 log reduction) — a common exam trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 4 Revision Checklist**

### Green Tea
- ☑ **Non-fermented / unoxidised**; PPO is the *target enzyme* of fixation.
- ☑ Two fixation methods: **Pan-firing (250 – 300 °C)** vs **Steaming (100 °C)**.
- ☑ Key bioactive: **EGCG** (Epigallocatechin gallate) — 50 – 80 mg/g.
- ☑ Brewing at **70 – 80 °C** for **1 – 3 min** (NOT boiling, to prevent bitterness).
- ☑ *Catechin → Theaflavin* pathway is **blocked**.

### Oolong Tea
- ☑ **Semi-fermented**, **DoF = 10 – 80 %** (typically 30 – 60 %).
- ☑ Unique step: **Bruising / Tossing** in bamboo drum.
- ☑ *Red-brown edge with green centre* appearance.
- ☑ Famous regions: **Fujian (Tieguanyin, Da Hong Pao), Taiwan (Dong Ding)**.
- ☑ *Repeated infusions* (gongfu cha) — 5 to 7+ brews.

### Dairy-Based Beverages
- ☑ **HTST: 72 °C × 15 s**; **UHT: 135 – 150 °C × 2 – 5 s**; **LTLT: 63 °C × 30 min**.
- ☑ **FDV (Flow Diversion Valve)** is the *critical control point* of HTST.
- ☑ Standardisation by **Pearson square** — $F_L, F_H, F_T$ with proportions.
- ☑ Fermented: pH drops to **4.5**; cultures: *Lb. bulgaricus* + *S. thermophilus*.
- ☑ FSSAI minimum SNF for flavoured milk: **8.5 %**.

### Fruit Juices
- ☑ **Codex definition:** 100 % juice, no added sugar or salt.
- ☑ **HTST pasteurisation: 90 – 95 °C × 15 – 60 s** (acid food, pH < 4.5).
- ☑ **Botulinum safety boundary: pH 4.6** — juices with pH < 4.6 are *acid foods*.
- ☑ **Brix-Acid Ratio:** orange juice target **14 – 16**.
- ☑ **Pectinase treatment:** 0.05 – 0.1 %, 40 – 50 °C, 1 – 2 h, before pressing.
- ☑ Concentration methods: **MEE (3 – 7 effect), RO, Freeze, OD**.

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- ☑ **Fermentation in tea is a misnomer** — refers to *enzymatic* oxidation, not microbial.
- ☑ **Log reduction in pasteurisation:** $n = t/D_T$, with safety factor **1.5 – 2×**.
- ☑ **Dilution principle:** $V_1 \times C_1 = V_2 \times C_2$ (or $M_1 Bx_1 = M_2 Bx_2$).
- ☑ **HACCP** critical control points: *Enzyme inactivation* (tea), *Pasteurisation* (dairy/juice), *pH* (acid foods).
- ☑ **Always verify the physical feasibility** of mass-balance answers (e.g., negative volumes).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nectar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nectar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nectar (as per FSSAI / Codex Alimentarius standards):**
> Nectar is a **pulpy, non-carbonated ready-to-drink beverage** obtained by blending whole fruit pulp, concentrate, or crushed fruit with water, sugar, honey, sweeteners, and permitted food additives, followed by thermal processing to achieve commercial sterility. It contains a **minimum of 20% fruit content (v/v)** (for standard fruits) or **25%–50%** for selected fruits such as mango, banana, and citrus variants, depending on statutory norms.

**Standard Operational Definition:**
Nectar is an intermediate moisture beverage system that physically suspends **fruit cellular solids (parenchyma tissue)** in an aqueous sugar-acid matrix, stabilized through homogenization, deaeration, and pasteurization. It is distinct from juice (which contains 100% extractable fruit water) and from fruit drinks (which may contain less than 20% fruit content).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a nectar as the **"thick, blended cousin of a fruit juice"**:

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you take a ripe mango, scoop out the soft pulp, but the pulp is too thick to drink directly. You add some water to make it pourable, a spoon of sugar to balance the tartness, and a splash of citric acid to keep the flavor bright. You then heat it briefly to make it safe on the shelf. **That pourable, smooth, fruit-pulp-rich drink is exactly what industry calls a NECTAR.**

Unlike a juice (which is essentially the *liquid squeezed out* of the fruit), a nectar **deliberately keeps the cellular solids** suspended in the liquid — giving it body, opacity, and the mouthfeel of the original fruit.

---

### 1.3 Key Categorical Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Range / Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Minimum Fruit Content** | **20%** (general); **25%–50%** (specific fruits) |
| **Soluble Solids (°Brix)** | **13° – 20° Brix** (post-sugar adjustment) |
| **Acidity (as Citric Acid)** | **0.3% – 0.7% w/v** |
| **pH** | **3.5 – 4.0** |
| **Thermal Process** | **Pasteurization at 90 °C – 95 °C for 30–60 seconds** |
| **Commercial Sterility (log reduction)** | **5-log reduction of target spoilage microbes** |
| **Shelf Life (Aseptic Pack)** | **9–12 months** at ambient storage |
| **Water Activity (aₐ)** | **0.95 – 0.99** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** Examiners love asking about *why nectar needs sugar adjustment* (Brix) and *why the pH must be below 4.5* (acidic environment inhibits *Clostridium botulinum* spore germination — making it a high-acid food, safe for hot-fill processing).

---

### 1.4 The Niche Position of Nectar in the Beverage Family

```
FRESH FRUIT
     │
     ▼
   JUICE (100% liquid extracted) ◄──── squeezed / pressed
     │
     ▼
   NECTAR (20–50% pulp + water + sugar) ◄── THIS MODULE
     │
     ▼
   FRUIT DRINK / BEVERAGE (< 20% fruit) ◄── flavored water base
```

This **positioning hierarchy** is the single most recurring exam point — students must clearly distinguish nectar from juice and from fruit drinks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Nectar Manufacturing

The nectar manufacturing workflow is an **8-stage unit operations chain**:

1. **Raw Material Reception & Ripening Control**
   - Fruits received at **firm-ripe (breaker stage)** to balance pectin integrity and aroma volatiles.
   - Sorting removes bruised, fungal-infected, or under-ripe fruits.

2. **Washing & Sanitization**
   - **Chlorinated water dip (50–100 ppm free chlorine)** for 5–10 minutes.
   - Reduces surface microbial load by **2–3 log CFU/g**.

3. **Peeling, Deseeding, Slicing (Pre-processing)**
   - Mechanical or manual peeling. **Lye peeling (NaOH 2–4%, 80 °C, 30–90 s)** for mango/peach skin removal.

4. **Pulping / Pulverizing**
   - **Pulper / Finisher (mesh size 0.5–1.0 mm)** to separate pulp from seeds, skin, and core.
   - Yield target: **65%–75% pulp recovery** from whole fruit.

5. **Standardization (Formulation Stage)**
   - The **most critical step** for product consistency.
   - Blends pulp with sugar syrup, water, citric acid, and stabilizers until target Brix and acidity are reached.

6. **Deaeration (Vacuum Degassing)**
   - Removes dissolved and entrained oxygen to **< 5 ppm**.
   - Prevents oxidative browning, flavor loss, and vitamin C degradation.

7. **Homogenization**
   - Pressure: **150–250 bar (15–25 MPa)** in a two-stage homogenizer.
   - Reduces particle size to **< 50 microns** for a smooth mouthfeel and prevents phase separation.

8. **Thermal Processing + Aseptic Packaging**
   - **High-Temperature Short-Time (HTST) pasteurization at 90–95 °C for 30–60 s.**
   - Hot-fill into pre-sterilized bottles or aseptic Tetra Pak pouches.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Reason (Engineering Logic) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Ripening control | Starch → Sugar conversion maximizes Brix; reduces astringency |
| Chlorination | Reduces *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria* surface contamination |
| Lye peeling | Preserves pulp yield vs. manual loss; inactivates polyphenol oxidase |
| Pulping | Removes inedible lignified fractions (seeds, core) |
| Standardization | Guarantees **batch-to-batch consistency** — the legal/QC requirement |
| Deaeration | Prevents **headspace oxidation** & preserves vitamin C (ascorbic acid) |
| Homogenization | Prevents **pulp sedimentation** (Stokes' Law suppression) |
| Pasteurization | Achieves **commercial sterility**; extends shelf-life |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **essential calculation tools** for KTU numerical questions on nectar formulation.

| # | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Brix_{mix} = \dfrac{(V_{pulp} \times Brix_{pulp}) + (V_{syrup} \times Brix_{syrup})}{V_{pulp} + V_{syrup}}$ | $V$ = volume (L), Brix in °Bx | Standardization calculations |
| 2 | $V_{syrup} = \dfrac{Brix_{final} \times V_{final} - Brix_{pulp} \times V_{pulp}}{Brix_{syrup} - Brix_{pulp}}$ | Derived from mass balance | Sugar syrup volume required |
| 3 | $\eta_{yield} = \dfrac{M_{pulp,recovered}}{M_{fruit,input}} \times 100$ | Mass in kg, $\eta$ in % | Pulper yield efficiency |
| 4 | $v_{terminal} = \dfrac{2 r^2 (\rho_{pulp} - \rho_{water}) g}{9 \mu}$ | $r$ = particle radius, $\rho$ = density, $\mu$ = viscosity, $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | Stokes' law — pulp sedimentation rate |
| 5 | $\log(N_0 / N) = \dfrac{t \times T - 18}{z}$ | $F$-value of thermal process, $z$ = 8 °C | Pasteurization lethality (decimal reduction) |
| 6 | $a_w = \dfrac{p_{partial}}{p_{pure water}}$ | Dimensionless | Microbial stability indicator |
| 7 | $P_{homo} = \dfrac{2 \gamma}{r} \times C$ | $\gamma$ = interfacial tension, $r$ = droplet radius | Homogenization pressure theory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Avoidance Note:** In all equations above, conditional or absolute values use the *italic English descriptor* "to" or "of" — never the **vertical bar** character — to maintain table integrity.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The nectar production line is the **bridge technology** between traditional country-sold fruit squashes (home-scale) and industrial aseptic-packing (high-volume FMCG). Every large beverage company (e.g., Tropicana, Dabur Real, Paper Boat) runs nectar lines because nectar:

- Uses **"imperfect" fruit** rejected for fresh-market sale (reduces post-harvest loss).
- Generates **higher per-kg revenue** than raw fruit sales.
- Achieves **logistics savings** (concentrate → reconstitute → pack at destination markets).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Brix Standardization for Nectar Formulation

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturer wants to prepare **1000 L of mango nectar** at a final **Brix of 15 °Bx**. The mango pulp has a Brix of **18 °Bx**, and the sugar syrup used has a Brix of **60 °Bx**. Calculate the volume of pulp and sugar syrup required.

**Given:**
- $V_{final} = 1000 \, \text{L}$
- $Brix_{final} = 15 \, \text{°Bx}$
- $Brix_{pulp} = 18 \, \text{°Bx}$
- $Brix_{syrup} = 60 \, \text{°Bx}$

**Step 1: Apply the Mass Balance on Sugar (Soluble Solids)**

Total soluble solids (in terms of °Bx × Volume) entering = Total soluble solids leaving.

$$(V_{pulp} \times Brix_{pulp}) + (V_{syrup} \times Brix_{syrup}) = V_{final} \times Brix_{final}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Mass Balance on Total Volume**

$$V_{pulp} + V_{syrup} = V_{final} \quad \text{(assuming water addition is implicitly negligible)}$$

For a more rigorous formulation with separate water addition $V_w$:

$$V_{pulp} + V_{syrup} + V_{water} = 1000 \, \text{L}$$

**Step 3: Solve the Two-Equation System**

From the volume balance:
$$V_{pulp} = 1000 - V_{syrup}$$

Substitute into the Brix balance:
$$(1000 - V_{syrup}) \times 18 + V_{syrup} \times 60 = 1000 \times 15$$

**Step 4: Expand the Algebra**

$$18000 - 18 V_{syrup} + 60 V_{syrup} = 15000$$

$$42 V_{syrup} = 15000 - 18000$$

$$42 V_{syrup} = -3000$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative result?** This means the pulp Brix is *higher* than the target — we should **dilute with water**, not add sugar syrup. Let us redo this as a **dilution problem**.

**Reformulated Problem:**
Mix pulp (18 °Bx) with water (0 °Bx) to reach 15 °Bx in 1000 L.

$$V_{pulp} \times 18 + V_{water} \times 0 = 1000 \times 15$$

$$V_{pulp} = \dfrac{15000}{18} = 833.33 \, \text{L}$$

$$V_{water} = 1000 - 833.33 = 166.67 \, \text{L}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Mango pulp required = **833.33 L**
- Water required = **166.67 L**
- No sugar syrup needed (since natural Brix exceeds target).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Process Lethality (F-Value Calculation)

**Problem Statement:**
Determine the pasteurization holding time required at **90 °C** to achieve a **5-log microbial reduction**, given that the reference temperature $T_{ref} = 70 \, \text{°C}$ and $z = 8 \, \text{°C}$. The reference $D$-value at 70 °C is $D_{70} = 1.5 \, \text{min}$.

**Step 1: Standard F-Value Definition**

$$F_T^z = D_T \times \log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)$$

where:
- $F_T^z$ = equivalent time at temperature $T$ for a $z$-value inactivation (minutes)
- $D_T$ = decimal reduction time at temperature $T$ (minutes)
- $\log(N_0 \div N)$ = number of log reductions required

**Step 2: Calculate $D_{90}$ from $D_{70}$**

Using the $z$-value relationship:
$$\log\left(\frac{D_{ref}}{D_T}\right) = \dfrac{T - T_{ref}}{z}$$

$$\log\left(\frac{1.5}{D_{90}}\right) = \dfrac{90 - 70}{8} = 2.5$$

$$\dfrac{1.5}{D_{90}} = 10^{2.5} = 316.23$$

$$D_{90} = \dfrac{1.5}{316.23} = 4.74 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{min} = 0.285 \, \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3: Compute Required Holding Time**

$$F_{90}^{8} = D_{90} \times \log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = 0.285 \times 5 = 1.425 \, \text{seconds}$$

**Final Answer:** The minimum holding time at 90 °C is approximately **1.4 seconds** (practically extended to **30–60 seconds** in industry to account for come-up time and process margin).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pulp Sedimentation (Stokes' Law Application)

**Problem Statement:**
A mango nectar has pulp particles of radius $r = 100 \, \mu\text{m}$, pulp density $\rho_{pulp} = 1050 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, water density $\rho_{water} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, and viscosity $\mu = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. Calculate the terminal settling velocity.

**Step 1: Apply Stokes' Law**

$$v_{terminal} = \dfrac{2 r^2 (\rho_{pulp} - \rho_{water}) g}{9 \mu}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Numerical Values**

$$v = \dfrac{2 \times (100 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (1050 - 1000) \times 9.81}{9 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate Numerator**

$$r^2 = (10^{-4})^2 = 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$2 \times 10^{-8} \times 50 \times 9.81 = 9.81 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate Denominator**

$$9 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.045$$

**Step 5: Final Division**

$$v = \dfrac{9.81 \times 10^{-6}}{0.045} = 2.18 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{terminal} \approx 0.218 \, \text{mm/s}}$$

**Interpretation:** In a 200 mm tall bottle, a particle would take **≈ 15 minutes** to settle visibly — which is why **homogenization is non-negotiable** to reduce $r$ and prevent sedimentation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Nectar vs Juice vs Squash vs RTS

| Attribute | Nectar | Juice | Squash (Concentrate) | RTS Beverage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fruit Content | 20–50% | 100% | Variable (diluted) | < 20% |
| Brix (°Bx) | 13–20 | 10–14 (natural) | 30–65 (concentrate) | 10–12 |
| Pulp Presence | Yes (suspended) | No (filtered) | No | No |
| Dilution Required Before Drinking | No (ready) | No (ready) | Yes (mandatory) | No (ready) |
| Common Fruits | Mango, Banana, Guava | Orange, Apple | Lemon, Orange, Pineapple | Mixed |
| Heat Process | Pasteurized | Pasteurized | Hot-fill concentrate | Pasteurized |
| Shelf Life | 6–12 months | 6–9 months | 9–18 months | 4–8 months |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Nectar Manufacturing Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Fruit Reception] --> B[Sorting and Grading]
    B --> C[Washing with Chlorinated Water 50 to 100 ppm]
    C --> D[Peeling and Deseeding]
    D --> E[Pulping Mesh 0.5 to 1.0 mm]
    E --> F[Pulp Collection Tank]
    F --> G[Standardization Sugar Syrup plus Water plus Acid]
    G --> H[Deaeration Vacuum 27 to 30 inHg]
    H --> I[Homogenization 150 to 250 bar]
    I --> J[HTST Pasteurization 90 to 95 C for 30 to 60 s]
    J --> K[Hot Fill at greater than 85 C]
    K --> L[Aseptic Packaging Tetra Pak or Glass Bottle]
    L --> M[Cooling Tunnel]
    M --> N[Labeling and Cartoning]
    N --> O[Finished Goods Warehouse]
```

**Description of Flow:**
The diagram maps the **8-stage linear process** with critical control points (CCP) marked at deaeration (oxygen < 5 ppm), homogenization (particle size < 50 µm), and pasteurization (90–95 °C). Each downstream node depends on the quality delivered by its upstream node — making **pulp quality** the root variable governing final product integrity.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Nectar Formulation Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        P1[Pulp Brix 18]
        P2[Water Brix 0]
        P3[Sugar Syrup Brix 60]
        P4[Citric Acid 50 percent solution]
    end

    subgraph MixingTank[Standardization Mixing Tank]
        M[Volumetric and Brix Blending with Inline Refractometer]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        O1[Final Nectar Brix 15]
        O2[Final Acidity 0.5 percent]
        O3[Final pH 3.8]
    end

    subgraph QC[Quality Control Sensors]
        Q1[Refractometer Brix Loop]
        Q2[pH Probe Loop]
        Q3[Conductivity Sensor Loop]
    end

    P1 --> M
    P2 --> M
    P3 --> M
    P4 --> M

    M --> Q1
    M --> Q2
    M --> Q3

    Q1 --> O1
    Q2 --> O3
    Q3 --> O2

    M --> N[Process Forward to Deaeration]
```

**Description of Architecture:**
The functional architecture isolates the **standardization stage** as a closed-loop control system. The inputs (pulp, water, syrup, acid) enter a mixing tank monitored by three inline sensors (refractometer, pH probe, conductivity sensor). The QC feedback loop dynamically adjusts flow rates to lock the **output Brix, acidity, and pH** at predefined targets. This is the **single most examined diagram-type in KTU 14-mark questions** — students must recognize the closed-loop feedback principle.

---

### 4.3 Cause-and-Effect Diagram: Common Nectar Defects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DEF[Nectar Quality Defect]

    DEF --> C1[Sedimentation]
    DEF --> C2[Phase Separation]
    DEF --> C3[Color Darkening Browning]
    DEF --> C4[Off Flavor]
    DEF --> C5[Short Shelf Life Spoilage]

    C1 --> R1[Inadequate Homogenization]
    C1 --> R2[Excessive Particle Size]

    C2 --> R3[Insufficient Stabilizer Pectin or CMC]
    C2 --> R4[pH Out of Range]

    C3 --> R5[Inadequate Deaeration]
    C3 --> R6[High Storage Temperature]

    C4 --> R7[Microbial Activity]
    R7 --> R8[Insufficient Pasteurization]

    C5 --> R9[Under Pasteurization]
    C5 --> R10[Contaminated Packaging]
```

**Description:**
A fishbone-style cause-effect map linking **5 major defect categories** to **10 root causes**. KTU examiners use this layout to test whether students can **trace defects upstream** to specific unit operations — a key outcome-based skill.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

*Define nectar. State the minimum fruit content requirement as per FSSAI regulations for mango and banana nectar.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Nectar is a pulpy, sweetened, non-carbonated beverage prepared by blending whole fruit pulp (or concentrate) with water, sugar, and permitted additives, followed by pasteurization to commercial sterility.
>
> **Minimum fruit content (FSSAI):**
> - **Mango nectar:** **25%** fruit content (minimum)
> - **Banana nectar:** **25%** fruit content (minimum)
> - General fruits: **20%** fruit content
>
> The product is preserved typically by heat pasteurization and has a shelf life of **6–12 months** in aseptic packaging. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**

*Differentiate between nectar and fruit juice with reference to (a) fruit content, (b) processing steps, and (c) typical Brix.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Nectar | Fruit Juice |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fruit Content | 20–50% (mandatory minimum) | 100% (or reconstituted to 100%) |
| Processing | Blending + Pasteurization | Extraction + Clarification + Pasteurization |
| Typical Brix | 13° – 20° Bx | 10° – 14° Bx (natural) |
| Pulp | Yes, suspended | Usually filtered out |
| Dilution Before Drinking | No | No |

> Nectar contains **visible pulp** and is sweetened; juice is the **filtered liquid extract**. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Part B questions carry **internal choice**. **Attempt ANY ONE** from the two questions provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a flow diagram, describe in detail the **step-by-step manufacturing process of mango nectar**. Indicate critical control points (CCPs) at each stage. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Calculate the volumes of pulp (Brix 18 °Bx), sugar syrup (Brix 60 °Bx), and water required to prepare **2000 L of mango nectar at 16 °Bx** with **25% fruit content by volume**. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> The manufacturing of mango nectar involves the following sequential unit operations:

| Step | Unit Operation | Description & CCP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Raw Material Reception | Mature firm-ripe mangoes, free from pests; **CCP-1: pesticide residue check** |
| 2 | Washing | Dip in 100 ppm chlorinated water for 5 min; **CCP-2: microbial load reduction 2-log** |
| 3 | Peeling & Slicing | Manual or lye peeling (2–4% NaOH, 80 °C, 60 s) |
| 4 | Pulping | Mesh 0.5 mm pulper; yield 65–70%; **CCP-3: visual pulp quality** |
| 5 | Standardization | Add sugar syrup + water + acid; Brix = 16 °Bx, pH = 3.8; **CCP-4: Brix & acidity** |
| 6 | Deaeration | Vacuum 27 inHg; dissolved O₂ < 5 ppm; **CCP-5: vitamin C retention** |
| 7 | Homogenization | 200 bar two-stage; particle size < 50 µm; **CCP-6: sedimentation prevention** |
| 8 | Pasteurization | HTST 95 °C for 45 s; **CCP-7: 5-log microbial kill** |
| 9 | Hot Fill / Aseptic Pack | Fill at > 85 °C; seal in pre-sterilized containers |
| 10 | Cooling & Labeling | Tunnel cooler to 30 °C; labeling and packaging |

> **Critical Control Points Summary (CCP 1–7):** These checkpoints ensure safety (pathogen control), quality (Brix, pH, color), and stability (shelf life). **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $V_{final} = 2000 \, \text{L}$
- $Brix_{final} = 16 \, \text{°Bx}$
- $Brix_{pulp} = 18 \, \text{°Bx}$
- $Brix_{syrup} = 60 \, \text{°Bx}$
- $Brix_{water} = 0 \, \text{°Bx}$
- **Constraint:** Fruit content = 25% of total volume

**Step 1: Calculate Pulp Volume**

$$V_{pulp} = 0.25 \times 2000 = 500 \, \text{L}$$

**Step 2: Apply Brix Balance**

$$\left(V_{pulp} \times Brix_{pulp}\right) + \left(V_{syrup} \times Brix_{syrup}\right) + \left(V_{water} \times Brix_{water}\right) = V_{final} \times Brix_{final}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Known Values**

$$\left(500 \times 18\right) + \left(V_{syrup} \times 60\right) + \left(V_{water} \times 0\right) = 2000 \times 16$$

$$9000 + 60 V_{syrup} = 32000$$

$$60 V_{syrup} = 23000$$

$$V_{syrup} = 383.33 \, \text{L}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Water Volume by Volume Balance**

$$V_{water} = V_{final} - V_{pulp} - V_{syrup}$$

$$V_{water} = 2000 - 500 - 383.33 = 1116.67 \, \text{L}$$

> **Final Answer:** $V_{pulp} = 500 \, \text{L}$; $V_{syrup} = 383.33 \, \text{L}$; $V_{water} = 1116.67 \, \text{L}$. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of each ingredient** in a typical nectar formulation: fruit pulp, sugar, citric acid, water, and permitted stabilizers. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A nectar pasteurizer operates at **88 °C** with $D_{88} = 0.5 \, \text{min}$ and $z = 8 \, \text{°C}$. Calculate the **holding time required to achieve 6-log reduction** of spoilage bacteria. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Role of Each Ingredient:**

| Ingredient | Functional Role | Typical Dose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fruit Pulp** | Source of flavor, color, vitamins, fiber | 20–50% v/v |
| **Sugar (Sucrose)** | Sweetness, Brix adjustment, mouthfeel enhancement | 8–15% w/v |
| **Citric Acid** | pH control (3.5–4.0), flavor enhancement, microbial inhibition | 0.3–0.7% w/v |
| **Water** | Diluent, viscosity adjustment, dissolves additives | Balance to 100% |
| **Stabilizers (Pectin/CMC)** | Prevent pulp sedimentation; increase viscosity | 0.1–0.5% w/v |
| **Ascorbic Acid** | Antioxidant, prevents browning | 0.02–0.05% w/v |

> **Engineering Insight:** Each ingredient is dosed within regulatory and sensory limits. Excess acid causes **syneresis**; excess stabilizer causes **gummy mouthfeel**. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $T = 88 \, \text{°C}$
- $D_{88} = 0.5 \, \text{min}$
- $z = 8 \, \text{°C}$
- Required log reduction = 6

**Step 1: Apply F-Value Equation**

$$F_{88}^{8} = D_{88} \times \log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**

$$F_{88}^{8} = 0.5 \times 6 = 3.0 \, \text{min}$$

> **Verification with $z$-value at reference 70 °C (Optional):**
> $D_{70} = D_{88} \times 10^{(88 - 70)/8} = 0.5 \times 10^{2.25} = 88.9 \, \text{min}$.
> This is consistent — at lower temperature the organism is far more resistant.

> **Final Answer:** Holding time required at 88 °C = **3.0 minutes**. In practice, a process margin of 25–50% is added → effective holding time ≈ **4–5 minutes**. **[7 marks]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 4 Mark-Deduction Traps in Nectar Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing NECTAR with JUICE** — Always mention the **mandatory 20–50% fruit content** and presence of **visible suspended pulp**. Failing to state the fruit-content percentage loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Volume Constraint** — When a question specifies a "25% fruit content" requirement, students often compute only the Brix balance and ignore the volume constraint. This produces an **incorrect formulation** and costs 3–4 marks.
>
> 3. **Missing the CCP Tag** — For process-flow questions, KTU examiners expect **each critical stage labelled with its CCP** (e.g., "Pasteurization — 5-log microbial kill"). Skipping the CCP tag costs 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Units in Brix Calculation** — A common error is writing Brix as "°Bx" without specifying that **°Bx ≈ % soluble solids (w/w)**. State it explicitly to avoid unit ambiguity.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nectar Module**

- **Definition:** Nectar = **20–50% fruit pulp + sugar + water + acid + additives**, pasteurized and aseptically packed. **[Must-know]**
- **Positioning:** Between **Juice** (100% fruit) and **Fruit Drink** (< 20% fruit). **[Hierarchy question]**
- **Brix Range:** Final product must be **13°–20° Bx** (sweetness + microbial stability).
- **pH Range:** **3.5–4.0** (acidic → inhibits *C. botulinum* → safe for hot-fill).
- **Core Formula:** Brix Balance → $V_{syrup} = \dfrac{(Brix_{final} \times V_{final}) - (Brix_{pulp} \times V_{pulp})}{Brix_{syrup} - Brix_{pulp}}$
- **Process Chain:** Washing → Pulping → Standardization → Deaeration → Homogenization → Pasteurization → Aseptic Pack.
- **Homogenization Pressure:** **150–250 bar**; reduces particle size to **< 50 µm**.
- **Pasteurization:** **90–95 °C for 30–60 s** (HTST); achieve **5-log microbial kill**.
- **Deaeration:** Oxygen **< 5 ppm**; prevents browning and vitamin loss.
- **Stabilizers:** **Pectin or CMC (0.1–0.5%)** to prevent pulp sedimentation.
- **Shelf Life:** **9–12 months** in aseptic Tetra Pak; **6 months** in glass bottles.
- **Stokes' Law:** $v_{terminal} = \dfrac{2 r^2 (\rho_{pulp} - \rho_{water}) g}{9 \mu}$ — homogenization reduces $r$ to lower $v$ exponentially.
- **F-Value Logic:** $F_T^z = D_T \times \log(N_0 / N)$ — required for thermal-process calculations.
- **FSSAI Specifics:** Mango/Banana nectar minimum **25%** fruit; general fruits **20%**.
- **Critical Control Points:** Chlorination (CCP), Pulping (CCP), Standardization (CCP), Pasteurization (CCP), Packaging sterility (CCP).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Squash
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT632 - BEVERAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Squash

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Squash** is a concentrated, sweetened, fruit-based beverage prepared by blending fruit juice/pulp with sugar, water, and permitted preservatives, with a minimum total soluble solids (TSS) content of **40°Brix** as per the **Fruit Products Order (FPO), 1955** and **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011 (FSSAI)**.

A standard squash must contain not less than **25% (v/v) fruit juice/pulp content** in the final finished product, ensuring authentic fruit character. The product is intended to be diluted with water (typically 1:3 or 1:4 ratio) by the consumer before consumption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The FPO specifications are the *defining legal standard* for squashes in India. Any deviation in TSS or juice content makes the product legally non-compliant for commercial sale.

### 1.2 Key Compositional Standards (FPO/FSSAI)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Total Soluble Solids (TSS) | **Not less than 40°Brix** |
| Fruit Juice / Pulp Content | **Not less than 25% (v/v)** |
| Acidity (as Citric Acid) | **1.0% – 3.5%** |
| Preservative (SO₂ / KMS) | **350 ppm (max)** |
| Sugar | Sufficient to achieve TSS |
| Water | Balance (q.s.) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Think of squash as a **"fruit concentrate in syrup"** — similar to how a stock cube is a concentrate of flavor:

- 🍊 **Fresh juice** = Pure, but bulky, perishable, expensive to transport.
- 🧃 **Squash** = The "essence" of juice + sugar + preservative, condensed into a shelf-stable, shippable form.
- 🥤 **Ready-to-drink (RTD)** = Just add water to the squash before drinking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** Popular brands like *Rooh Afza*, *Kissan*, and *Mapro* are classic squashes. They are *not* ready-to-drink; they require dilution.

### 1.4 Comparison with Other Beverages

$$ \text{Squash} \neq \text{Cordial} \neq \text{Nectar} \neq \text{Ready-to-Serve (RTS)} $$

- **Cordial** uses clarified juice (no pulp), TSS 30°Brix, juice 25%.
- **Squash** uses juice/pulp (may be turbid), TSS 40°Brix, juice 25%.
- **Nectar** TSS 20°Brix, juice 25%, unsweetened dilute drink.
- **RTS Beverage** TSS 10°Brix, juice 10–25%, ready to consume.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Raw Material Requirements

The quality of squash is determined at the raw material stage. The following inputs are essential:

- **Fruit Juice / Pulp:** Mature, ripe, sound fruits; free from microbial spoilage.
- **Sugar (Sucrose):** Refined cane sugar (pharma/plantation white grade) is preferred over jaggery to avoid color and microbial issues.
- **Water:** Potable, demineralized, and microbiologically safe water.
- **Acidity Regulator:** Citric acid (food grade) or malic acid for natural fruit pH adjustment.
- **Preservatives:** 
  - **Potassium Metabisulphite (KMS)** — most common; releases SO₂.
  - **Sodium Benzoate** — for low-pH fruits where SO₂ may bleach anthocyanins.
- **Optional Additives:** Permitted colors (Sunset Yellow, Tartrazine), permitted flavors, and stabilizers (e.g., pectin for cloudy squashes).

### 2.2 Theoretical Role of Each Component

- **Sugar (Sucrose):** Acts as a humectant, osmotic stressor to microbes, and primary contributor to °Brix.
- **Acid (Citric):** Lowers pH (typically 2.8–3.5) creating a hostile environment for pathogens like *Clostridium botulinum*.
- **SO₂ (from KMS):** Antimicrobial (inhibits yeasts, molds, bacteria); antioxidant (prevents browning); enzyme inhibitor.
- **Heat (during boiling):** Pasteurizes the mixture and inverts some sucrose to glucose/fructose.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
