# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define "Commercial Sterility" in the context of canned beverages. How does it differ from absolute sterility?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Commercial sterility** is the condition achieved by a thermal canning process in which all **pathogenic, toxigenic, and spoilage microorganisms of public health significance**, along with their spores, are inactivated, such that the product is safe for human consumption and shelf-stable under **normal (non-refrigerated) storage conditions**. A small population of **non-pathogenic thermophilic spores** may survive.
>
> In contrast, **absolute sterility** implies the *complete absence* of all viable microorganisms including their spores — a state required for pharmaceuticals and surgical instruments, but **neither necessary nor practically achievable** in food and beverage canning.
>
> *Marking pattern (KTU):* Definition (1.5 marks) + comparison (1 mark) + example (0.5 mark).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three differences between canning and aseptic packaging of beverages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Canning | Aseptic Packaging |
|-------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Product is sterilized *inside* the sealed container | Product is sterilized *outside* (bulk), then filled aseptically |
| 2 | Long time, moderate temperature (e.g., 121 °C, 15 min) | Short time, high temperature (e.g., 140 °C, 4 s) |
| 3 | Lower product quality retention | Superior retention of flavour, colour, and vitamins |

*(Any three such points with one-line justifications → 3 marks.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Canning of Beverages

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, Pattern 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **complete canning process** for a fruit juice beverage, with emphasis on the retorting step. List the critical process parameters and the quality control checks performed at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A canned mango juice (pH = 3.8) is processed in a retort. The D-value of *Alicyclobacillus acidoterrestris* (the target spoilage organism) at 95 °C is 1.5 minutes, with a z-value of 8.5 °C. The initial spore load is $N_0 = 1 \times 10^4$ spores per can. The process is to achieve a 5-log reduction. Calculate the **required F-value** at the can's cold point and the **equivalent holding time at 121.1 °C** (use $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C, $z = 10$ °C). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (a)

**Process flow of canning mango juice:**

1. **Raw material preparation:** Mango pulp is filtered, deaerated, and standardized to 15 °Brix and 0.4 % acidity. Citric acid is added to maintain pH < 3.8 (acidic beverage category, which allows milder thermal processing).
2. **Container preparation:** Tinplate cans (250 mL) are washed, dried, and internally coated with an **epoxy-phenolic lacquer** to prevent tin-iron interaction with the acidic juice.
3. **Filling:** Hot-fill at 88 °C using a rotary piston filler; headspace of 6 % maintained.
4. **Sealing:** Double-seam applied by a mechanical seamer. **Seam tightness** verified by go/no-go gauges.
5. **Retorting:** Cans are loaded into a **batch retort (overpressure type)**. Steam at 105 °C is introduced; temperature is ramped to 100 °C in 8 minutes (CUT) and held for 15 minutes. The overpressure prevents can buckling.
6. **Cooling:** Cans are cooled under 1.0 bar counter-pressure to 35 °C, then discharged.
7. **Quality checks:** Seam teardown analysis, microbiological swab tests (TVC, yeast & mould), Brix/acid verification, headspace vacuum measurement (> 250 mm Hg).

**Critical Control Parameters (CCPs):**

| CCP | Parameter | Target |
|-----|-----------|--------|
| Hot-fill temperature | ≥ 85 °C | 85–92 °C |
| Retort holding temperature | 100 °C ± 1 °C | for pH < 4.5 |
| Holding time | 15 min | per validated schedule |
| Seaming tightness | 70–80 % | per can |
| Headspace vacuum | ≥ 250 mm Hg | post-cooling |

*(7 marks: process description 4 marks + CCPs 2 marks + QC 1 mark)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the required log reduction L:**

$$L = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^4}{10^{-1}}\right) = 5$$

(5-log reduction from $10^4$ to $10^{-1}$, i.e., less than 1 surviving spore per 10 cans — standard commercial target for acid-tolerant spoilage spores.)

**Step 2 — Compute the F-value at the reference temperature of 95 °C (the target organism's reference):**

$$F_{95} = L \cdot D_{95} = 5 \times 1.5 = 7.5 \text{ minutes at 95 °C with } z = 8.5 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Convert $F_{95}$ (z = 8.5 °C) to the standard $F_0$ (z = 10 °C, T_ref = 121.1 °C):**

The general lethality equivalence equation:

$$F_{ref1} \cdot 10^{(T_{ref2} - T_{ref1})/z_{12}} = F_{ref2}$$

But here the z-values differ, so we use the standard conversion:

$$F_0 = F_{T} \cdot 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{z_{ref}}}$$

where the temperature-time integrator is applied to the whole lethal process. Since $F_{95}$ is the equivalent lethality at 95 °C, the F₀ is:

$$F_0 = F_{95} \cdot 10^{\frac{95 - 121.1}{10}} = 7.5 \times 10^{-2.61}$$

$$F_0 = 7.5 \times 2.455 \times 10^{-3} = 1.84 \times 10^{-2} \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent holding time at 121.1 °C:**

This *is* the F₀ value, expressed as a single holding time at 121.1 °C:

$$\boxed{F_0 = 0.0184 \text{ minutes} = 1.1 \text{ seconds at 121.1 °C}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> An acidic beverage (pH 3.8) requires a **very small F₀** because the low pH synergizes with heat to inactivate *A. acidoterrestris*. The 15-minute hold at 100 °C delivers vastly more than the required lethality; this conservative over-processing is standard industry practice to account for the **slowest-heating can** in the retort.

*(7 marks: log reduction 1.5 + F at 95 °C 1.5 + conversion to F₀ 2.5 + interpretation 1.5)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** Aseptic Packaging (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **aseptic packaging system** for UHT-processed fruit juice. Highlight the three pillars of aseptic packaging. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of aseptic packaging compared to conventional canning. Mention at least two industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (a)

**Block diagram of UHT + Aseptic Carton Line:**

Refer to **Section 4.2** above for the full Mermaid schematic. In the descriptive answer:

1. **UHT Section (Bulk Product Sterilization):**
   - Plate heat exchanger heats juice from 25 °C → 95 °C (regenerative) → 140 °C (heating section) in < 4 s.
   - Holding tube (Ø = 25 mm, L = 6 m) maintains 140 °C for 4 s — delivering F₀ ≈ 50 minutes equivalent (more than sufficient for *B. stearothermophilus* spores).
   - Regenerative cooling back to 25 °C; final trim cooling by chilled water to 20 °C.

2. **Aseptic Filler Section:**
   - **Packaging material:** Multilayer laminate (PE / paperboard / Al foil / PE) fed as a reel.
   - **Sterilization of package:** 35 % H₂O₂ bath (6 s) → hot air knife at 250 °C → UV lamp exposure.
   - **Forming:** Roll is shaped into a tube inside the sterile tunnel, longitudinally sealed, cross-sealed at the bottom to form a pouch.
   - **Filling:** Sterile juice is metered into the formed pouch via an aseptic nozzle.
   - **Cross-sealing:** Top is heat-sealed below the juice level, cut into individual cartons.

3. **Sterile Environment:**
   - The filler operates inside a **Class 100 (ISO 5) laminar flow cabinet** with HEPA-filtered (0.3 µm) air at 0.45 m/s, maintaining positive pressure of 12 Pa.

**The Three Pillars (Boxed Statement):**

> **Pillar 1 → Sterile Product** (UHT 140 °C / 4 s)
> **Pillar 2 → Sterile Package** (H₂O₂ + UV + hot air)
> **Pillar 3 → Sterile Environment** (HEPA laminar flow, +12 Pa pressure)

*(7 marks: block diagram 3 marks + UHT section 1.5 marks + filler section 1.5 marks + three pillars 1 mark)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (b)

**Advantages of Aseptic Packaging over Canning:**

1. **Superior Product Quality:** Short-time high-temperature (HTST/UHT) processing preserves heat-labile vitamins (Vitamin C retention > 90 % vs. 60–70 % in retorted juice), colour, and flavour.
2. **Energy Efficiency:** Regenerative heat exchangers recover 85–95 % of the heat, reducing steam consumption by up to 60 % compared to retort-based canning.
3. **Continuous High-Speed Production:** Single lines operate at 5,000–50,000 packs/hour, ideal for large-scale beverage plants.
4. **Logistics Flexibility:** Light-weight laminated cartons (paper-based) reduce transport cost and warehouse footprint compared to heavy metal cans.
5. **No Refrigeration Needed in Distribution:** Provides 6–12 months ambient shelf life for UHT milk; up to 24 months for fruit juices.

**Limitations:**

1. **High Capital Cost:** A complete UHT + aseptic filler line costs ₹15–50 crore vs. ₹2–5 crore for a batch retort line.
2. **Operational Complexity:** Requires strict hygienic discipline, sterile zone validation, and skilled personnel. A single breach in sterile barrier leads to whole-batch spoilage.
3. **Particulate Products are Difficult:** Beverages with suspended particles (e.g., pulpy juices, fruit chunks) cause heat-exchanger fouling and require scraped-surface or ohmic heaters.
4. **Shorter Shelf Life vs. Retort:** Some aseptic products have lower shelf life than canned equivalents, particularly for high-acid beverages.

**Industrial Applications (any two):**

1. **Tetra Pak carton for UHT milk** (Amul, Mother Dairy) — global market leader.
2. **Aseptic PET bottles for flavoured milk** (e.g., Hershey's, Nestlé) using dry-heat + H₂O₂ sterilization of preforms.
3. **Bag-in-box wine and fruit concentrates** (Tetra Brik, Scholle IPN).
4. **Aseptic pouches for energy gels and isotonic beverages.**

*(7 marks: 5 advantages 2.5 + 3 limitations 1.5 + applications 1.5 + comparison statement 1.5)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **F₀ is a *time equivalent*, not a temperature.** Do not state "F₀ = 121.1 °C" — this is a recurring error that costs 0.5–1 mark. Always write **"F₀ = X minutes at the reference temperature of 121.1 °C."**
> 2. **Do not confuse z = 10 °C (the *C. botulinum* standard) with the z of the actual target organism.** When the question specifies a different z (e.g., 8.5 °C for *Alicyclobacillus*), you must convert to F₀ using the *standard* z = 10 °C — examiners deduct 1.5 marks for skipping this conversion.
> 3. **In the "three pillars" of aseptic packaging, students often mention only two (sterile product + sterile package), forgetting the *sterile environment*.** This omission costs 1.5 marks and is the single most common mark-loss in Module 4.
> 4. **Avoid writing "aseptic packaging is just sterilized packaging."** Aseptic packaging is a *system*, not a property of the package.
> 5. **For Part A (3-mark) questions, do not exceed one page.** Lengthy 3-mark answers signal poor exam-time management to the examiner.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Canning** = Fill → Seal → Sterilize in container (retort). Used for beer, soft drinks, energy drinks, acidic vegetable juices.
- **Aseptic Packaging** = Sterilize product (UHT) → Sterilize package (H₂O₂/UV) → Fill inside HEPA-positive-pressure sterile zone.
- **Three Pillars of Aseptic Packaging:** Sterile Product + Sterile Package + Sterile Environment. Memorize this phrase verbatim.
- **Commercial Sterility ≠ Absolute Sterility.** Thermophilic non-pathogenic spores may survive; this is acceptable.
- **D-value** = time for 1-log (90 %) reduction of target organism at a given temperature.
- **z-value** = 10 °C for *C. botulinum* (standard reference); differs for other organisms.
- **F₀** = equivalent sterilization time (in minutes) at the *reference temperature* 121.1 °C with z = 10 °C.
- **F₀ formula:** $F_0 = \int 10^{(T(t) - 121.1)/z} \, dt$. For discrete data, use the Bigelow summation: $F_0 = \sum 10^{(T_i - 121.1)/10} \cdot \Delta t$.
- **Log reduction $L = \log_{10}(N_0/N) = t/D$**.
- **Acidic beverages (pH < 4.5)** can use mild "hot-fill-hold" (e.g., 88 °C / 2 min) because the low pH synergizes with heat. *C. botulinum* cannot grow below pH 4.5.
- **Low-acid beverages (pH > 4.5)** — e.g., milk, dairy beverages, ready-to-drink teas — require full retort (F₀ ≥ 3 min) or UHT (140 °C / 4 s) for *C. botulinum* control.
- **Double-seam integrity** is the single most critical QC check in canning — any defect = whole batch rejected.
- **Headspace** in cans is typically 6–10 % of volume; a partial vacuum forms on cooling.
- **UHT systems:** Plate (for low-viscosity, particle-free) vs. Tubular (slightly viscous) vs. Steam-Injection (DSI, premium quality) vs. Scraped-Surface (for pulpy products).
- **Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂, 30–35 %)** is the most common package sterilant; it decomposes to water + oxygen, leaving no toxic residue.
- **HEPA filter efficiency:** ≥ 99.97 % for 0.3 µm particles (industry standard).
- **Positive pressure** in sterile zone: 10–15 Pa above ambient — any breach causes air to flow *outward*, preventing ingress of contaminants.
- **Industrial aseptic speeds:** 5,000 – 50,000 packs/hour (Tetra Pak, ProMach, GEA).
- **Canning vs. Aseptic:** Canning = better for particulates and high-acid; Aseptic = better for heat-sensitive, continuous, large-volume, low-weight packaging.
- **Shelf life:** Canned beverages 1–3 years; Aseptic cartons 6–12 months (UHT milk) up to 2 years (juice cartons).
- **Indian Regulatory Note:** All canning/aseptic operations must comply with **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) licensing and FSSR 2011** standards, and BIS standards for metal cans (IS 8184, IS 14225).
- **Famous industrial example:** Tetra Brik Aseptic (1972) — revolutionized global juice and milk distribution; Frooti, Amul Taaza, Tropicana, Real Activ — all use aseptic packaging in India.
